title: Q: [S18] A developer wants to log every incoming banking request and the  time it took. Which Spring MVC component is best?
text: [S18] A developer wants to log every incoming banking request and the time it took. Which Spring MVC component is best? A HandlerInterceptor is the best choice because it runs inside the Spring MVC lifecycle and has access to the controller handler and request attributes. I create a class implementing HandlerInterceptor and override preHandle to capture the start time and store it in a request attribute. In afterCompletion, I retrieve the start time, calculate the duration, and log the HTTP method, URI, status code, and elapsed milliseconds. I register the interceptor globally by implementing WebMvcConfigurer and overriding addInterceptors. This gives consistent request logging across all controllers without polluting each method with timing code. A servlet filter is an alternative, but it has less context about the Spring handler.

title: Q: [S19] A custom validation rule checks whether a banking account  number is active. How do you implement it?
text: [S19] A custom validation rule checks whether a banking account number is active. How do you implement it? I implement it as a custom Bean Validation constraint. First, I create an annotation @ActiveAccount annotated with @Constraint(validatedBy = ActiveAccountValidator.class). Then I write the ActiveAccountValidator class implementing ConstraintValidator. Inside isValid, I inject AccountRepository and check whether the account number exists and its status equals ACTIVE. I place @ActiveAccount on the DTO field, such as private String fromAccountNumber. When the controller parameter is annotated with @Valid, Spring runs all validators including this custom one before calling the service. If validation fails, Spring throws MethodArgumentNotValidException, which the global exception handler converts to a 400 response.

title: Q: [S20] A supply-chain service must process a list of delayed orders and  send a notification for each. Write a concise stream-based approach.
text: [S20] A supply-chain service must process a list of delayed orders and send a notification for each. Write a concise stream-based approach. orders.stream().filter(o -> "DELAYED".equals(o.getStatus())).forEach(notificationService::send DelayAlert). This filters delayed orders and invokes the notification service for each. If the notification is slow, replace forEach with parallelStream cautiously or hand it off to an async task executor.

title: Q: [S21] A banking report endpoint currently returns entities directly. The  audit team asks you to hide certain fields. What is the safest approach?
text: [S21] A banking report endpoint currently returns entities directly. The audit team asks you to hide certain fields. What is the safest approach? Introduce DTOs and map only the fields permitted by the audit policy. Use MapStruct or a manual mapper to convert from entity to DTO. Remove any @JsonIgnore band-aids on entities, because entities should not be shaped by API concerns. Return the DTO from the controller.

title: Q: [S22] A PATCH request to update a banking customer profile fails  because required fields are missing. Is this expected?
text: [S22] A PATCH request to update a banking customer profile fails because required fields are missing. Is this expected? Yes, this is expected if validation annotations require fields that are not being sent. For PATCH, the DTO should only contain updatable fields, and validation groups should mark those fields as optional for the patch group. Alternatively, use a separate CustomerPatchDto with relaxed validation and apply @Null or optional checks explicitly.

title: Q: [S23] A supply-chain endpoint returns LazyInitializationException  after moving to a REST controller. Why?
text: [S23] A supply-chain endpoint returns LazyInitializationException after moving to a REST controller. Why? The exception happens because a lazily loaded collection, such as order.getItems(), is accessed after the Hibernate session has closed. In a REST controller, Jackson serializes the returned entity to JSON. If Jackson tries to read a lazy collection during serialization, but the transaction and persistence context are already closed, Hibernate cannot fetch the data and throws LazyInitializationException. The proper fix is to load all needed data inside the service's transaction before returning it. Options include using JOIN FETCH in the repository query, defining an entity graph with @EntityGraph, using a DTO projection with @Query, or mapping the entity to a DTO within the transaction so Jackson only sees already- loaded data. Returning entities directly from REST controllers is generally discouraged for this reason.

title: Q: [S24] A banking application must ensure no duplicate account numbers  are submitted in a batch import. How can streams help?
text: [S24] A banking application must ensure no duplicate account numbers are submitted in a batch import. How can streams help? Convert the list to a stream and collect into a Set to detect duplicates, or use Collectors.groupingBy and filter groups with count greater than one. For large batches, combine stream checking with a database unique constraint as the final guard against duplicates.

title: Q: [S25] A Spring Boot test for a controller fails because the service is not a  bean. What annotation fixes this?
text: [S25] A Spring Boot test for a controller fails because the service is not a bean. What annotation fixes this? Add @MockBean on the service field inside the @WebMvcTest class. Spring replaces the real service with a Mockito mock in the test context. Then configure stubbing with when(service.method(...)).thenReturn(...) to control the controller's behavior during the test.

title: Q: [Q1] What is an ORM, and what does Hibernate do?
text: [Q1] What is an ORM, and what does Hibernate do? ORM stands for Object-Relational Mapping. It maps Java objects to database tables so developers can work with objects instead of writing raw SQL. Hibernate is an ORM implementation for Java. In a banking app, instead of writing JDBC to insert an account row, you create an Account entity object and call repository.save(account). Hibernate translates that into an INSERT statement. Remember that Hibernate is one JPA provider; EclipseLink and OpenJPA are others, and switching providers changes provider-specific tuning but keeps the annotation model.

title: Q: [Q2] What is the difference between JPA and Hibernate?
text: [Q2] What is the difference between JPA and Hibernate? JPA is a specification that defines how to map Java objects to relational tables. Hibernate is the most popular implementation of JPA. Spring Data JPA sits on top of Hibernate and generates repository implementations for you. When a query misbehaves, you usually need to inspect Hibernate SQL logs or SessionFactory settings, not just the Spring Data method name.

title: Q: [Q3] What happens internally when you call  repository.save(entity)?
text: [Q3] What happens internally when you call repository.save(entity)? Spring Data JPA routes the call to SimpleJpaRepository.save. It checks the entity ID. If the ID is null or the version indicates a new instance, it calls entityManager.persist(entity). Otherwise it calls merge(entity). The entity becomes associated with the current persistence context. The actual INSERT/UPDATE is usually deferred until flush time, typically at transaction commit. If the JSON payload already contains an ID, Hibernate may perform a merge and update an existing row instead of inserting.

title: Q: [Q4] What is the difference between persist() and merge()?
text: [Q4] What is the difference between persist() and merge()? persist() takes a transient entity and makes it managed. It should not have an existing ID. merge() copies the state of a detached entity into a managed instance, inserting if needed. persist() returns the same instance; merge() returns the managed copy. Calling merge() with a partially populated detached entity can overwrite non-null database fields with nulls, so partial updates should fetch the managed entity first.

title: Q: [Q5] What is an Entity lifecycle in Hibernate?
text: [Q5] What is an Entity lifecycle in Hibernate? Entities move through four states: transient (new object, no ID, unknown to Hibernate), persistent (associated with the EntityManager, changes tracked and flushed at commit), detached (was persistent but session closed, changes not tracked), and removed (marked for deletion, row deleted at flush). A controller that maps JSON to an entity and calls save() may accidentally update an existing row if the JSON contains an ID.

title: Q: [Q6] What is the persistence context, and how does it act as a first-level  cache?
text: [Q6] What is the persistence context, and how does it act as a first-level cache? The persistence context is the EntityManager’s in-memory cache of managed entities. Within a single session, loading the same entity by ID twice returns the same Java instance, and dirty checking happens against this cache at flush time. Reading an entity, modifying it, and reading it again in the same transaction shows the in-memory change before flush, but another process updating the database between reads can cause stale data because the cache is isolated to one session.

title: Q: [Q7] What is the difference between CrudRepository,  PagingAndSortingRepository, and JpaRepository?
text: [Q7] What is the difference between CrudRepository, PagingAndSortingRepository, and JpaRepository? CrudRepository provides basic CRUD operations. PagingAndSortingRepository adds pagination and sorting support. JpaRepository adds bulk operations (saveAll, deleteInBatch), saveAndFlush, flush, getReferenceById, and Query By Example. Use JpaRepository for new Spring Boot code and CrudRepository only when you want to avoid coupling to JPA-specific features.

title: Q: [Q8] What is FetchType.LAZY vs FetchType.EAGER?
text: [Q8] What is FetchType.LAZY vs FetchType.EAGER? LAZY loads related data only when it is accessed. EAGER loads it immediately with the parent entity. Eager loading on broad object graphs causes large SQL joins, Cartesian products, memory bloat, and unpredictable performance. Lazy loading keeps the initial query small but requires explicit loading inside a transaction to avoid LazyInitializationException.

title: Q: [Q9] What is the N+1 SELECT problem?
text: [Q9] What is the N+1 SELECT problem? It happens when one query fetches N parent rows, and then accessing a lazy collection on each parent triggers N additional SELECT statements. Fix it with JOIN FETCH, entity graphs, batch fetching, DTO projections, or subselect fetching. A customer list endpoint that returns 100 orders and lazily loads each order’s items outside the service can make 101 queries instead of one, causing high database CPU under load.

title: Q: [Q10] What is LazyInitializationException, and what are the  correct fixes?
text: [Q10] What is LazyInitializationException, and what are the correct fixes? It occurs when code accesses a lazy association after the EntityManager has closed. In Spring web apps this often happens during JSON serialization because the session was bound to the now-completed transaction. Fix it by loading all needed data inside the transaction using JOIN FETCH or an entity graph, or by returning DTO projections. Do not enable Open Session in View as a default fix because it hides the problem and causes N+1 issues in production.

title: Q: [Q11] What is the difference between findById() and  getReferenceById()?
text: [Q11] What is the difference between findById() and getReferenceById()? findById() hits the database immediately and returns an Optional with the full entity. getReferenceById() returns a Hibernate proxy without a database round trip and is useful when you only need a foreign-key reference. If the referenced row does not exist, the proxy throws EntityNotFoundException only when a property is accessed.

title: Q: [Q12] Explain mappedBy and @JoinColumn with an example.
text: [Q12] Explain mappedBy and @JoinColumn with an example. In a bidirectional relationship, only one side owns the foreign key. The owning side uses @JoinColumn; the inverse side uses mappedBy. @Entity public class Order { @OneToMany(mappedBy = "order", cascade = CascadeType.ALL, orphanRemoval = true) private List<OrderItem> items = new ArrayList<>(); } @Entity public class OrderItem { @ManyToOne(fetch = FetchType.LAZY) @JoinColumn(name = "order_id") private Order order; } Forgetting mappedBy or putting @JoinColumn on both sides creates a duplicate foreign-key column or a join table, leading to inconsistent data.

title: Q: [Q13] What are cascade types and orphanRemoval?
text: [Q13] What are cascade types and orphanRemoval? Cascade types propagate operations from a parent to its children: PERSIST, MERGE, REMOVE, REFRESH, DETACH, and ALL. orphanRemoval = true deletes a child entity when it is removed from the parent collection, even if the parent still exists. CascadeType.ALL on broad or shared relationships is dangerous because deleting an order line could cascade to a shared product catalog.

title: Q: [Q14] What is @Transactional, and what does it do under the hood?
text: [Q14] What is @Transactional, and what does it do under the hood? Spring creates a proxy around the annotated bean. When the method is called, the proxy starts or joins a transaction, binds an EntityManager to it, executes the method, and commits or rolls back based on the outcome. By default, rollback happens for unchecked exceptions and Error, not for checked exceptions. A @Transactional annotation on a private, final, or static method is ignored because Spring cannot proxy it, and calling the method from within the same class bypasses the proxy.

title: Q: [Q15] What are transaction propagation levels?
text: [Q15] What are transaction propagation levels? REQUIRED joins the existing transaction or creates a new one and is the default. REQUIRES_NEW suspends the current transaction and starts a new one, useful for audit logging that must commit even if the main transaction rolls back. MANDATORY requires an existing transaction and throws if none exists. SUPPORTS, NOT_SUPPORTED, and NEVER are rarely used and control whether the method participates in an existing transaction. REQUIRES_NEW still uses the same physical database connection unless configured otherwise.

title: Q: [Q16] What does @Transactional(readOnly = true) do?
text: [Q16] What does @Transactional(readOnly = true) do? It tells Spring and Hibernate that the transaction only reads. Hibernate can skip dirty checking and avoid unnecessary flushes, and some connection pools or replication drivers may route read-only transactions to read replicas. It does not prevent writes at the database level; a modifying native query inside a read-only transaction will still execute.

title: Q: [Q17] What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic locking?
text: [Q17] What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic locking? Optimistic locking uses a @Version column. Each update checks that the version has not changed. If another transaction modified the row, the update fails with OptimisticLockException. Pessimistic locking uses database locks (PESSIMISTIC_READ / PESSIMISTIC_WRITE) to serialize access. Optimistic locking is better for read-heavy workloads with occasional contention; pessimistic locking is better when contention is high and you want to serialize access rather than retry.

title: Q: [Q18] What are the ACID properties?
text: [Q18] What are the ACID properties? Atomicity means a transaction completes entirely or not at all. Consistency means the database remains in a valid state after the transaction. Isolation means concurrent transactions do not interfere with each other. Durability means committed changes survive failures. A fund transfer must debit one account and credit another atomically, otherwise the books will be out of balance.

title: Q: [Q19] What are SQL isolation levels?
text: [Q19] What are SQL isolation levels? READ UNCOMMITTED allows dirty reads. READ COMMITTED prevents dirty reads. REPEATABLE READ prevents dirty and non-repeatable reads. SERIALIZABLE prevents all anomalies, including phantom reads. Spring/Hibernate usually follows the database default, such as READ COMMITTED in Oracle and PostgreSQL, and REPEATABLE READ in MySQL InnoDB.

title: Q: [Q20] What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING?
text: [Q20] What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING? WHERE filters rows before grouping. HAVING filters groups after aggregation. You cannot use aggregate functions in WHERE except inside a subquery.

title: Q: [Q21] What are the differences between DDL, DML, DCL, and TCL?
text: [Q21] What are the differences between DDL, DML, DCL, and TCL? DDL (Data Definition Language) includes CREATE, ALTER, DROP, and TRUNCATE. DML (Data Manipulation Language) includes SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE. DCL (Data Control Language) includes GRANT and REVOKE. TCL (Transaction Control Language) includes COMMIT, ROLLBACK, and SAVEPOINT.

title: Q: [Q22] Explain INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, and FULL JOIN.
text: [Q22] Explain INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, and FULL JOIN. INNER JOIN returns rows that have matches in both tables. LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right, filling with nulls where there is no match. RIGHT JOIN is the reverse of LEFT JOIN. FULL JOIN returns rows when there is a match in either table. A report that lists all customers and their orders should use LEFT JOIN; using INNER JOIN silently drops customers who have not placed an order.

title: Q: [Q23] What is the difference between UNION and UNION ALL?
text: [Q23] What is the difference between UNION and UNION ALL? UNION removes duplicates and is slower. UNION ALL keeps duplicates and is faster. Use UNION ALL when the sources are already disjoint or duplicates are acceptable.

title: Q: [Q24] What is normalization vs denormalization?
text: [Q24] What is normalization vs denormalization? Normalization reduces redundancy and improves data integrity by organizing data into related tables. Denormalization intentionally adds redundancy to improve read performance. Denormalize for read-heavy reporting, pre-computed aggregates, and low-write / high-read workloads, but you must have a strategy to keep redundant data consistent.

title: Q: [Q25] What is an index, and how does it help?
text: [Q25] What is an index, and how does it help? An index is a data structure, typically a B-tree, that speeds up data retrieval by letting the database find rows without scanning the entire table. Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes and consume disk space.

title: Q: [Q26] What is a composite index?
text: [Q26] What is a composite index? A composite index is an index on multiple columns. Column order matters. A composite index on (status, created_date) helps a query filtering by both columns, but may not help a query that filters only on created_date.

title: Q: [Q27] What is a covering index?
text: [Q27] What is a covering index? A covering index contains all the columns needed by a query. The database can answer the query entirely from the index without looking up the table heap, reducing I/O significantly. It helps read-heavy queries with stable access patterns.

title: Q: [Q28] What is the difference between a primary key and a unique key?
text: [Q28] What is the difference between a primary key and a unique key? A primary key uniquely identifies a row, cannot be null, and there is only one per table. A unique key also enforces uniqueness but allows one null per column in most databases, and a table can have multiple unique keys.

title: Q: [Q29] What is a foreign key?
text: [Q29] What is a foreign key? A foreign key enforces referential integrity between tables. It prevents orphaned rows and can cascade deletes or updates. The cost is additional index maintenance and potential locking overhead during writes.

title: Q: [Q30] What are stored procedures and triggers?
text: [Q30] What are stored procedures and triggers? A stored procedure is a precompiled set of SQL statements stored in the database. A trigger automatically executes in response to an INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE event. Triggers can make debugging hard because business logic is hidden from the application code. Stored procedures can centralize complex logic but reduce portability.

title: Q: [Q31] What is the difference between DELETE, TRUNCATE, and DROP?
text: [Q31] What is the difference between DELETE, TRUNCATE, and DROP? DELETE removes specific rows, logs each row, can be rolled back in a transaction, and fires triggers. TRUNCATE removes all rows quickly with minimal logging, generally cannot be rolled back, and resets auto-increment counters. DROP removes the entire table structure, data, and dependencies. Prefer DELETE with a WHERE clause for controlled removal in production; TRUNCATE and DROP require change-management scrutiny.

title: Q: [Q32] What are aggregate functions in SQL?
text: [Q32] What are aggregate functions in SQL? Aggregate functions perform calculations on a set of values. Common examples are COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, and MAX. They are usually used with GROUP BY.

title: Q: [Q33] What is the difference between GROUP BY and ORDER BY?
text: [Q33] What is the difference between GROUP BY and ORDER BY? GROUP BY groups rows with the same values into summary rows. ORDER BY sorts the result set. You can use them together.

title: Q: [Q34] What are window functions?
text: [Q34] What are window functions? Window functions perform calculations across a set of rows related to the current row without collapsing the result set. Examples are ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), LEAD(), LAG(), and SUM() OVER. To find the most recent transaction per account: SELECT account_number, amount, transaction_date FROM ( SELECT account_number, amount, transaction_date, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY account_number ORDER BY transaction_date DESC) rn FROM transactions ) t WHERE rn = 1;

title: Q: [Q35] What is a CTE?
text: [Q35] What is a CTE? A Common Table Expression (WITH ...) defines a temporary named result set. It improves readability for complex queries and enables recursion with WITH RECURSIVE. Some databases materialize CTEs, which can affect performance.

title: Q: [Q36] What is pagination in Spring Data JPA?
text: [Q36] What is pagination in Spring Data JPA? Pagination splits large result sets into pages. A repository method accepts a Pageable and returns a Page that includes content, total count, total pages, and metadata. Example: Page findByAccountNumber(String accountNumber, Pageable pageable);. Always cap the page size to prevent unbounded result sets.

title: Q: [Q37] What is @Query in Spring Data JPA?
text: [Q37] What is @Query in Spring Data JPA? @Query lets you define custom JPQL or native SQL for a repository method. @Query("SELECT o FROM Order o WHERE o.status = :status") List<Order> findByStatus(@Param("status") String status); Use native SQL when you need database-specific features like window functions, CTEs, or hints.

title: Q: [Q38] What is @Modifying?
text: [Q38] What is @Modifying? @Modifying marks a @Query method that performs an UPDATE or DELETE. It requires an active transaction. Use clearAutomatically = true if you want the persistence context cleared afterward to avoid stale entities.

title: Q: [Q39] What is the Specification API?
text: [Q39] What is the Specification API? It lets you build dynamic, type-safe predicates programmatically. Each filter is a Specification that returns a Predicate, and they can be combined with and() / or(). It is best for search screens with optional filters.

title: Q: [Q40] What are projections?
text: [Q40] What are projections? Projections return only selected fields instead of full entities. They reduce memory use, avoid lazy-loading issues, and improve serialization performance. Interface-based projections are called closed projections; class-based DTOs are open projections.

title: Q: [Q41] What is Query By Example?
text: [Q41] What is Query By Example? QBE lets you pass a probe entity with non-null fields as search criteria. It is useful for simple exact-match searches but falls short for range queries, LIKE, nested conditions, and dynamic optional filters.

title: Q: [Q42] What is the difference between first-level and second-level cache?
text: [Q42] What is the difference between first-level and second-level cache? The first-level cache is the persistence context; it caches entities within one EntityManager session. The second-level cache sits above the persistence context and caches entities across sessions using providers such as EhCache, Caffeine, or Redis. The second-level cache is best for reference data that changes rarely, such as country codes or product categories.

title: Q: [Q43] What is the query cache, and why can it be a trap?
text: [Q43] What is the query cache, and why can it be a trap? The query cache stores the IDs returned by a query. The second time the query runs, Hibernate looks up the IDs and fetches the entities from the second-level cache. If the second-level cache is not configured, or if the underlying tables change frequently, the query cache adds overhead without benefit.

title: Q: [Q44] How do you optimize batch inserts with Spring Data JPA?
text: [Q44] How do you optimize batch inserts with Spring Data JPA? Set spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.jdbc.batch_size to 50 or 100, process data in chunks and call saveAll(chunk), periodically flush and clear the persistence context, use a SEQUENCE ID strategy with a matching allocation size, and avoid IDENTITY for large batches. For very large volumes, consider StatelessSession or raw JDBC batching.

title: Q: [Q45] What is a schema migration tool?
text: [Q45] What is a schema migration tool? Tools like Flyway and Liquibase manage database schema changes through versioned scripts. They make environments reproducible and avoid the risks of ddl-auto = update in production. Using spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=update in production can drop columns or alter data unexpectedly.

title: Q: [Q46] What is the difference between a view and a materialized view?
text: [Q46] What is the difference between a view and a materialized view? A view is a virtual query; it does not store data. A materialized view stores the result physically and must be refreshed. Materialized views are useful for expensive aggregations in reporting but add refresh complexity.

title: Q: [Q47] What is a covering index, and when does it help?
text: [Q47] What is a covering index, and when does it help? A covering index includes all columns a query needs. The database answers the query from the index alone, avoiding table lookups. It helps read-heavy queries with stable access patterns.

title: Q: [Q48] How do you detect whether a query uses an index?
text: [Q48] How do you detect whether a query uses an index? Use the database execution plan. In MySQL use EXPLAIN SELECT ..., in PostgreSQL use EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT ..., in Oracle use EXPLAIN PLAN FOR ..., and in SQL Server use the execution plan viewer or SET SHOWPLAN_ALL ON. Look for type=ALL or Seq Scan as signs of a full table scan.

title: Q: [Q49] What is the difference between save() and saveAndFlush()?
text: [Q49] What is the difference between save() and saveAndFlush()? save() schedules the entity for insertion/update and defers SQL until flush. saveAndFlush() forces an immediate flush. Use saveAndFlush sparingly, only when the next step in the same transaction needs the row visible in the database.

title: Q: [Q50] What are composite keys, and how do you map them?
text: [Q50] What are composite keys, and how do you map them? A composite key uses multiple columns to uniquely identify a row. Use @EmbeddedId with an @Embeddable class for clean mapping, or @IdClass when matching legacy schemas.

title: Q: [Q51] What is @EnableJpaAuditing?
text: [Q51] What is @EnableJpaAuditing? It enables automatic population of audit fields such as @CreatedDate, @LastModifiedDate, @CreatedBy, and @LastModifiedBy. It removes audit boilerplate and supports compliance requirements.

title: Q: [Q52] What is Hibernate Envers?
text: [Q52] What is Hibernate Envers? Hibernate Envers provides entity versioning and audit history. It tracks every insert, update, and delete, storing snapshots in audit tables. It is useful for compliance and debugging data changes.

title: Q: [Q53] What is the difference between getReferenceById and  findById?
text: [Q53] What is the difference between getReferenceById and findById? findById loads the full entity immediately. getReferenceById returns a proxy without hitting the database. Use getReferenceById when you only need a foreign-key reference; use findById when you need the data.

title: Q: [Q54] How do you handle an OptimisticLockException in production?
text: [Q54] How do you handle an OptimisticLockException in production? For user-facing flows, return 409 Conflict or retry a bounded number of times with backoff. For batch jobs, log the conflict and continue or move the record to a retry queue. For high- contention flows, consider redesigning with queues or pessimistic locking.

title: Q: [Q55] What is a deadlock, and how do you prevent it?
text: [Q55] What is a deadlock, and how do you prevent it? A deadlock occurs when two transactions each hold a lock and wait for a lock held by the other. Prevent it by always locking resources in a consistent order, such as by account ID ascending, or by using a single update statement where possible.

title: Q: [Q56] What is the purpose of @Column, @Table, and @Entity?
text: [Q56] What is the purpose of @Column, @Table, and @Entity? @Entity marks a class as mapped. @Table maps it to a specific table and schema. @Column maps a field to a specific column with constraints like name, nullable, and length. A new Java entity Transaction may map to a legacy Oracle table TXN_HISTORY with columns like ACCT_NBR and TXN_AMT.

title: Q: [Q57] What is @Temporal, and is it still relevant?
text: [Q57] What is @Temporal, and is it still relevant? @Temporal maps java.util.Date or Calendar to a SQL date/time/timestamp. With Java 8+, prefer java.time types such as LocalDate, LocalDateTime, or Instant, which are type-safe and avoid timezone ambiguities.

title: Q: [Q58] What is a Hibernate proxy?
text: [Q58] What is a Hibernate proxy? A Hibernate proxy is a runtime subclass created for lazy loading. It loads data only when a method is called. Because of this, entity.getClass() may return a proxy class name, and equality checks using getClass() can fail. Use Hibernate.getClass(entity) or instanceof in equals().

title: Q: [Q59] What are the risks of returning JPA entities directly from a REST  controller?
text: [Q59] What are the risks of returning JPA entities directly from a REST controller? Serialization may trigger LazyInitializationException, bidirectional relationships can cause infinite recursion, internal fields leak to the API contract, and future schema changes break API consumers. The better approach is to return a DTO mapped inside the service layer.

title: Q: [Q60] What is the difference between Page<t> and Slice<t>?</t></t>
text: [Q60] What is the difference between Page<t> and Slice<t>?</t></t> Page includes the total count of records, which requires an extra COUNT query. Slice only knows whether there is a next page, so it is cheaper when the total count is not needed.

title: Q: [S1] A banking customer reports that a duplicate transaction was created  during a fund transfer. How do you investigate and prevent this?
text: [S1] A banking customer reports that a duplicate transaction was created during a fund transfer. How do you investigate and prevent this? Duplicate transactions usually come from retry behavior on the client side, missing idempotency, or a race condition on the server. I start by checking the request logs for the same client request sent twice, looking at timestamps, correlation IDs, and business reference numbers. I query the transaction table for rows with the same reference or amount around the reported time. To prevent recurrence I introduce an idempotency layer: either store processed keys in a dedicated table with a unique constraint keyed by idempotency_key and client_id, or use a naturally unique business reference. The service should first check for an existing record before processing, and on a duplicate it returns the previously computed result. The operation must remain inside a transaction so the idempotency record and the business outcome are committed atomically.

title: Q: [S2] An API that worked in staging is slow in production. What do you  check first?
text: [S2] An API that worked in staging is slow in production. What do you check first? The first step is to reproduce the slow call in production and capture the exact SQL being executed using show-sql, a query log, or an APM trace. Then I compare the production execution plan against staging with the same query shape. Production has larger data volumes, different data distributions, and possibly missing indexes. Common causes are N+1 selects from lazy collections, a missing index that existed in staging, stale table statistics leading to bad plans, lock contention, or a different Hibernate/datasource configuration. I also verify whether connection- pool settings, batch size, and cache configuration are identical. The fix depends on the root cause: add or rebuild the index, rewrite the query with JOIN FETCH or a projection, tune batching, or paginate large result sets.

title: Q: [S3] A report query times out after a schema change. What is your  approach?
text: [S3] A report query times out after a schema change. What is your approach? I run EXPLAIN / EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the report query against the new schema to see if the plan changed. The usual culprits are a dropped index, a changed column type that prevents index usage, stale statistics after the change, or the optimizer choosing a nested-loop join over a hash join because cardinality estimates are wrong. I also confirm the query still references the correct columns and that no implicit conversion was introduced. If the report is intrinsically expensive, I consider a materialized view, a pre-aggregated summary table, or moving the workload to a read replica. If the performance regression is blocking business users and the fix will take time, the safest step is to roll back the schema change while a permanent solution is designed and tested.

title: Q: [S4] After a deployment, orders and their items are out of sync — some  items have no order. What happened?
text: [S4] After a deployment, orders and their items are out of sync — some items have no order. What happened? Orphaned items usually mean the relationship was modified without maintaining both sides of a bidirectional mapping, or a cascade was misconfigured. For example, if code removes an item from Order.items but does not set orderItem.order = null, Hibernate may not recognize the change. Another cause is CascadeType.REMOVE or orphanRemoval set incorrectly on the wrong side. A bulk delete or a manual migration that bypassed JPA cascades can also leave child rows behind. To fix it I audit the relationship code, ensure helper methods update both sides, remove dangerous cascades, and add a database-level foreign key if one is missing. For existing orphans I run a cleanup script and add a not-null constraint on the foreign key to prevent recurrence.

title: Q: [S5] A nightly batch job starts failing with OutOfMemoryError. It  processes millions of rows with saveAll().
text: [S5] A nightly batch job starts failing with OutOfMemoryError. It processes millions of rows with saveAll(). The persistence context accumulates every entity loaded or saved until the transaction commits, so a single transaction over millions of rows eventually exhausts heap memory. The fix is to process data in chunks, for example 500 or 1,000 records at a time, and after each chunk call entityManager.flush() followed by entityManager.clear() to detach the processed entities. I also set hibernate.jdbc.batch_size to match the chunk size and use a SEQUENCE ID generator with a matching allocation size, because IDENTITY forces an immediate insert per entity and breaks batching. If the job is purely insert-only and extremely large, StatelessSession or raw JDBC batching is a better choice because it avoids the dirty- checking and event-listener overhead of the full persistence context.

title: Q: [S6] A dashboard endpoint causes LazyInitializationException  only under load. Why?
text: [S6] A dashboard endpoint causes LazyInitializationException only under load. Why? Under normal load the controller serializes the response before the transaction-bound session closes, so lazy associations happen to work. Under load, thread scheduling, connection-pool wait times, or asynchronous serialization can delay JSON rendering until after the transaction and session have ended. The real fix is not to rely on timing. Load every lazy association inside the service transaction with JOIN FETCH or an entity graph, or return DTOs that contain only the data needed by the UI. Enabling Open Session in View (OSIV) is a workaround that keeps the session open during rendering, but it hides the underlying design problem and often leads to N+1 queries in production.

title: Q: [S7] Two concurrent fund transfers between the same accounts  deadlock. How do you resolve it?
text: [S7] Two concurrent fund transfers between the same accounts deadlock. How do you resolve it? A deadlock happens when transaction A locks account 1 and waits for account 2, while transaction B locks account 2 and waits for account 1. I reproduce it with a test that runs two transfers in opposite directions concurrently. The permanent fix is to always acquire locks in a deterministic order, such as by account ID ascending, in every code path that updates multiple accounts. If locking is required, use LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE on the accounts within a transaction. I also add a small, bounded retry with exponential backoff for legitimate lock waits, but the primary defense is consistent lock ordering. An alternative is to serialize transfers between the same accounts through a queue or a single account-pair mutex.

title: Q: [S8] A cache hit rate is high, but users still see stale data after an update.  What could be wrong?
text: [S8] A cache hit rate is high, but users still see stale data after an update. What could be wrong? A high hit rate only means the cache is being read; it does not mean the data is correct. Stale data usually means the update path did not invalidate or refresh the cache entry. In a multi- instance deployment, each node may have a local cache that is not synchronized, so one instance updates the database while another continues serving old data from its local cache. Solutions include switching to a shared cache such as Redis, adding explicit cache invalidation in the write path, using cache eviction events, or choosing a shorter TTL if eventual staleness is acceptable. The cache key must also include all parameters that affect the result, otherwise an update in one dimension may leave stale entries for another dimension.

title: Q: [S9] A query returns different totals each time it is run during market  hours. Why?
text: [S9] A query returns different totals each time it is run during market hours. Why? The query is running against a live transactional table while new rows are continuously inserted, committed, or rolled back. Aggregations over active tables are inherently non-repeatable unless the transaction runs in a snapshot isolation level or against a static snapshot. Solutions include reading from a read replica that reduces contention on the primary, using snapshot isolation if the database supports it, running the report against a materialized view or pre-aggregated table, or scheduling the report during a quiet window when write volume is low. For business-critical reports, an event-sourced or CDC-based reporting pipeline gives the most consistent results.

title: Q: [S10] A service throws DataIntegrityViolationException after a  code change. How do you debug?
text: [S10] A service throws DataIntegrityViolationException after a code change. How do you debug? DataIntegrityViolationException is a Spring wrapper, so I read the nested root cause to identify the exact constraint that failed: a unique constraint, a not-null constraint, a check constraint, or a foreign-key violation. The name of the constraint usually points to the table and column. I then compare the failing request against the changed code to see whether it now inserts duplicates, omits a required field, passes a value that is too long, or references a parent row that no longer exists. The fix generally includes adding server-side validation before the database call and returning a clear domain-specific error to the caller instead of leaking the raw SQL exception.

title: Q: [S11] A new @Transactional service does not roll back on a custom  checked exception. What is the fix?
text: [S11] A new @Transactional service does not roll back on a custom checked exception. What is the fix? By default Spring rolls back only on unchecked exceptions and Error, not on checked exceptions. The fix is to add rollbackFor = MyException.class to the @Transactional annotation, or rollbackFor = Exception.class if every exception should roll back. I also verify that the exception is not being caught and swallowed inside the method, because a swallowed exception never reaches the Spring proxy. Finally I confirm the method is public and is called through the Spring proxy; if it is private, final, or invoked from within the same bean, the annotation is ignored entirely.

title: Q: [S12] After switching databases, native queries fail. What do you check?
text: [S12] After switching databases, native queries fail. What do you check? Native queries are tied to a specific database dialect, so moving from MySQL to PostgreSQL or Oracle often breaks function names, pagination syntax, string concatenation, date functions, and keywords like LIMIT, ROWNUM, or FETCH FIRST. I check each native query against the new dialect, starting with the ones in the error logs. The long-term fix is to use JPQL where possible and keep native SQL only for cases that require database-specific features. For those cases, I maintain per-dialect query versions or use a thin abstraction that selects the correct SQL based on the active dialect.

title: Q: [S13] A microservice loses updates during a database failover. How do  you investigate?
text: [S13] A microservice loses updates during a database failover. How do you investigate? First I check whether the application was writing while the primary database was failing over. If a transaction was in-flight and not yet acknowledged by the database, the write may or may not have been applied, leading to ambiguity. I verify the JDBC driver and connection-pool settings for failover and reconnect behavior, and I review application timeouts and retry logic. The safest architectural pattern for critical writes is to make them idempotent using a business key, and to use the outbox pattern so the event is persisted in the same transaction as the business update and published reliably later. That way a failover does not lose the intent of the write; replay simply reprocesses idempotent operations.

title: Q: [S14] A scheduled reconciliation job reports missing rows that the API  claims were saved. What do you check?
text: [S14] A scheduled reconciliation job reports missing rows that the API claims were saved. What do you check? I first confirm the API transaction was actually committed before the job started. If the job reads from a read replica, replication lag can make recently committed rows invisible. I also check whether the job and the API agree on status values, soft-delete flags, and date ranges. Timezone mismatches in date filters are a common cause: a row saved at 23:58 UTC on one day may be interpreted as saved on the next day depending on the time zone used by the job. If both services use an event log or CDC, I verify the consumer offset is not lagging behind the producer.

title: Q: [S15] A @Modifying update runs but the count seems too low. Why?
text: [S15] A @Modifying update runs but the count seems too low. Why? The update count can be lower than expected for several reasons. The WHERE clause may be stricter than intended, for example due to a wrong status filter or a date range that excludes rows. If the same entities were loaded into the current persistence context before the update, the later flush can overwrite the updated columns with the old in-memory values, effectively reverting the change. If the method is not inside a transaction, it may run in auto-commit mode and fail silently or partially. Finally, another transaction may have modified or deleted the rows between the read and the update, causing a lost update or lower match count. The fix is to validate the filter, clear the persistence context before the bulk update, ensure the method is @Transactional, and use optimistic or pessimistic locking if concurrent updates are possible.

title: Q: [S16] A developer adds FetchType.EAGER to every relationship to avoid  lazy-loading errors. What do you say?
text: [S16] A developer adds FetchType.EAGER to every relationship to avoid lazy-loading errors. What do you say? This is an anti-pattern. Eager loading pulls in data even when it is not needed, producing larger SQL joins, Cartesian products, and unpredictable memory and network usage. It does not scale as the object graph grows. The correct fix is to understand why the lazy load was needed outside the transaction: either load the association explicitly with JOIN FETCH or an entity graph inside the service, or return a DTO that contains only the data the caller needs. FetchType.EAGER should be reserved for small, tightly coupled relationships that are truly always required together.

title: Q: [S17] A migration script alters a column type in a table with hundreds of  millions of rows. What concerns you?
text: [S17] A migration script alters a column type in a table with hundreds of millions of rows. What concerns you? Changing a column type on a huge table can take a long time, hold a metadata lock or table lock, fill transaction logs, and cause replication lag or application timeouts. Rollback is also slow and risky. In production I prefer online schema-change tools such as pt-online-schema- change for MySQL, gh-ost, or Oracle DBMS_REDEFINITION. A safer manual approach is backward-compatible: add a new column with the desired type, backfill it in small batches while the old column is still in use, switch the application to read and write the new column, and only then drop the old one. This avoids a long lock and gives a quick rollback path.

title: Q: [S18] A service method returns a JPA entity directly to a REST controller.  What problems can arise?
text: [S18] A service method returns a JPA entity directly to a REST controller. What problems can arise? Returning entities directly couples the API contract to the database schema. Serialization may trigger LazyInitializationException if associations were not loaded. Bidirectional relationships can cause infinite recursion during JSON serialization unless Jackson annotations are added. Internal fields such as IDs, versions, or audit columns leak into the API, and any future schema change can break API consumers. The better pattern is to map the entity to a DTO inside the service layer and return the DTO. This gives full control over the exposed fields, avoids lazy- loading problems, and makes versioning the API easier.

title: Q: [S19] Someone uses @Transactional on a private method. What do you  tell the author?
text: [S19] Someone uses @Transactional on a private method. What do you tell the author? It will not work. Spring transactional behavior is implemented through proxies, and proxies can only intercept public methods on Spring beans. private, final, static, and package-private methods are not intercepted. If the annotation is placed on a private helper, the code runs without a transaction and changes may commit or fail unexpectedly. The method should be made public, or the logic should be moved to a public method or another bean. If internal calls need transactionality, consider using AopContext.currentProxy() or restructuring the service.

title: Q: [S20] A repository method returns Page<order> but the service ignores  the total count. What could be improved?</order>
text: [S20] A repository method returns Page<order> but the service ignores the total count. What could be improved?</order> Page issues an extra COUNT query to compute total pages, which can be expensive on large tables. If the service does not use the total count, switching to Slice removes the count query while still supporting pagination and sorting. If even the next-page check is not needed, a simple List with Pageable is sufficient. Removing the count query reduces database load and simplifies the query plan, especially for complex filters.

title: Q: [S21] A @Query uses string concatenation to build a WHERE clause with  user input. What is wrong?
text: [S21] A @Query uses string concatenation to build a WHERE clause with user input. What is wrong? String concatenation with user input is vulnerable to SQL injection. Even if the input comes from an internal system, it should never be embedded directly into SQL. The correct approach is to use named parameters (:param) or positional placeholders (?1) in the @Query annotation. For dynamic WHERE clauses, use the Criteria API or the Specification API, which build the query safely. If native SQL must be constructed dynamically, use a prepared-statement style approach and validate inputs against an allow-list of values, column names, and sort fields.

title: Q: [S22] A developer writes CascadeType.ALL on a @ManyToOne  relationship. What is the danger?
text: [S22] A developer writes CascadeType.ALL on a @ManyToOne relationship. What is the danger? Cascading should flow from the parent to the child, not from the child to the parent or to shared entities. Placing CascadeType.ALL on a @ManyToOne from OrderItem to Product means deleting an order line could cascade a delete to the shared product, corrupting the catalog. Similarly, persisting an order item could try to persist or merge an existing product unexpectedly. The cascade should be on the Order -> OrderItem side, and even then only the exact operations that match the business rule should be enabled. Shared reference entities should never be cascaded from transactional children.

title: Q: [S23] A service does entityManager.flush() after every save inside a  loop. What is the impact?
text: [S23] A service does entityManager.flush() after every save inside a loop. What is the impact? Each flush forces Hibernate to synchronize the persistence context with the database, executing the pending SQL immediately. Doing this after every iteration defeats the purpose of batching and generates many database round trips, which increases latency and load. The correct pattern is to accumulate a chunk of entities, call saveAll(chunk), then flush and clear the EntityManager once per chunk. Setting hibernate.jdbc.batch_size to the chunk size and using a SEQUENCE ID generator allows the driver to send multiple inserts in a single network round trip.

title: Q: [S24] You see @Cacheable on a method that returns a list of orders from  today. What is the problem?
text: [S24] You see @Cacheable on a method that returns a list of orders from today. What is the problem? Orders from today change frequently, so the cached value becomes stale almost immediately and cache invalidation is constantly triggered. Caching is most valuable for slow- changing or expensive-to-compute data, such as reference data, product catalogs, or heavy reports. The cache key must also include every parameter that affects the result, including filters, date ranges, and page information. Otherwise an update in one dimension may leave stale entries for another. For dynamic operational data, query optimization, indexing, and read replicas are usually better tools than caching.

title: Q: [S25] A method annotated with @Transactional calls another  @Transactional method in a different bean. How do the transactions  interact?
text: [S25] A method annotated with @Transactional calls another @Transactional method in a different bean. How do the transactions interact? With the default propagation REQUIRED, the second method joins the existing transaction. Both methods commit or roll back together. If the second method uses REQUIRES_NEW, Spring suspends the first transaction, starts a new physical transaction, executes the second method, commits or rolls it back independently, and then resumes the first transaction. This is useful when the second operation must commit regardless of the first operation’s outcome, such as audit logging. It is important to understand that REQUIRES_NEW still uses the same database connection by default unless connection handling is configured otherwise, so it is not a general solution for concurrency.

title: Q: [Q1] What is Spring Boot, and why is it popular for building Java  backend applications?
text: [Q1] What is Spring Boot, and why is it popular for building Java backend applications? Spring Boot is an opinionated framework built on top of the Spring Framework that simplifies application setup and deployment. It provides auto-configuration, starter dependencies, an embedded server, and production-ready features like Actuator out of the box. Instead of writing a lot of XML or Java configuration, a developer can add spring-boot-starter-web and start a runnable web application with a main method. It is popular because it reduces boilerplate, speeds up development, and makes microservices and cloud-native deployments easier.

title: Q: [Q2] What does @SpringBootApplication do internally?
text: [Q2] What does @SpringBootApplication do internally? @SpringBootApplication is a convenience annotation that combines @Configuration, @EnableAutoConfiguration, and @ComponentScan. @Configuration marks the class as a source of bean definitions. @ComponentScan tells Spring where to look for components, services, repositories, and controllers. @EnableAutoConfiguration triggers Spring Boot’s auto-configuration mechanism, which inspects the classpath and existing beans to configure common infrastructure automatically, such as a datasource, embedded Tomcat, or Spring MVC. You can exclude specific auto-configurations using the exclude attribute.

title: Q: [Q3] What are Spring Boot starters, and how do they help?
text: [Q3] What are Spring Boot starters, and how do they help? Starters are pre-packaged dependency descriptors that bundle a set of compatible libraries for a specific use case. For example, spring-boot-starter-web includes Spring MVC, Tomcat, and Jackson, while spring-boot-starter-data-jpa includes Hibernate, Spring Data JPA, and a transaction manager. They reduce the need to hunt for compatible library versions and keep the build file clean. The spring-boot-starter-parent POM provides dependency management, default plugin configurations, and version alignment.

title: Q: [Q4] How do you create a simple REST controller in Spring Boot?
text: [Q4] How do you create a simple REST controller in Spring Boot? You annotate a class with @RestController, which combines @Controller and @ResponseBody. You then map HTTP methods using annotations like @GetMapping, @PostMapping, @PutMapping, and @DeleteMapping. For example, @GetMapping("/users/{id}") public User getUser(@PathVariable Long id) handles a GET request and returns the user object, which Jackson serializes to JSON automatically. Using @RestController avoids the need to annotate every method with @ResponseBody.

title: Q: [Q5] What is the difference between @Controller and  @RestController?
text: [Q5] What is the difference between @Controller and @RestController? @Controller is the traditional Spring MVC annotation used when you want to return a view name that a ViewResolver renders. @RestController is a shortcut for @Controller plus @ResponseBody, meaning every method returns a domain object or data that is serialized directly to JSON, XML, or another response body. If you use @Controller for a REST API and forget @ResponseBody, Spring will look for a view with that name and likely throw a 404.

title: Q: [Q6] How does Spring Boot handle the embedded server, and how do  you change the default port?
text: [Q6] How does Spring Boot handle the embedded server, and how do you change the default port? Spring Boot includes an embedded server, usually Tomcat, by adding spring-boot- starter-web. The application can run as a standalone JAR because the embedded server starts when the SpringApplication.run method is executed. You can change the port with server.port=9090 in application.properties, or disable the web server entirely with spring.main.web-application-type=none. To use Jetty or Undertow, exclude Tomcat from the starter and add spring-boot-starter-jetty or spring-boot-starter- undertow.

title: Q: [Q7] What is auto-configuration, and how can you override or disable it?
text: [Q7] What is auto-configuration, and how can you override or disable it? Auto-configuration is Spring Boot’s ability to guess the configuration a project needs based on the classpath and existing beans. It is implemented by classes annotated with @ConditionalOnClass, @ConditionalOnMissingBean, and similar conditions. You can override auto-configuration by defining your own beans with the same type, which takes precedence due to @ConditionalOnMissingBean. You can disable specific auto- configuration classes using spring.autoconfigure.exclude in application.properties or the exclude attribute of @SpringBootApplication.

title: Q: [Q8] How do you set up a Spring Boot project with Spring Boot Web MVC  and Spring Data JPA?
text: [Q8] How do you set up a Spring Boot project with Spring Boot Web MVC and Spring Data JPA? Add the starters spring-boot-starter-web for the web layer and spring- boot-starter-data-jpa for persistence. Include a database driver such as mysql- connector-j or postgresql. Configure the datasource in application.properties with spring.datasource.url, username, password, and driver-class-name. Define entity classes with @Entity and repository interfaces extending JpaRepository. Spring Boot auto-configures the datasource, transaction manager, and Hibernate. Use @Transactional in the service layer and keep controllers thin.

title: Q: [Q9] What is Spring Boot DevTools, and why should it not be used in  production?
text: [Q9] What is Spring Boot DevTools, and why should it not be used in production? Spring Boot DevTools is a development-time dependency that provides automatic restart when code changes, LiveReload for browser refresh, and enhanced debugging features. It should not be used in production because it adds runtime overhead, can expose internal application details, and is designed only for the local development loop. In production builds, it should be excluded or marked with a non-transitive scope so that it does not end up in the deployed artifact.

title: Q: [Q10] How do you enable basic auditing in Spring Boot?
text: [Q10] How do you enable basic auditing in Spring Boot? Enable JPA auditing with @EnableJpaAuditing on a configuration class and annotate entity fields with @CreatedDate, @LastModifiedDate, @CreatedBy, and @LastModifiedBy. Spring Data JPA automatically fills these fields when entities are persisted or updated. For @CreatedBy and @LastModifiedBy, you provide an AuditorAware bean that returns the current user. This removes manual audit field population and helps with compliance and traceability.

title: Q: [Q11] What is Hibernate Envers, and when would you use it?
text: [Q11] What is Hibernate Envers, and when would you use it? Hibernate Envers is an auditing module that tracks every insert, update, and delete on entities by maintaining separate revision tables. Annotating an entity with @Audited makes Envers store a snapshot of each change along with a revision number and timestamp. It is useful when you need a complete history of who changed what and when, such as in regulated domains or for debugging data issues. The trade-off is extra storage, write overhead, and the need to plan retention and archival of revision data.

title: Q: [Q12] How do you call a third-party REST API from a Spring Boot  application?
text: [Q12] How do you call a third-party REST API from a Spring Boot application? You can use RestTemplate for synchronous calls, WebClient for reactive or non- blocking calls, or Feign for declarative clients. With RestTemplate, you inject a bean and call methods like getForObject or exchange. With WebClient, you build a request chain and subscribe to the response. With Feign, you declare an interface with method mappings and Spring Cloud generates the implementation. For new Spring Boot 3 projects, the RestClient API offers a modern, fluent synchronous alternative to RestTemplate.

title: Q: [Q13] What is the difference between RestTemplate, WebClient, Feign,  and RestClient?
text: [Q13] What is the difference between RestTemplate, WebClient, Feign, and RestClient? RestTemplate is synchronous and simple but is now in maintenance mode. WebClient is reactive and non-blocking, part of Spring WebFlux, and better for high- concurrency or streaming scenarios. Feign is a declarative Spring Cloud client that lets you define HTTP clients as interfaces, making service-to-service calls concise. RestClient, introduced in Spring Framework 6.1 and Spring Boot 3.2, is a modern synchronous client with a fluent API. Blocking a reactive pipeline with RestTemplate is a common mistake that kills throughput in WebFlux applications.

title: Q: [Q14] How do you handle failures, retries, and timeouts when calling  external APIs?
text: [Q14] How do you handle failures, retries, and timeouts when calling external APIs? External API calls should always have connection and read timeouts configured so that a slow downstream does not hang your service. Use Resilience4j for circuit breaking, retries with exponential backoff, rate limiting, and bulkheads. Wrap the client so that failures degrade gracefully, for example by returning cached or default data. Log correlation IDs to trace calls across services, and use MockWebServer or WireMock in tests instead of hitting real third-party endpoints.

title: Q: [Q15] What is the default logging framework in Spring Boot, and how do  you configure it?
text: [Q15] What is the default logging framework in Spring Boot, and how do you configure it? The default logging framework is Logback, accessed through the SLF4J facade. You can configure logging levels and output in application.properties with properties like logging.level.root=INFO and logging.level.com.example=DEBUG. For file logging, use logging.file.name or logging.file.path. For more control, provide a logback.xml or log4j2.xml file in the classpath. A common mistake is enabling DEBUG in production, which degrades performance and can leak sensitive information.

title: Q: [Q16] What is SLF4J, and why is it recommended over direct Log4j  usage?
text: [Q16] What is SLF4J, and why is it recommended over direct Log4j usage? SLF4J is a logging facade that lets application code use a standard API while the actual logging implementation, such as Logback or Log4j2, is configured at deployment time. This means you can switch implementations by changing dependencies and configuration, without touching application code. It also supports parameterized messages such as logger.info("Processing order {}", orderId), which avoid the cost of string concatenation when the log level is disabled.

title: Q: [Q17] What is Spring Boot Actuator, and what are its most useful  endpoints?
text: [Q17] What is Spring Boot Actuator, and what are its most useful endpoints? Actuator is a production-ready feature that exposes operational endpoints for monitoring and managing the application. After adding spring-boot-starter-actuator, endpoints such as /health, /info, /metrics, /env, /loggers, /beans, and /heapdump become available. /health shows application health, /metrics exposes JVM and application metrics, and /loggers lets you change log levels at runtime. Exposing all endpoints publicly is dangerous, so only the endpoints needed for monitoring should be enabled and secured.

title: Q: [Q18] How do you secure Spring Boot Actuator endpoints?
text: [Q18] How do you secure Spring Boot Actuator endpoints? Limit exposed endpoints using management.endpoints.web.exposure.include to only those required, such as health, info, and metrics. Use Spring Security to protect them with authentication and a dedicated role like ACTUATOR_ADMIN. Run Actuator over HTTPS and place it on a separate management port if needed. Endpoints like /env, /beans, /heapdump, and /configprops can reveal secrets or internal details, so they should be disabled or heavily restricted in production.

title: Q: [Q19] How do you create a custom health indicator in Spring Boot?
text: [Q19] How do you create a custom health indicator in Spring Boot? Implement the HealthIndicator interface and override the health method to check a specific dependency, such as a downstream API or a message broker. Register the class as a Spring bean. Spring Boot automatically includes it in the /health response. This is useful for readiness and liveness probes in Kubernetes, where the platform can restart or stop routing traffic to an unhealthy instance.

title: Q: [Q20] What is OpenAPI, and how does Swagger help document REST  APIs?
text: [Q20] What is OpenAPI, and how does Swagger help document REST APIs? OpenAPI is a standardized specification for describing REST APIs. Swagger provides tooling to design, document, and test APIs based on the OpenAPI specification. In a Spring Boot project, you can use SpringDoc OpenAPI to generate documentation automatically from controllers, DTOs, and annotations. It produces an interactive UI where developers can see endpoints, parameters, request bodies, and response schemas, and even try calls directly.

title: Q: [Q21] Should Swagger UI be enabled in production, and why or why not?
text: [Q21] Should Swagger UI be enabled in production, and why or why not? Swagger UI should generally be disabled or restricted in production. While it is helpful during development and integration testing, it exposes the complete API contract, including endpoints, parameter names, and response structures, which can aid attackers. The recommended approach is to enable Swagger only in non-production profiles or protect it behind authentication and authorization.

title: Q: [Q22] What are Spring profiles, and how do you use them?
text: [Q22] What are Spring profiles, and how do you use them? Spring profiles let you define environment-specific beans and configuration. You activate a profile with spring.profiles.active=prod in application.properties or via command-line arguments. Beans annotated with @Profile("dev") only load when that profile is active. You can also have separate property files like application-dev.properties and application-prod.properties. Profiles help keep database URLs, log levels, feature flags, and secrets isolated per environment.

title: Q: [Q23] How do you externalize configuration and secrets in a Spring Boot  application?
text: [Q23] How do you externalize configuration and secrets in a Spring Boot application? Configuration should be moved out of the application code into property files, environment variables, or external config servers. Spring Boot supports application.properties, application.yml, environment variables, and command-line arguments with a defined precedence order. Secrets such as database passwords and API keys should not be stored in source control. Use Spring Cloud Config, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or HashiCorp Vault to inject secrets securely at runtime.

title: Q: [Q24] What is the Spring MVC request lifecycle?
text: [Q24] What is the Spring MVC request lifecycle? An incoming request first reaches the DispatcherServlet, which consults handler mappings to find the right controller method. The method processes the request, possibly using @RequestBody to deserialize JSON into objects and @PathVariable or @RequestParam to extract values. The result is returned, and if the controller uses @RestController, it is written directly to the response body. Interceptors can run before and after the controller, and @ControllerAdvice can handle exceptions globally.

title: Q: [Q25] What is ResponseEntity, and when should you use it?
text: [Q25] What is ResponseEntity, and when should you use it? ResponseEntity is a Spring class that represents the full HTTP response, including status code, headers, and body. It gives complete control over what is returned to the client. For example, ResponseEntity.created(location).body(user) returns HTTP 201 with a Location header, while ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND).build() returns a 404. It is preferred over returning raw domain objects when you need to set custom headers or status codes.

title: Q: [Q26] How do you handle exceptions globally in Spring Boot?
text: [Q26] How do you handle exceptions globally in Spring Boot? Use @RestControllerAdvice or @ControllerAdvice with methods annotated by @ExceptionHandler. A global advice class catches exceptions thrown by controllers and returns consistent error responses. For example, a ResourceNotFoundException can be mapped to HTTP 404, while a ValidationException can be mapped to HTTP 400. This keeps controllers clean and ensures the API always returns a predictable error format.

title: Q: [Q27] What is application.properties vs application.yml, and  which should you use?
text: [Q27] What is application.properties vs application.yml, and which should you use? Both files store externalized configuration. application.properties uses simple key-value pairs, while application.yml uses nested YAML syntax, which is cleaner for hierarchical configuration. The choice is mostly a team preference. If the configuration is deeply nested, such as multiple datasources or logging appenders, YAML is usually more readable. Properties files are simpler for flat configurations.

title: Q: [Q28] What is the Spring Boot main class, and why does it need  SpringApplication.run?
text: [Q28] What is the Spring Boot main class, and why does it need SpringApplication.run? The main class is the entry point annotated with @SpringBootApplication. Calling SpringApplication.run(MyApplication.class, args) bootstraps the Spring application context, starts the embedded web server, runs command-line runners, and initializes all beans. It is what turns a plain Java application into a running Spring Boot service. The class is usually placed at the root package so component scanning covers the entire project.

title: Q: [Q29] How do you write integration tests in Spring Boot?
text: [Q29] How do you write integration tests in Spring Boot? Use @SpringBootTest to load the full application context, @WebMvcTest for controller-layer tests with mocked services, and @DataJpaTest for repository tests against an embedded database. Use TestRestTemplate or WebTestClient to call endpoints. For external dependencies, use @MockBean or tools like WireMock. Tests should be fast and deterministic, so prefer sliced tests over full context tests when possible.

title: Q: [Q30] What is the difference between @Component, @Service,  @Repository, and @Controller?
text: [Q30] What is the difference between @Component, @Service, @Repository, and @Controller? All four are stereotype annotations that mark classes as Spring-managed beans. @Component is the generic one. @Service is used for business logic classes and helps document intent. @Repository is used for data access and enables Spring’s exception translation for persistence exceptions. @Controller is used for MVC controllers. Functionally they all result in bean creation during component scanning, but the specialized annotations carry semantic meaning and enable framework features.

title: Q: [Q31] What is dependency injection, and why is constructor injection  preferred?
text: [Q31] What is dependency injection, and why is constructor injection preferred? Dependency injection is a design pattern where Spring supplies a bean’s dependencies instead of the bean creating them itself. Constructor injection is preferred because it makes dependencies explicit and immutable, allows easy unit testing without reflection, and ensures the object is fully initialized before use. Field injection hides dependencies and makes testing harder, while setter injection can leave an object in a partially constructed state.

title: Q: [Q32] How do you schedule tasks in Spring Boot?
text: [Q32] How do you schedule tasks in Spring Boot? Add @EnableScheduling on a configuration class and use @Scheduled on methods to run them at fixed intervals, fixed delays, or using cron expressions. For example, @Scheduled(cron = "0 0 * * * *") runs every hour. For reliability and clustering, consider using an external scheduler or Quartz with a JDBC job store so that jobs are not duplicated across multiple application instances.

title: Q: [Q33] What is the difference between @Async and @Scheduled?
text: [Q33] What is the difference between @Async and @Scheduled? @Async marks a method to run in a separate thread from a task executor, returning immediately to the caller. It is useful for fire-and-forget operations like sending emails or notifications. @Scheduled triggers a method repeatedly based on a timer. A common mistake is calling an @Async method from within the same bean, which bypasses the Spring proxy and runs synchronously.

title: Q: [Q34] How do you version REST APIs in Spring Boot?
text: [Q34] How do you version REST APIs in Spring Boot? Common approaches include URI versioning (/v1/users, /v2/users), request header versioning (Accept: application/vnd.company.v1+json), and query parameter versioning (?version=1). URI versioning is the simplest and most visible. Header versioning keeps URLs clean but is harder to test in a browser. The choice should be documented and consistent across the API surface.

title: Q: [Q35] What is content negotiation in Spring Boot?
text: [Q35] What is content negotiation in Spring Boot? Content negotiation is the mechanism by which a client and server agree on the format of the response, usually JSON or XML. Spring Boot uses the Accept header to decide which HttpMessageConverter to use. Jackson is configured by default for JSON, and adding Jackson XML or JAXB dependencies enables XML responses. Producing the wrong format because of an Accept header mismatch is a subtle bug that appears during client integration.

title: Q: [Q36] How do you validate request input in Spring Boot?
text: [Q36] How do you validate request input in Spring Boot? Use Bean Validation by adding the spring-boot-starter-validation dependency and annotating DTO fields with constraints like @NotBlank, @Email, @Min, @Max, and @Valid. In the controller, annotate the request body parameter with @Valid so that validation runs before the method executes. Validation failures result in a MethodArgumentNotValidException, which can be handled globally to return a structured error response.

title: Q: [Q37] What is the purpose of CommandLineRunner and  ApplicationRunner?
text: [Q37] What is the purpose of CommandLineRunner and ApplicationRunner? Both interfaces let you run logic once the Spring application context is fully initialized. CommandLineRunner accepts raw command-line arguments as a string array, while ApplicationRunner exposes them as structured ApplicationArguments. They are commonly used for one-time setup, data seeding, or checking external dependencies at startup. Multiple runners can be ordered with @Order.

title: Q: [Q38] How do you package and deploy a Spring Boot application?
text: [Q38] How do you package and deploy a Spring Boot application? Spring Boot applications are typically packaged as an executable JAR using the Spring Boot Maven or Gradle plugin. The JAR includes the embedded server, so you deploy it by running java -jar app.jar. For containers, you can build a Docker image with a Dockerfile or use spring-boot:build-image to create an optimized image. Configuration is externalized through environment variables or mounted config files so the same artifact can run in any environment.

title: Q: [Q39] What is graceful shutdown in Spring Boot?
text: [Q39] What is graceful shutdown in Spring Boot? Graceful shutdown allows the application to finish processing active requests before stopping when it receives a termination signal. In Spring Boot 2.3+, you can enable it with server.shutdown=graceful. The embedded server stops accepting new requests, waits for in-flight requests up to a configured timeout, and then shuts down. This prevents dropped requests during rolling deployments and Kubernetes pod terminations.

title: Q: [Q40] What is the Spring Boot fat JAR, and how is it different from a  regular JAR?
text: [Q40] What is the Spring Boot fat JAR, and how is it different from a regular JAR? A fat JAR, also called an uber JAR, is a Spring Boot executable JAR that includes the application classes and all runtime dependencies, including the embedded server. A regular JAR usually contains only the project classes and needs external dependencies on the classpath. The fat JAR is self-contained, making deployment simple because you only need to run java -jar.

title: Q: [Q41] How do you add a custom Actuator endpoint?
text: [Q41] How do you add a custom Actuator endpoint? Create a class annotated with @Endpoint and a unique ID. Define operations using @ReadOperation, @WriteOperation, and @DeleteOperation. Register the class as a Spring bean. For example, a @ReadOperation method can return a custom metric or configuration value. Make sure the endpoint is exposed in management.endpoints.web.exposure.include and protected by security rules.

title: Q: [Q42] What is Micrometer, and how does it relate to Actuator?
text: [Q42] What is Micrometer, and how does it relate to Actuator? Micrometer is a metrics facade that provides a vendor-neutral API for recording timers, counters, gauges, and distributions. Spring Boot Actuator exposes Micrometer metrics through the /metrics endpoint and can ship them to backends like Prometheus, Datadog, CloudWatch, or Graphite. This lets you monitor application performance, throughput, and errors using the tools your organization already has.

title: Q: [Q43] How do you change the Actuator base path and port?
text: [Q43] How do you change the Actuator base path and port? You can change the Actuator web base path with management.endpoints.web.base-path=/admin, so endpoints appear under /admin/health instead of /actuator/health. You can also run Actuator on a separate management port with management.server.port=8081, which isolates operational endpoints from public application traffic and makes security rules easier to apply.

title: Q: [Q44] What is the difference between @Value and  @ConfigurationProperties?
