text: [Q44] What is the difference between @Value and @ConfigurationProperties? @Value injects a single property using SpEL expressions, such as @Value("$ {server.port}"). @ConfigurationProperties binds a group of related properties to a typed bean, which is better for structured configuration like a datasource or an external API client. For example, app.mail.host, app.mail.port, and app.mail.username can bind to a single MailProperties class. @ConfigurationProperties supports relaxed binding and JSR-303 validation.

title: Q: [Q45] How do you implement pagination in a Spring Boot REST API?
text: [Q45] How do you implement pagination in a Spring Boot REST API? Add a Pageable parameter to the controller or repository method. Spring resolves Pageable from query parameters like ?page=0&size=10&sort=name,asc. The repository returns a Page that includes content, total elements, total pages, and metadata. Always cap the page size to prevent abuse and avoid returning huge responses that exhaust memory or slow serialization.

title: Q: [Q46] What is a DTO, and why should you return DTOs instead of entities  from controllers?
text: [Q46] What is a DTO, and why should you return DTOs instead of entities from controllers? A DTO is a plain object that carries only the data needed by the API consumer. Returning entities directly couples the API contract to the database schema, can trigger LazyInitializationException, may expose internal fields, and makes future schema changes risky. Mapping entities to DTOs inside the service layer gives full control over the exposed shape and avoids serialization problems.

title: Q: [Q47] How do you handle file uploads in Spring Boot?
text: [Q47] How do you handle file uploads in Spring Boot? Accept MultipartFile as a controller parameter, usually with @RequestParam("file") or @ModelAttribute. Use transferTo to save the file or stream it directly to cloud storage like S3. For large files, stream the content instead of loading it fully into memory, and configure spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size and max-request-size to enforce limits. Store files in external storage rather than local disk in containerized environments.

title: Q: [Q48] What is an API Gateway, and how does it fit with Spring Boot  microservices?
text: [Q48] What is an API Gateway, and how does it fit with Spring Boot microservices? An API Gateway sits in front of microservices and handles cross-cutting concerns like routing, load balancing, authentication, rate limiting, SSL termination, and request/response transformation. Spring Cloud Gateway is a common choice in the Spring ecosystem. Each Spring Boot service exposes its own endpoints, and the gateway routes client requests to the right service, centralizing security and observability.

title: Q: [Q49] How do you implement distributed tracing in Spring Boot?
text: [Q49] How do you implement distributed tracing in Spring Boot? In Spring Boot 3, use Micrometer Tracing with Brave or OpenTelemetry to propagate trace and span IDs across service calls. Add the tracing starter and configure an exporter such as Zipkin or Jaeger. The trace ID is included in logs via MDC, which helps you follow a request across multiple services. In Spring Boot 2.x this was often done with Spring Cloud Sleuth.

title: Q: [Q50] What are the key things to check before deploying a Spring Boot  application to production?
text: [Q50] What are the key things to check before deploying a Spring Boot application to production? Before production deployment, disable DevTools and Swagger UI or restrict them, secure Actuator endpoints, externalize secrets, set appropriate log levels, enable health checks, configure graceful shutdown, set reasonable connection and thread pool sizes, run integration tests, verify database migrations, and ensure the artifact is built with the production profile. Also confirm that metrics and centralized logging are wired up.

title: Q: [S1] A Spring Boot application starts up locally but fails to start in  production with a DataSource bean creation error. How do you  investigate?
text: [S1] A Spring Boot application starts up locally but fails to start in production with a DataSource bean creation error. How do you investigate? Start by reading the full stack trace to identify whether the failure is a missing URL, invalid credentials, unreachable host, driver not on the classpath, or a port/firewall issue. Compare the production application.properties or environment variables with the local configuration. Check that the correct profile is active and that secrets are being injected properly by Vault or the config server. Verify the database driver dependency is included in the production build. If the app runs in a container, confirm that the container can reach the database and that DNS resolution works. Finally, test connectivity with a simple JDBC connection from the production environment before restarting the application.

title: Q: [S2] A REST endpoint works in development but returns 404 in  production. What could be wrong?
text: [S2] A REST endpoint works in development but returns 404 in production. What could be wrong? A 404 usually means the request URL does not match any registered handler. Check whether the production base path or servlet context path is different, such as server.servlet.context-path=/api. Verify the HTTP method is correct and that the controller is in a package scanned by Spring. If there are multiple @RequestMapping variants or a conflicting global path configuration, they may behave differently under another profile. Also confirm that no security filter is blocking the request and returning a misleading 404 instead of 403.

title: Q: [S3] An API that handles file uploads starts throwing  OutOfMemoryError under load. How do you fix it?
text: [S3] An API that handles file uploads starts throwing OutOfMemoryError under load. How do you fix it? Loading the entire multipart file into memory for every request consumes heap space quickly. The fix is to stream the file directly to disk or cloud storage instead of keeping it in memory. Configure spring.servlet.multipart.file-size-threshold so large files are written to disk, and set max-file-size and max-request-size limits to reject oversized uploads. Use MultipartFile.getInputStream() and upload chunks to S3 or similar storage. In containerized environments, avoid writing to ephemeral local disk; send the stream straight to object storage.

title: Q: [S4] A Spring Boot service calls an external payment API, and payments  sometimes time out. What do you do?
text: [S4] A Spring Boot service calls an external payment API, and payments sometimes time out. What do you do? First configure explicit connection and read timeouts on the HTTP client so the call fails fast instead of blocking threads indefinitely. Add retries with exponential backoff for transient failures, but avoid retrying idempotent mutations blindly without idempotency keys. Wrap the client with a circuit breaker such as Resilience4j so failures do not cascade. Log every outbound call with a trace ID and the response time. If the external API is unreliable, consider an asynchronous queue- based integration where the payment status is polled or pushed later.

title: Q: [S5] A microservice using Feign starts failing with  NoSuchBeanDefinitionException. How do you debug it?
text: [S5] A microservice using Feign starts failing with NoSuchBeanDefinitionException. How do you debug it? NoSuchBeanDefinitionException means Spring could not create the Feign client bean. Verify that @EnableFeignClients is on the main class or a configuration class and that the base package is correct. Confirm the interface is annotated with @FeignClient and has a valid name and URL or service ID. Check that spring-cloud-starter-openfeign is on the classpath. If the service uses a custom Feign configuration, ensure it is not accidentally excluding the default beans needed for auto-configuration.

title: Q: [S6] A Spring Boot application running on Kubernetes is being killed  before requests finish. How do you handle it?
text: [S6] A Spring Boot application running on Kubernetes is being killed before requests finish. How do you handle it? Kubernetes sends a SIGTERM to the pod when scaling down or redeploying. If the application exits immediately, in-flight requests are dropped. Enable graceful shutdown with server.shutdown=graceful and set spring.lifecycle.timeout-per- shutdown-phase to a value larger than the longest expected request. Configure the pod’s terminationGracePeriodSeconds to match. The container should keep running until active requests complete or the grace period expires, after which Kubernetes sends SIGKILL.

title: Q: [S7] A newly deployed feature works for some users but returns stale  data for others. What could be happening?
text: [S7] A newly deployed feature works for some users but returns stale data for others. What could be happening? Stale data for some users often points to caching issues. If each application instance has a local cache, one instance may update the database without invalidating the cache on other instances. The fix is to use a shared cache such as Redis or to publish cache invalidation events across the cluster. Also check whether the cache TTL is too long, whether the cache key includes all relevant parameters, and whether the feature depends on a read replica that lags behind the primary database.

title: Q: [S8] A Spring Boot batch job processes orders, but the same order is  processed twice when the job runs on two pods. How do you prevent  duplicate processing?
text: [S8] A Spring Boot batch job processes orders, but the same order is processed twice when the job runs on two pods. How do you prevent duplicate processing? Duplicate processing happens because each pod independently polls the same work queue or table. The solution depends on the architecture. If using a scheduler, make it a singleton with a distributed lock via ShedLock or Quartz JDBC job store. If pulling from a message broker, use a consumer group so each message is processed by only one consumer. If reading from a database, mark rows as PROCESSING with an atomic update, or use a processed_at timestamp and a unique constraint on the business key to ensure idempotency.

title: Q: [S9] Actuator /health returns UP even though the database is  unreachable. How do you fix it?
text: [S9] Actuator /health returns UP even though the database is unreachable. How do you fix it? By default, Spring Boot health checks only verify the datasource if it is auto-configured and the application uses it. If the health indicator is disabled or the datasource is custom, the database may not be checked. Add a custom HealthIndicator that performs a lightweight query like SELECT 1 against the database, or enable detailed health with management.endpoint.health.show-details=always and include db in the exposed endpoints. For Kubernetes liveness and readiness probes, ensure the readiness probe points to /health/readiness and the liveness probe to /health/liveness so traffic is routed away before the pod is restarted.

title: Q: [S10] A Spring Boot application shows high CPU after a deployment.  How do you troubleshoot it?
text: [S10] A Spring Boot application shows high CPU after a deployment. How do you troubleshoot it? High CPU after a deployment usually means inefficient loops, excessive garbage collection, or a hot code path. Start by collecting thread dumps and heap dumps, and look at JVM metrics through Actuator or a profiler. Check whether a new query runs full table scans, whether a cache was disabled, or whether a new loop processes an unexpectedly large list. Review recent changes for missing pagination, inefficient algorithms, or blocking calls on event loops. Revert the deployment if the root cause cannot be found quickly and business impact is high.

title: Q: [S11] A developer stores database passwords in  application.properties. What do you recommend?
text: [S11] A developer stores database passwords in application.properties. What do you recommend? Storing passwords in source-controlled properties is a security risk. Move secrets to an external secret store such as HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or Spring Cloud Config with encryption. At runtime, the application reads the secret through environment variables or the config server. If you must use property files locally, keep them out of version control with a .gitignore entry and use a secrets manager for all non-local environments. Rotate credentials regularly and audit access.

title: Q: [S12] A service using @Async runs synchronously when called from  within the same bean. Why?
text: [S12] A service using @Async runs synchronously when called from within the same bean. Why? Spring AOP creates a proxy around the bean to handle @Async, @Transactional, and similar annotations. When the method is called from within the same bean, the call bypasses the proxy and runs directly, so the async behavior is ignored. The fix is to move the async method to another bean and inject it, or use AopContext.currentProxy() to invoke the method through the proxy. Constructor injection and clear separation of concerns reduce this problem.

title: Q: [S13] A Spring Boot application logs are not reaching the centralized  logging system. What do you check?
text: [S13] A Spring Boot application logs are not reaching the centralized logging system. What do you check? Verify that the application writes logs to stdout or to a file that the log shipper can read. Check the logging configuration for incorrect appenders or file paths that do not exist in the container. Confirm the log level is not set too high, filtering out the messages you expect. In Kubernetes, make sure the container runtime captures stdout and that the log aggregation agent such as Fluentd or Fluent Bit is running. For structured JSON logging, ensure the format matches what the central system expects and that the MDC fields such as trace ID are included.

title: Q: [S14] An OpenAPI-generated contract does not match the actual API  response. How do you fix it?
text: [S14] An OpenAPI-generated contract does not match the actual API response. How do you fix it? The contract becomes stale when DTOs, controller signatures, or annotations change without updating the OpenAPI generation. Use SpringDoc or Springfox annotations such as @Operation, @ApiResponse, @Schema, and @Parameter to document behavior explicitly. Generate the OpenAPI spec as part of the build and fail the build if it drifts from a committed baseline. In CI, run contract tests that verify the generated spec against real controller responses. Restrict manual edits of the spec so that the code remains the single source of truth.

title: Q: [S15] A Spring Boot application needs to support two databases. How do  you configure it?
text: [S15] A Spring Boot application needs to support two databases. How do you configure it? Define two separate DataSource beans, each with its own properties prefix using @ConfigurationProperties. Create two EntityManagerFactory beans, each scanning a different package of entities, and two JpaTransactionManager beans. Mark one set as @Primary so that default autowiring works for the main database. Use @Qualifier when injecting the secondary datasource, entity manager, or transaction manager. Avoid mixing entities from both databases in the same transaction unless you use a distributed transaction manager such as Atomikos or accept eventual consistency with application-level coordination.

title: Q: [S16] A production endpoint exposes sensitive user data in stack traces.  How do you prevent it?
text: [S16] A production endpoint exposes sensitive user data in stack traces. How do you prevent it? Configure a global exception handler with @RestControllerAdvice that catches exceptions and returns a sanitized error response containing only a generic message and a correlation ID. Never return raw stack traces, SQL errors, or internal class names to API consumers. Set server.error.include-stacktrace=never and server.error.include- message=never in production. Log the full exception with the correlation ID internally so developers can debug without leaking details.

title: Q: [S17] A Spring Boot application deployed as a Docker container is slow  to start. How do you optimize it?
text: [S17] A Spring Boot application deployed as a Docker container is slow to start. How do you optimize it? Slow startup is often caused by classpath scanning, large dependency trees, or inefficient database migration checks. Reduce startup time by moving to Java 17 or later, using layered JARs or CDS archives, trimming unnecessary dependencies, and lazy-initializing beans where appropriate. Pre-run database migrations during the build or as an init container instead of blocking startup. Build optimized container images with spring-boot:build-image or a custom Dockerfile based on a small JRE. Avoid running in DEBUG logging in production.

title: Q: [S18] A Spring Boot API returns different responses in dev and prod for  the same request. How do you investigate?
text: [S18] A Spring Boot API returns different responses in dev and prod for the same request. How do you investigate? Compare the active profiles and the resolved property values using /actuator/env if available. Check for environment-specific beans enabled by @Profile and for conditional properties that differ between environments. Verify the database state, including schema version, seed data, and replication lag if a read replica is used. Look for feature flags or cached data that may differ. Capture the request and response in both environments and diff them to isolate whether the difference is in data, serialization, or business logic.

title: Q: [S19] A Spring Boot service under heavy load starts returning  connection timeout errors. What do you check?
text: [S19] A Spring Boot service under heavy load starts returning connection timeout errors. What do you check? Check the embedded server thread pool configuration, the database connection pool size, and the HTTP client connection pool for outbound calls. If any pool is too small, requests queue up and eventually time out. Use metrics to see whether the database is the bottleneck or whether an external API is slow. Tune server.tomcat.threads.max, HikariCP maximum-pool- size, and client timeouts. Add circuit breakers and bulkheads to prevent failures from cascading. If the load is legitimate, scale horizontally behind a load balancer.

title: Q: [S20] A new developer enables all Actuator endpoints for easier  debugging. What do you tell them?
text: [S20] A new developer enables all Actuator endpoints for easier debugging. What do you tell them? Exposing all endpoints, especially /env, /beans, /heapdump, /configprops, and /loggers, leaks configuration values, internal structure, and potentially secrets. The right approach is to expose only the endpoints needed for monitoring, such as /health, /info, /metrics, and /prometheus, and protect them with Spring Security and a dedicated management role. Use a separate management port or restrict network access so operational endpoints are not reachable from the public internet.

title: Q: [S21] A Spring Boot application receives a request with a JSON body,  but the fields are null even though the JSON has values. Why?
text: [S21] A Spring Boot application receives a request with a JSON body, but the fields are null even though the JSON has values. Why? This usually happens when the JSON property names do not match the Java field names and there are no matching getters and setters, or when the DTO lacks a default constructor. If you use Lombok, verify that @Getter, @Setter, and @NoArgsConstructor are present and that the annotation processor is configured in the build. Another cause is a custom HttpMessageConverter or ObjectMapper configuration that disables default deserialization. Use @JsonProperty if the API names differ from Java naming conventions.

title: Q: [S22] A scheduled job in a Spring Boot application works on one  instance but runs twice in a clustered deployment. How do you fix it?
text: [S22] A scheduled job in a Spring Boot application works on one instance but runs twice in a clustered deployment. How do you fix it? By default, Spring’s scheduler runs on every node independently. In a cluster you need a distributed lock so only one instance executes the job. Options include ShedLock, which uses a database or Redis to coordinate, or Quartz with a JDBC job store. If the job is triggered by a message broker, use a consumer group so each message is processed exactly once. Avoid relying on cron expressions alone in clustered environments because every node will fire at the same time.

title: Q: [S23] A Spring Boot service is supposed to send an email after user  registration, but emails are not sent when the registration API is under  load. How do you debug?
text: [S23] A Spring Boot service is supposed to send an email after user registration, but emails are not sent when the registration API is under load. How do you debug? If the email sending is synchronous, the external SMTP server can become a bottleneck, causing requests to time out or fail. Move the email logic to an @Async method or a message queue so registration completes quickly. Verify the thread pool used by @Async is large enough and has a queue capacity. Check logs for SMTP exceptions and configure a circuit breaker around the email provider. For reliability, persist the email intent in an outbox table or queue and process it with a retry mechanism.

title: Q: [S24] A Spring Boot application uses Swagger, and the security team  asks to remove it from production. What is the best approach?
text: [S24] A Spring Boot application uses Swagger, and the security team asks to remove it from production. What is the best approach? The best approach is to disable Swagger UI in production by using a Spring profile, such as adding the SpringDoc configuration only when the dev or qa profile is active. Alternatively, you can restrict the Swagger endpoints with Spring Security so only authorized internal users can access them. Removing the dependency entirely from the production build is the safest option if it is not needed there. Keep the OpenAPI spec generation in CI so documentation stays accurate, but do not expose the interactive UI publicly.

title: Q: [S25] A Spring Boot service needs to call another internal service, but  the target service has frequent short outages. How do you make the  integration resilient?
text: [S25] A Spring Boot service needs to call another internal service, but the target service has frequent short outages. How do you make the integration resilient? Add a circuit breaker like Resilience4j around the client so failures fail fast instead of blocking threads. Combine it with retries that have exponential backoff and jitter, but only for safe, idempotent operations. Use a fallback strategy that returns cached data, a default response, or a queued intent when the downstream service is unavailable. Set sensible connection and read timeouts on the HTTP client. Monitor the circuit breaker state with Actuator and alert when it opens frequently, because that signals a deeper downstream problem.

title: Q: [Q1] What is Spring Security, and why is it important in a Spring Boot  application?
text: [Q1] What is Spring Security, and why is it important in a Spring Boot application? Spring Security is a powerful, customizable authentication and access-control framework for Java applications. In a Spring Boot project, adding spring-boot-starter-security auto-configures basic security, including a default form login and password-based authentication. It protects REST endpoints, method invocations, and even view-layer resources. It is important because security cannot be bolted on later; it must be part of the design, from request filtering to method-level authorization and secret management.

title: Q: [Q2] How does Spring Security work internally at a high level?
text: [Q2] How does Spring Security work internally at a high level? Spring Security works through a chain of servlet filters known as the SecurityFilterChain. Every incoming request passes through this chain, where filters handle tasks such as extracting credentials, checking sessions, validating CSRF tokens, and enforcing access rules. The result is stored in the SecurityContext, which is held by SecurityContextHolder. Later, authorization decisions consult this context. In modern Spring Boot 3 applications, you configure security by exposing a SecurityFilterChain bean instead of extending WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter, which is now removed.

title: Q: [Q3] What are the core components of Spring Security?
text: [Q3] What are the core components of Spring Security? The core components are SecurityContext, SecurityContextHolder, Authentication, AuthenticationManager, ProviderManager, and AuthenticationProvider. SecurityContext stores the currently authenticated user. SecurityContextHolder carries it across the application, typically bound to the current thread. Authentication represents the credential token. AuthenticationManager delegates validation to ProviderManager, which tries each registered AuthenticationProvider until one succeeds. UserDetailsService loads user data, and PasswordEncoder hashes passwords. Understanding how these pieces fit together is essential for debugging 403 and 401 errors.

title: Q: [Q4] What is the difference between AuthenticationManager and  ProviderManager?
text: [Q4] What is the difference between AuthenticationManager and ProviderManager? AuthenticationManager is an interface that defines a single authenticate method. ProviderManager is its most common implementation. It maintains a list of AuthenticationProvider implementations and asks each one whether it can authenticate the given token. For example, one provider might handle username-and-password tokens, while another handles JWT tokens. If no provider supports the token or all fail, the authentication fails. This design lets you plug in different authentication mechanisms without changing the rest of the application.

title: Q: [Q5] What is SecurityContextHolder, and how does it relate to  SecurityContext?
text: [Q5] What is SecurityContextHolder, and how does it relate to SecurityContext? SecurityContextHolder is a strategy-based container that holds the SecurityContext for the current execution context. By default it uses thread-local storage, so each request thread has its own context. The SecurityContext contains the Authentication object, which includes the principal, credentials, and granted authorities. This is how controllers and services later call SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication() to know who is logged in. In asynchronous or reactive scenarios, the strategy must be updated to propagate context across threads.

title: Q: [Q6] What is a SecurityFilterChain, and how do you configure it in  Spring Boot 3?
text: [Q6] What is a SecurityFilterChain, and how do you configure it in Spring Boot 3? A SecurityFilterChain is a bean that defines the order and behavior of security filters for a set of requests. In Spring Boot 3, you expose it as a bean instead of overriding WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter. You use HttpSecurity to define which endpoints are public, which require authentication, which require specific roles, and how login, logout, CSRF, and session management behave. A typical configuration disables CSRF for stateless APIs, requires authentication for protected paths, and sets the session creation policy to stateless when using JWT.

title: Q: [Q7] What is CSRF, and how does Spring Security protect against it?
text: [Q7] What is CSRF, and how does Spring Security protect against it? Cross-Site Request Forgery is an attack where a malicious site tricks a logged-in user’s browser into making an unwanted request to a trusted site. Spring Security protects browser-based sessions by issuing a random CSRF token per session and requiring it for state-changing requests such as POST, PUT, DELETE, and PATCH. The token is typically rendered in forms or returned as a cookie/header for JavaScript clients. For pure REST APIs consumed by mobile apps or other backends, CSRF is usually disabled because the client does not maintain a session cookie and the token is not practical to manage.

title: Q: [Q8] When is it safe to disable CSRF, and what is the risk of disabling it  globally?
text: [Q8] When is it safe to disable CSRF, and what is the risk of disabling it globally? It is safe to disable CSRF for stateless APIs where authentication is carried by a header token such as JWT, because the browser does not automatically send custom headers. It is dangerous to disable CSRF globally for browser-based applications that rely on session cookies, because an attacker can forge requests using the victim’s stored credentials. If you must expose both browser forms and APIs from the same application, configure CSRF per request matcher rather than turning it off everywhere.

title: Q: [Q9] What is XSS, and how do you prevent it in a Spring Boot  application?
text: [Q9] What is XSS, and how do you prevent it in a Spring Boot application? Cross-Site Scripting is an attack where an attacker injects malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users. In a Spring Boot REST API, the main defense is never trusting user input and always escaping output. Use templating engines that auto-escape content. For JSON APIs, ensure the client escapes data before rendering it in HTML. Set a strong Content Security Policy header, use HttpOnly and SameSite cookies, and validate input on the server. Storing raw HTML from users and rendering it later is a common way XSS vulnerabilities appear.

title: Q: [Q10] What is SQL injection, and how does Spring Data JPA help prevent  it?
text: [Q10] What is SQL injection, and how does Spring Data JPA help prevent it? SQL injection is an attack where an attacker inserts malicious SQL into input fields to manipulate database queries. Spring Data JPA helps prevent it because JPQL and named parameters use prepared statements, separating code from data. A query written as SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.email = :email is safe, while concatenating user input into a native query is not. Even with JPA, native queries and dynamic SQL must use placeholders or the Criteria API. Input validation and least-privilege database accounts provide additional defense.

title: Q: [Q11] What is the difference between authentication and authorization?
text: [Q11] What is the difference between authentication and authorization? Authentication is the process of verifying who you are, for example by checking a username and password or a JWT signature. Authorization decides what you are allowed to do after you are authenticated, for example whether you can access /admin/reports. Authentication answers the question "Who is this user?" while authorization answers "Is this user allowed to perform this action?" Both are needed; knowing the user’s identity is useless unless the system also enforces access rules.

title: Q: [Q12] What is a JWT, and what are its three parts?
text: [Q12] What is a JWT, and what are its three parts? JWT stands for JSON Web Token. It is a compact, self-contained token used to transmit identity and claims between parties. A JWT has three parts separated by dots: the header, which contains the signing algorithm and token type; the payload, which contains claims such as subject, issuer, expiration, and custom roles; and the signature, which proves the token was issued by a trusted party and was not tampered with. Because the token carries its own claims, a stateless server can verify it without storing session data.

title: Q: [Q13] How do you create and verify a JWT token in a Spring Boot  application?
text: [Q13] How do you create and verify a JWT token in a Spring Boot application? Creation happens after the user proves their identity, typically in a login endpoint. You build a JWT with a library such as JJWT or Nimbus, set claims like subject, issued-at, expiration, and roles, and sign it with a secret key or a private key. Verification happens in a custom filter or authentication provider that reads the Authorization header, parses the token, validates the signature with the same secret or public key, and checks that the token has not expired. If valid, the filter populates the SecurityContext with an authenticated Authentication object.

title: Q: [Q14] What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric signing  for JWT?
text: [Q14] What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric signing for JWT? Symmetric signing uses a single shared secret to both sign and verify the token. It is simple but means every service that verifies tokens must possess the secret, increasing exposure if one service is compromised. Asymmetric signing uses a private key to sign and a public key to verify. The authentication service keeps the private key, while other services only need the public key. Asymmetric signing is preferred in microservices because verification services cannot forge tokens even if they are breached.

title: Q: [Q15] Where should you store the JWT signing key, and why does it  matter?
text: [Q15] Where should you store the JWT signing key, and why does it matter? The signing key must never be hard-coded in source control or plain property files. It should be stored in a secret manager such as HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or Spring Cloud Config with encryption. For asymmetric keys, the private key stays only in the token issuer, and public keys are distributed to verification services. If a secret key leaks, an attacker can forge valid tokens, which is a complete authentication bypass until all keys are rotated.

title: Q: [Q16] How do you implement login and sign-up using JWT in Spring  Boot?
text: [Q16] How do you implement login and sign-up using JWT in Spring Boot? For sign-up, the controller receives user details, validates them, hashes the password with BCryptPasswordEncoder, and saves the user. For login, the controller receives credentials and uses AuthenticationManager to validate them. If authentication succeeds, a service creates a JWT containing the user ID, roles, and expiration. The token is returned to the client, which sends it in the Authorization: Bearer header on subsequent requests. A custom filter intercepts protected requests, validates the token, and sets the authentication in the SecurityContext.

title: Q: [Q17] How does a client use a JWT to access protected endpoints?
text: [Q17] How does a client use a JWT to access protected endpoints? After receiving the JWT from the login response, the client stores it securely, for example in memory or a secure cookie, and sends it in the Authorization header as Bearer with every protected request. The server extracts the header in a filter, strips the Bearer prefix, validates the signature and expiration, and uses the claims to build an Authentication object. If the token is missing, malformed, expired, or signed with the wrong key, the server returns 401.

title: Q: [Q18] What is the difference between an access token and a refresh  token?
text: [Q18] What is the difference between an access token and a refresh token? An access token is short-lived, usually minutes to an hour, and grants access to protected resources. A refresh token is long-lived and is used only to obtain a new access token when the old one expires. The access token travels with every API request, so keeping it short limits the window of misuse if it is stolen. The refresh token is stored more securely, rotated on use, and can be revoked independently. Storing refresh tokens in plain browser storage is a common mistake that makes theft easy.

title: Q: [Q19] How do you implement a refresh token flow securely?
text: [Q19] How do you implement a refresh token flow securely? When the user logs in, issue both an access token and a refresh token. Store a hash of the refresh token in the database along with the user ID, device identifier, and expiration. When the access token expires, the client sends the refresh token to a dedicated refresh endpoint. The server hashes the incoming token, looks it up, verifies it has not expired or been revoked, and issues a new access token and a new refresh token, invalidating the old refresh token. This rotation limits the impact of token theft. Revocation happens by deleting the stored token hash.

title: Q: [Q20] What is session management in Spring Security, and how do you  make it stateless?
text: [Q20] What is session management in Spring Security, and how do you make it stateless? Session management controls how user sessions are created and tracked. By default, Spring Security creates a session for browser applications and stores the authentication server-side. For JWT-based REST APIs, you set sessionCreationPolicy(SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS) in the SecurityFilterChain. This tells Spring Security not to create or use sessions; every request is authenticated independently using the JWT. Stateless authentication is easier to scale horizontally because no session affinity or shared session store is needed.

title: Q: [Q21] What is the difference between roles and authorities in Spring  Security?
text: [Q21] What is the difference between roles and authorities in Spring Security? A role is a coarse-grained grouping of permissions, such as ROLE_ADMIN or ROLE_USER. An authority is a finer-grained permission, such as user:read, user:write, or report:delete. Internally Spring Security treats both as strings in the GrantedAuthority collection. The hasRole expression automatically prepends ROLE_, so hasRole("ADMIN") checks for ROLE_ADMIN. hasAuthority("ADMIN") checks for the exact string. Using authorities gives more granular control and is preferred when permissions vary across users with the same role.

title: Q: [Q22] How do you implement role-based authorization in Spring  Security?
text: [Q22] How do you implement role-based authorization in Spring Security? You can enforce roles at the URL level in the SecurityFilterChain using requestMatchers("/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN"). You can also enforce them at the method level with @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')") or @Secured("ROLE_ADMIN"). The user must have the matching role stored in their GrantedAuthority list, typically populated from the database when the UserDetails object is built. Be careful that hasRole adds the ROLE_ prefix automatically, so storing ADMIN in the database and checking hasRole("ADMIN") will not match unless you configure the prefix or use hasAuthority.

title: Q: [Q23] How do you implement granular authorization with authorities and  permissions?
text: [Q23] How do you implement granular authorization with authorities and permissions? Granular authorization maps specific actions to authority strings rather than broad roles. For example, an invoice module might use authorities like invoice:read, invoice:write, and invoice:approve. You load these permissions from a database when building the UserDetails, and enforce them with @PreAuthorize("hasAuthority('invoice:approve')"). For dynamic rules, you can write SpEL expressions that check method arguments, such as @PreAuthorize("hasAuthority('invoice:write') and #userId == authentication.principal.id"). This gives much finer control than role-based checks alone.

title: Q: [Q24] What is the difference between @Secured, @PreAuthorize, and  @PostAuthorize?
text: [Q24] What is the difference between @Secured, @PreAuthorize, and @PostAuthorize? @Secured checks roles before method execution and only supports role names. @PreAuthorize is more expressive because it supports SpEL expressions, allowing you to check roles, authorities, method arguments, and custom conditions. @PostAuthorize runs after the method executes and can filter the returned object, for example @PostAuthorize("returnObject.owner == authentication.name"). @PreAuthorize is the most commonly used for method-level authorization. Modern Spring Security uses @EnableMethodSecurity instead of the older @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity.

title: Q: [Q25] Why might method security annotations not work even though  they look correct?
text: [Q25] Why might method security annotations not work even though they look correct? Method security annotations only work when the method is invoked through a Spring- generated proxy. If a method annotated with @PreAuthorize is called from another method in the same bean, the proxy is bypassed and the security check is skipped. This is a common and dangerous mistake. The fix is to move the secured method to another bean and inject it, or use AopContext.currentProxy(). Also verify that @EnableMethodSecurity is present and that the expression syntax matches the actual authority strings.

title: Q: [Q26] What is OAuth2, and how does the Authorization Code flow work?
text: [Q26] What is OAuth2, and how does the Authorization Code flow work? OAuth2 is a delegation protocol that lets a user grant a third-party application limited access to their resources without sharing their password. In the Authorization Code flow, the user clicks login, the application redirects the browser to the OAuth2 provider, the user authenticates and consents, and the provider redirects back with an authorization code. The backend exchanges this code for an access token and optionally a refresh token. The access token is then used to call the provider’s APIs or to establish an identity. The flow is secure because the user’s password never reaches the client application.

title: Q: [Q27] How do you implement Google OAuth2 client authentication in  Spring Security?
text: [Q27] How do you implement Google OAuth2 client authentication in Spring Security? Add spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client and register Google as a provider in application.yml with client ID, client secret, scope, and redirect URI. Expose a SecurityFilterChain that uses oauth2Login() to handle the Authorization Code flow. Spring Security manages the redirect to Google, the code exchange, and the userinfo call. You can map the returned user details to local application roles using an OAuth2UserService. For mobile or single-page apps, use PKCE and keep the client secret on the backend only.

title: Q: [Q28] What is the difference between OAuth2 Login and OAuth2  Resource Server?
text: [Q28] What is the difference between OAuth2 Login and OAuth2 Resource Server? OAuth2 Login makes your application an OAuth2 client that lets users sign in through an external provider such as Google or GitHub. It handles redirects and cookies or sessions. OAuth2 Resource Server makes your application an API that accepts bearer tokens, typically JWT, and validates them. It is used when a mobile app, SPA, or another service calls your API with an access token. A single application can be both a client for login and a resource server for its own APIs.

title: Q: [Q29] How do you handle Spring Security exceptions properly?
text: [Q29] How do you handle Spring Security exceptions properly? Spring Security throws AuthenticationException when the user cannot be authenticated and AccessDeniedException when the user lacks permission. You handle these by configuring an AuthenticationEntryPoint for 401 responses and an AccessDeniedHandler for 403 responses inside the SecurityFilterChain. For REST APIs, return structured JSON with a clear message and correlation ID rather than redirecting to an HTML page. A global @RestControllerAdvice is useful for application-level exceptions, but security exceptions are caught by the filter chain before they reach the controller.

title: Q: [Q30] How do you debug a 403 Forbidden response in Spring Security?
text: [Q30] How do you debug a 403 Forbidden response in Spring Security? Start by checking the logs with logging.level.org.springframework.security=DEBUG to see which filter rejected the request and why. Verify the request reached the right SecurityFilterChain. Confirm the user is authenticated and that the Authentication object contains the expected authorities. Check whether the path matcher is correct and whether hasRole or hasAuthority matches the stored authority string exactly. If the issue is method-level, ensure the method is being called through a Spring proxy and that @EnableMethodSecurity is active.

title: Q: [Q31] What is UserDetailsService, and how is it used?
text: [Q31] What is UserDetailsService, and how is it used? UserDetailsService is an interface with a single method, loadUserByUsername, that Spring Security calls to fetch user data during authentication. Your implementation typically queries the database and returns a UserDetails object containing username, password, enabled status, and authorities. This interface separates user retrieval from authentication logic. A common mistake is returning null or throwing a generic exception when the user is not found; the correct behavior is to throw UsernameNotFoundException so Spring Security can handle it consistently.

title: Q: [Q32] What is PasswordEncoder, and why should you use  BCryptPasswordEncoder?
text: [Q32] What is PasswordEncoder, and why should you use BCryptPasswordEncoder? PasswordEncoder is the Spring Security interface for hashing and verifying passwords. BCryptPasswordEncoder uses the bcrypt algorithm, which automatically salts and stretches passwords to resist brute-force and rainbow-table attacks. You never store plaintext passwords. During login, the encoder hashes the submitted password and compares it to the stored hash. Bcrypt also has a configurable strength parameter that controls how slow the hash is, letting you increase cost as hardware improves.

title: Q: [Q33] How do you secure passwords and secrets in a Spring Boot  application?
text: [Q33] How do you secure passwords and secrets in a Spring Boot application? Passwords and secrets must be externalized from source code and configuration files. Use environment variables for non-sensitive values and a secret manager such as Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or Spring Cloud Config with encryption for credentials. Rotate keys and passwords periodically. For JWT signing keys, use asymmetric keys and keep the private key only in the issuer. Never log secrets, and avoid returning them in Actuator endpoints or error responses.

title: Q: [Q34] What is Remember-Me authentication, and when should you avoid  it?
text: [Q34] What is Remember-Me authentication, and when should you avoid it? Remember-Me authentication allows a user to remain logged in across browser sessions using a persistent token stored in a cookie or database. It improves convenience but increases risk because a stolen remember-me cookie can grant long-term access. It should be avoided for sensitive applications such as banking or admin portals. If used, store the token securely, bind it to the user’s IP or device fingerprint, and give users a way to revoke all sessions.

title: Q: [Q35] How do you test Spring Security in a Spring Boot application?
text: [Q35] How do you test Spring Security in a Spring Boot application? Use MockMvc with @AutoConfigureMockMvc and @WithMockUser to simulate authenticated users. Use @WithAnonymousUser for unauthenticated scenarios. Test both the happy path and the denied path by checking the status code. For method security, verify that methods protected by @PreAuthorize reject users without the required authority. For JWT, test the filter behavior by generating a valid token in the test and sending it in the Authorization header. Integration tests should also verify that the security configuration does not accidentally expose sensitive endpoints.

title: Q: [Q36] What is HttpSecurity vs WebSecurity in Spring Security?
text: [Q36] What is HttpSecurity vs WebSecurity in Spring Security? HttpSecurity configures HTTP-level security such as URL access rules, form login, logout, CSRF, session management, and filters. WebSecurity is used for web-level configuration that is not request-specific, such as ignoring certain static resources like CSS, JavaScript, and images so they do not pass through the security filter chain. In Spring Boot 3, WebSecurityCustomizer is exposed as a bean for this purpose. Confusing the two can lead to static resources being unnecessarily secured or protected endpoints being accidentally ignored.

title: Q: [Q37] What is the purpose of ExceptionTranslationFilter?
text: [Q37] What is the purpose of ExceptionTranslationFilter? ExceptionTranslationFilter sits near the end of the security filter chain and catches AuthenticationException and AccessDeniedException thrown by other filters or access decision managers. It delegates to the configured AuthenticationEntryPoint to start authentication or to the AccessDeniedHandler to report authorization failure. Without this filter, security exceptions would bubble up as raw errors. It is why a missing token results in a 401 and an insufficient role results in a 403.

title: Q: [Q38] What is CORS, and how does it interact with Spring Security?
text: [Q38] What is CORS, and how does it interact with Spring Security? CORS, or Cross-Origin Resource Sharing, is a browser mechanism that controls whether a web page can request resources from a different domain. Spring Security can enforce CORS through the SecurityFilterChain using cors() with a CorsConfigurationSource bean. If you enable CORS at the security layer and also at the controller layer, the more restrictive of the two may apply. Allowing all origins with credentials enabled is dangerous because it lets any website make authenticated requests on behalf of users.

title: Q: [Q39] How do you implement logout in a JWT-based stateless  application?
text: [Q39] How do you implement logout in a JWT-based stateless application? True logout is tricky in a stateless system because the server does not track tokens. The common approach is token blacklisting: store the token or its identifier in a deny list such as Redis until it expires, and check every request against this list. Alternatively, keep access tokens very short-lived and revoke refresh tokens on logout. The client must also delete the token from its storage. Without server-side revocation, a stolen token remains valid until it expires.

title: Q: [Q40] How do you secure service-to-service communication in a  microservices architecture?
text: [Q40] How do you secure service-to-service communication in a microservices architecture? Service-to-service calls should use short-lived tokens, mutual TLS, or OAuth2 client credentials. Each service authenticates to a token issuer, receives an access token, and presents it when calling downstream services. The receiving service validates the token and enforces scopes or authorities. Mutual TLS adds an extra layer by verifying both client and server certificates. Use service mesh sidecars such as Istio or Linkerd if you want encryption and identity managed at the infrastructure layer rather than in application code.

title: Q: [Q41] What is an API Gateway’s role in security?
text: [Q41] What is an API Gateway’s role in security? An API Gateway sits in front of microservices and centralizes cross-cutting security concerns such as SSL termination, token validation, rate limiting, IP allow-listing, and request routing. It can validate JWTs or OAuth2 tokens and forward enriched headers to downstream services. However, the gateway is not a substitute for service-level security; each service should still enforce its own authorization rules so that internal traffic or a compromised gateway cannot bypass protection.

title: Q: [Q42] What are the risks of storing JWTs in browser local storage?
text: [Q42] What are the risks of storing JWTs in browser local storage? Local storage is accessible to JavaScript, so any XSS vulnerability allows an attacker to steal the token and impersonate the user. The safer approach is to store the access token in memory and keep the refresh token in an HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookie. If cookies are not an option, use short-lived access tokens and strict Content Security Policies to reduce the XSS attack surface. Tokens stored in local storage should never be used for highly sensitive applications.

title: Q: [Q43] How do you protect against brute-force login attacks?
text: [Q43] How do you protect against brute-force login attacks? Implement account lockout or progressive delays after repeated failed attempts. Use CAPTCHA after a threshold of failures. Rate-limit the login endpoint by IP or username using a tool such as Bucket4j, Resilience4j, or an API gateway. Log and alert on unusual login patterns. Strong password requirements and BCryptPasswordEncoder slow down offline cracking if the database is ever breached.

title: Q: [Q44] What is the principle of least privilege, and how do you apply it in  Spring Security?
text: [Q44] What is the principle of least privilege, and how do you apply it in Spring Security? The principle of least privilege means giving users and services only the minimum permissions they need. In Spring Security, this translates to using specific authorities instead of broad roles, denying access by default, and granting access only to required endpoints. Instead of giving every user ROLE_ADMIN, create fine-grained authorities like order:read, order:write, and order:delete. Regularly audit roles and permissions, and remove unused access during access reviews.

title: Q: [Q45] How do you configure HTTPS for a Spring Boot application?
text: [Q45] How do you configure HTTPS for a Spring Boot application? Place an SSL certificate and private key in the application or load them from a secret manager. Configure server.ssl.enabled=true, server.ssl.key-store, server.ssl.key-store-password, and related properties. In production, HTTPS is often terminated at a load balancer, reverse proxy, or API gateway rather than the application itself. If termination happens upstream, configure the proxy to forward the original protocol so Spring Security redirects and cookies work correctly.

title: Q: [Q46] What is the difference between permitAll, authenticated,  anonymous, and denyAll?
text: [Q46] What is the difference between permitAll, authenticated, anonymous, and denyAll? permitAll allows any request, including unauthenticated ones. authenticated requires a valid authentication. anonymous matches requests from users who have not logged in but have been assigned the default anonymous authentication object. denyAll blocks every request. These are used to build the access matrix in the SecurityFilterChain. A common error is using permitAll on health endpoints but accidentally exposing sensitive data through them.

title: Q: [Q47] How do you handle token expiration gracefully in a client-server  JWT flow?
text: [Q47] How do you handle token expiration gracefully in a client-server JWT flow? The server returns 401 with an error indicating the token is expired. The client checks for this specific error and uses the refresh token to request a new access token from a dedicated refresh endpoint. If the refresh token is also expired or revoked, the client redirects the user to login. The server should not extend access token lifetimes automatically, because that defeats the purpose of short-lived tokens and increases the window of misuse if a token is stolen.

title: Q: [Q48] What is GrantedAuthority, and how is it different from a role?
text: [Q48] What is GrantedAuthority, and how is it different from a role? GrantedAuthority is the interface that represents a permission assigned to a user, usually implemented as SimpleGrantedAuthority. A role is technically an authority that starts with ROLE_, and hasRole expects that prefix. An authority is any permission string. The difference is mostly convention: roles are broad, authorities are fine-grained. You can store both in the same collection and check them with hasRole, hasAuthority, or custom SpEL.

title: Q: [Q49] How do you implement multi-factor authentication in Spring  Security?
text: [Q49] How do you implement multi-factor authentication in Spring Security? Multi-factor authentication requires a second factor beyond the password. After the user enters the password, the system verifies the second factor, such as a TOTP code from an authenticator app, an SMS code, or a hardware key. In Spring Security, this is usually implemented with a custom authentication flow that stores a partially authenticated principal in the session, prompts for the second factor, and then marks the authentication as fully authenticated. Commercial identity providers such as Keycloak or Auth0 handle this out of the box and can be integrated as an OAuth2 provider.

title: Q: [Q50] What are the most important security checks before releasing a  Spring Boot application?
text: [Q50] What are the most important security checks before releasing a Spring Boot application? Before release, ensure HTTPS is enforced, CSRF is configured correctly for the client type, security headers are set, secrets are externalized, passwords are hashed with bcrypt, Actuator endpoints are restricted, error responses do not leak stack traces, JWT tokens are short-lived with secure key storage, method-level security is tested, and dependencies are scanned for known vulnerabilities. Perform penetration testing and review the security configuration in code review. Security is a continuous process, not a one-time checklist.

title: Q: [S1] A user reports they can access the admin dashboard even though  they are not an admin. How do you investigate?
text: [S1] A user reports they can access the admin dashboard even though they are not an admin. How do you investigate? First I verify the endpoint is actually protected in the SecurityFilterChain and that the user does not have the admin role due to a data issue. I check the authentication object in the logs or a debug endpoint to see the granted authorities. I look for bugs in the role assignment logic, such as assigning ROLE_ADMIN to all users during sign-up or failing to strip legacy roles after a promotion. I also check whether method-level security is being bypassed by self-invocation from within the same bean. The fix is to tighten the URL and method authorization rules, add tests for non-admin access, and audit the user-role mapping code.

title: Q: [S2] A penetration test report says your login endpoint is vulnerable to  SQL injection. How do you respond?
text: [S2] A penetration test report says your login endpoint is vulnerable to SQL injection. How do you respond? I immediately confirm whether the endpoint uses parameterized queries or an ORM. If the login query concatenates the username or password into SQL, I rewrite it to use named parameters or the JPA/Criteria API. I add server-side input validation and review all other endpoints for similar patterns. I run static analysis tools and add tests that pass malicious payloads such as ' OR '1'='1. Finally, I verify database accounts use least privilege so that even if injection occurs, the damage is limited.

title: Q: [S3] A developer disables CSRF to make a React frontend work, and now  the security team is concerned. What do you say?
text: [S3] A developer disables CSRF to make a React frontend work, and now the security team is concerned. What do you say? Disabling CSRF globally is only safe for stateless APIs that do not rely on session cookies. If the React app uses session cookies for authentication, disabling CSRF exposes the application to cross-site request forgery. The proper fix is to keep CSRF enabled and provide the token to the frontend, either as a cookie readable by JavaScript or through an endpoint, and have the React app send it back in a header for state-changing requests. Alternatively, switch to JWT in an HttpOnly cookie or an Authorization header, where CSRF is not relevant.

title: Q: [S4] A JWT token works in dev but is rejected in production with a 401.  How do you debug?
text: [S4] A JWT token works in dev but is rejected in production with a 401. How do you debug? I check whether the production signing key matches the one used to sign the token. If the token was issued in dev and production uses a different secret, it will fail validation. I verify the token has not expired and that the issuer and audience claims match the production configuration. I also check the clock skew between the issuer and verifier, and I confirm the filter reads the Authorization header correctly without being blocked by a proxy or gateway. The fix is to align key management and claims validation across environments, ideally using a single issuer or public key distribution.

title: Q: [S5] A user logged out, but their JWT is still able to access protected  resources. How do you fix this?
text: [S5] A user logged out, but their JWT is still able to access protected resources. How do you fix this? In a stateless JWT system, logout only works if the server keeps a deny list of tokens until they expire. I implement token blacklisting using Redis or a database, storing the token identifier or a hash keyed by expiration time. A custom filter checks every request against this list and rejects blacklisted tokens. Alternatively, I keep access tokens very short-lived and revoke refresh tokens on logout, so the user must re-authenticate when the short token expires. The client must also delete tokens from storage.

title: Q: [S6] A production API returns a 500 error with a full stack trace  containing the database password. How do you prevent this?
text: [S6] A production API returns a 500 error with a full stack trace containing the database password. How do you prevent this? A stack trace with secrets means an unhandled exception leaked through the default error response. I add a global @RestControllerAdvice that catches exceptions and returns a sanitized JSON response with only a generic message and a correlation ID. I set server.error.include-stacktrace=never and server.error.include- message=never in production. I ensure no secrets are placed in exception messages. Finally, I review the error logging pipeline to make sure full stack traces go to the centralized logging system, not the API response.

title: Q: [S7] A Spring Security filter you added is being ignored. What could be  wrong?
text: [S7] A Spring Security filter you added is being ignored. What could be wrong? Custom filters must be registered at the correct position in the filter chain using addFilterBefore, addFilterAfter, or addFilterAt. If the filter is added at the wrong position, authentication or authorization may run before it, causing the request to be processed or rejected before your filter executes. Also, the filter must be a Spring bean if it depends on other beans, but it should not be registered as a servlet filter automatically unless you want it to run twice. I verify the filter order in the logs with debug logging enabled and adjust the registration accordingly.

title: Q: [S8] A third-party OAuth2 provider integration is failing after a certificate  rotation. How do you investigate?
text: [S8] A third-party OAuth2 provider integration is failing after a certificate rotation. How do you investigate? I check the TLS truststore used by the JVM to verify the provider’s new certificate. If the certificate chain is not trusted, the token exchange or userinfo call fails with a certificate validation error. I update the truststore with the new issuer certificates or switch to a well-known CA bundle. I also verify the client ID, client secret, redirect URI, and scopes match the provider’s current configuration. Finally, I confirm that the token issuer and userinfo endpoints have not changed.

title: Q: [S9] A @PreAuthorize annotation on a service method does not work  when called from the controller. Why?
text: [S9] A @PreAuthorize annotation on a service method does not work when called from the controller. Why? If the method is called from another method in the same bean, the Spring proxy is bypassed and the annotation is ignored. This is a classic self-invocation problem. I move the annotated method to another bean and inject it, or use AopContext.currentProxy() to force a proxied call. I also verify that @EnableMethodSecurity is present and that the SpEL expression uses the correct role or authority names, remembering that hasRole adds the ROLE_ prefix.

title: Q: [S10] Your application uses Google OAuth2 login, but users are getting  assigned the same default role. How do you fix role mapping?
text: [S10] Your application uses Google OAuth2 login, but users are getting assigned the same default role. How do you fix role mapping? By default, Spring Security assigns limited authorities based on the OAuth2 provider response. I implement a custom OAuth2UserService that calls Google’s userinfo endpoint and maps returned attributes to local roles stored in the database. For example, I might check a domain, email pattern, or a pre-registered admin list. I then build a new OAuth2User with the mapped GrantedAuthority collection. I also ensure that roles are not hard-coded and can be updated without redeploying the application.

title: Q: [S11] A load test shows that JWT validation is becoming a CPU  bottleneck. How do you optimize it?
text: [S11] A load test shows that JWT validation is becoming a CPU bottleneck. How do you optimize it? I first check whether validation uses symmetric key signing with a shared secret that requires expensive HMAC operations on every request, and whether the token is parsed from scratch each time. Switching to an asymmetric key pair lets verification services use only the public key, which is faster and safer. I cache the parsed token or the decoded public key. I also verify the token format before parsing to fail fast on malformed tokens. If the bottleneck persists, I move token validation to an API Gateway or sidecar so backend services receive pre-validated claims.

title: Q: [S12] A user changed their password, but old sessions on other devices  remain active. How do you handle this?
text: [S12] A user changed their password, but old sessions on other devices remain active. How do you handle this? For session-based applications, I invalidate all sessions associated with the user by using Spring Security’s session registry, specifically for concurrent session control. For JWT-based applications, I issue new access and refresh tokens and revoke or blacklist all existing refresh tokens for that user. The client must re-authenticate on other devices. I also add a tokenVersion or passwordChangedAt claim in the JWT and reject tokens issued before the password change.

title: Q: [S13] A penetration test found that the refresh token endpoint does not  rotate tokens. How do you fix it?
text: [S13] A penetration test found that the refresh token endpoint does not rotate tokens. How do you fix it? Token rotation means issuing a new refresh token every time a refresh request is made and invalidating the old one. I update the refresh endpoint to verify the incoming refresh token, delete or mark the stored token as used, and issue a new access token along with a new refresh token. This limits the window in which a stolen refresh token can be used. I also bind refresh tokens to the device fingerprint or a rotating family identifier so that reuse of an old token invalidates the entire family, preventing silent token theft.

title: Q: [S14] A mobile app reports that tokens expire too quickly, causing  frequent login prompts. What is the balance?
text: [S14] A mobile app reports that tokens expire too quickly, causing frequent login prompts. What is the balance? The balance is between security and user experience. Access tokens should remain short, for example 5 to 15 minutes, because they travel with every request. Refresh tokens can live longer, for example days or weeks, but must be stored securely and rotated. For mobile apps, using a secure enclave or encrypted storage for the refresh token and implementing silent refresh on 401 responses gives a smooth experience without sacrificing safety. Long-lived access tokens are not the right answer because theft becomes much more dangerous.

title: Q: [S15] A developer stores the JWT secret key in  application.properties and checks it into Git. What do you  recommend?
text: [S15] A developer stores the JWT secret key in application.properties and checks it into Git. What do you recommend? I treat the exposed secret as compromised and rotate it immediately. The secret key must be removed from source control and moved to a secret manager such as Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or Azure Key Vault. For microservices, I recommend switching to asymmetric signing so verification services only need the public key. I add the properties file to .gitignore, scan the repository history for other secrets, and implement pre-commit hooks to prevent future commits of credentials.

title: Q: [S16] A state-changing API does not check user ownership and allows  modifying any record by ID. How do you fix this?
text: [S16] A state-changing API does not check user ownership and allows modifying any record by ID. How do you fix this? This is an insecure direct object reference and missing authorization. The fix is to add an ownership check inside the service layer, for example by comparing the record’s owner to the current principal. Use @PreAuthorize with a SpEL expression that checks the owner, or perform the check in code after loading the entity from the database. Never trust the client to send the correct ID without verifying it against the authenticated user. I also add tests that confirm a user cannot update another user’s records.

title: Q: [S17] A production incident shows brute-force attempts on the login  endpoint. How do you respond?
text: [S17] A production incident shows brute-force attempts on the login endpoint. How do you respond? I immediately implement rate limiting on the login endpoint by IP address and username using Bucket4j, Resilience4j, or the API Gateway. I add progressive delays and CAPTCHA after repeated failures. I log and alert on attack patterns and consider temporarily blocking IPs at the firewall or WAF level. I verify that passwords are hashed with BCryptPasswordEncoder so offline cracking is slow if the database is breached. I also enable monitoring for credential-stuffing patterns and encourage users with weak or reused passwords to change them.

title: Q: [S18] A frontend developer reports CORS errors after enabling Spring  Security. How do you resolve them without weakening security?
text: [S18] A frontend developer reports CORS errors after enabling Spring Security. How do you resolve them without weakening security? CORS errors happen when the browser blocks a cross-origin request. I configure CORS in the SecurityFilterChain using a CorsConfigurationSource bean that defines allowed origins, methods, headers, and whether credentials are allowed. I do not use a wildcard origin when credentials are enabled, because that allows any website to make authenticated requests. Instead, I explicitly list trusted origins and keep allowed methods minimal. I also ensure the preflight OPTIONS request is permitted without authentication.

title: Q: [S19] A JWT-based API needs to revoke access for a user immediately  due to a security incident. How do you do it?
text: [S19] A JWT-based API needs to revoke access for a user immediately due to a security incident. How do you do it? Because JWTs are stateless, I cannot revoke an access token directly unless it is expired. I implement token blacklisting using Redis keyed by token identifier or expiration time, and I update the authentication filter to reject blacklisted tokens. For faster response, I keep access tokens very short-lived and revoke all refresh tokens for the user. I also update the user record to mark the account as locked or disabled so that any new login attempt fails. If the incident is severe, I rotate the signing key, which invalidates all existing tokens across the system.

title: Q: [S20] An internal service calls your API with a service account token.  How do you authorize it differently from a human user?
text: [S20] An internal service calls your API with a service account token. How do you authorize it differently from a human user? I distinguish service accounts from human users by claim values such as client_id, sub, or a custom account_type claim. Service accounts get authorities that represent API scopes or permissions, not UI roles. I use @PreAuthorize("hasAuthority('service:invoice:read')") to restrict service endpoints and keep service account tokens short-lived, ideally with a lifetime tied to the request. I also log service-to-service calls separately and monitor for unusual call volumes or scope creep.

title: Q: [S21] A developer uses permitAll on the /actuator/health endpoint  but it still returns 401. Why?
text: [S21] A developer uses permitAll on the /actuator/health endpoint but it still returns 401. Why? The endpoint might be configured in the wrong SecurityFilterChain or the request might be hitting a different base path. If management endpoints are on a separate port or context path, the main SecurityFilterChain may not apply. Another cause is that the request is being rejected by an earlier filter, such as a custom JWT filter that returns 401 for any request without a token before the authorization filter can evaluate permitAll. I debug by enabling Spring Security logging and checking the exact chain that processes the request.

title: Q: [S22] A user role hierarchy should allow managers to do everything  users can. How do you model this?
text: [S22] A user role hierarchy should allow managers to do everything users can. How do you model this? Spring Security supports role hierarchies through a RoleHierarchy bean. I define that ROLE_MANAGER implies ROLE_USER, and then use hasRole("USER") in authorization rules. A manager will automatically be granted the implied authority. This avoids duplicating permission checks across roles and keeps the authority model maintainable. Without role hierarchy, I would have to explicitly check both roles in every rule, which is error-prone.

title: Q: [S23] A Swagger UI is publicly exposed in production and the security  team wants it removed. What is the best approach?
text: [S23] A Swagger UI is publicly exposed in production and the security team wants it removed. What is the best approach? The best approach is to disable Swagger UI in production using Spring profiles, for example by only enabling the SpringDoc configuration when the dev or qa profile is active. Alternatively, I restrict Swagger endpoints with Spring Security to internal users only. If Swagger is not needed in production at all, I remove the dependency from the production build. I keep OpenAPI spec generation in CI so documentation stays accurate, but the interactive UI is not reachable publicly.

title: Q: [S24] A user with a valid token sees 403 on some endpoints after a  deployment. What do you check?
text: [S24] A user with a valid token sees 403 on some endpoints after a deployment. What do you check? I check whether the deployment changed authority strings or role mappings. For example, a role renamed from ADMIN to ROLE_ADMIN or an authority renamed from user.write to user:create breaks existing @PreAuthorize expressions. I verify the UserDetailsService or token claims builder populates the same authorities the rules expect. I also check whether the endpoints moved to a new path that is no longer covered by the URL authorization rule. Logging the authentication object and the denied request path quickly shows the mismatch.

title: Q: [S25] A Spring Security application must support both human users and  API clients. How do you configure it?
text: [S25] A Spring Security application must support both human users and API clients. How do you configure it? I configure multiple SecurityFilterChain beans with different request matchers and authentication mechanisms. One chain handles browser users with OAuth2 login or form login and session cookies. Another chain handles API clients with JWT bearer tokens and a stateless session policy. Each chain matches based on path or header patterns. This separation prevents a misconfiguration meant for browser users, such as CSRF protection, from breaking API clients, and vice versa. Both chains still enforce authorization based on roles and authorities.

title: Q: [Q1] Why is testing important in a Spring Boot application, and what  does Spring Boot provide out of the box?
text: [Q1] Why is testing important in a Spring Boot application, and what does Spring Boot provide out of the box? Testing is important because it catches regressions early, documents expected behavior, and gives confidence during refactoring or production incidents. Spring Boot provides the spring-boot-starter-test starter, which bundles JUnit 5, Mockito, AssertJ, Hamcrest, JSONassert, and JsonPath. It also offers slice test annotations such as @WebMvcTest, @DataJpaTest, and @RestClientTest, which load only the relevant slice of the application context so tests run faster than a full @SpringBootTest. The starter also configures the build tools to separate unit tests from integration tests, which is useful on CI where you want quick feedback before running slower integration suites.

title: Q: [Q2] What is the difference between unit testing and integration testing  in a Spring Boot project?
text: [Q2] What is the difference between unit testing and integration testing in a Spring Boot project? A unit test isolates a single class or method and replaces all collaborators with test doubles, so a failure points directly to the unit under test. An integration test loads part or all of the Spring context and exercises real collaborators such as the database, the web layer, or message brokers. Unit tests are fast, deterministic, and run without a server or database. Integration tests are slower but verify wiring, transaction behavior, serialization, and configuration that unit tests cannot. A healthy project runs unit tests on every commit and integration tests in a separate CI stage or nightly job.

title: Q: [Q3] What is JUnit 5, and how is it different from JUnit 4?
text: [Q3] What is JUnit 5, and how is it different from JUnit 4? JUnit 5 is the current generation of the JUnit testing framework. It has a modular architecture made of JUnit Platform, which discovers and runs tests; JUnit Jupiter, which provides the modern programming and extension model; and JUnit Vintage, which can run JUnit 3 and 4 tests. Unlike JUnit 4, it uses @Test, @BeforeEach, @AfterEach, @BeforeAll, and @AfterAll annotations, supports parameterized tests, dynamic tests, and a powerful extension API. The extension model replaces JUnit 4 runners and rules, so Spring uses SpringExtension instead of the old SpringRunner.

title: Q: [Q4] Explain the lifecycle annotations @BeforeAll, @BeforeEach,  @AfterEach, and @AfterAll in JUnit 5.
