text: [Q4] Explain the lifecycle annotations @BeforeAll, @BeforeEach, @AfterEach, and @AfterAll in JUnit 5. @BeforeAll runs once before any test in the class and is typically used for expensive setup such as starting a database container. @BeforeEach runs before every test and is used to reset state, create fresh mocks, or reinitialize the object under test. @AfterEach runs after every test to clean up resources like open files or database rows inserted by the test. @AfterAll runs once after all tests complete and is used to stop containers or close connection pools. Static setup methods must be static, which is a common trap when you need shared state across tests.

title: Q: [Q5] What is AssertJ, and why do developers prefer it over standard  JUnit assertions?
text: [Q5] What is AssertJ, and why do developers prefer it over standard JUnit assertions? AssertJ is a fluent assertion library that makes test failures readable and assertions easy to write. Instead of assertEquals(expected, actual), you write assertThat(actual).isEqualTo(expected). It has rich support for collections, exceptions, strings, dates, and JSON, and the failure messages are often clearer than JUnit's defaults. For example, assertThatThrownBy(() -> service.findById(-1)).isInstanceOf(NotFoundException.class).hasMes sageContaining("not found") reads like a sentence and bundles multiple checks. AssertJ also avoids confusion about the expected-versus-actual parameter order, which is a common source of misleading failure messages.

title: Q: [Q6] How do you write a basic unit test for a Spring Boot service  method?
text: [Q6] How do you write a basic unit test for a Spring Boot service method? You test the service class directly without loading the Spring context. You create the service instance yourself or use Mockito's @InjectMocks, and you provide its dependencies with @Mock. In the test you arrange the inputs and mock behavior, act by calling the service method, and assert the result or verify interactions. For example, if OrderService depends on OrderRepository, you mock the repository to return an order, call orderService.findById(1L), and assert that the returned order has the expected customer name. Since the Spring context is not loaded, the test starts in milliseconds.

title: Q: [Q7] What is Mockito, and what problem does it solve?
text: [Q7] What is Mockito, and what problem does it solve? Mockito is a mocking framework that lets you create test doubles for dependencies so you can isolate the class you are testing. It solves the problem of slow or unavailable collaborators such as databases, external REST services, or message queues during unit tests. With Mockito you can define stubbed responses, verify that certain methods were called, and capture the arguments passed to mocked methods. This keeps unit tests focused, fast, and deterministic. A common mistake is mocking everything including simple value objects, which makes tests brittle and less trustworthy.

title: Q: [Q8] What is the difference between @Mock, @Spy, and @InjectMocks in  Mockito?
text: [Q8] What is the difference between @Mock, @Spy, and @InjectMocks in Mockito? @Mock creates a fully mocked object where every method returns default values unless stubbed. @Spy creates a partial mock that wraps a real instance and calls real methods by default, unless you stub a specific method. @InjectMocks creates an instance of the class under test and tries to inject mocks or spies into its constructor, fields, or setter methods. @InjectMocks is convenient for service-layer tests, but constructor injection is cleaner because it makes dependencies explicit and avoids reflection-based injection, which can silently fail if field names do not match.

title: Q: [Q9] How do you stub a method with Mockito, and what happens if a  stubbed method is called with unexpected arguments?
text: [Q9] How do you stub a method with Mockito, and what happens if a stubbed method is called with unexpected arguments? You stub a method with when(mock.method(args)).thenReturn(value), or with the BDD-style given(mock.method(args)).willReturn(value). If the method is called with arguments that do not match any stubbing, Mockito returns the default value for the return type, such as null for objects, 0 for numbers, or false for booleans. This silent default return is a frequent source of confusion because the test may pass even though the mock interaction is wrong. To catch this, use Mockito.strictness(Strictness.STRICT_STUBS), which fails the test if a stubbed mock is called with unexpected arguments or if a stubbed method is never called.

title: Q: [Q10] What is the purpose of verify() in Mockito, and when should you  use it?
text: [Q10] What is the purpose of verify() in Mockito, and when should you use it? verify() checks that a specific interaction happened with a mock, such as a method being called a certain number of times or with specific arguments. You use it when the side effect of the method under test is more important than the returned value, for example when a service saves a record or sends an email. verify(repository).save(order) confirms that persistence happened, while verify(emailClient, never()).send(any()) confirms that no email was sent for an invalid order. However, overusing verify() on every collaborator makes tests brittle; prefer asserting outcomes and verify only meaningful interactions.

title: Q: [Q11] What is @MockBean, and when do you use it instead of @Mock?
text: [Q11] What is @MockBean, and when do you use it instead of @Mock? @MockBean is a Spring test annotation that replaces an existing bean in the Spring application context with a Mockito mock. You use it in integration or slice tests where the context is loaded, such as @WebMvcTest or @SpringBootTest, and you need a real controller but a fake service. @Mock is used in plain unit tests where there is no Spring context. A common pitfall is using @MockBean in a class with many tests; it triggers context caching invalidation because Spring sees a modified context, which can slow down the whole suite.

title: Q: [Q12] How do you test the persistence layer in Spring Boot, and what  does @DataJpaTest do?
text: [Q12] How do you test the persistence layer in Spring Boot, and what does @DataJpaTest do? The persistence layer is tested with @DataJpaTest, which configures an in-memory or real database, loads @Entity classes, and creates a default TestEntityManager and repository beans. It does not load the full application context, so web components and services are excluded. You write tests that save entities through the repository and assert that custom query methods return the expected results. Because @DataJpaTest is transactional, each test runs inside a transaction that rolls back automatically, leaving the database clean. For complex queries, an in-memory database may behave differently from production, so many teams switch to Testcontainers with the real database vendor.

title: Q: [Q13] What are Testcontainers, and why would you use them for  repository tests?
text: [Q13] What are Testcontainers, and why would you use them for repository tests? Testcontainers is a Java library that starts lightweight, throwaway Docker containers for databases, message brokers, and other services during tests. For repository tests, it lets you run PostgreSQL, MySQL, or Oracle inside a container instead of relying on H2. This eliminates subtle differences in SQL dialect, transaction behavior, and vendor-specific features between the test database and production. The container starts once per test class, tests run against the real database, and the container is stopped after the tests finish. The trade-off is slower startup, but the confidence gained is worth it for queries that use native SQL, JSON columns, or stored procedures.

title: Q: [Q14] How do you configure Testcontainers for a Spring Boot  @DataJpaTest?
text: [Q14] How do you configure Testcontainers for a Spring Boot @DataJpaTest? You define a container as a static field annotated with @Container, start it in @BeforeAll, and set Spring DataSource properties so the test context connects to the running container. With the latest Spring Boot and Testcontainers integration, you can also use @ServiceConnection so Spring Boot automatically resolves the connection details from the container. You add the database driver and Testcontainers dependency, and you may reuse the same container across tests with a singleton pattern or the Testcontainers reuse feature to reduce startup time. The key is to keep container lifecycle separate from the test transaction so the container keeps running while individual tests still roll back.

title: Q: [Q15] What is @WebMvcTest, and what does it load?
text: [Q15] What is @WebMvcTest, and what does it load? @WebMvcTest is a slice test annotation that loads only the Spring MVC web layer. It auto-configures MockMvc, registers @Controller and @ControllerAdvice beans, and excludes the full service, repository, and component scan unless explicitly imported. It is ideal for testing REST controllers because you can simulate HTTP requests, validate status codes, response bodies, and exception handling without starting an embedded server. Collaborating services are provided with @MockBean. A common mistake is expecting security filters to be fully configured; while Spring Security filters are registered, authentication must be mocked or disabled depending on the test intent.

title: Q: [Q16] What is MockMvc, and how does it differ from TestRestTemplate  or WebTestClient?
text: [Q16] What is MockMvc, and how does it differ from TestRestTemplate or WebTestClient? MockMvc is a Spring testing utility that dispatches HTTP requests through the DispatcherServlet without starting a real servlet container. It tests controller behavior, binding, validation, exception handling, and filter interactions in a lightweight way. TestRestTemplate and WebTestClient are used with @SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = RANDOM_PORT) to send real HTTP requests to an embedded server. MockMvc is faster and more focused, while the real-client approach verifies the full stack including actual serialization and container filters. For most controller tests, MockMvc is sufficient; reserve the real server approach for integration tests that need end-to-end HTTP semantics.

title: Q: [Q17] What is @SpringBootTest, and what are its web environment  options?
text: [Q17] What is @SpringBootTest, and what are its web environment options? @SpringBootTest loads the complete Spring Boot application context and is used for full integration tests. Its webEnvironment attribute controls how the web layer is started. MOCK loads a web context but does not start a real server, RANDOM_PORT starts an embedded server on a random port, DEFINED_PORT uses the configured server.port, and NONE does not start a web environment. RANDOM_PORT is the safest choice for integration tests because it avoids port conflicts on CI and lets TestRestTemplate or WebTestClient send real requests. MOCK is useful when you want the web beans configured but prefer MockMvc.

title: Q: [Q18] What is the Spring TestContext framework, and how does  caching affect test performance?
text: [Q18] What is the Spring TestContext framework, and how does caching affect test performance? The Spring TestContext framework manages the application context for tests, including dependency injection, transaction management, and context caching. When multiple test classes use the same context configuration, Spring reuses the cached context instead of creating a new one, which saves significant startup time. However, adding or replacing beans with @MockBean, @SpyBean, or different @TestPropertySource values changes the context key and forces a new context to load. A suite that uses many different @MockBean combinations can suffer from context cache pollution and slow builds, so it is worth grouping tests that share the same context.

title: Q: [Q19] How do you test an asynchronous method in Spring Boot?
text: [Q19] How do you test an asynchronous method in Spring Boot? Asynchronous methods are usually annotated with @Async and executed by a TaskExecutor. In a unit test, you call the method and use CompletableFuture.get() or Future.get() with a timeout to wait for the result. In an integration test, you may inject the executor and wait for active tasks to finish, or use Awaitility to poll until the expected side effect occurs. A common mistake is asserting immediately after the async call, which usually fails because the task has not run yet. You should also test the error path by verifying that exceptions are handled by the returned Future or by an AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler for void async methods.

title: Q: [Q20] What is JaCoCo, and what does it measure?
text: [Q20] What is JaCoCo, and what does it measure? JaCoCo is a Java code coverage library that instruments bytecode during test execution and reports how much of the code was executed. It measures instruction coverage, branch coverage, line coverage, method coverage, and class coverage. The HTML report shows which lines were covered and which branches were missed, while the XML report is consumed by CI tools like SonarQube. Coverage is a useful signal but not a goal by itself; 100 percent line coverage with weak assertions is misleading because the code can be exercised without being verified. JaCoCo should be paired with meaningful assertions and reviewed alongside the test suite quality.

title: Q: [Q21] How do you exclude classes or methods from JaCoCo coverage?
text: [Q21] How do you exclude classes or methods from JaCoCo coverage? You configure exclusions in the JaCoCo Maven or Gradle plugin using patterns such as **/config/*, **/dto/**, or **/*Exception. Lombok-generated methods can be excluded with the lombok.addLombokGeneratedAnnotation = true setting, which marks generated code so JaCoCo ignores it. Exclusions are useful for boilerplate such as equals and hashCode, mapping classes, or generated sources, but excluding real business logic to hit a coverage target is a harmful practice. The goal is to reduce noise, not to game the metric.

title: Q: [Q22] What are parameterized tests in JUnit 5, and when are they useful?
text: [Q22] What are parameterized tests in JUnit 5, and when are they useful? Parameterized tests let you run the same test logic with multiple sets of inputs. You can use @ValueSource for simple values, @CsvSource for tabular data, @MethodSource for complex objects, or @ArgumentsSource for custom suppliers. They are useful for validation rules, pricing calculations, or any logic that must behave consistently across many cases. For example, a single parameterized test can check that a discount calculator returns the correct amount for customer tiers GOLD, SILVER, and BRONZE. Without parameterized tests, you would copy- paste nearly identical test methods, which is hard to maintain.

title: Q: [Q23] What is the difference between @MockBean and @SpyBean in  Spring tests?
text: [Q23] What is the difference between @MockBean and @SpyBean in Spring tests? @MockBean completely replaces a bean in the Spring context with a Mockito mock, so calls return stubbed values by default. @SpyBean wraps the real bean, so real behavior runs unless a specific method is stubbed. Use @MockBean when the real implementation should not execute, such as an external payment gateway or a repository that is not needed for a controller test. Use @SpyBean when you want to verify that the real bean was called or when you need to override one method of an otherwise real service. Spies are useful but can make tests harder to debug because the real code runs and may trigger unexpected side effects.

title: Q: [Q24] How do you handle test data setup in integration tests without  making tests depend on each other?
text: [Q24] How do you handle test data setup in integration tests without making tests depend on each other? Each test should start from a clean, well-defined state. Use the transactional rollback behavior of @Transactional on tests so database changes are undone. For data that must survive across transactions, such as when testing a service that calls @Transactional(propagation = REQUIRES_NEW), use TestEntityManager or a custom cleanup in @AfterEach to delete rows. Avoid relying on data created by a previous test because it makes execution order matter and causes flaky failures. Some teams use SQL scripts or libraries like Flyway for baseline schema and small seed data sets, but test-specific data should be created and cleaned up by the test itself.

title: Q: [S1] A developer writes a service test that passes locally but fails on CI  because of timezone differences. How do you investigate and fix it?
text: [S1] A developer writes a service test that passes locally but fails on CI because of timezone differences. How do you investigate and fix it? I first look at any date or timestamp assertions in the test and in the production code. If the test creates an expected value using LocalDateTime.now() or new Date() and compares it against a value returned by the service, the comparison will fail when CI runs in a different zone or at a different millisecond. The fix is to avoid comparing against wall-clock time in unit tests; instead, inject a Clock bean or use a fixed timestamp provided by the test. For integration tests, I ensure the JVM timezone and database timezone are explicit, for example by setting -Duser.timezone=UTC in the CI pipeline and using TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE columns. I also check whether the test relies on the current date for filtering and replace it with a controlled reference date.

title: Q: [S2] A @DataJpaTest passes on its own but fails when the whole suite  runs because of leftover data. What is the cause?
text: [S2] A @DataJpaTest passes on its own but fails when the whole suite runs because of leftover data. What is the cause? The cause is usually a test that commits data and does not clean up, or a non-transactional operation that leaks state. @DataJpaTest is transactional by default, so most tests roll back, but a test calling a service method annotated with @Transactional(propagation = REQUIRES_NEW) commits in a new transaction and leaves data behind. Another cause is a @Sql script that inserts shared rows without cleanup, or a test that uses native JdbcTemplate without a transaction. I find the offending test by running the suite with logging enabled and inspecting the database state after each test class. The fix is to add explicit cleanup in @AfterEach, use unique identifiers per test, or refactor the production code so tests can rely on automatic rollback.

title: Q: [S3] A controller test with @WebMvcTest returns a 200 status but the  response body is empty. How do you debug it?
text: [S3] A controller test with @WebMvcTest returns a 200 status but the response body is empty. How do you debug it? An empty body usually means the controller returns a response object but it is not serialized, or the mock did not return the expected object. I first verify that the controller method is actually returning the object and that @RestController or @ResponseBody is present. Then I check the mocked service; if when(service.findById(1L)).thenReturn(order) uses a different argument than the controller passes, Mockito returns null and Jackson serializes an empty body. I also check that the object has getters, because Jackson needs them. Finally, I inspect the MockMvc result to see whether an exception was swallowed, and I enable debug logging for org.springframework.web to trace the request through the filters.

title: Q: [S4] A service test using Mockito throws an  UnnecessaryStubbingException after a refactor. What is happening?
text: [S4] A service test using Mockito throws an UnnecessaryStubbingException after a refactor. What is happening? UnnecessaryStubbingException is thrown when strict stubbing is enabled and a stubbed interaction was never used during the test. After the refactor, the production code may no longer call the stubbed method, or the test may have stubbed a method that was only relevant to an older implementation. I review the test to remove unused stubs and to confirm that the remaining stubs are actually required by the scenario being tested. If strict stubbing is too noisy for a class with shared setup, I either split the test into smaller focused tests or apply Mockito.lenient() only where a shared stub is acceptable. The goal is to keep the test honest about what it is verifying.

title: Q: [S5] An integration test with @SpringBootTest(webEnvironment =  RANDOM_PORT) fails because the actuator health endpoint returns 404.  How do you fix it?
text: [S5] An integration test with @SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = RANDOM_PORT) fails because the actuator health endpoint returns 404. How do you fix it? The actuator endpoint is not loaded because @SpringBootTest does not auto- configure actuator unless the actuator starter is on the classpath and the endpoint is enabled. I check that spring-boot-starter-actuator is a dependency and that the management.endpoints.web.exposure.include property includes health. If the test uses webEnvironment = NONE, actuator web endpoints are not available, so I switch to RANDOM_PORT or MOCK. I also verify that the test does not override management.server.port to a fixed port that collides with another test. When actuator is intentionally disabled for a test, I either enable it for that test class or assert against an internal service bean instead of the HTTP endpoint.

title: Q: [S6] A test with Testcontainers is extremely slow because the database  container starts for every test class. How do you speed it up?
text: [S6] A test with Testcontainers is extremely slow because the database container starts for every test class. How do you speed it up? I start by moving the container declaration to a base class or a shared utility so the same container instance is reused across multiple test classes. With Testcontainers, I enable the testcontainers.reuse.enable flag in ~/.testcontainers.properties so containers stay alive between test runs during local development. On CI, I ensure Docker layer caching is enabled and that the base image is pulled before the build. I also look for unnecessary @DirtiesContext annotations that force the Spring context to restart. If the suite is still slow, I move the fastest unit-like repository tests to H2 and reserve Testcontainers only for tests that exercise vendor-specific SQL or stored procedures.

title: Q: [S7] A developer disables CSRF in a @WebMvcTest and now real  requests fail with 403 in production. What went wrong?
text: [S7] A developer disables CSRF in a @WebMvcTest and now real requests fail with 403 in production. What went wrong? The test disabled CSRF to make the mock setup easier, but the developer assumed the controller behavior was the only thing being tested. CSRF is part of Spring Security, and disabling it in tests hides the fact that state-changing browser requests require a valid token. The fix is to configure MockMvc with the same security settings used in production and to include CSRF tokens in state-changing requests using csrf(). For pure API clients using bearer tokens, CSRF is legitimately disabled, but that decision belongs in the production configuration, not hidden in tests. Tests should mirror production security as closely as possible to catch integration issues early.

title: Q: [S8] A test with @MockBean runs fine alone but causes context reloads  and slow builds across the suite. How do you address it?
text: [S8] A test with @MockBean runs fine alone but causes context reloads and slow builds across the suite. How do you address it? @MockBean changes the Spring context fingerprint, so every unique combination of mocked beans triggers a new context cache entry. If many test classes mock different sets of beans, the cache fills up and contexts are constantly created and destroyed. I address this by grouping tests that need the same mocks into the same test class or by using @Import to share a common test configuration that declares the mocks once. For unit tests that do not need the Spring context at all, I replace @MockBean with plain Mockito @Mock and remove @SpringBootTest, which eliminates context reloads entirely. When a slice test such as @WebMvcTest is sufficient, I use it instead of the full context.

title: Q: [S9] A repository test using H2 passes, but the same query fails in  production on PostgreSQL. Why?
text: [S9] A repository test using H2 passes, but the same query fails in production on PostgreSQL. Why? H2 supports a different SQL dialect than PostgreSQL, and some queries that work in H2 may use syntax, functions, or type casting that PostgreSQL does not accept. Native queries, JSON operators, window functions, and custom types are common sources of mismatch. The fix is to move the test from H2 to a Testcontainers PostgreSQL container so the test database matches production. I also review the query for dialect-specific features and consider using JPQL or the Criteria API for portable logic. If a native query is unavoidable, I write a dedicated integration test against the real database and keep the H2 test only for simple repository methods where dialect differences do not matter.

title: Q: [S10] A developer writes a unit test that calls a private method using  reflection. Is this a good practice, and what would you suggest?
text: [S10] A developer writes a unit test that calls a private method using reflection. Is this a good practice, and what would you suggest? Testing private methods through reflection is usually not a good practice because it couples the test to internal implementation details and makes refactoring painful. Private methods are tested indirectly by testing the public methods that call them. If the private method is complex enough to need its own tests, that is often a sign that it should be extracted into a separate class with public methods that can be tested directly. The exception is legacy code that cannot be refactored safely; in that case, reflection may be a temporary bridge, but the goal should be to refactor the code rather than maintain reflection-based tests.

title: Q: [S11] A code coverage report shows 90 percent line coverage, but a bug  still reaches production. How is that possible?
text: [S11] A code coverage report shows 90 percent line coverage, but a bug still reaches production. How is that possible? Line coverage only measures whether a line was executed, not whether it was verified. A test can execute error-handling code without asserting that the correct exception is thrown, or it can call a method with a weak assertion that misses the bug. Coverage also does not measure edge cases, concurrency, or integration failures. I treat coverage as a hygiene metric, not a quality guarantee. I review tests for meaningful assertions, add mutation testing or property-based testing for critical paths, and run integration tests against realistic data. If a bug escapes, I add a regression test that fails before the fix and passes after it.

title: Q: [S12] A test that verifies an email was sent keeps failing intermittently.  How do you stabilize it?
text: [S12] A test that verifies an email was sent keeps failing intermittently. How do you stabilize it? Intermittent failures in asynchronous email sending usually come from timing issues. The test asserts before the background thread has finished, or the mock interaction happens on a different thread than the test thread. I stabilize the test by using Awaitility to wait until the mock receives the expected call, or by returning a CompletableFuture from the email service so the test can wait for completion. If the email client runs inside an @Async method, I inject the TaskExecutor and wait for active tasks. I also make the test deterministic by controlling the thread pool or by making the email service synchronous in the test configuration.

title: Q: [S13] A build fails because JaCoCo reports zero coverage for classes  generated by MapStruct or Lombok. How do you handle it?
text: [S13] A build fails because JaCoCo reports zero coverage for classes generated by MapStruct or Lombok. How do you handle it? Generated code should not count against coverage because it is not hand-written business logic. For Lombok, I add lombok.config with lombok.addLombokGeneratedAnnotation = true so JaCoCo recognizes the @Generated annotation and skips those methods. For MapStruct, I configure the JaCoCo exclusion list to ignore the generated mapper implementation classes, for example with **/mapper/*Impl. I keep the domain and service classes included in coverage so the report reflects actual code quality. If the team uses other generators, I add their output packages to the exclusions and document why those classes are not measured.

title: Q: [S14] A controller test passes in isolation but fails after adding Spring  Security to the project. How do you update the test?
text: [S14] A controller test passes in isolation but fails after adding Spring Security to the project. How do you update the test? Adding Spring Security introduces filters that intercept every request, so unauthenticated requests to protected endpoints return 401 instead of 200. I update the test by configuring MockMvc with the security filters and either providing test credentials or disabling authentication for the test. For @WebMvcTest, I add @AutoConfigureMockMvc(addFilters = false) only if the goal is to test controller logic without security, but I prefer to keep security enabled and use @WithMockUser to simulate an authenticated user. I also add tests that verify the endpoint correctly rejects unauthenticated requests and enforces role-based access, so security behavior is covered, not bypassed.

title: Q: [S15] A test suite reports IllegalStateException: Failed to load  ApplicationContext after adding a new dependency. What is your  debugging approach?
text: [S15] A test suite reports IllegalStateException: Failed to load ApplicationContext after adding a new dependency. What is your debugging approach? I start by reading the full stack trace and the caused-by chain, because the root cause is usually buried several levels deep. Common reasons are a missing property, a bean that fails to initialize, a conditional configuration that activates incorrectly under test, or a dependency that requires a real service not available in the test environment. I run a single failing test with debug logging for org.springframework.boot and org.springframework.test to see which bean fails. If the new dependency is not needed for the test, I exclude it with @SpringBootTest(classes = { ... }) or a test-specific profile. If it is needed, I provide a mock or a lightweight implementation with @TestConfiguration.

title: Q: [Q1] What are the main things to consider when moving a Spring Boot  application from local development to production?
text: [Q1] What are the main things to consider when moving a Spring Boot application from local development to production? Production readiness is much more than deploying a JAR file. You need a managed database with backups, environment-specific configuration, secrets management, health monitoring, centralized logging, and a repeatable deployment pipeline. On AWS this usually means RDS for the database, Elastic Beanstalk or ECS/EKS for compute, a load balancer for traffic distribution, and CloudWatch for metrics and alarms. You also need to externalize configuration using Spring profiles and environment variables so the same artifact runs in dev, staging, and production without code changes. A common pitfall is shipping the production database password inside the JAR or leaving debug endpoints exposed.

title: Q: [Q2] How do you set up a production database with Amazon RDS?
text: [Q2] How do you set up a production database with Amazon RDS? I create a DB subnet group that spans at least two availability zones for high availability, then provision the RDS instance or Aurora cluster with the desired engine, instance class, storage, and backup retention. Security groups restrict inbound traffic to the application tier only, and the database is placed in private subnets so it is unreachable from the internet. I enable automated backups, multi-AZ failover for production workloads, and encryption at rest using KMS. The application connects through an environment variable such as SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL, which points to the RDS endpoint, rather than hard-coding the host. I also create a read replica if reporting queries need to offload traffic from the primary instance.

title: Q: [Q3] What is the difference between RDS Multi-AZ and a read replica?
text: [Q3] What is the difference between RDS Multi-AZ and a read replica? Multi-AZ maintains a synchronous standby in another availability zone for high availability. If the primary fails, RDS automatically fails over to the standby without manual intervention. It is for availability, not scaling. A read replica is an asynchronous copy of the primary that can serve read traffic and be promoted to a standalone database. It is for scaling read-heavy workloads and disaster recovery, but it may lag behind the primary by a small amount. Teams sometimes confuse the two and enable Multi-AZ expecting read scaling, or create a read replica expecting automatic failover, so it is important to choose based on the actual goal.

title: Q: [Q4] How do you manage database credentials securely in a Spring Boot  application on AWS?
text: [Q4] How do you manage database credentials securely in a Spring Boot application on AWS? Credentials should never be committed to source control or baked into the Docker image. In AWS, the cleanest approach is to store the username and password in AWS Secrets Manager and have the application retrieve them at startup, either by integrating Spring Cloud AWS or by mounting the secret as environment variables through Elastic Beanstalk or ECS. Another option is to use IAM database authentication for supported engines, which issues short-lived tokens and removes long-lived passwords. In a pinch you can pass the secret as environment variables, but rotation then becomes manual. Whatever the mechanism, the application must fail clearly at startup if the secret cannot be read, so misconfiguration is discovered immediately rather than masked by a connection error hours later.

title: Q: [Q5] What are Spring profiles, and how do they help with environment- specific configuration?
text: [Q5] What are Spring profiles, and how do they help with environment- specific configuration? Spring profiles let you define sets of beans and properties that apply only in specific environments. You activate a profile with spring.profiles.active=prod, and Spring loads application-prod.properties or application-prod.yml alongside the main configuration. Profiles are useful for switching datasources, logging levels, feature flags, and external service URLs. The real value is that the same deployable artifact can run anywhere. A common mistake is putting environment-specific values directly in application.properties; instead, put defaults there and override them per profile or through environment variables so the artifact remains portable.

title: Q: [Q6] How do you structure application.properties or  application.yml for dev, staging, and production?
text: [Q6] How do you structure application.properties or application.yml for dev, staging, and production? I keep common settings in the main application.yml, such as application name, Jackson settings, and logging format. I then create profile-specific files like application- dev.yml, application-staging.yml, and application-prod.yml that contain environment-specific overrides. Sensitive values such as passwords are not stored in any file; they are supplied through environment variables or a secret manager. For example, spring.datasource.url can point to a local Docker database in dev and an RDS endpoint in prod. I also use a local profile for developers that disables cloud integrations and uses an in- memory or Dockerized database.

title: Q: [Q7] What is the @Profile annotation, and when would you use it?
text: [Q7] What is the @Profile annotation, and when would you use it? @Profile lets you register a bean only when a specific profile is active. For example, you might have a MockNotificationService annotated with @Profile("local") and a real AwsSesNotificationService annotated with @Profile("!local"). This keeps the application runnable locally without external credentials. It is also useful for feature flags during gradual rollouts. The risk is overusing profiles to toggle behavior; if every environment behaves differently, the production path is not well tested. Profiles should mainly handle externalized configuration and infrastructure wiring, not business logic branches.

title: Q: [Q8] What is AWS Elastic Beanstalk, and why is it a common choice for  Spring Boot deployments?
text: [Q8] What is AWS Elastic Beanstalk, and why is it a common choice for Spring Boot deployments? Elastic Beanstalk is a managed platform service that handles capacity provisioning, load balancing, auto-scaling, and application health monitoring for you. You upload your JAR or Docker image, choose a platform such as Java 17, and Elastic Beanstalk deploys it onto EC2 instances behind a load balancer. It is a common choice for Spring Boot because it reduces operational overhead; you get a production-grade environment without manually configuring EC2, load balancers, and scaling policies. The trade-off is less control than ECS, EKS, or raw EC2, and some teams outgrow it as they need custom networking or advanced deployment strategies.

title: Q: [Q9] How do you deploy a Spring Boot JAR to Elastic Beanstalk with a  load balancer and auto-scaling?
text: [Q9] How do you deploy a Spring Boot JAR to Elastic Beanstalk with a load balancer and auto-scaling? I create an Elastic Beanstalk web server environment and choose a load-balanced configuration. The environment provisions an Application Load Balancer, an Auto Scaling group, and EC2 instances running the Java platform. I upload the Spring Boot JAR as an application version and deploy it to the environment. In the environment configuration, I set health check URL to /actuator/health, configure auto-scaling triggers based on CPU or request count, and set instance type and minimum/maximum counts. The load balancer forwards traffic on port 80 or 443 to the instances on the port Spring Boot listens on, usually 5000 for Elastic Beanstalk Java platforms.

title: Q: [Q10] What port does a Spring Boot application run on when deployed to  Elastic Beanstalk, and how do you configure it?
text: [Q10] What port does a Spring Boot application run on when deployed to Elastic Beanstalk, and how do you configure it? Elastic Beanstalk Java platforms default to running the application on port 5000, and the nginx proxy forwards requests from port 80 to port 5000. If your Spring Boot application uses a different port, you must set SERVER_PORT to 5000 or update the nginx configuration. The platform can pass the port through the PORT environment variable. A common issue is that the application starts on 8080 locally and fails health checks on Elastic Beanstalk because the load balancer expects it on 5000. I always verify the port setting in the environment properties and use server.port=5000 or the equivalent environment variable.

title: Q: [Q11] How do you connect an Elastic Beanstalk application to an RDS  database?
text: [Q11] How do you connect an Elastic Beanstalk application to an RDS database? I create the RDS instance first and then configure the Elastic Beanstalk environment properties to point to it. The properties include SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL, SPRING_DATASOURCE_USERNAME, and SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD. The security group of the RDS instance must allow inbound traffic from the security group attached to the Elastic Beanstalk EC2 instances. It is best not to use Elastic Beanstalk's integrated RDS option for production because it ties the database lifecycle to the environment; instead, create RDS separately and reference it through environment properties. This keeps the database alive if the environment is recreated or scaled down.

title: Q: [Q12] What is auto-scaling, and what metrics should trigger it in a Spring  Boot application?
text: [Q12] What is auto-scaling, and what metrics should trigger it in a Spring Boot application? Auto-scaling automatically adds or removes EC2 instances based on demand to maintain performance and control cost. Common triggers are CPU utilization above a threshold for several minutes, request count per target, network traffic, or custom CloudWatch metrics such as queue depth. For Spring Boot APIs, CPU is the simplest starting point, but request count can scale faster for IO-bound workloads. Scaling policies should have cooldowns so instances are not added and removed too quickly. I also configure a minimum instance count for availability and a maximum to prevent runaway costs during traffic spikes or attacks.

title: Q: [Q13] What is CI/CD, and how does AWS CodePipeline and CodeBuild  help?
text: [Q13] What is CI/CD, and how does AWS CodePipeline and CodeBuild help? CI/CD is the practice of automatically building, testing, and deploying code changes. AWS CodeBuild compiles the code, runs tests, and produces artifacts such as a JAR or Docker image. AWS CodePipeline orchestrates the stages: source from GitHub, build with CodeBuild, and deploy to Elastic Beanstalk, ECS, or S3. GitHub hooks trigger the pipeline whenever code is pushed, so there is no manual handoff. The benefit is fast, repeatable, and auditable releases. A common mistake is skipping tests in the pipeline or deploying directly to production without a staging stage, which defeats the purpose of having a pipeline.

title: Q: [Q14] How do you configure GitHub webhooks to trigger AWS  CodePipeline?
text: [Q14] How do you configure GitHub webhooks to trigger AWS CodePipeline? In CodePipeline, I set the source provider to GitHub version 2, which uses a GitHub App connection to authenticate. During setup, CodePipeline creates a webhook in the GitHub repository that sends push events to the pipeline. Alternatively, you can use a webhook created through API Gateway or a Lambda function that starts the pipeline. The webhook payload is filtered by branch, so only pushes to main or release branches trigger a build. I also ensure the pipeline has a manual approval stage between staging and production so a human can gate the final deployment.

title: Q: [Q15] What buildspec file does CodeBuild need for a Spring Boot Maven  project?
text: [Q15] What buildspec file does CodeBuild need for a Spring Boot Maven project? CodeBuild uses a buildspec.yml file to define phases and artifacts. The phases typically include install, pre-build, build, and post-build. In the build phase, I run mvn clean verify to compile, run unit and integration tests, and package the JAR. In post-build, I copy the JAR to the artifacts directory. The artifacts section tells CodeBuild which files to pass to the next pipeline stage, such as target/*.jar and an application.properties if needed. I also cache the Maven dependencies in ~/.m2 to speed up subsequent builds. If tests fail, the build stops and nothing is deployed.

title: Q: [Q16] What is database migration, and why is it important in production?
text: [Q16] What is database migration, and why is it important in production? Database migration is the controlled process of applying schema changes such as adding tables, columns, indexes, or constraints as the application evolves. In production, manual changes are risky because they are hard to reproduce, review, and roll back. Migration tools such as Flyway or Liquibase version schema changes as scripts and apply them automatically in a defined order. This makes deployments repeatable across environments and keeps the schema in sync with the code. A production incident often happens when a code change expects a column that was never added to the production schema.

title: Q: [Q17] How does Flyway work in a Spring Boot application?
text: [Q17] How does Flyway work in a Spring Boot application? Flyway is a database migration tool that applies SQL or Java migration scripts stored in db/migration. Scripts are named with a version prefix such as V1__create_users_table.sql, and Flyway executes them in order. It maintains a flyway_schema_history table to track which migrations have already been applied, so it never runs the same script twice. In Spring Boot, adding flyway-core to the classpath enables auto-configuration that runs migrations at startup. This means the application will not start if a migration fails, which prevents running code against an incompatible schema.

title: Q: [Q18] What is the difference between versioned migrations and  repeatable migrations in Flyway?
text: [Q18] What is the difference between versioned migrations and repeatable migrations in Flyway? Versioned migrations are numbered scripts that run exactly once and are used for schema changes that must not be repeated, such as adding a column or creating a table. Repeatable migrations have a description but no version number, and they run whenever their checksum changes. They are useful for views, stored procedures, functions, and reference data that should be recreated when the script is updated. A typical setup uses versioned migrations for structural changes and repeatable migrations for database logic that depends on those structures.

title: Q: [Q19] How do you handle destructive changes in a Flyway migration  safely?
text: [Q19] How do you handle destructive changes in a Flyway migration safely? Destructive changes such as dropping a column or table should be done in multiple deployments to avoid data loss and downtime. Instead of dropping a column immediately, I first deploy a migration that adds a new column and copies data, update the application to read from both, then deploy another migration that removes the old column once the application no longer uses it. Flyway's versioning guarantees order, but it does not protect you from the business impact of a bad script. I always review migrations in a pull request, test them against a copy of production data, and have a rollback plan such as restoring from a snapshot.

title: Q: [Q20] How do you manage environment-specific Flyway configuration in  Spring Boot?
text: [Q20] How do you manage environment-specific Flyway configuration in Spring Boot? Flyway reads properties from the active Spring profile, so you can have different schemas, locations, or baseline settings per environment. For example, application-prod.yml might set spring.flyway.locations=classpath:db/migration,classpath:db/migrat ion/prod and disable clean so the production database is never wiped. In dev, you might enable spring.flyway.clean-disabled=false and use a Dockerized database. Sensitive connection details come from environment variables or AWS Secrets Manager, not from the Flyway configuration file. I also set spring.flyway.baseline-on-migrate=true when introducing Flyway to an existing database that already has a schema.

title: Q: [Q21] What is the difference between blue-green deployment and rolling  deployment on Elastic Beanstalk?
text: [Q21] What is the difference between blue-green deployment and rolling deployment on Elastic Beanstalk? A rolling deployment updates instances in place a few at a time, which is simple but means old and new versions run simultaneously during the transition. A blue-green deployment creates a separate environment with the new version, verifies it, and then swaps the load balancer to point to the new environment. Blue-green deployments are safer because you can rollback by swapping back, but they cost more because both environments run briefly. Elastic Beanstalk supports swap URLs for blue-green deployments, while rolling updates are a built-in deployment policy.

title: Q: [Q22] How do you monitor a Spring Boot application deployed on AWS?
text: [Q22] How do you monitor a Spring Boot application deployed on AWS? I expose health, metrics, and info endpoints through Spring Boot Actuator and secure them so only authorized systems can read them. CloudWatch can collect logs from the Elastic Beanstalk environment and trigger alarms on error counts, CPU, or memory. For application-level metrics, I use Micrometer to publish custom metrics such as request duration, order creation count, or payment failure rate to CloudWatch or Prometheus. I also set up SNS notifications so the team is alerted when alarms fire. Health checks on the load balancer should use /actuator/health so unhealthy instances are replaced automatically.

title: Q: [Q23] How do you handle log aggregation for distributed Spring Boot  instances?
text: [Q23] How do you handle log aggregation for distributed Spring Boot instances? In Elastic Beanstalk, instances come and go, so logs stored locally are lost when an instance is terminated. I configure the environment to stream logs to CloudWatch Logs using the Elastic Beanstalk log streaming option, or I use the CloudWatch agent inside Docker deployments. The application should write structured JSON logs with correlation IDs so log entries can be traced across services. For larger systems, I forward logs to a centralized tool such as ELK, Splunk, or Datadog. I also ensure that sensitive data is not logged, and that log levels are configurable per profile.

title: Q: [Q24] What is infrastructure as code, and why is it useful for AWS  deployments?
text: [Q24] What is infrastructure as code, and why is it useful for AWS deployments? Infrastructure as code is the practice of defining AWS resources such as RDS, Elastic Beanstalk, load balancers, and IAM roles in declarative templates using tools like Terraform, AWS CloudFormation, or the AWS CDK. It makes deployments repeatable, version-controlled, and reviewable. If a production environment is hand-built, recreating it after an incident requires guessing what was configured. With infrastructure as code, you recreate the environment by running the same template. It also supports separate environments that are identical except for size and secrets, which reduces "works in dev, fails in prod" issues.

title: Q: [S1] A production deployment fails because the application cannot  connect to RDS after switching from dev. How do you investigate?
text: [S1] A production deployment fails because the application cannot connect to RDS after switching from dev. How do you investigate? I begin by reading the application startup logs carefully, because the exact exception tells me whether this is a network reachability problem or an authentication problem. A connection timeout usually means the security group is blocking traffic, the RDS instance is in a different VPC, or the subnet routing is wrong, while an authentication failure means the username, password, or database name is incorrect. I verify the environment variables in Elastic Beanstalk or ECS match the values shown in the RDS console, and I confirm that the RDS security group has an inbound rule allowing the application security group on the database port. I also check that the RDS instance is in an available state and that the subnet group spans availability zones compatible with the application. If the database was recently restored or recreated, the endpoint will have changed and the application configuration must be updated before the next deployment. I avoid the trap of trying different credentials blindly or opening the security group to the internet, because both increase risk and do not fix a routing problem.

title: Q: [S2] A Spring Boot application works in dev but uses the dev database in  production because of hard-coded configuration. How do you fix the  configuration strategy?
text: [S2] A Spring Boot application works in dev but uses the dev database in production because of hard-coded configuration. How do you fix the configuration strategy? I first search the codebase for any hard-coded localhost URLs, default usernames, or passwords that are checked into the repository, because these are the values the application will use if no environment override is present. I move all dev-only values out of application.properties and into application-dev.yml, and I make the main configuration file contain only safe defaults or placeholders. For production, I set SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod and supply SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL, SPRING_DATASOURCE_USERNAME, and SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD through Elastic Beanstalk environment properties or, better, AWS Secrets Manager. I validate the fix by running the application locally with the prod profile and dummy credentials to confirm it attempts to connect to the supplied URL rather than a hard-coded dev database. I also add a CI step that scans the repository for hard-coded secrets and localhost database URLs, failing the build if any are found. The lesson is that the same artifact should run everywhere, with environment-specific values injected at runtime rather than baked into the build.

title: Q: [S3] A deployment to Elastic Beanstalk succeeds, but health checks fail  and the environment rolls back. What do you check?
text: [S3] A deployment to Elastic Beanstalk succeeds, but health checks fail and the environment rolls back. What do you check? I open the Elastic Beanstalk events and the application logs in CloudWatch immediately, because a successful deployment only means the files were copied, not that the application is healthy. The most common reasons for failed health checks are the application listening on the wrong port, the health endpoint returning a non-200 status, or the application crashing during startup because it cannot reach the database or apply migrations. I verify that the platform expects the application on port 5000 and that SERVER_PORT is set accordingly. I check that /actuator/health is accessible anonymously or that the load balancer health check path is configured to a public endpoint. If Spring Security is blocking the health check, I permit access to /actuator/health and /actuator/info in the security configuration. I also verify that Flyway migrations run successfully at startup, because a failed migration can leave the application unable to start and therefore unable to pass health checks. Once the cause is fixed, I deploy again and monitor the health dashboard before declaring the release successful.

title: Q: [S4] A Flyway migration runs successfully in dev but fails in production  because a table already exists. How do you resolve it?
text: [S4] A Flyway migration runs successfully in dev but fails in production because a table already exists. How do you resolve it? This typically happens when Flyway is introduced to a database that was already created and modified manually, so the production schema is at a later state than the first migration script assumes. I start by checking the flyway_schema_history table, or its absence, to understand what Flyway thinks has been applied. I then baseline the production database by setting spring.flyway.baseline-on-migrate=true and choosing a baseline version that matches the current schema state, which tells Flyway to mark earlier migrations as applied without running them. After baselining, all future migrations must be written against the current production schema. I never manually edit the flyway_schema_history table to force a migration through, because that corrupts Flyway's internal state and leads to unpredictable failures later. Before applying the baseline in production, I restore a snapshot to a staging environment, run the baseline and the planned migrations there, and run the application smoke tests to confirm everything works. The long-term fix is to ensure every environment is created from the same migration sequence so manual drift does not happen again.

title: Q: [S5] A CI/CD pipeline deploys to production automatically whenever  code is pushed to main. After a bad release, the team wants a staging  gate. How do you redesign the pipeline?
text: [S5] A CI/CD pipeline deploys to production automatically whenever code is pushed to main. After a bad release, the team wants a staging gate. How do you redesign the pipeline? I restructure the pipeline into clear stages: source, build, staging, manual approval, and production. In the build stage, CodeBuild compiles the code, runs unit and integration tests, and produces the deployable artifact. In the staging stage, the artifact is deployed to a staging Elastic Beanstalk environment that mirrors production as closely as possible, and a set of automated smoke tests and integration tests run against it. I then add a manual approval action in CodePipeline that requires a team member to approve the deployment after reviewing the test results, the change log, and any monitoring anomalies. For production, I keep the previous application version available and use blue-green deployment so that if the new version fails health checks, the load balancer can be swapped back quickly. I also add GitHub branch protection rules requiring pull request reviews before code can reach main, so the pipeline is not triggered by unreviewed changes. The key principle is that no change should reach customers without being exercised in a staging environment first.

title: Q: [S6] A migration script drops an unused column, and the rollback  requires restoring from backup. How do you prevent this next time?
text: [S6] A migration script drops an unused column, and the rollback requires restoring from backup. How do you prevent this next time? This is a classic destructive migration mistake, and the immediate lesson is that dropping a column should always be the final step of a multi-release process. The first step is to remove every application reference to the column and deploy that change. The second step is to verify that no code, report, or downstream consumer reads from or writes to the column. Only then do I write a migration that drops it, and before applying that migration I take a manual snapshot of the production database. I also run the migration against a restored copy of production data in staging to confirm it behaves as expected. For columns that might contain useful historical data, I copy the data to an archive table or a data lake before removal. In the pull request, I label the migration as destructive and require two approvals. If the column was already dropped and data is needed, I restore the pre-drop snapshot to a separate instance and extract the data, rather than trying to undo the migration by hand.

title: Q: [S7] Auto-scaling adds instances during peak hours but costs spike  unexpectedly. How do you balance performance and cost?
text: [S7] Auto-scaling adds instances during peak hours but costs spike unexpectedly. How do you balance performance and cost? I begin by reviewing the auto-scaling history and CloudWatch metrics to see exactly when and why instances were added. CPU is the default trigger, but for an IO-bound Spring Boot application the CPU may stay low while response time degrades, so scaling based on CPU alone can add instances too late or unnecessarily. I consider switching the scaling metric to request count per target, average response time, or a custom CloudWatch metric such as queue depth. I set a maximum instance count as a hard cost ceiling and use scheduled scaling to reduce capacity during known quiet periods. I also examine whether the application can handle more load per instance by tuning the JVM heap, database connection pool size, or slow queries, because adding instances is not always the cheapest fix. For predictable baseline load, I use Reserved Instances or Savings Plans to reduce the per-hour cost, and I reserve Spot instances only for stateless, fault-tolerant batch work. The caution is that aggressive cost optimization should never remove the minimum instances needed for availability.

title: Q: [S8] A load balancer is distributing traffic, but one instance is unhealthy  and still receiving requests. Why?
text: [S8] A load balancer is distributing traffic, but one instance is unhealthy and still receiving requests. Why? I first check the health check configuration on the target group, because an incorrect path, a long interval, or a high number of healthy thresholds can delay the load balancer from detecting a failure. If the health check path is a static page that always returns 200 even when the application cannot reach the database, the load balancer will continue sending traffic to a broken instance. I change the health check to /actuator/health or another endpoint that genuinely reflects application readiness. Even with a correct health check, there is a short window between the first failed check and deregistration when the instance can still receive requests, and connection draining or deregistration delay lengthens that window. I tune these values to balance fast failure detection with graceful shutdown of in-flight requests. I also enable the load balancer access logs and target group metrics so I can see exactly when an instance was marked unhealthy and how long it remained in rotation.

title: Q: [S9] A developer checks AWS access keys into the GitHub repository.  What do you do immediately?
text: [S9] A developer checks AWS access keys into the GitHub repository. What do you do immediately? I treat the keys as compromised the moment they touch version control, even if they were removed quickly, because Git history retains them. I immediately rotate the credentials in the AWS IAM console by deactivating and deleting the exposed access keys and creating new ones for the affected user or service. I then inspect CloudTrail logs for any unauthorized API calls made with the old keys, paying attention to unusual regions, resource creation, or data access. I run GitHub secret scanning or git-secrets across the repository history to find any other exposed credentials, and if necessary I rewrite history to remove them. I add pre-commit hooks and repository scanning to prevent future commits of secrets, and I update the team's onboarding documentation to explain why access keys must be provided through environment variables or IAM roles rather than code. I also recommend switching from long-term access keys to IAM roles wherever possible, because roles do not expose static credentials.

title: Q: [S10] The team wants to switch from Elastic Beanstalk to containers on  ECS. What changes in the deployment pipeline?
text: [S10] The team wants to switch from Elastic Beanstalk to containers on ECS. What changes in the deployment pipeline? The biggest change is that the build artifact becomes a Docker image rather than an executable JAR. In CodeBuild, I update the buildspec.yml to run docker build, tag the image with the commit hash or build ID, and push it to Amazon ECR. The deploy stage then uses ECS task definitions and service updates instead of Elastic Beanstalk application versions, so the pipeline must generate or update a task definition with the new image URI and call ecs update- service. Load balancing shifts to an Application Load Balancer that points at an ECS target group, and auto-scaling becomes ECS service auto-scaling. Environment configuration is still passed through environment variables, but in ECS these live in the task definition or are sourced from AWS Secrets Manager and Systems Manager Parameter Store. I also add container health checks and CloudWatch logging sidecars so observability is consistent with the previous setup. The transition requires the team to understand container networking, task lifecycle, and image versioning, but it gives more flexibility for blue-green and canary deployments.

title: Q: [S11] A new environment variable is added to the application, but  production crashes on startup because it is missing. How do you  prevent this in the pipeline?
text: [S11] A new environment variable is added to the application, but production crashes on startup because it is missing. How do you prevent this in the pipeline? I add a validation step early in the pipeline that compares the environment configuration against a required manifest. This can be a simple script in CodeBuild that reads a required- env.properties file and checks that every listed variable is present in the target environment before deployment proceeds. In the Spring Boot application, I make the critical property required using constructor binding with validation annotations or by implementing an ApplicationRunner that fails fast with a clear message if a required value is missing. I keep environment-specific configuration documented in version control, ideally as Terraform or CloudFormation templates, so the infrastructure and the application expectations stay in sync. I also run the application with the prod profile and minimal environment variables in a CI smoke test to catch missing configuration before it reaches production. The broader lesson is that configuration changes should be reviewed and applied through the same pipeline as code changes.

title: Q: [S12] A migration fails because two developers wrote scripts with the  same version number. How do you resolve the conflict?
text: [S12] A migration fails because two developers wrote scripts with the same version number. How do you resolve the conflict? Flyway requires every versioned migration to have a unique prefix, so two files named V5__... will cause validate to fail. I coordinate with the other developer to decide which script logically comes first, then I rename one script to the next available version number and make sure the ordering still makes sense from a database state perspective. If either script was already applied to a shared environment, I check the flyway_schema_history table before renaming, because renaming an applied script will change its checksum and cause validation errors. To prevent this from recurring, I introduce a naming convention based on ticket numbers or the current date, and I add a CI step that runs flyway validate or checks for duplicate version prefixes before the pull request can be merged. I also encourage the team to announce when they are adding migrations on shared branches so two people do not independently pick the same version number.

title: Q: [S13] The application logs are full of stack traces, but the team cannot  find the original error quickly. How do you improve observability?
text: [S13] The application logs are full of stack traces, but the team cannot find the original error quickly. How do you improve observability? I start by adding a correlation ID at the entry point of every request, usually through a servlet filter or interceptor, and I propagate that ID through the MDC so that every log statement emitted during the request includes the same identifier. I then change the log format to structured JSON with fields such as timestamp, level, logger, message, correlation ID, request path, and user ID, and I send those logs to CloudWatch Logs. This lets the team use CloudWatch Logs Insights to query all log lines for a single request in seconds. I also classify errors more carefully: business validation failures return a clear 4xx response with a short message and log at WARN, while unexpected internal errors log the full stack trace at ERROR and return only a generic incident message to the client. I make sure sensitive data such as passwords or tokens are never logged, and I add alerts on ERROR rate spikes so the team knows about problems before users report them.

title: Q: [S14] A production deployment happens during business hours and  causes downtime. How do you move to zero-downtime deployments?
text: [S14] A production deployment happens during business hours and causes downtime. How do you move to zero-downtime deployments? I first move the deployment window outside business hours as a short-term measure, but the real fix is to change the deployment strategy. In Elastic Beanstalk, I use blue-green deployment by cloning the environment, deploying the new version, running smoke tests, and swapping the load balancer URL to point to the new environment; if anything goes wrong, I swap back. In ECS, I configure a rolling update with minimum healthy percent of 100 and maximum percent of 200 so new containers start and pass health checks before old containers stop. For higher confidence, I add a canary stage that routes a small percentage of traffic to the new version while monitoring error rates and latency. All database migrations are applied before the code deployment and must be backward-compatible, meaning old code can still run against the new schema until the deployment finishes. I also verify that the load balancer health checks are strict enough to prevent traffic from reaching instances that have started but are not yet ready.

title: Q: [S15] A staging deployment uses the same RDS instance as production  to save cost. What are the risks?
text: [S15] A staging deployment uses the same RDS instance as production to save cost. What are the risks? Sharing a database between staging and production is one of the riskiest cost optimizations because it breaks environment isolation completely. A bug in a staging feature can corrupt or delete production data, a load test can starve production queries and cause timeouts for real users, and a schema migration tested in staging is applied directly to the production schema. There is also no safe way to test destructive changes or data fixes without affecting production. The right approach is to use a separate RDS instance or Aurora cluster for staging, ideally restored periodically from a sanitized copy of production data so tests run against realistic schema and volumes. If budget is tight, I use a smaller instance class for staging, schedule it to stop during nights and weekends, or use RDS snapshots to recreate it on demand. The database is the one resource where the cost of sharing always outweighs the savings.

title: Q: [S16] The RDS storage usage is growing rapidly and approaching the  maximum provisioned size. How do you respond?
text: [S16] The RDS storage usage is growing rapidly and approaching the maximum provisioned size. How do you respond? I first identify what is consuming storage by checking CloudWatch metrics for database size growth, transaction logs, and temporary files. If the growth comes from normal data accumulation, I enable RDS storage auto-scaling so the volume grows automatically up to a defined maximum without manual intervention. If storage auto-scaling is not available for the engine or instance class, I plan a maintenance window to increase the allocated storage manually. If the growth is caused by excessive transaction logs, I review the backup retention period and log expiration settings, and I make sure long-running transactions or replication lag are not preventing log cleanup. For audit or historical data that does not need to be in the primary database, I archive old rows to S3 or a data warehouse and purge them in batches. I avoid filling the disk to 100 percent, because many databases become read-only or crash at that point, and I set CloudWatch alarms at 70 and 85 percent so the team has time to act.

title: Q: [S17] HTTPS requests to the Elastic Beanstalk environment fail with a  certificate error. How do you fix it?
text: [S17] HTTPS requests to the Elastic Beanstalk environment fail with a certificate error. How do you fix it? I check whether the Application Load Balancer or the instance itself is terminating TLS. In most Elastic Beanstalk setups, the load balancer terminates HTTPS using an ACM certificate, and the instances handle HTTP. I open the load balancer listener configuration and verify that port 443 is present, that it has a valid ACM certificate attached, and that the certificate covers the domain being requested. If the certificate has expired, I renew it in ACM and update the listener. If the application is accessed directly by IP or an old DNS name that is not on the certificate, the browser will show a name mismatch error, so I make sure the DNS record points to the load balancer's canonical name. I also confirm that the security group allows inbound HTTPS traffic on 443. I avoid putting self-signed certificates on the instances unless absolutely necessary, because ACM provides free managed certificates that renew automatically.

title: Q: [S18] The CodeBuild project builds successfully on a developer's laptop,  but the same tests fail in the pipeline. What do you check?
text: [S18] The CodeBuild project builds successfully on a developer's laptop, but the same tests fail in the pipeline. What do you check? I start by reading the CodeBuild log to see exactly which tests failed and how they failed, because environment differences are the most common cause. The pipeline may run tests against a different database profile, a different timezone, or missing environment variables that are present on the developer's machine. I check the buildspec.yml to confirm that the correct Maven profile is active and that any required services or resources are available during the build. I also verify that the build does not rely on files that are in the developer's local workspace but not in the repository, such as untracked configuration files. Differences in Java version, Maven version, or locale between the laptop and the CodeBuild image can also cause test failures, so I pin those versions in the buildspec. Once I identify the difference, I make the build environment reproducible, often by adding a Docker-based build or by running the same buildspec in a local CodeBuild agent.

title: Q: [S19] After scaling out, the application runs out of database connections  and starts failing. How do you fix it?
text: [S19] After scaling out, the application runs out of database connections and starts failing. How do you fix it? Running out of connections usually means the total pool size across all instances exceeds the database's maximum connection limit. I calculate the expected total connections by multiplying the per-instance HikariCP maximum pool size by the number of instances and compare that to the RDS max_connections setting. If the total is close to or above the limit, I reduce the per- instance pool size, because a larger pool on each instance does not help if the database cannot accept them. I also check for connection leaks caused by missing try-with-resources or long-running queries that hold connections. For read-heavy workloads, I add read replicas and route read traffic to them, which both scales capacity and reduces pressure on the primary. If connection demand is genuinely high, I scale the RDS instance class vertically to support more connections or use RDS Proxy to pool and share database connections efficiently. I avoid simply raising the pool size as the first fix, because that usually makes the problem worse.

title: Q: [S20] A production secret in AWS Secrets Manager is rotated, and the  application stops working. How do you handle secret rotation without  downtime?
text: [S20] A production secret in AWS Secrets Manager is rotated, and the application stops working. How do you handle secret rotation without downtime? The problem is likely that the application reads the secret only at startup and never refreshes it, so when the secret value changes the cached credentials become invalid. I first restore service by restarting the affected instances or rolling back the secret rotation if possible. For a proper fix, I change the application to refresh the secret periodically or on authentication failure, using the AWS SDK to retrieve the latest value from Secrets Manager. When rotating database credentials, I use the Secrets Manager rotation feature that stores both the current and pending versions during the rotation window, so the application has time to pick up the new value while the old value remains valid. I also make sure the database user is not dropped immediately after rotation, because open connections from the old credential must finish their work. Ideally, the application uses IAM database authentication or RDS Proxy so password rotation is handled outside the application entirely. The key is that secret rotation should be graceful, not an all-at-once event.

title: Q: [Q1] What is Generative AI, and how does it differ from traditional AI?
text: [Q1] What is Generative AI, and how does it differ from traditional AI? Generative AI refers to models that can create new content such as text, images, audio, or code by learning patterns from training data. Unlike traditional AI, which is often used for classification, regression, or clustering, generative AI produces outputs that resemble its training distribution. Large language models such as GPT, Gemini, and Claude are generative AI systems trained to predict the next token in a sequence. For backend developers, generative AI enables features like chatbots, code assistants, content generation, and summarization. The key difference is that traditional AI maps inputs to known labels, while generative AI synthesizes novel outputs conditioned on inputs.

title: Q: [Q2] What is a Large Language Model, and how does it work at a high level?
text: [Q2] What is a Large Language Model, and how does it work at a high level? A Large Language Model is a deep neural network, typically based on the Transformer architecture, trained on vast amounts of text to predict the next token in a sequence. During training, the model learns statistical relationships between words, grammar, facts, and reasoning patterns. At inference, it takes a prompt, converts it into tokens, processes it through many layers of self-attention and feed-forward networks, and generates tokens one at a time until a stop condition is reached. The model does not truly understand text in a human sense; it predicts likely sequences based on learned patterns. Modern LLMs have billions of parameters and are ne-tuned for instruction following and safety.

title: Q: [Q3] What is tokenization, and why does it matter?
text: [Q3] What is tokenization, and why does it matter? Tokenization is the process of splitting text into smaller units called tokens, which can be words, subwords, or characters. LLMs operate on tokens rather than raw characters, so the input text is first converted to tokens, and the model predicts the next token. The number of tokens affects cost, context window usage, and latency. Different models use different tokenizers, so the same sentence may produce different token counts across providers. For backend developers, tokenization matters because APIs charge per token, prompt length is limited by the context window, and output must often be bounded by max tokens.

title: Q: [Q4] What is the Transformer architecture?
text: [Q4] What is the Transformer architecture? The Transformer is a neural network architecture introduced in the paper Attention Is All You Need. It is built from encoder and decoder stacks, though modern LLMs typically use decoder-only variants. The core component is the self-attention mechanism, which allows the model to weigh the importance of different tokens in the input when producing each output token. Transformers also use positional encoding to understand token order, feed-forward layers to transform representations, layer normalization, and residual connections. The architecture is highly parallelizable during training and scales well to billions of parameters.
