title: Q: [S11] A Feign client works with Eureka in dev but fails in production with a connection error. What do you check?
text: [S11] A Feign client works with Eureka in dev but fails in production with a connection error. What do you check? I check the url attribute on @FeignClient. If a hard-coded URL is left from dev testing, it will not use Eureka in production. I also verify that the production profile does not disable Eureka, that the service name matches exactly, and that the Feign client is not using a different service discovery implementation. I check network connectivity between the caller and Eureka, and between the caller and the target service instances. If load balancing is failing, I inspect Spring Cloud LoadBalancer configuration and confirm that spring.cloud.loadbalancer.ribbon.enabled is not set to true in a way that con icts with the modern load balancer.

title: Q: [S12] A gateway needs to authenticate requests before routing them. Where should the authentication logic go?
text: [S12] A gateway needs to authenticate requests before routing them. Where should the authentication logic go? The gateway is a good place to terminate authentication and add security headers for downstream services. I use Spring Security in the gateway to validate JWT tokens or OAuth2 access tokens, and then I forward relevant claims to downstream services using filters such as AddRequestHeader or TokenRelay. The downstream services can then trust the headers added by the gateway but should still validate signatures or perform their own authorization checks. I avoid putting complex business authorization logic in the gateway; that belongs in the services. Keeping authentication at the gateway centralizes security and prevents every service from handling token parsing.

title: Q: [S13] A service-to-service call needs to pass a correlation ID for distributed tracing. How do you implement it with Feign?
text: [S13] A service-to-service call needs to pass a correlation ID for distributed tracing. How do you implement it with Feign? I create a RequestInterceptor that reads the correlation ID from the current request's MDC or a request attribute and adds it as a header to every outgoing Feign request. The same interceptor also propagates the authorization token if needed. I make sure the receiving service extracts the correlation ID and adds it to its own MDC so logs across all services can be correlated. For asynchronous calls, I use a TaskDecorator to copy MDC values to new threads. If I am using Micrometer Tracing or OpenTelemetry, the propagation happens automatically through tracing instrumentation, but a custom Feign interceptor is still useful for legacy or non-standard headers.

title: Q: [S14] A microservices team is using synchronous REST calls everywhere, and deployments are tightly coupled. How do you reduce coupling?
text: [S14] A microservices team is using synchronous REST calls everywhere, and deployments are tightly coupled. How do you reduce coupling? I introduce asynchronous event-driven communication for operations that do not need an immediate response. For example, after an order is placed, the order service publishes an OrderCreated event to Kafka, and the payment, inventory, and notification services consume it independently. This decouples the order service from the availability of the other services and allows each service to be deployed independently. For operations that still require a response, I keep synchronous calls but add circuit breakers, fallbacks, and clear service contracts. The goal is not to eliminate synchronous calls entirely but to use events where eventual consistency is acceptable.

title: Q: [S15] A circuit breaker fallback method is not being invoked. What could be wrong?
text: [S15] A circuit breaker fallback method is not being invoked. What could be wrong? I first check that the fallback method signature matches the original method exactly, including parameter types, and that it has an additional Throwable parameter at the end if the exception is needed. I verify that the method is public and in the same class as the annotated method. I also confirm that the call goes through the Spring proxy; if the method is called via self-invocation, the circuit breaker advice is bypassed. Another cause is that the exception is caught inside the method before it reaches the circuit breaker, or that the configuration for the circuit breaker instance is not loaded. I add tests that directly exercise the circuit breaker through the proxy and inspect the Resilience4J registry to confirm the instance exists.

title: Q: [S16] A Resilience4J bulkhead is configured, but under load the service still becomes unresponsive. Why?
text: [S16] A Resilience4J bulkhead is configured, but under load the service still becomes unresponsive. Why? A bulkhead protects only the operations it wraps. If other parts of the service consume all threads or if the bulkhead limit is set too high, the service can still become unresponsive. I check whether the bulkhead is semaphore-based or thread-pool-based; for blocking I/O, a thread-pool bulkhead provides better isolation but requires the method to return CompletableFuture. I also verify that the bulkhead applies to the correct method and that calls go through the proxy. If the service is failing due to a different dependency, such as a database, the bulkhead around the HTTP client will not help. Proper monitoring of bulkhead metrics shows whether the limit is actually being hit.

title: Q: [S17] A microservice architecture needs centralized configuration. How do you handle it?
text: [S17] A microservice architecture needs centralized configuration. How do you handle it? I use Spring Cloud Con g Server backed by a Git repository to store environment-specific configuration. Each service fetches its configuration at startup from the con g server, and profiles such as dev, staging, and prod are separated. I also use Spring Cloud Bus with a message broker to push configuration refresh events to all services without restarting them. Services expose /actuator/refresh to reload configuration on demand. Secrets are not stored in plain text in Git; instead, I use encrypted values with Spring Cloud Con g encryption or store secrets in AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault and reference them from the configuration. Centralized configuration prevents configuration drift and makes environments reproducible.

title: Q: [S18] An order service calls a payment service, an inventory service, and a notification service to complete an order. How do you manage consistency if one call fails?
text: [S18] An order service calls a payment service, an inventory service, and a notification service to complete an order. How do you manage consistency if one call fails? I use the Saga pattern because a single ACID transaction across multiple services is not possible. In a choreography saga, the order service publishes events, and each service handles its own step and publishes the next event. If a step fails, a compensating event is published, such as PaymentFailed causing the order to be canceled and inventory reservation released. In an orchestration saga, a central orchestrator coordinates each step and invokes compensations directly. The orchestration style is easier to trace and reason about for complex ows, while choreography is more decoupled. I also make each local step idempotent so retries and compensations are safe.

title: Q: [S19] A microservice deployment causes a brief outage because dependent services are not ready. How do you handle startup ordering?
text: [S19] A microservice deployment causes a brief outage because dependent services are not ready. How do you handle startup ordering? Microservices should not require a strict startup order, because services come and go dynamically. I design services to be resilient to missing dependencies at startup by using retries, circuit breakers, and fallbacks. In Kubernetes, I add readiness probes so traffic is not routed to a service until it can handle requests. Liveness probes restart unhealthy pods. I also use init containers or job hooks for one-time dependencies such as database migrations, but I avoid waiting for other services to start because that creates fragile coupling. The correct approach is to make every service tolerate the absence of its peers temporarily.

title: Q: [S20] A gateway route needs to support blue-green deployment by routing 10 percent of traffic to the new version. How do you configure it?
text: [S20] A gateway route needs to support blue-green deployment by routing 10 percent of traffic to the new version. How do you configure it? I use the Weight predicate to split traffic between two routes that point to different service versions or instances. For example, one route with Weight=group1, 9 points to the stable version, and another route with Weight=group1, 1 points to the new version, giving a 90/10 split. Both routes can use the same path predicate. I monitor error rates and latency for the new version, and if it is healthy, I gradually shift the weights. This canary-style deployment through the gateway reduces the risk of rolling out a bad version to all users. I also ensure that both versions are registered under the same or different service names depending on the service discovery setup.

title: Q: [S21] A microservice exposes many small REST endpoints, and consumers complain about too many network calls. How do you reduce chattiness?
text: [S21] A microservice exposes many small REST endpoints, and consumers complain about too many network calls. How do you reduce chattiness? I introduce an API composition layer, either in the API Gateway or in a dedicated backend-for- frontend service, that aggregates multiple downstream calls into a single response. For example, a mobile app might need user profile, order history, and recommendations in one screen; instead of making three calls, it calls one composed endpoint. Another approach is to expand the domain boundaries so related data is returned together, though this must be done carefully to avoid creating a distributed monolith. Caching at the gateway or in the consumer also reduces repeated calls. The trade- off is that the composer becomes a new component to maintain, but it significantly improves client performance.

title: Q: [S22] A team wants to use gRPC instead of REST for internal communication. What are the trade-offs?
text: [S22] A team wants to use gRPC instead of REST for internal communication. What are the trade-offs? gRPC uses HTTP/2 and Protocol Buffers, which provides smaller payloads, faster serialization, and strong service contracts through .proto files. It supports bi-directional streaming and is excellent for low-latency internal communication. The trade-offs are that gRPC is less human-readable than JSON, harder to debug with simple tools, and not as universally supported by browsers and rewalls. Contract changes require careful schema evolution. For external-facing APIs, REST or GraphQL is usually better, while gRPC shines for high-throughput service-to-service communication. The choice should be based on latency requirements, team familiarity, and ecosystem compatibility.

title: Q: [S23] A service registry like Eureka is unavailable. How do services continue to communicate?
text: [S23] A service registry like Eureka is unavailable. How do services continue to communicate? Eureka clients cache the registry locally and refresh it periodically. If the Eureka Server is unavailable, clients can still use their cached copy for a while. The refresh interval and eviction policy determine how long the cached copy remains useful. I also add health checks and retry logic so services can survive short registry outages. For longer outages, I consider a multi-node Eureka cluster for high availability, or a service mesh such as Istio that handles discovery independently. The key is that service discovery is a helper, not a runtime dependency for every single call, as long as clients cache and gracefully handle stale registry data.

title: Q: [S24] A gateway route with a circuit breaker filter forwards to a fallback controller. How do you pass the original failure details to the fallback?
text: [S24] A gateway route with a circuit breaker filter forwards to a fallback controller. How do you pass the original failure details to the fallback? I use the FallbackHeaders filter, which adds headers such as Execution-Exception- Type, Execution-Exception-Message, Root-Cause-Exception-Type, and Root- Cause-Exception-Message to the forwarded fallback request. The fallback controller reads these headers and can return a meaningful error response or log the original cause. Without this filter, the fallback receives only the request path and standard headers, making it hard to explain why the circuit opened. I also make sure the circuit breaker name and fallback URI are configured correctly in the route definition.

title: Q: [S25] A team is debating whether to use an API Gateway or direct service-to-service calls for internal traffic. What do you recommend?
text: [S25] A team is debating whether to use an API Gateway or direct service-to-service calls for internal traffic. What do you recommend? For external client traffic, an API Gateway is almost always the right choice because it centralizes security, routing, and cross-cutting concerns. For internal service-to-service calls, direct calls through Feign or a service mesh are usually preferred because adding an extra gateway hop for every internal call increases latency and creates a single point of failure. However, in some organizations a dedicated internal gateway is used for policy enforcement and observability. My recommendation depends on the organization size and operational needs: small teams use a single gateway for external traffic and direct internal calls; large enterprises may add an internal gateway layer for governance.

title: Q: [Q1] What are API Gateway filters, and why are they important in a microservices architecture?
text: [Q1] What are API Gateway filters, and why are they important in a microservices architecture? API Gateway filters are components that process requests and responses as they pass through the gateway. They are important because they centralize cross-cutting concerns that every service would otherwise have to implement itself. Filters can modify requests before they reach downstream services, modify responses before they return to clients, reject requests, log traffic, add security headers, enforce rate limits, and handle circuit breaker fallbacks. In a microservices architecture, this centralization reduces duplication and makes it easier to enforce consistent policies such as authentication, logging, and traffic management across all services.

title: Q: [Q2] What is the difference between a GatewayFilter and a  GlobalFilter in Spring Cloud Gateway?
text: [Q2] What is the difference between a GatewayFilter and a GlobalFilter in Spring Cloud Gateway? A GatewayFilter is route-specific and is attached to individual routes through filter factories. It modifies requests or responses only for the routes where it is configured. A GlobalFilter applies to all routes and is used for cross-cutting concerns such as routing, load balancing, metrics, and logging. Both implement the same filter(exchange, chain) signature and participate in the same ordered filter chain. Spring Cloud Gateway merges route-specific GatewayFilter instances with global filters at runtime, ordered by Ordered. The highest precedence filter runs first in the pre phase and last in the post phase.

title: Q: [Q3] What are default filters in Spring Cloud Gateway?
text: [Q3] What are default filters in Spring Cloud Gateway? Default filters are filters configured under spring.cloud.gateway.default-filters that apply to every route automatically. They are useful for adding common behavior such as a response header, a prefix path, or a correlation ID without repeating the filter in every route definition. For example, you can add a default response header that applies to all responses, or a default request header that downstream services can use for tracing. Default filters are merged with route-specific filters for each route, so they behave as if they were declared on every route.

title: Q: [Q4] How do you write a custom GatewayFilterFactory in Spring Cloud Gateway?
text: [Q4] How do you write a custom GatewayFilterFactory in Spring Cloud Gateway? You extend AbstractGatewayFilterFactory where T is a configuration class that holds filter properties. The class name must end with GatewayFilterFactory, for example JwtAuthGatewayFilterFactory, and it is referenced in YAML by the short name JwtAuth. Inside the factory, you implement the apply method to return a GatewayFilter that performs the desired logic using ServerWebExchange. The filter can inspect or modify the request, decide whether to proceed, and transform the response. Custom filter factories are useful when a filter needs configuration per route and should behave like a built-in gateway filter.

title: Q: [Q5] How do you write a custom GlobalFilter for authentication in Spring Cloud Gateway?
text: [Q5] How do you write a custom GlobalFilter for authentication in Spring Cloud Gateway? You create a class that implements both GlobalFilter and Ordered, and register it as a bean. In the filter method, you inspect the incoming request, for example by reading the Authorization header, validate the token, and either continue the chain or return an unauthorized response. Because Spring Cloud Gateway is reactive, all operations must return Mono and avoid blocking the event loop. If authentication succeeds, you can add user information to the request headers so downstream services receive a trusted identity. Ordering matters: the authentication filter should run before routing filters so unauthenticated requests are rejected early.

title: Q: [Q6] What is the TokenRelay filter, and how does it help with authentication propagation?
text: [Q6] What is the TokenRelay filter, and how does it help with authentication propagation? TokenRelay is a built-in Spring Cloud Gateway filter that forwards an OAuth2 access token from the authenticated gateway session to downstream services. When a user logs in through the gateway using OAuth2, the gateway holds the access token in the security context. The TokenRelay filter copies that token into the Authorization header of the proxied request so downstream services can validate the same token. This avoids requiring users to authenticate separately with each service and centralizes token handling at the gateway. It requires the OAuth2 client dependency and proper spring.security.oauth2.client configuration.

title: Q: [Q7] Why should JWT validation happen at the API Gateway instead of in every microservice?
text: [Q7] Why should JWT validation happen at the API Gateway instead of in every microservice? Validating JWT at the gateway centralizes security logic, reduces duplication, and prevents every downstream service from needing token parsing libraries, public key configuration, and token expiration handling. The gateway rejects invalid or expired tokens before traffic reaches the backend, which reduces load on services and gives clients consistent error responses. Downstream services can then trust headers added by the gateway, such as X-User-Id or X-Roles, though they should still perform their own authorization checks based on those claims. Centralization also makes key rotation simpler because only the gateway needs to know about the new public key.

title: Q: [Q8] What are common pitfalls when implementing authentication in Spring Cloud Gateway?
text: [Q8] What are common pitfalls when implementing authentication in Spring Cloud Gateway? Common pitfalls include blocking the reactive event loop during token validation, which kills gateway throughput; assuming Spring Security context propagates automatically to downstream services without forwarding headers; caching JWK keys forever and failing when the identity provider rotates them; mixing servlet-based security code with the reactive gateway; and putting complex business authorization logic in the gateway instead of the services. Another mistake is not handling expired tokens cleanly, which can cause confusing errors for clients. The gateway should validate authentication and forward identity, while services decide what the authenticated user is allowed to do.

title: Q: [Q9] What is Spring Cloud Con g Server, and why is it used?
text: [Q9] What is Spring Cloud Con g Server, and why is it used? Spring Cloud Con g Server provides centralized externalized configuration for distributed systems. It serves configuration properties from a backend store, usually a Git repository, to client applications at runtime. Each microservice fetches its configuration by application name, profile, and label. Centralized configuration makes it easier to manage environment-specific settings, track changes through version control, and avoid embedding secrets or environment URLs in application packages. It is especially useful in microservices where dozens of services need consistent and auditable configuration.

title: Q: [Q10] How do you set up a Spring Cloud Con g Server backed by GitHub?
text: [Q10] How do you set up a Spring Cloud Con g Server backed by GitHub? You create a Spring Boot application, add @EnableConfigServer and the spring- cloud-config-server dependency, and configure spring.cloud.config.server.git.uri to point to the Git repository. Optionally, set clone- on-start: true so the server clones the repository at startup. The Git repository contains property files named after client applications, such as order-service.properties or order-service- dev.yml. The con g server exposes endpoints at /{application}/{profile}/{label}, for example http://localhost:8888/order-service/dev/main. Clients connect to the server using spring.config.import=optional:configserver:http://localhost:8888/ and set spring.application.name to match the file name.

title: Q: [Q11] How does a Spring Cloud Con g client load configuration at startup?
text: [Q11] How does a Spring Cloud Con g client load configuration at startup? A con g client connects to the Con g Server during bootstrap, before the Spring context nishes initializing. It uses spring.application.name to determine which files to fetch, and the active profile to select environment-specific overrides. The configuration is merged with local application.properties or application.yml, with remote properties taking precedence. If the Con g Server is unavailable, the client can fail, retry, or fall back depending on the optional setting in the import and failure settings. Once loaded, the properties are available to @Value, @ConfigurationProperties, and the Environment just like local properties.

title: Q: [Q12] What is @RefreshScope, and how does dynamic configuration refresh work?
text: [Q12] What is @RefreshScope, and how does dynamic configuration refresh work? @RefreshScope is a Spring Cloud annotation that marks a bean to be recreated when a refresh event occurs. When configuration changes in the Con g Server repository, calling POST /actuator/refresh on a client triggers a refresh event. Spring recreates beans annotated with @RefreshScope so they pick up the new property values without restarting the JVM. Only beans that use the changed properties need the annotation; beans that do not need refresh should not use it to avoid unnecessary recreation. Dynamic refresh is useful for updating feature ags, timeouts, rate limits, and other non-disruptive settings in production.

title: Q: [Q13] How do you refresh configuration across all clients without restarting them?
text: [Q13] How do you refresh configuration across all clients without restarting them? To refresh all clients, you use Spring Cloud Bus combined with a message broker such as RabbitMQ or Kafka. When a property changes, you call POST /actuator/busrefresh on one client or the Con g Server, and the event is broadcast to all connected clients through the bus. Each client then refreshes its own @RefreshScope beans. Alternatively, you can configure a GitHub webhook to notify the Con g Server's /monitor endpoint whenever the repository changes, and the server publishes a refresh event to the bus. This push-based approach scales better than calling /actuator/refresh on every instance manually.

title: Q: [Q14] How does the Con g Server handle updates in the Git repository?
text: [Q14] How does the Con g Server handle updates in the Git repository? By default, the Con g Server clones or pulls the Git repository on each configuration request to ensure clients receive the latest values. The frequency can be controlled with the refreshRate property so the server does not pull on every request. When using Spring Cloud Bus, the /monitor endpoint can be triggered by a GitHub webhook on push events, forcing an immediate refresh across clients. The server maintains a local copy of the repository, so it can continue serving configuration even if GitHub is temporarily unreachable, though the data may be slightly stale.

title: Q: [Q15] What is distributed tracing, and why is it important in microservices?
text: [Q15] What is distributed tracing, and why is it important in microservices? Distributed tracing is the practice of tracking a request as it ows through multiple services. Each request is assigned a trace ID, and each unit of work within a service is assigned a span ID. These identifiers are propagated across service boundaries using HTTP headers and recorded in logs and trace collectors. Distributed tracing is important in microservices because a single user request may touch many services, and without tracing it is nearly impossible to pinpoint where latency or errors occur. Tracing helps debug slow requests, identify failing services, and understand dependencies in a distributed system.

title: Q: [Q16] What are trace ID and span ID, and how do they relate?
text: [Q16] What are trace ID and span ID, and how do they relate? A trace ID is a unique identifier assigned to the entire request journey across all services. A span ID is a unique identifier for a single unit of work within one service, such as handling an HTTP request or calling a database. One trace contains many spans, and spans can be nested as parent and child to show the call hierarchy. For example, an order trace might have spans for the gateway, order service, payment client call, and database query. The trace ID ties them all together, while span IDs show the individual operations and their durations.

title: Q: [Q17] What is the difference between Spring Cloud Sleuth and Micrometer Tracing?
text: [Q17] What is the difference between Spring Cloud Sleuth and Micrometer Tracing? Spring Cloud Sleuth was the original distributed tracing solution in the Spring Cloud ecosystem. It automatically added trace and span IDs to logs and propagated them across services. In Spring Boot 3, Sleuth is deprecated and replaced by Micrometer Tracing, which is the vendor-neutral observation and tracing facade for Spring. Micrometer Tracing integrates with Brave for Zipkin or OpenTelemetry for other backends. The configuration namespace changed from spring.sleuth.* to management.tracing.*. Micrometer Tracing also provides better integration with metrics and the Observation API.

title: Q: [Q18] How do you configure distributed tracing with Micrometer and Zipkin in Spring Boot 3?
text: [Q18] How do you configure distributed tracing with Micrometer and Zipkin in Spring Boot 3? You add the micrometer-tracing-bridge-brave and zipkin-reporter-brave dependencies, along with spring-boot-starter-actuator. In application.yml, you set management.tracing.sampling.probability to control what fraction of requests are traced, and management.zipkin.tracing.endpoint to point to the Zipkin collector, such as http://localhost:9411/api/v2/spans. You also set spring.application.name so each service appears correctly in the Zipkin UI. The tracing library automatically propagates trace context through HTTP clients such as RestTemplate, WebClient, and OpenFeign when they are built through Spring's builders.

title: Q: [Q19] What are B3 and W3C trace context propagation formats?
text: [Q19] What are B3 and W3C trace context propagation formats? B3 is the propagation format originally defined by Zipkin, using headers such as X-B3- TraceId, X-B3-SpanId, and X-B3-ParentSpanId. W3C trace context is a newer standard that uses a single traceparent header and an optional tracestate header. Both formats carry the trace and span identifiers across service boundaries. The choice depends on the ecosystem: older Zipkin-based systems often use B3, while OpenTelemetry and newer standards use W3C. In a mixed environment, services may need to support both formats to ensure propagation across all components.

title: Q: [Q20] How do you correlate logs with distributed tracing?
text: [Q20] How do you correlate logs with distributed tracing? You configure the logging pattern to include the trace ID and span ID from the MDC, which Micrometer Tracing or Sleuth populates automatically. For example, the log pattern can include traceId=%X{traceId},spanId=%X{spanId}. When logs are sent to a centralized system such as Elasticsearch or Splunk, you can filter by trace ID to see all log entries from every service that participated in the same request. This bridges the three pillars of observability: logs, metrics, and traces. The key is to propagate the trace context into every service and make sure the logging framework has access to it.

title: Q: [Q21] What is the ELK stack, and how does it enable centralized logging?
text: [Q21] What is the ELK stack, and how does it enable centralized logging? ELK stands for Elasticsearch, Logstash, and Kibana. Elasticsearch stores and indexes log data. Logstash collects, parses, enriches, and forwards logs from multiple sources into Elasticsearch. Kibana provides a web interface to search, visualize, and dashboard the logs. In a microservices environment, each service sends its logs to Logstash, either directly over TCP, through a file shipped by Filebeat, or through a logging appender. Once in Elasticsearch, all logs are searchable from a single place. This makes it possible to debug issues across services without logging into individual machines.

title: Q: [Q22] What is the role of Filebeat in the ELK stack?
text: [Q22] What is the role of Filebeat in the ELK stack? Filebeat is a lightweight log shipper from Elastic that reads log files and sends them to Logstash or directly to Elasticsearch. In a Spring Boot microservices deployment, each container or host runs Filebeat, which monitors the application's log files and forwards new entries as they are written. Filebeat is more efficient than running a full Logstash agent on every host, and it handles backpressure and delivery guarantees. It is often combined with Logstash for parsing and enrichment, especially when logs need complex transformation before being indexed.

title: Q: [Q23] What is structured logging, and why is it useful with ELK?
text: [Q23] What is structured logging, and why is it useful with ELK? Structured logging means writing log entries as JSON or another parseable format with consistent fields such as timestamp, level, logger, message, trace ID, and service name. It is useful with ELK because Logstash or Filebeat can parse the fields automatically, and Elasticsearch can index them for fast searching and aggregation. Without structured logs, log parsing is fragile and full-text search is slower. Spring Boot can output JSON logs using Logback or Log4j2 configurations. Structured logging also makes it easier to build dashboards in Kibana and to set alerts based on field values.

title: Q: [Q24] What are the alternatives to the ELK stack for centralized logging?
text: [Q24] What are the alternatives to the ELK stack for centralized logging? Alternatives include the EFK stack, which replaces Logstash with Fluentd; Grafana Loki, which is lightweight and integrates well with Grafana; Splunk, which is enterprise-grade and expensive; Datadog and New Relic, which combine logs, metrics, and traces in a hosted service; and cloud-native solutions such as AWS CloudWatch Logs and Google Cloud Logging. The choice depends on cost, scale, existing tooling, and whether the team wants a self-hosted or managed solution. For many Kubernetes deployments, Loki is popular because it indexes only labels and stores compressed raw logs, making it cheaper than Elasticsearch at large scale.

title: Q: [S1] A Spring Cloud Gateway route needs to authenticate JWT tokens and forward user identity downstream. How do you implement it?
text: [S1] A Spring Cloud Gateway route needs to authenticate JWT tokens and forward user identity downstream. How do you implement it? I implement a custom GlobalFilter that runs early in the filter chain and validates the JWT from the Authorization header. The validation should be non-blocking, using a reactive JWT library such as Spring Security OAuth2 Resource Server or Nimbus JOSE. If the token is invalid or missing, the filter returns 401 and stops the chain. If valid, it extracts claims such as sub and roles, and adds them as headers like X-User-Id and X-User-Roles to the downstream request by mutating ServerWebExchange. Downstream services can then trust these headers. I order the filter before routing so unauthenticated requests never reach the backend. I also cache the JSON Web Key set with a sensible TTL to avoid repeated fetches from the identity provider, and I handle key rotation by refreshing the cache on validation failures. Real-time example: imagine an e-commerce mobile app sends a request to https://api.shop.com/orders. The gateway validates the JWT, extracts the user ID u-98231 and role CUSTOMER, then forwards the request to the order service with headers X-User-Id: u-98231 and X-User-Roles: CUSTOMER. The order service does not need to talk to the identity provider; it simply trusts the gateway and enforces that the user can only see their own orders.

title: Q: [S2] A custom GatewayFilter works locally but is not applied to a route in production. What do you check?
text: [S2] A custom GatewayFilter works locally but is not applied to a route in production. What do you check? I first check the class name. For Spring Cloud Gateway to reference a filter by short name in YAML, the class name must end with GatewayFilterFactory. If the class is named JwtAuthFilter instead of JwtAuthGatewayFilterFactory, the YAML reference JwtAuth will not resolve. I also verify that the filter class is in a package scanned by Spring and registered as a bean. If the route uses the filter with arguments, I check that the configuration class matches the YAML structure and that constructor binding is correct. Finally, I inspect the actuator gateway routes endpoint to see whether the filter is attached to the route and whether there are errors during route loading. Real-time example: a developer writes AuditLogFilter and registers it in YAML as - AuditLog. Locally, the YAML is small and the error may be silently ignored, but in production the gateway fails to bind the route and the filter is skipped. Renaming the class to AuditLogGatewayFilterFactory and using - AuditLog fixes the binding. I also check that the production build includes the package in component scanning; if the class is in a utility module, it may not be scanned by the gateway's main application.

title: Q: [S3] A global logging filter in the gateway logs every request, but it also logs sensitive tokens. How do you fix it?
text: [S3] A global logging filter in the gateway logs every request, but it also logs sensitive tokens. How do you fix it? I update the logging filter to sanitize or skip sensitive headers before logging. I maintain a deny list of headers such as Authorization, Cookie, X-Api-Key, and Refresh-Token, and I replace their values with a mask such as *** or remove them entirely. If the filter logs the request body, I apply the same redaction to fields known to contain passwords or tokens. I also consider logging only metadata such as method, path, status code, duration, trace ID, and client IP, and avoid logging payloads in production. For audit purposes that require full payloads, I send those to a separate secure system with access controls rather than to general application logs. Real-time example: during a security audit, the operations team discovers that raw Authorization: Bearer eyJ... tokens appear in CloudWatch Logs because the gateway logs the full request headers. This is a breach risk because anyone with log access can replay the token. I update the filter to iterate over headers, replace any header in the deny list with [REDACTED], and add a review rule that any new logging filter must be checked for sensitive data exposure before merge.

title: Q: [S4] A Spring Cloud Con g Server fails to start because it cannot clone the Git repository. How do you troubleshoot it?
text: [S4] A Spring Cloud Con g Server fails to start because it cannot clone the Git repository. How do you troubleshoot it? I check the spring.cloud.config.server.git.uri property for typos or incorrect protocol. If the repository is private, I verify that credentials are supplied through properties, environment variables, or SSH keys and not hard-coded in the source. I also check network connectivity from the Con g Server to GitHub, including proxy settings and rewall rules. If clone-on-start is true, the server tries to clone at startup, so any authentication or network issue is immediate. I set clone-on-start to false during troubleshooting to defer cloning, or I use a local file backend for development. I also look at the stack trace for HTTPS certificate errors, which can happen in environments with custom TLS interception. Real-time example: after a production deployment, the Con g Server pod enters a CrashLoopBackOff because it cannot clone https://github.com/acme/config-repo.git. The repository was recently made private, but the deployment still uses the old public URI without credentials. I add a username and personal access token through Kubernetes secrets and reference them in spring.cloud.config.server.git.username and password, then restart the server. I also rotate the token and move to SSH or a GitHub App in the long term.

title: Q: [S5] A configuration value is updated in Git, but a microservice still uses the old value after calling /actuator/refresh. Why?
text: [S5] A configuration value is updated in Git, but a microservice still uses the old value after calling /actuator/refresh. Why? The bean that uses the property is probably not annotated with @RefreshScope. Only beans with @RefreshScope are recreated during a refresh event. If the value is read into a static field or initialized in a constructor without re-reading after refresh, the old value persists. Another cause is that the property file in Git was not matched by the client's application name or profile, so the Con g Server never served the new value. I verify the Con g Server endpoint for the exact application/profile/label to confirm the value is returned. I also check that the refresh actuator endpoint is exposed and that the call returned the changed property names. Real-time example: the team changes feature.new-checkout.enabled from false to true in order-service-prod.yml and calls /actuator/refresh. The feature ag service bean is not @RefreshScope, so it still reads the cached false value. I annotate the ag service with @RefreshScope, confirm the refresh response includes feature.new-checkout.enabled, and verify the new checkout UI appears for a small test segment before rolling out widely.

title: Q: [S6] A team wants to refresh configuration across 50 microservice instances without calling each one. How do you design it?
text: [S6] A team wants to refresh configuration across 50 microservice instances without calling each one. How do you design it? I use Spring Cloud Bus with RabbitMQ or Kafka as the message broker. When configuration changes, I configure a GitHub webhook to call the Con g Server's /monitor endpoint, or I manually call POST /actuator/busrefresh on one instance. The bus publishes a refresh event that all connected clients consume, and each client refreshes its own @RefreshScope beans automatically. This avoids the operational burden of calling every instance. I also ensure the message broker is highly available because the refresh mechanism depends on it. For critical changes that require a restart, such as connection pool size changes at the JVM level, I use a rolling deployment instead of a refresh. Real-time example: during a sale event, marketing wants to update the maximum discount percentage across all services. Instead of SSH-ing into 50 pods, a developer pushes the new value to Git, the GitHub webhook hits /monitor, and Spring Cloud Bus broadcasts RefreshRemoteApplicationEvent to all order-service, pricing-service, and cart-service instances. Within seconds, every service picks up the new limit without a restart.

title: Q: [S7] A distributed trace shows spans from some services but is broken in the middle. How do you debug it?
text: [S7] A distributed trace shows spans from some services but is broken in the middle. How do you debug it? I first identify which service is not propagating the trace context. I check whether the service uses an instrumented HTTP client; RestTemplate must be built with RestTemplateBuilder, WebClient with WebClient.Builder, and OpenFeign with Spring Cloud OpenFeign, otherwise trace headers are not added automatically. I also check whether the service has custom code that creates a new thread or uses a thread pool without copying the trace context. For async operations, I use TaskDecorator or reactor context propagation. I verify the propagation format; if one service uses W3C and another expects B3, the trace will be broken. Finally, I check that the service name is configured correctly so spans appear under the right service in Zipkin. Real-time example: a trace for GET /orders/123 shows spans for the gateway, order service, and database, but the payment service span is missing. The payment service uses a manually created RestTemplate with new RestTemplate() instead of RestTemplateBuilder, so Micrometer Tracing does not inject the traceparent header. I switch to RestTemplateBuilder, verify the header is sent with a request test, and the next trace shows the full chain including the payment service and its downstream fraud check.

title: Q: [S8] A service logs are not appearing in Kibana even though Filebeat is running. What do you check?
text: [S8] A service logs are not appearing in Kibana even though Filebeat is running. What do you check? I start by checking the Filebeat logs for connection errors, authentication failures, or parsing errors. I verify that Filebeat is configured to read the correct log path and that the application is actually writing logs to that path. I check whether Logstash or Elasticsearch is reachable from the Filebeat host and whether the index or data stream is configured. If the logs are in JSON but Filebeat expects plain text, or vice versa, parsing can fail silently. I also check Kibana index patterns to ensure they match the Elasticsearch indices where the logs are stored. In container environments, I verify that log rotation is not deleting files before Filebeat reads them. Real-time example: after deploying a new service, the team cannot find its logs in Kibana. Filebeat is configured to read /var/log/app/*.log, but the Spring Boot application logs to stdout and Docker sends output to /var/lib/docker/containers/.... I update the Filebeat input to use the Docker container log path or the docker input, add the service.name field, and the logs start appearing under the correct index pattern.

title: Q: [S9] A Spring Boot application writes logs in plain text, and the ELK stack cannot parse them consistently. How do you fix it?
text: [S9] A Spring Boot application writes logs in plain text, and the ELK stack cannot parse them consistently. How do you fix it? I switch the application to structured JSON logging using a Logback or Log4j2 JSON encoder. JSON logs have consistent fields that Filebeat, Logstash, and Elasticsearch can parse without fragile regex patterns. I include fields such as timestamp, level, logger, message, service name, trace ID, span ID, and environment. I configure the logging pattern in logback-spring.xml or log4j2- spring.xml and make it conditional on the active profile so local logs remain readable. After switching, I verify the mapping in Elasticsearch and update Kibana dashboards to use the new fields. Real-time example: one team writes logs like 2025-07-01 10:23:45 ERROR com.example.OrderService Failed to save order. Another team uses ERROR | OrderService | save failed. Logstash has two grok filters, and both break when formats change. I standardize all services on JSON output with the logstash-logback-encoder so every log entry has the same timestamp, level, logger, message, traceId, and service fields, and dashboards become reliable.

title: Q: [S10] A Con g Client crashes on startup because the Con g Server is down. How do you make startup more resilient?
text: [S10] A Con g Client crashes on startup because the Con g Server is down. How do you make startup more resilient? I make the con g server import optional by using spring.config.import=optional:configserver:http://localhost:8888/. With optional, the client starts using local configuration if the server is unavailable. I also configure retry with spring.cloud.config.fail-fast=false or a retry policy so the client attempts to connect multiple times before failing. For non-optional configurations, I run the Con g Server in a high- availability setup with multiple instances behind a load balancer. I also add health checks and readiness probes in Kubernetes so pods are not marked ready until they can fetch configuration, preventing traffic from reaching misconfigured instances. Real-time example: during a regional outage, the Con g Server in us-east-1 becomes unreachable, and new pods in the order service fail to start. I change the import to optional:configserver and include sensible defaults in application.yml so pods can start with degraded con g. After the outage, I deploy a second Con g Server in us-west-2 and use a DNS failover so clients always reach a healthy instance.

title: Q: [S11] A gateway filter must add different headers depending on the route. Should you use a GlobalFilter or a GatewayFilterFactory?
text: [S11] A gateway filter must add different headers depending on the route. Should you use a GlobalFilter or a GatewayFilterFactory? A GatewayFilterFactory is the better choice because it allows per-route configuration. The factory can accept a configuration object that specifies which headers to add, and the same filter can be used on different routes with different settings. A GlobalFilter applies to all routes and would need custom logic to inspect the route and decide what to do, which is harder to maintain. For route-specific transformations, custom GatewayFilter factories are cleaner, more declarative, and easier to test. I implement AbstractGatewayFilterFactory with a Config class and reference the filter by short name in each route's YAML. Real-time example: internal admin routes need X- Internal: true, while public API routes need X-Public: true. With a HeaderInjectionGatewayFilterFactory, I declare - HeaderInjection=headerName=X-Internal,headerValue=true on admin routes and - HeaderInjection=headerName=X-Public,headerValue=true on public routes. A global filter would need if-else logic based on path, making it harder to extend when a third route category is added.

title: Q: [S12] A microservice wants to change a log level in production without restarting. How do you do it?
text: [S12] A microservice wants to change a log level in production without restarting. How do you do it? I expose the Spring Boot Actuator loggers endpoint and send a POST request to change the level of a specific logger at runtime. For example, POST /actuator/loggers/com.example.service with a JSON body setting configuredLevel to DEBUG changes the level immediately. This does not require a restart and is useful for troubleshooting. I secure the endpoint so only authorized users can change log levels, and I document that these changes are temporary because they are lost on restart unless persisted. For a more centralized approach, I combine this with Spring Cloud Con g and /actuator/refresh so log level changes are managed through configuration. Real-time example: a support ticket reports intermittent failures in the payment service. The on-call engineer temporarily sets com.example.payment.PaymentClient to DEBUG via the Actuator endpoint, captures a few failing requests, identifies a malformed downstream response, and then reverts the level to INFO once the issue is understood.

title: Q: [S13] A trace ID is present in the gateway logs but missing from database query logs inside a service. Why?
text: [S13] A trace ID is present in the gateway logs but missing from database query logs inside a service. Why? Database query logs are usually written by a separate library or connection pool that does not have access to the trace context stored in the request's MDC. If the query is executed synchronously during the request, the MDC should be populated, but some logging frameworks or JDBC drivers do not propagate MDC into their own log statements. I check whether the trace context is in the MDC when the database call is made and whether the logging pattern includes trace ID. For async database operations or reactive stacks, context propagation may be lost. I also consider using Micrometer Observation or a custom data source proxy that tags spans with database query information. Real-time example: the order service uses HikariCP and logs slow queries through a separate logger that does not include MDC. When a support engineer filters Kibana by trace ID abc-123, they see gateway and order service logs but no database entries. I wrap the data source with a custom proxy that copies MDC into the query thread, or I switch to Micrometer Observation so the database query becomes a span with the same trace ID.

title: Q: [S14] A Zipkin collector is receiving too many spans and becoming a bottleneck. How do you reduce load?
text: [S14] A Zipkin collector is receiving too many spans and becoming a bottleneck. How do you reduce load? I reduce the sampling probability so only a percentage of requests are traced, for example management.tracing.sampling.probability=0.1 in production. This gives enough visibility without overwhelming the collector. I also avoid creating spans for very high-frequency internal operations that are not useful for debugging. I batch and compress span reports, and I consider running multiple Zipkin collector instances behind a load balancer. For very large scale, I replace Zipkin with a more scalable backend such as Jaeger, Grafana Tempo, or a managed tracing service. Sampling is the first and most effective lever to reduce load. Real-time example: during a ash sale, the Zipkin server falls behind and drops spans because 100 percent sampling is enabled. I change the sampling probability to 0.05 for high-traffic endpoints and 1.0 for critical payment ows. This reduces volume by 95 percent while preserving visibility where it matters. I also add a queue between services and Zipkin to absorb bursts.

title: Q: [S15] A Con g Server stores secrets in a Git repository in plain text. How do you secure them?
text: [S15] A Con g Server stores secrets in a Git repository in plain text. How do you secure them? Storing secrets in plain text in Git is a serious risk. I use Spring Cloud Con g's encryption and decryption features to store encrypted values, or I move secrets to a dedicated secret store such as HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or Azure Key Vault. Spring Cloud Con g supports composite backends, so it can fetch configuration from Git and secrets from Vault. Another approach is to store secrets in environment variables provided by the deployment platform, while keeping non-secret configuration in Git. I also review Git history for leaked secrets and rotate any exposed credentials immediately. Real-time example: a security scan finds spring.datasource.password=prod- password-123 in order-service-prod.yml. I rotate the password immediately, remove the value from Git history, and configure a composite backend that fetches the database password from Vault while keeping non-secret con g in Git. Developers now see ${vault://secret/order- service/db/password} instead of a plain password.

title: Q: [S16] A Spring Cloud Gateway route with a custom filter returns 500 for a specific request. How do you debug it?
text: [S16] A Spring Cloud Gateway route with a custom filter returns 500 for a specific request. How do you debug it? I enable debug logging for org.springframework.cloud.gateway and inspect the stack trace. I check whether the filter is throwing an exception due to malformed input, missing configuration, or a downstream failure. Because the gateway is reactive, I look for blocking calls on the event loop, which manifest as warnings or deadlocks under load. I use the actuator gateway routes endpoint to verify the route and filter configuration, and I add unit tests for the filter in isolation. If the issue is intermittent, I correlate gateway logs with downstream service logs using the trace ID to see whether the failure originates in the backend. Real-time example: a custom rate-limiting filter throws NullPointerException for requests that do not have a specific query parameter because the key resolver assumes the parameter is always present. I add a default value, write a unit test with and without the parameter, and update the filter to return 400 instead of 500 for invalid input.

title: Q: [S17] A centralized logging dashboard shows logs from some services with different timestamp formats. How do you standardize them?
text: [S17] A centralized logging dashboard shows logs from some services with different timestamp formats. How do you standardize them? I standardize the log output across all services by using a common logging configuration that writes timestamps in ISO-8601 UTC format, such as yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.SSSZ. I include the timezone explicitly or use UTC to avoid ambiguity. If services use different logging frameworks, I configure each to match the same pattern or, better, switch all services to JSON logging with a common schema. In Logstash, I use date filters to parse incoming timestamps and store them in a consistent @timestamp field. Standardized timestamps are critical for sorting and correlating events across services in Kibana. Real-time example: one legacy service logs in local IST, a new service logs in UTC, and the gateway uses a custom format. When debugging an incident, the team cannot sort events chronologically. I migrate all services to UTC ISO-8601 in JSON and add a Logstash date filter that normalizes every incoming timestamp to @timestamp before indexing.

title: Q: [S18] A team wants to use Spring Cloud Con g but also needs local overrides per developer machine. How do you support both?
text: [S18] A team wants to use Spring Cloud Con g but also needs local overrides per developer machine. How do you support both? I use the configuration loading order to my advantage. Spring Cloud Con g provides shared and application-specific configuration, and local application.yml or environment variables can override remote values. I give developers a default remote configuration in Git and allow them to create a local profile or use environment variables for personal overrides. I keep the spring.config.import for the Con g Server but make it optional so developers can run offline. I also use .gitignore for local override files and document the override precedence so developers understand that local properties win over remote ones. Real-time example: developers want to use a local MySQL container while the shared con g points to a cloud RDS instance. I add spring.datasource.url to application-local.yml in each developer's project, ignored by Git, and tell them to run with --spring.profiles.active=local. The shared application.yml still points to the cloud database for CI and production.

title: Q: [S19] A gateway filter needs to call an external service for enrichment before forwarding the request. What do you consider?
text: [S19] A gateway filter needs to call an external service for enrichment before forwarding the request. What do you consider? Calling an external service from a gateway filter adds latency and a failure point to every request. I consider whether the call can be avoided by caching the enrichment data or by pushing the responsibility to the downstream service. If the call is necessary, I make it reactive and non-blocking, and I add a timeout and a circuit breaker around it so a slow external service does not degrade the gateway. I also use a bulkhead to limit how many concurrent enrichment calls can run. The filter should handle failures gracefully, either by forwarding without enrichment or by returning an error, depending on business requirements. Real-time example: a gateway filter calls a user-profile service to add a loyalty tier header to every request. When the profile service slows down, gateway latency increases and threads are exhausted. I add a 200-millisecond timeout and a circuit breaker around the call, cache the loyalty tier in Redis with a 5-minute TTL, and return a default tier when the service is unavailable instead of failing the entire request.

title: Q: [S20] A distributed system has services in multiple programming languages. How do you ensure trace propagation works across all of them?
text: [S20] A distributed system has services in multiple programming languages. How do you ensure trace propagation works across all of them? I standardize on a propagation format supported by all services, typically W3C trace context because it is language-agnostic and widely supported. Each service extracts the traceparent header from incoming requests and injects it into outgoing requests. For services that do not have native tracing libraries, I instrument them with OpenTelemetry, which supports many languages. I also use a service mesh such as Istio or Linkerd, which can inject and propagate trace headers transparently without changing application code. The gateway or ingress layer can be configured to accept incoming trace headers and ensure they are forwarded to the first service in the system. Real-time example: a Python-based recommendation service sits between Java order and notification services. The Java services use Micrometer Tracing with B3, but the Python service uses OpenTelemetry with W3C. I configure the Java services to propagate both B3 and W3C headers and update the Python service to read whichever is present. Over time, I migrate everything to W3C for consistency.

title: Q: [Q1] What is Apache Kafka, and why is it used?
text: [Q1] What is Apache Kafka, and why is it used? Apache Kafka is a distributed event-streaming platform designed for high-throughput, fault- tolerant, real-time data pipelines and stream-processing applications. It combines messaging, storage, and stream processing in a single platform. Kafka is used because it can handle millions of events per second, store data durably for replay, decouple producers from consumers, and scale horizontally across clusters. Common use cases include event sourcing, log aggregation, real-time analytics, change data capture, metrics collection, and asynchronous communication between microservices.

title: Q: [Q2] What are the core components of Kafka?
text: [Q2] What are the core components of Kafka? The core components of Kafka are producers, consumers, topics, partitions, brokers, and ZooKeeper or KRaft controllers. Producers publish events to topics. Topics are logical streams of events. Topics are divided into partitions, which are ordered, append-only logs distributed across brokers. Consumers read events from partitions, often as part of a consumer group. Brokers are Kafka server nodes that store partitions and handle client requests. Controllers manage metadata such as topic creation, partition leadership, and replica placement. Modern Kafka uses KRaft mode, where controllers are Kafka nodes themselves, replacing the older ZooKeeper-based architecture.

title: Q: [Q3] What is a topic and a partition in Kafka?
text: [Q3] What is a topic and a partition in Kafka? A topic is a logical category or stream of events, similar to a table in a database but without enforced schema by default. A partition is a subdivision of a topic that acts as an ordered, immutable sequence of records. Each record in a partition gets a monotonically increasing offset that identifies its position. Partitions allow Kafka to scale horizontally because different partitions can be stored on different brokers and consumed by different consumers in parallel. Ordering is guaranteed only within a single partition, not across partitions of the same topic.

title: Q: [Q4] What is a Kafka broker, and how does it work?
text: [Q4] What is a Kafka broker, and how does it work? A Kafka broker is a server node in a Kafka cluster. It receives and serves messages, manages storage, handles replication, and coordinates with other brokers through the controller. Each broker is identified by a unique broker ID. Producers connect to any broker to discover metadata about leaders and partitions, then send records directly to the broker that is the leader for the target partition. Consumers similarly fetch records from partition leaders. Multiple brokers form a cluster, and data is replicated across them for fault tolerance.

title: Q: [Q5] What is a producer in Kafka?
text: [Q5] What is a producer in Kafka? A producer is a client application that publishes records to Kafka topics. It sends records to the leader broker of the partition determined by the record key and partitioner strategy. Producers can configure acknowledgment levels, retries, batching, compression, and idempotence. By default, the producer uses a partitioner that hashes the key to choose a partition. If no key is provided, it uses a sticky or round-robin strategy depending on the Kafka version and configuration.

title: Q: [Q6] What is a consumer and a consumer group in Kafka?
text: [Q6] What is a consumer and a consumer group in Kafka? A consumer is a client application that reads records from Kafka topics. Consumers subscribe to topics and fetch records from partitions. A consumer group is a set of consumers that cooperate to consume a topic. Kafka assigns each partition to exactly one consumer in the group, so records are load-balanced across group members. If there are more consumers than partitions, some consumers remain idle. If a consumer fails, its partitions are reassigned to other group members through a process called rebalancing.

title: Q: [Q7] What is an offset in Kafka?
text: [Q7] What is an offset in Kafka? An offset is a unique, monotonically increasing integer assigned to each record within a partition. It identifies the position of a record in the partition log. Consumers track their progress by committing offsets, which tells Kafka the last record they have successfully processed. Offsets can be committed automatically at fixed intervals, synchronously after processing, or manually by the application for precise control. The committed offset is stored in Kafka's internal __consumer_offsets topic.

title: Q: [Q8] What are the differences between Kafka and traditional message queues like RabbitMQ?
text: [Q8] What are the differences between Kafka and traditional message queues like RabbitMQ? Kafka is a distributed log that stores events durably and allows multiple consumers to read the same data independently. Traditional message queues like RabbitMQ usually delete messages after delivery and focus on task distribution. Kafka scales horizontally through partitions, supports high throughput, and allows replay of events from the beginning. RabbitMQ is better for complex routing, priority queues, and request-response patterns. Kafka is better for event streaming, log aggregation, and decoupled event-driven microservices.

title: Q: [Q9] What are the main delivery semantics in Kafka?
text: [Q9] What are the main delivery semantics in Kafka? Kafka supports three delivery semantics. At-most-once means a message is delivered zero or one time and may be lost. This happens when offsets are committed before processing. At-least-once means a message is delivered one or more times and may be duplicated if processing succeeds but offset commit fails. Exactly-once means each message is delivered and processed exactly one time. Exactly-once requires idempotent producers, transactions, and consumer-side handling such as storing output offsets together with processing results in a transactional store.

title: Q: [Q10] What is the role of replication in Kafka?
text: [Q10] What is the role of replication in Kafka? Replication makes Kafka fault-tolerant by copying partition data across multiple brokers. Each partition has one leader replica that handles all reads and writes, and one or more follower replicas that replicate the leader's log. The number of copies is controlled by the replication factor. If the leader fails, one of the in-sync replicas is elected as the new leader. Replication ensures that data is not lost when a broker crashes, but it adds some latency and storage overhead.

title: Q: [Q11] What is the ISR list in Kafka?
text: [Q11] What is the ISR list in Kafka? ISR stands for In-Sync Replicas. It is the set of replicas for a partition that are fully caught up with the leader. Only replicas in the ISR are eligible to become leaders if the current leader fails. A follower that falls too far behind is removed from the ISR. A producer configured with acks=all waits for acknowledgment from all ISR replicas before considering a record committed, which improves durability at the cost of latency.

title: Q: [Q12] What is the controller in Kafka, and how does KRaft change it?
text: [Q12] What is the controller in Kafka, and how does KRaft change it? The controller is the broker responsible for managing cluster metadata, including partition leadership, replica assignments, topic configuration, and broker registration. In the older ZooKeeper mode, the controller was elected by ZooKeeper and stored metadata in ZooKeeper znodes. In KRaft mode, Kafka uses its own Raft consensus protocol to manage metadata in an internal metadata log. A small set of controller nodes form a quorum, and one active controller leads the metadata log. KRaft removes the ZooKeeper dependency, improves scalability, and enables faster controller failover because a new leader already has the metadata log replicated.

title: Q: [Q13] How does Kafka handle message ordering?
text: [Q13] How does Kafka handle message ordering? Kafka guarantees message ordering within a single partition. If a producer sends multiple records to the same partition, they are appended in the order they are received. If a consumer reads from that partition, it receives records in the same order. Ordering is not guaranteed across partitions. To preserve the order of related events, applications use the same key for those events so they all go to the same partition. For example, all events for a given order ID should use the order ID as the key.

title: Q: [Q14] What are Kafka partitions used for?
text: [Q14] What are Kafka partitions used for? Partitions are the unit of parallelism in Kafka. They allow a topic to be split across multiple brokers, enabling horizontal scaling of both storage and throughput. Partitions also allow multiple consumers in the same group to process data concurrently, because each partition is assigned to only one consumer. However, more partitions increase the overhead of replication, leadership management, and consumer rebalancing, so the partition count should be chosen based on throughput needs and cluster capacity.

title: Q: [Q15] How do you install and run Kafka locally?
text: [Q15] How do you install and run Kafka locally? You download Kafka from the Apache Kafka website, extract the archive, and start ZooKeeper if using an older version, or start a KRaft broker in newer versions. For KRaft, you run kafka- storage.sh random-uuid to generate a cluster UUID, format the storage with kafka- storage.sh format, and start the broker with kafka-server-start.sh. You can create topics with kafka-topics.sh, send messages with kafka-console-producer.sh, and consume with kafka-console-consumer.sh. Con uent Platform and Docker images are also popular for local development because they bundle Kafka, Schema Registry, and control center.

title: Q: [Q16] What tools can be used to visualize and manage Kafka?
text: [Q16] What tools can be used to visualize and manage Kafka? Common Kafka visualization and management tools include Con uent Control Center, Kafdrop, AKHQ, Kafka-UI by Provectus, Offset Explorer, and command-line scripts included with Kafka. Con uent Control Center provides monitoring, topic management, stream lineage, and alerting. Kafdrop and AKHQ are lightweight web UIs for browsing topics, partitions, consumer groups, and offsets. These tools help developers inspect messages, understand lag, and diagnose issues without writing custom scripts.

title: Q: [Q17] How do you configure a Spring Boot application to produce messages to Kafka?
text: [Q17] How do you configure a Spring Boot application to produce messages to Kafka? You add the spring-kafka starter dependency and configure properties such as spring.kafka.bootstrap-servers, spring.kafka.producer.key-serializer, and spring.kafka.producer.value-serializer. You can then inject KafkaTemplate into a service and call send(topic, key, value) to publish records. Spring Boot auto-configures the producer factory based on properties. For JSON payloads, you set the value serializer to JsonSerializer or configure a custom serializer. You should also handle send results asynchronously using ListenableFuture or CompletableFuture and add error callbacks.

title: Q: [Q18] How do you consume messages from Kafka in Spring Boot?
text: [Q18] How do you consume messages from Kafka in Spring Boot? You use the @KafkaListener annotation on a method and configure properties such as spring.kafka.bootstrap-servers, spring.kafka.consumer.group-id, spring.kafka.consumer.key-deserializer, and spring.kafka.consumer.value- deserializer. The annotated method receives the deserialized message, either as a String, a POJO, or a ConsumerRecord. Spring manages offset commits based on the AckMode setting. With AckMode.RECORD, the offset is committed after each record. With AckMode.BATCH, it is committed after a batch. For manual control, you can use Acknowledgment.acknowledge() and set enable-auto-commit to false.

title: Q: [Q19] What is KafkaTemplate in Spring Boot?
text: [Q19] What is KafkaTemplate in Spring Boot? KafkaTemplate is a high-level Spring abstraction for sending messages to Kafka. It wraps a ProducerFactory and provides convenient methods such as send, sendDefault, and executeInTransaction. It returns a ListenableFuture or CompletableFuture that can be used to inspect the record metadata or handle failures asynchronously. KafkaTemplate simpli es producer configuration and integrates with Spring transaction management for exactly-once semantics when combined with @Transactional.

title: Q: [Q20] How do you configure multiple Kafka producers or consumers in Spring Boot?
text: [Q20] How do you configure multiple Kafka producers or consumers in Spring Boot? You define multiple ProducerFactory or ConsumerFactory beans manually with distinct property prefixes and create separate KafkaTemplate or ConcurrentKafkaListenerContainerFactory beans. You then use @Qualifier or bean names to inject the correct factory where needed. For listeners, you specify the containerFactory attribute of @KafkaListener. This is useful when different topics need different serializers, security settings, or transaction configurations.

title: Q: [Q21] What is idempotence in Kafka producers, and why does it matter?
text: [Q21] What is idempotence in Kafka producers, and why does it matter? Idempotence means that sending the same record multiple times has the same effect as sending it once. Kafka producers can be configured with enable.idempotence=true to guarantee that duplicate retries do not produce duplicate messages within a single producer session. This is important because network failures can cause the producer to retry requests, and without idempotence, retries may result in duplicate records. Idempotence works by assigning sequence numbers to records per partition and tracking them on the broker.

title: Q: [Q22] What are the most important Kafka producer configuration properties?
text: [Q22] What are the most important Kafka producer configuration properties? Important producer properties include bootstrap.servers, key.serializer, value.serializer, acks, retries, enable.idempotence, batch.size, linger.ms, compression.type, buffer.memory, and max.in.flight.requests.per.connection. acks controls durability, with all being the safest. batch.size and linger.ms control throughput by grouping records. compression.type reduces network usage. max.in.flight.requests.per.connection affects throughput and ordering when retries occur.

title: Q: [Q23] What are the most important Kafka consumer configuration properties?
text: [Q23] What are the most important Kafka consumer configuration properties? Important consumer properties include bootstrap.servers, group.id, key.deserializer, value.deserializer, auto.offset.reset, enable.auto.commit, max.poll.records, max.poll.interval.ms, session.timeout.ms, heartbeat.interval.ms, and fetch.min.bytes. auto.offset.reset determines behavior when no previous offset exists, such as reading from earliest or latest. max.poll.records limits the number of records returned per poll. max.poll.interval.ms defines the maximum allowed time between polls before the consumer is considered failed and rebalanced.

title: Q: [Q24] What is the Kafka Schema Registry, and why is it used?
text: [Q24] What is the Kafka Schema Registry, and why is it used? The Kafka Schema Registry is a service that stores and manages schemas for data used in Kafka topics. It is commonly used with the Con uent Platform and supports Avro, Protobuf, and JSON Schema. Producers register schemas with the registry and serialize messages using the schema ID. Consumers fetch the schema by ID to deserialize messages. The registry enforces compatibility rules such as backward, forward, and full compatibility, preventing incompatible schema changes from breaking producers or consumers. It enables centralized schema management, schema evolution, and data governance in event-driven systems.

title: Q: [S1] A Kafka producer sends messages but some records are lost during a broker restart. How do you prevent data loss?
text: [S1] A Kafka producer sends messages but some records are lost during a broker restart. How do you prevent data loss? I investigate the producer configuration and cluster setup. Data loss during a broker restart often happens because the producer uses acks=1, meaning it waits only for the leader acknowledgment and does not wait for replicas. If the leader crashes before followers replicate the data, the record is lost. I change the producer to acks=all so the producer waits for all in-sync replicas to confirm. I also verify that the topic has a replication factor of at least three and that min.insync.replicas is set to at least two. Additionally, I enable enable.idempotence=true to avoid duplicates during retries. I test the scenario by killing a broker during a load test and verifying no records are lost. Real-time example: a payment event producer writes to a topic with replication.factor=1 and acks=1. When a broker restarts during deployment, some payment records vanish and downstream reconciliation fails. I recreate the topic with replication.factor=3, min.insync.replicas=2, update the producer to acks=all, and re-run the load test. The event loss drops to zero.

title: Q: [S2] A consumer group is rebalancing too frequently and causing delays. What do you check?
text: [S2] A consumer group is rebalancing too frequently and causing delays. What do you check? Frequent rebalances usually mean consumers are failing heartbeats, taking too long to process records, or crashing. I check session.timeout.ms and heartbeat.interval.ms to ensure they are appropriate for the processing time. I look at max.poll.interval.ms, which is the maximum time allowed between poll calls; if processing takes longer than this, the consumer is kicked out and the group rebalances. I reduce max.poll.records so each poll returns fewer records and processing nishes within the interval. I also check for exceptions causing consumer thread death and ensure consumer code does not block the poll loop. Real-time example: an order processing consumer downloads large files during message handling, causing max.poll.interval.ms to expire. The consumer is removed, partitions reassigned, and the same records reprocessed repeatedly. I move file downloads to a separate thread pool, reduce max.poll.records from 500 to 100, and set max.poll.interval.ms to 10 minutes. Rebalances stop and throughput stabilizes.

title: Q: [S3] A consumer is reading the same messages multiple times after a restart. What is happening?
text: [S3] A consumer is reading the same messages multiple times after a restart. What is happening? This is likely at-least-once delivery with auto-commit enabled and offsets committed before processing completes. If the application restarts after receiving messages but before committing offsets, it will reprocess those messages on restart. I switch to manual acknowledgment by setting enable.auto.commit=false and the listener container AckMode to MANUAL or MANUAL_IMMEDIATE, then call acknowledgment.acknowledge() only after successful processing. For non-idempotent operations, I also deduplicate using a database unique key or idempotency cache. Real-time example: a notification service sends emails and uses auto-commit. After a deployment, it sends duplicate welcome emails to users. I switch to manual commit, store a notification_sent record in the database as part of the same transaction, and acknowledge the Kafka offset only after the insert succeeds. Duplicates stop even if the pod restarts mid-batch.

title: Q: [S4] Messages for the same customer are appearing out of order in a topic. How do you fix it?
text: [S4] Messages for the same customer are appearing out of order in a topic. How do you fix it? Ordering is only guaranteed within a partition, so related messages must use the same key to land in the same partition. If the producer sends customer events without a key, they are distributed across partitions and may be consumed out of order. I ensure that all events for the same customer use the customer ID as the message key. I also check that there is only one consumer per partition in the group, because multiple consumers on the same partition is not possible in a consumer group.
