If stronger ordering is needed across many partitions, I reduce the partition count or use a custom partitioner. Real-time example: an e-commerce platform sends OrderCreated, PaymentReceived, and OrderShipped events without keys. A consumer sometimes sees OrderShipped before PaymentReceived. I update the producer to use orderId as the key for all order-related events, guaranteeing that the entire order lifecycle stays in one partition and is processed in order.

title: Q: [S5] A topic is producing millions of messages per hour and a single consumer cannot keep up. How do you scale consumption?
text: [S5] A topic is producing millions of messages per hour and a single consumer cannot keep up. How do you scale consumption? I increase the number of partitions for the topic and add more consumers to the consumer group. Kafka assigns one partition per consumer, so the maximum parallelism equals the number of partitions. If the topic has only three partitions but the consumer group has one consumer, adding consumers will not help beyond three. After increasing partitions, I add consumers up to the partition count. I also optimize the consumer logic by reducing I/O, using batch processing, and avoiding synchronous calls. If the bottleneck is external, such as database writes, I consider buffering and writing in batches. Real-time example: a telemetry topic ingests sensor data and one consumer writes to a database. Lag grows continuously. I increase partitions from 3 to 24, add six consumer instances, and batch database inserts. Consumer lag drops from 500,000 records to near zero within minutes.

title: Q: [S6] A Spring Boot @KafkaListener is not receiving messages. How do you debug it?
text: [S6] A Spring Boot @KafkaListener is not receiving messages. How do you debug it? I verify that the bootstrap servers are correct and reachable. I check the topic name spelling and that the consumer group ID is set. I look at the consumer logs for connection errors, authentication failures, or deserialization errors. If auto.offset.reset is latest and no new messages are produced, the listener will appear idle. I use kafka-console-consumer.sh or a UI tool to confirm messages exist. I check the container factory configuration, including key and value deserializers. If a custom error handler is configured, I verify it is not silently swallowing exceptions. Real-time example: a new microservice's listener uses spring.kafka.consumer.group-id=order-processor- local while production expects order-processor. Messages are consumed by the wrong group and the new service sees none. I align the group ID, verify offsets in the UI, and add a startup health check that fails if the listener container is not assigned partitions.

title: Q: [S7] A Kafka cluster is running out of disk space. What are the likely causes and fixes?
text: [S7] A Kafka cluster is running out of disk space. What are the likely causes and fixes? Causes include unbounded topic retention, large message payloads, high replication factor, or log compaction not being enabled where appropriate. I check retention settings such as retention.ms, retention.bytes, and segment.bytes. I identify high-volume topics and either reduce retention, enable compaction, or add more storage. I also verify that consumers are keeping up, because lagging consumers cause Kafka to retain messages longer than intended. For long- term storage needs, I move older data to object storage using Kafka Connect or tiered storage if available. Real-time example: a logging topic retains data for 30 days and accumulates 10 TB. Analytics only needs 7 days. I change retention.ms to 7 days for the analytics topic and enable tiered storage for audit logs. Disk usage drops by 70 percent and the cluster avoids an outage.

title: Q: [S8] You need to guarantee exactly-once processing for a Kafka consumer that writes to a database. How do you design it?
text: [S8] You need to guarantee exactly-once processing for a Kafka consumer that writes to a database. How do you design it? I use Kafka transactions for exactly-once semantics. The producer is configured with transactional.id and enable.idempotence=true. The consumer has isolation.level=read_committed. I wrap the database write and the offset commit in a single transaction using @Transactional or kafkaTemplate.executeInTransaction. If the database transaction fails, the Kafka offset is not committed, so the records can be reprocessed. For the consumer side, I prefer storing the processed offset in the same database table as the business data using a unique identifier derived from the topic, partition, and offset. This makes processing idempotent without needing Kafka transactions on the consumer. Real-time example: a fraud detection service writes alerts to PostgreSQL. To avoid missing or duplicating alerts, I store the alert along with kafka_topic, partition, and offset in a unique composite key. On restart, duplicate records are rejected by the database constraint, achieving exactly-once behavior.

title: Q: [S9] A producer is experiencing high latency. What Kafka settings can improve throughput?
text: [S9] A producer is experiencing high latency. What Kafka settings can improve throughput? I tune producer settings for batching and compression. I increase batch.size so more records are sent together, and increase linger.ms so the producer waits brie y to ll batches. I enable compression with compression.type=lz4 or snappy to reduce network traffic. I increase buffer.memory if the producer is producing faster than the broker can acknowledge. I also verify that acks is not set to all if lower durability is acceptable, though this is a trade-off. If the bottleneck is broker-side, I add more partitions or brokers. Real-time example: a real-time bidding producer sends one record at a time and has 200ms p99 latency. I set linger.ms=20, batch.size=65536, compression.type=snappy, and buffer.memory=67108864. Latency drops to 30ms and throughput increases tenfold.

title: Q: [S10] A Kafka topic must support both a fast consumer and a slow batch consumer. How do you handle this?
text: [S10] A Kafka topic must support both a fast consumer and a slow batch consumer. How do you handle this? I use separate consumer groups for each consumer. Kafka allows multiple consumer groups to read the same topic independently, each maintaining its own offsets. The fast consumer uses a small max.poll.records and commits frequently. The slow batch consumer uses a larger max.poll.records, longer max.poll.interval.ms, and commits after each batch. This decouples their processing speeds. If needed, I can also use Kafka Streams or Connect to fan out data to specialized topics for each consumer type. Real-time example: a recommendation service needs near-real-time events, while an analytics service builds daily reports. I create two consumer groups: recommendation-fast with max.poll.records=50 and analytics-batch with max.poll.records=1000. Each progresses at its own pace without affecting the other.

title: Q: [S11] A consumer processes messages and calls an external HTTP service. The external service is slow and the consumer is kicked out of the group. What do you do?
text: [S11] A consumer processes messages and calls an external HTTP service. The external service is slow and the consumer is kicked out of the group. What do you do? I move the external HTTP call out of the polling thread. I use a thread pool to process records asynchronously and pause polling if the queue lls up. Alternatively, I reduce max.poll.records and increase max.poll.interval.ms to give more processing time. The cleanest solution is to o oad the external call to a separate worker stage, either by publishing to a worker topic or by using a buffering queue with pause and resume logic. I also add timeouts and circuit breakers around the external call. Real-time example: a shipping consumer calls a carrier API that occasionally takes 30 seconds. Consumers are evicted and records reprocess. I implement an in-memory queue, process the carrier calls in a separate thread pool, and pause the listener when the queue exceeds 100 items. The consumer stays in the group and reprocessing drops.

title: Q: [S12] You need to replay messages from a Kafka topic from the beginning for a new service. How do you do it?
text: [S12] You need to replay messages from a Kafka topic from the beginning for a new service. How do you do it? I deploy the new service with a unique consumer group ID and set auto.offset.reset=earliest. Because the group has no committed offsets, it starts from the beginning of each partition. If the topic has retention limits, some older messages may already be gone, so I verify retention settings first. For a one-time back ll, I can also use kafka-consumer- groups.sh --reset-offsets to move an existing group's offsets to earliest. I ensure the new consumer is idempotent so replay does not cause duplicate side effects. Real-time example: a new reporting service needs a full year of order events. I create a new consumer group reporting- backfill, set auto.offset.reset=earliest, and scale up consumers to match partitions. The service replays a billion records over a weekend and populates its data warehouse.

title: Q: [S13] A Kafka topic currently has six partitions, but business growth requires twelve. How do you add partitions?
text: [S13] A Kafka topic currently has six partitions, but business growth requires twelve. How do you add partitions? I use kafka-topics.sh --alter --topic --partitions 12 to increase the partition count. Kafka redistributes existing data only for new messages; existing segments stay on their current brokers unless I run a partition reassignment. After adding partitions, I review consumer group assignment to ensure all consumers are used. I also consider the impact on keyed messages: new keys may map to new partitions, and ordering is still guaranteed per partition. For topics with strict ordering, I plan partition expansion carefully and possibly repartition data using a temporary topic. Real-time example: a checkout topic grows from 6 to 12 partitions to handle Black Friday traffic. I run the alter command, update the consumer deployment to 12 pods, and verify balanced partition assignment. I also update dashboards and alerting because key-based ordering now spans more partitions.

title: Q: [S14] A producer and consumer use JSON serialization, but the consumer fails with deserialization errors after a producer change. How do you prevent this?
text: [S14] A producer and consumer use JSON serialization, but the consumer fails with deserialization errors after a producer change. How do you prevent this? I introduce a Schema Registry with Avro, Protobuf, or JSON Schema to enforce compatible schema evolution. Producers register schemas and consumers fetch compatible schemas by ID. I set a compatibility mode such as BACKWARD or FULL so breaking changes like renaming fields or changing types are rejected. If I must keep JSON, I use a stable schema with additive changes only and configure the consumer to ignore unknown properties. I also version events or use separate topics for incompatible formats. Real-time example: a producer changes a date field from String to Long timestamp. The JSON consumer crashes with NumberFormatException. I migrate the topic to Avro and Con uent Schema Registry, define the schema with a union type, and enforce backward compatibility. Future changes are validated in CI before deployment.

title: Q: [S15] A Schema Registry enforces backward compatibility, but a developer removes a required field and the schema is rejected. What should the developer do?
text: [S15] A Schema Registry enforces backward compatibility, but a developer removes a required field and the schema is rejected. What should the developer do? The developer should not remove required fields when backward compatibility is required, because existing consumers expect that field. If the field is truly no longer needed, the developer can mark it as optional with a default value, or add a new schema version that deprecates the field. In some cases, a topic migration or a new major version topic is the correct path. The safest approach is to add fields rather than remove them, and to test schema changes against the registry's compatibility endpoint in CI. Real-time example: a developer removes the customerEmail field from an Avro order schema. The Schema Registry rejects the new version because active consumers still read customerEmail. I help the developer add the field back as optional with a default null value, update the consumers to stop using it, and then, once all consumers are updated, remove the field in a later version.

title: Q: [S16] A Kafka cluster using ZooKeeper needs to be migrated to KRaft. What do you consider?
text: [S16] A Kafka cluster using ZooKeeper needs to be migrated to KRaft. What do you consider? I verify the Kafka version supports migration, such as Apache Kafka 3.4 or later. I plan the migration using the online migration process described in KIP-866, which includes dual-write mode for rollback. I back up ZooKeeper metadata, provision a KRaft controller quorum, and enable metadata migration on both ZooKeeper and KRaft nodes. I migrate brokers in a rolling fashion, monitor metadata consistency, and nalize the migration once all nodes are on KRaft. Because ZooKeeper mode is removed in Kafka 4.0, migration is mandatory for upgrades beyond that version. I also update operational runbooks and monitoring to remove ZooKeeper dependencies. Real-time example: a company runs Kafka 3.5 with ZooKeeper and wants to upgrade to Kafka 4.0. I set up three KRaft controllers, enable dual-write, roll brokers to KRaft mode over several maintenance windows, and nalize once metrics and partition assignments are stable. ZooKeeper is then decommissioned.

title: Q: [S17] A Kafka producer receives timeout errors under heavy load. How do you investigate?
text: [S17] A Kafka producer receives timeout errors under heavy load. How do you investigate? I check broker metrics for CPU, disk, and network saturation. I review producer metrics for record-queue-time-avg, request-latency-avg, and buffer-available-bytes. If the buffer is full, the producer blocks waiting for network threads to catch up. I increase buffer.memory or reduce linger.ms if batches are forming too slowly. I also check whether the topic has enough partitions and whether some brokers are hot spots due to uneven partition distribution. If all brokers are healthy, the issue may be a slow consumer causing retention pressure, but that usually affects brokers rather than producer timeouts. Real-time example: during a ash sale, producers start throwing timeout exceptions. Broker CPU is at 95 percent because partition leadership is concentrated on two brokers. I run a partition reassignment to spread leaders evenly, add two brokers, and tune buffer.memory. Producer timeouts disappear and throughput doubles.

title: Q: [S18] A consumer group is stuck and not making progress. How do you find the root cause?
text: [S18] A consumer group is stuck and not making progress. How do you find the root cause? I check consumer lag using kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe or a UI. If lag is growing, the consumer is slower than the producer. I check application logs for exceptions, look at processing time per record, and inspect max.poll.interval.ms for evictions. If no partitions are assigned, the group may have more consumers than partitions, or a rebalance is in progress. I also verify that the consumer is not stuck on a poison pill record by checking error handlers and dead-letter topic configuration. If the topic has no new data, the consumer is correctly idle. Real-time example: a payment reconciliation consumer shows increasing lag. The logs show no errors, but each record triggers a 2-second database query. I batch the database lookups, reduce query time to 50 milliseconds per batch, and lag clears within an hour.

title: Q: [S19] You want to use Kafka for event sourcing. What design choices do you make?
text: [S19] You want to use Kafka for event sourcing. What design choices do you make? I use topics as event streams, with each aggregate type having its own topic. Events are immutable and appended to partitions keyed by aggregate ID to preserve ordering. I version events, include metadata such as event type and timestamp, and avoid deleting or updating events. Consumers build read models by replaying events. I use log compaction for state-snapshot topics so the latest state per key is retained. I also design consumers to be idempotent and handle replays safely. Schema Registry is important to maintain event schema compatibility over time. Real-time example: an inventory system uses event sourcing where each product has its own event stream keyed by productId. Events include StockReceived, StockReserved, and StockShipped. A separate projection service replays the topic to build a read-optimized view in Elasticsearch. If the projection needs rebuilding, I reset the consumer group to earliest and replay all events.

title: Q: [S20] A Spring Boot application must publish to and consume from Kafka with Avro and Schema Registry. How do you configure it?
text: [S20] A Spring Boot application must publish to and consume from Kafka with Avro and Schema Registry. How do you configure it? I add dependencies for spring-kafka, kafka-avro-serializer, kafka-avro- deserializer, and the Avro Maven or Gradle plugin to generate Java classes from schemas. I configure spring.kafka.producer.value-serializer to io.confluent.kafka.serializers.KafkaAvroSerializer and spring.kafka.consumer.value-deserializer to KafkaAvroDeserializer. I set schema.registry.url for both producer and consumer. For consumers, I set specific.avro.reader=true to use generated classes instead of generic records. I generate Avro classes from .avsc files during the build. I also configure compatibility mode in the Schema Registry, typically BACKWARD, and set auto.register.schemas=false in production to prevent accidental schema registration. Real-time example: an order service publishes OrderCreated Avro events to a topic. The build generates OrderCreated.java from OrderCreated.avsc. The producer serializes using KafkaAvroSerializer, which registers the schema if needed and embeds the schema ID. A downstream ful llment service uses KafkaAvroDeserializer with specific.avro.reader=true and fetches the schema by ID. CI validates schema changes against the registry before deployment, ensuring compatibility.

title: Q: [S21] A Kafka topic receives events from two producers with different Avro schemas. How do you handle this?
text: [S21] A Kafka topic receives events from two producers with different Avro schemas. How do you handle this? I use Schema Registry subjects and schema references to support multiple event types in the same topic. One approach is to define a wrapper schema such as an envelope with a type discriminator, or to use Schema Registry's support for multiple schemas per subject if the registry and clients support it. Another approach is to separate event types into different topics if they have different schemas and lifecycles. If I keep a single topic, I ensure the consumer can handle all expected schemas, often by using generic Avro records and routing based on a type field. I also enforce compatibility carefully because mixing schemas in one topic complicates evolution. Real-time example: a user-events topic receives both UserRegistered and UserProfileUpdated events. I create an Avro union type or an envelope schema with a type field and a union payload. The Schema Registry stores both referenced schemas, and the consumer uses the type field to cast the payload to the correct generated class.

title: Q: [Q1] What is Docker, and how does it differ from a virtual machine?
text: [Q1] What is Docker, and how does it differ from a virtual machine? Docker is a containerization platform that packages an application with its dependencies so it runs consistently across environments. A Docker container shares the host operating system kernel and runs as an isolated process using namespaces and cgroups. A virtual machine, in contrast, emulates an entire computer with its own guest operating system, kernel, and virtual hardware. Containers are lighter and start faster than VMs because they do not boot a full OS. VMs provide stronger isolation but consume more resources. For backend developers, Docker solves the it works on my machine problem. For DevOps engineers, Docker standardizes build, test, and deployment environments across laptops, CI/CD, and production.

title: Q: [Q2] What is the difference between a Docker image and a Docker container?
text: [Q2] What is the difference between a Docker image and a Docker container? A Docker image is a read-only template that contains the application code, runtime, libraries, environment variables, and configuration needed to run an application. It is built from a Docker file and stored in layers. A Docker container is a running instance of an image. It adds a writable layer on top of the image and executes the process defined by CMD or ENTRYPOINT. Multiple containers can be created from the same image. Images are immutable and versioned, while containers are ephemeral by default and their local changes disappear when removed.

title: Q: [Q3] What happens when you run docker run ?
text: [Q3] What happens when you run docker run ? The Docker client sends the command to the Docker daemon. The daemon checks if the image exists locally and pulls it from the configured registry if it does not. It then creates a writable container layer over the image, sets up isolated namespaces for process, network, mounts, and other resources, assigns an IP address on the default bridge network, executes the command specified by CMD or ENTRYPOINT, and starts the main process as PID 1. If port mappings are specified with -p, the daemon configures iptables rules to forward traffic from the host port to the container port.

title: Q: [Q4] What are Docker namespaces and cgroups?
text: [Q4] What are Docker namespaces and cgroups? Namespaces provide process isolation by giving each container its own view of system resources. Docker uses PID namespaces for process isolation, NET namespaces for network interfaces, MNT namespaces for lesystem mounts, UTS namespaces for hostnames, IPC namespaces for shared memory, and USER namespaces for user and group IDs. Cgroups, or control groups, limit and account for resource usage such as CPU, memory, disk I/O, and network bandwidth. Namespaces keep containers separate, while cgroups prevent a single container from consuming all host resources. Together they make containers lightweight, isolated, and resource-controlled.

title: Q: [Q5] What is a Docker file, and what are the most common instructions?
text: [Q5] What is a Docker file, and what are the most common instructions? A Docker file is a text file that defines how to build a Docker image. Common instructions include FROM to set the base image, RUN to execute commands during the build, COPY to copy files from the build context, ADD to copy and optionally extract archives or fetch URLs, WORKDIR to set the working directory, ENV to set environment variables, ARG to define build-time variables, EXPOSE to document ports, CMD to set the default command, ENTRYPOINT to set the main executable, and USER to switch to a non-root user. Best practice is to order instructions from least frequently changed to most frequently changed to maximize layer caching.

title: Q: [Q6] What is the difference between CMD and ENTRYPOINT in a Docker file?
text: [Q6] What is the difference between CMD and ENTRYPOINT in a Docker file? ENTRYPOINT defines the executable that runs when a container starts, while CMD provides default arguments to that executable. If both are present, CMD arguments are appended to ENTRYPOINT. If the user passes arguments to docker run, they override CMD but are appended to ENTRYPOINT unless --entrypoint is used. A common pattern is ENTRYPOINT ["java", "- jar"] and CMD ["app.jar"]. Using the exec form, which is JSON syntax, avoids shell interpretation and allows proper signal handling. For backend services, ENTRYPOINT is useful when you want to enforce the runtime, and CMD is useful for providing default JVM options or profiles.

title: Q: [Q7] What is the difference between COPY and ADD in a Docker file?
text: [Q7] What is the difference between COPY and ADD in a Docker file? COPY copies files or directories from the build context into the image. ADD does the same but can also automatically extract tar archives and fetch remote URLs. Because ADD has hidden behaviors, Docker best practice recommends using COPY for most cases to make the Docker file predictable and easier to understand. ADD should only be used when you genuinely need local tar extraction or remote file download. For Java applications, COPY is the standard choice for JAR files and configuration.

title: Q: [Q8] How does Docker layer caching work, and why does instruction order matter?
text: [Q8] How does Docker layer caching work, and why does instruction order matter? Docker builds images in layers, and each instruction creates a new layer. If the build context for a layer has not changed, Docker reuses the cached layer instead of rebuilding it. Instruction order matters because once a layer changes, every layer after it must be rebuilt. To maximize caching, you should place instructions that change rarely at the top of the Docker file and instructions that change frequently at the bottom. For example, copy the Maven pom.xml and download dependencies before copying the rest of the source code, so dependency resolution is cached across builds.

title: Q: [Q9] What is a multi-stage build, and why is it useful?
text: [Q9] What is a multi-stage build, and why is it useful? A multi-stage build uses multiple FROM instructions in a single Docker file to separate the build environment from the runtime environment. For example, a Java application can be compiled and packaged in a Maven image, then only the resulting JAR is copied into a smaller runtime image such as Eclipse Temurin JRE or a distroless image. This reduces final image size, removes build tools and source code from production images, reduces attack surface, and improves deployment speed. Backend and DevOps teams use multi-stage builds to keep production images minimal and secure.

title: Q: [Q10] What are Docker volumes and bind mounts, and when do you use each?
text: [Q10] What are Docker volumes and bind mounts, and when do you use each? A volume is a managed storage area managed by Docker, usually stored under /var/lib/docker/volumes/, and identified by a name. Volumes are portable, easy to back up, and work well for persistent data such as database files. A bind mount maps a specific host file or directory into the container using an absolute path. Bind mounts are useful for development when you want source code or configuration files on the host to re ect immediately inside the container. In production, named volumes are preferred because they do not depend on host path layout and are managed by Docker or orchestrators. Bind mounts can introduce host coupling and permission issues.

title: Q: [Q11] What is Docker Compose, and when should you use it?
text: [Q11] What is Docker Compose, and when should you use it? Docker Compose is a tool for de ning and running multi-container applications using a YAML file, typically docker-compose.yml. It allows you to define services, networks, volumes, environment variables, ports, and dependencies in a declarative format. Compose is ideal for local development, integration testing, and small multi-service deployments. For production orchestration at scale, Kubernetes or Docker Swarm is usually more appropriate. Backend developers use Compose to run an application alongside its database, cache, and message broker locally with a single command. DevOps engineers use it for CI pipelines and staging environments.

title: Q: [Q12] What is the difference between Docker Compose ports and  expose?
text: [Q12] What is the difference between Docker Compose ports and expose? ports maps a host port to a container port, making the service accessible from outside the Docker network, for example 8080:8080. expose only documents that a container listens on a port and makes it available to other services on the same Docker network, but does not publish it to the host. In Compose, expose is mainly informational. For backend services, ports is used when developers or load balancers need to reach the container from the host, while expose is used for internal service- to-service communication.

title: Q: [Q13] What is the depends_on limitation in Docker Compose, and how do you handle readiness?
text: [Q13] What is the depends_on limitation in Docker Compose, and how do you handle readiness? depends_on only guarantees container start order, not service readiness. A database container may start before the application, but the database may still be initializing when the application tries to connect. To handle readiness, you should configure the application to retry connections with exponential backoff, or use a startup probe wrapper script that waits for dependencies. Docker Compose also supports condition: service_healthy or condition: service_completed_successfully with health checks in newer versions. In production, readiness checks and retry logic are more reliable than startup ordering alone.

title: Q: [Q14] What are Docker bridge, host, and overlay networks?
text: [Q14] What are Docker bridge, host, and overlay networks? Bridge is the default network driver that creates a private internal network on a single host and allows containers to communicate using DNS names or IP addresses. Host mode removes network isolation and uses the host network stack directly, which can improve performance but reduces isolation. Overlay networks connect containers across multiple Docker hosts, commonly used in Docker Swarm or overlay plugins. For backend development on a single machine, bridge networks provide sufficient isolation and service discovery. In production multi-host clusters, overlay or Kubernetes networking is used.

title: Q: [Q15] How do you persist data for a database running in a Docker container?
text: [Q15] How do you persist data for a database running in a Docker container? You use a named volume or bind mount to store database files outside the container's writable layer. For example, a PostgreSQL container maps /var/lib/postgresql/data to a named volume. If the container is removed, the data remains in the volume and a new container can mount it. You should also set the correct ownership and permissions for the database user, especially when using bind mounts. Backing up volumes and testing restore procedures is essential for production. For DevOps, this means including volume backup and disaster recovery in the operational runbook.

title: Q: [Q16] How do you run a Docker container as a non-root user?
text: [Q16] How do you run a Docker container as a non-root user? You create a dedicated user in the Docker file using RUN groupadd and useradd, switch to that user with USER, and ensure files and directories the application needs are writable by that user. Running as root inside a container increases security risk because an attacker who escapes the container gains root privileges on the host. For Java applications, the base image may already provide a non-root user such as appuser, or you can add one. You also verify that port binding does not require root by using non-privileged ports such as 8080 instead of 80.

title: Q: [Q17] What are Docker health checks, and how do you configure them?
text: [Q17] What are Docker health checks, and how do you configure them? A health check is a command Docker runs periodically to determine if a container is healthy. You can define it in the Docker file with HEALTHCHECK or in Docker Compose with the healthcheck section. For a Spring Boot application, a common health check is curl -f http://localhost:8080/actuator/health. Docker marks the container as healthy, starting, or unhealthy based on the exit code. Orchestrators use health status for readiness probes and restart decisions. Health checks should be lightweight and should not overload the application or dependencies.

title: Q: [Q18] What is the difference between docker stop and docker  kill?
text: [Q18] What is the difference between docker stop and docker kill? docker stop sends a SIGTERM signal to the main process and waits for a grace period, default 10 seconds, before sending SIGKILL. This allows the application to shut down gracefully by closing connections, ushing buffers, and completing in- ight requests. docker kill sends SIGKILL immediately, forcing the process to terminate without cleanup. For backend services, graceful shutdown is important to avoid data loss and incomplete requests. The Docker file should use the exec form of ENTRYPOINT so the application receives signals directly as PID 1.

title: Q: [Q19] How do you pass secrets to a Docker container securely?
text: [Q19] How do you pass secrets to a Docker container securely? You should avoid hard-coding secrets in images or environment variables in plain text. Secure options include using Docker secrets if running on Docker Swarm, mounting secrets through BuildKit - -secret during build, using environment files with restricted permissions at runtime, or integrating with external secret managers such as HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or Azure Key Vault. In Kubernetes, secrets are stored as Secret resources and mounted as files or environment variables. The principle is to keep secrets out of image layers and build logs, rotate them regularly, and limit access to authorized users and processes.

title: Q: [Q20] What is the difference between Docker Swarm and Kubernetes?
text: [Q20] What is the difference between Docker Swarm and Kubernetes? Docker Swarm is Docker's native clustering and orchestration tool. It is simpler to set up and uses Docker Compose files for service definitions. Kubernetes is a more powerful and widely adopted orchestration platform with a richer feature set for service discovery, scaling, self-healing, networking, storage, and rolling updates. Kubernetes has a steeper learning curve but is the de facto standard for production container orchestration. For small teams or simple deployments, Swarm may be sufficient. For enterprise microservices, Kubernetes is usually preferred. Both can manage multi-container applications, but Kubernetes offers more ecosystem support and vendor integration.

title: Q: [Q21] What is .dockerignore, and why is it important?
text: [Q21] What is .dockerignore, and why is it important? .dockerignore is a file that tells Docker which files and directories to exclude from the build context. Excluding files such as target/, .git/, IDE directories, local configuration, and sensitive files reduces the build context size, speeds up image builds, and prevents accidental inclusion of secrets or unnecessary files in the image. It also improves cache efficiency because changes to ignored files do not invalidate build cache. Backend developers should keep .dockerignore up to date with build artifacts and source-control metadata.

title: Q: [Q22] How do you reduce the size of a Docker image?
text: [Q22] How do you reduce the size of a Docker image? I use a smaller base image such as Alpine Linux, Distroless, or Eclipse Temurin JRE instead of a full JDK. I use multi-stage builds to keep only the compiled artifact in the final image. I minimize the number of layers by combining related RUN commands. I clean package caches within the same RUN instruction. I use .dockerignore to avoid copying unnecessary files. I remove build tools, compilers, and shells from production images when possible. I also avoid installing recommended packages unless needed. Smaller images reduce attack surface, startup time, storage cost, and network transfer time during deployments.

title: Q: [Q23] What are container restart policies, and when should you use each?
text: [Q23] What are container restart policies, and when should you use each? Docker supports restart policies that define what happens when a container exits. no means the container does not restart automatically. always restarts the container regardless of exit code. unless-stopped restarts unless the container was manually stopped. on-failure restarts only if the container exited with a non-zero code, optionally limited to a maximum retry count. For backend services, unless-stopped or on-failure are common choices. on-failure is useful when you want to retry after application crashes but not after a clean shutdown. Restart policies help improve availability but should be combined with proper logging and alerting.

title: Q: [Q24] How do you build a Docker image from a Spring Boot application?
text: [Q24] How do you build a Docker image from a Spring Boot application? You create a Docker file in the project root. A typical approach uses a multi-stage build. The first stage uses a Maven or Gradle image to build the application and produce a JAR file. The second stage copies the JAR into a lightweight JRE image such as eclipse-temurin:17-jre-alpine and sets the entrypoint to run the JAR. Alternatively, Spring Boot 2.3 and later support Cloud Native Buildpacks through the Maven spring-boot:build-image goal, which builds an optimized image without writing a Docker file. You can also use the dockerfile-maven-plugin or jib-maven- plugin to build and push images from Maven.

title: Q: [S1] A Spring Boot container builds successfully but exits immediately with exit code 1. How do you debug it?
text: [S1] A Spring Boot container builds successfully but exits immediately with exit code 1. How do you debug it? I run docker ps -a to find the exited container and then docker logs to read the application output. Exit code 1 usually indicates the application failed to start, often because of a missing database connection, invalid configuration, or a missing profile. I check whether environment variables expected by Spring Boot are passed correctly, whether the JAR path in ENTRYPOINT is correct, and whether the active profile matches the running environment. I also run the container interactively with docker run -it --rm --entrypoint sh to inspect files and run the JAR manually if needed. Real-time example: a developer builds an order service image and runs it in a Compose stack. The container exits immediately. Logs show Failed to configure a DataSource: 'url' attribute is not specified. The SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL environment variable was defined in a .env file that was not loaded because the file was named .env.local. I rename the file, verify with docker compose config, and the container starts successfully.

title: Q: [S2] A container starts but the application is unreachable on the mapped host port. What do you check?
text: [S2] A container starts but the application is unreachable on the mapped host port. What do you check? I first verify that the port mapping was specified correctly in docker run or Compose, for example -p 8080:8080. I check docker ps to see if the port is actually published. I confirm the application inside the container is listening on 0.0.0.0 and not only 127.0.0.1, because binding to localhost inside a container makes the port unreachable from the host. I check rewall rules, SELinux, and whether another process is already using the host port. I also look at application logs for startup errors and use docker exec to run netstat or curl inside the container. Real-time example: a Node.js service binds to 127.0.0.1:3000 and the Docker file exposes port 3000. The container runs but curl http://localhost:3000 from the host returns connection refused. I change the application to bind to 0.0.0.0 and the port becomes reachable.

title: Q: [S3] A Docker build fails with COPY failed: file not found in  build context. What is the cause?
text: [S3] A Docker build fails with COPY failed: file not found in build context. What is the cause? The COPY instruction references a file or directory that does not exist in the build context, or it is excluded by .dockerignore. I verify the path is relative to the build context root, not the Docker file directory if they differ. I check that the file was generated before the build, especially JAR files in target/ or build/libs/. I also inspect .dockerignore to make sure the file is not ignored. If the file is generated by a previous build step, I ensure the build command includes the correct context path. Real-time example: a CI pipeline runs docker build -t app . without first running mvn package. The Docker file copies target/app.jar, but the directory is empty. I add mvn package before the Docker build step and the build succeeds.

title: Q: [S4] A Java application container exits with exit code 137. What does it mean, and how do you fix it?
text: [S4] A Java application container exits with exit code 137. What does it mean, and how do you fix it? Exit code 137 means the container received SIGKILL, almost always because it exceeded its memory limit and was Out-Of-Memory killed by the kernel. I check docker inspect for OOMKilled status and review memory settings with docker stats. To fix it, I either increase the memory limit or reduce the application's heap size. For JVM applications, I set -Xmx to a value well below the container memory limit to leave room for off-heap memory, native libraries, and the JVM itself. I also investigate memory leaks if usage grows continuously. Real-time example: a Spring Boot container is limited to 512 MB but the JVM uses -Xmx1g. The container is OOM-killed under load. I change the Docker file to use -Xmx384m and raise the container limit to 768 MB, then verify with load testing that the application stays stable.

title: Q: [S5] A container cannot resolve the hostname of another container in Docker Compose. What do you check?
text: [S5] A container cannot resolve the hostname of another container in Docker Compose. What do you check? I check whether both containers are on the same custom network. Containers on the default bridge network cannot resolve each other by name; only custom networks provide embedded DNS. In Docker Compose, all services in the same Compose project are automatically connected to a default custom network, so the issue is usually a typo in the service name or using localhost instead of the service name. I verify the service name in docker-compose.yml, check that both services are running with docker compose ps, and test resolution with docker exec and nslookup or ping. Real-time example: an application service connects to localhost:5432 expecting the Postgres container. Because each container has its own localhost, the connection fails. I change the connection string to postgres:5432, which resolves through the Compose network, and the application connects successfully.

title: Q: [S6] A Docker Compose service depends on a database, but the application crashes before the database is ready. How do you fix it?
text: [S6] A Docker Compose service depends on a database, but the application crashes before the database is ready. How do you fix it? I implement application-level retry logic for database connections instead of relying solely on depends_on. I configure the connection pool to retry with exponential backoff during startup, or use a small wait-for-it script that checks database readiness before starting the application. In newer Docker Compose versions, I use depends_on with condition: service_healthy and define a health check on the database service. The health check ensures the database is truly accepting connections before the dependent service starts. I avoid naive sleep scripts because they make startup slow and unreliable. Real-time example: a Spring Boot application starts before Postgres nishes initialization. I add a healthcheck to the Postgres service using pg_isready -U appuser -d orders, set the app service depends_on with condition: service_healthy, and configure the connection pool with a 30-second acquisition retry. The stack now starts reliably.

title: Q: [S7] A Docker image contains a large layer from COPY . . because of unnecessary files. How do you optimize it?
text: [S7] A Docker image contains a large layer from COPY . . because of unnecessary files. How do you optimize it? I create or update .dockerignore to exclude files that are not needed in the image. This includes build output directories like target/ and build/, version control directories like .git/, IDE files like .idea/, local environment files, test resources, and logs. Excluding these reduces the build context, speeds up the build, and prevents sensitive or irrelevant files from being copied. I also order Docker file instructions so that dependency resolution happens before source code changes, maximizing layer caching. Real-time example: a 2 GB build context slows every build because it includes a local .git directory and test data files. After adding those paths to .dockerignore, the build context drops to 30 MB, build time decreases by 80 percent, and the final image no longer contains source code.

title: Q: [S8] A production container needs to read secrets. Why should you not use environment variables, and what do you use instead?
text: [S8] A production container needs to read secrets. Why should you not use environment variables, and what do you use instead? Environment variables are often logged by orchestrators, debugging tools, and crash dumps. They can be exposed through docker inspect, process listings, or CI logs. Instead, I use Docker secrets for Swarm, Kubernetes Secrets, or a secret manager that mounts secrets as files into the container. For build-time secrets, I use BuildKit secret mounts so secrets never become part of the image layer. At runtime, the application reads secrets from files in a mounted directory or fetches them from Vault or a cloud secret manager. This keeps secrets out of image layers, process environments, and version control. Real-time example: a developer passes DATABASE_PASSWORD=secret123 in the Compose file and checks it into Git. I replace it with a Docker secret mounted at /run/secrets/db_password, update the application to read from the file, remove the variable from the Compose file, and rotate the leaked password immediately.

title: Q: [S9] A multi-stage Docker file copies dependencies in every build even when they did not change. How do you fix it?
text: [S9] A multi-stage Docker file copies dependencies in every build even when they did not change. How do you fix it? I copy only the dependency descriptor files first, resolve dependencies, then copy the source code. For Maven, I copy pom.xml and any parent POMs, run mvn dependency:go-offline or mvn install -DskipTests, and then copy the source. For Gradle, I copy build.gradle, settings.gradle, and the Gradle wrapper first. Because Docker caches layers based on file changes, dependency resolution is cached until the descriptor files change. Source code changes only invalidate the later layers. Real-time example: a Docker file starts with COPY . . and runs mvn package. Every code change re-downloads all Maven dependencies. I restructure the Docker file to copy pom.xml first, run mvn dependency:resolve, then copy src/. Builds now reuse the dependency layer and complete in seconds after small code changes.

title: Q: [S10] A Spring Boot container logs to a file inside the container, but logs are lost when the container restarts. How do you persist them?
text: [S10] A Spring Boot container logs to a file inside the container, but logs are lost when the container restarts. How do you persist them? I map the log directory to a named volume or bind mount on the host so logs survive container restarts. Better yet, I configure the application to log to standard output and let Docker capture the logs, then centralize logs with a log shipper such as Filebeat, Fluentd, or the container orchestrator's logging driver. Logging to stdout is the Twelve-Factor App recommendation and simpli es log collection, rotation, and aggregation. If file logging is required, I ensure the volume is backed up and rotated to avoid lling the disk. Real-time example: an application writes logs to /app/logs/application.log. After a crash, the container restarts and the logs are gone, making it impossible to diagnose. I change the logging configuration to output JSON to stdout, configure Docker's json-file log driver with rotation, and ship logs to Elasticsearch with Filebeat. Logs are now searchable and retained.

title: Q: [S11] A container running as non-root cannot write to a bind-mounted directory. Why?
text: [S11] A container running as non-root cannot write to a bind-mounted directory. Why? Bind mounts preserve the user and group ownership from the host. If the container user has a different user ID or group ID than the owner of the host directory, write permission is denied. I fix this by ensuring the container user ID matches the host directory owner, by setting appropriate host permissions, or by using a named volume which is managed by Docker and usually owned by the container user. In development, matching UIDs is common. In production, named volumes are preferred to avoid host coupling. For Linux, SELinux may also block access unless the mount is relabeled with :z or :Z. Real-time example: a Spring Boot container runs as UID 1000 but the host bind mount /opt/data is owned by UID 1001. The application fails to write uploaded files. I create a host user with UID 1000, chown the directory, and the writes succeed.

title: Q: [S12] A Docker image size is over 1 GB for a simple Spring Boot application. How do you reduce it?
text: [S12] A Docker image size is over 1 GB for a simple Spring Boot application. How do you reduce it? I switch from a full JDK base image to a JRE or distroless image. I use a multi-stage build so only the JAR and runtime dependencies remain in the final image. I remove build tools, shells, and package managers from the production image. I combine RUN commands to reduce layers and clean package caches in the same layer they are created. I also use .dockerignore to avoid copying unnecessary files. For example, replacing eclipse-temurin:17-jdk with eclipse- temurin:17-jre-alpine and using a distroless final stage can reduce a 1 GB image to under 200 MB. Real-time example: a team ships a 1.2 GB image that includes Maven, Gradle, source code, and a full JDK. I restructure the Docker file to build in a Maven stage and copy the JAR into eclipse- temurin:17-jre-alpine. The final image shrinks to 180 MB, deployment time drops, and the attack surface is reduced.

title: Q: [S13] A containerized application does not shut down gracefully when  docker stop is issued. How do you fix it?
text: [S13] A containerized application does not shut down gracefully when docker stop is issued. How do you fix it? I ensure the main application process runs as PID 1 and receives signals directly. This means using the exec form of ENTRYPOINT such as ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-jar", "app.jar"] instead of the shell form, which runs the command as a child of a shell process that may not forward signals. I configure the application to handle SIGTERM by closing resources, draining connections, and stopping the server. I also verify that the stop timeout is sufficient for graceful shutdown. Real-time example: a Spring Boot container uses shell-form ENTRYPOINT java -jar app.jar. On docker stop, the shell ignores SIGTERM and Docker waits 10 seconds before sending SIGKILL, causing in- ight requests to abort. I change to exec form, add graceful shutdown in Spring Boot with server.shutdown=graceful, and extend the stop grace period to 30 seconds. The application now nishes active requests before exiting.

title: Q: [S14] A Docker Compose stack works on one machine but fails on another. How do you troubleshoot?
text: [S14] A Docker Compose stack works on one machine but fails on another. How do you troubleshoot? I compare Docker versions, Compose file syntax versions, and platform differences such as Linux versus Windows containers. I check for environment variables or .env files that differ between machines. I verify that the other machine has sufficient resources, including memory, disk space, and CPU. I inspect for port con icts where a service tries to bind to a host port already in use. I also check for differences in file permissions, line endings if using bind mounts, and the presence of required secrets or certificates. Running docker compose config expands the file and reveals substitution and parsing issues. Real-time example: a stack runs on a developer's Mac but fails on a CI runner. The CI runner has an older Docker Compose version that does not support the profiles syntax used in the file. I either update the runner or remove the unsupported syntax, and the stack starts consistently.

title: Q: [S15] A microservice architecture with  ve services needs to run locally for development. How do you Dockerize it?
text: [S15] A microservice architecture with ve services needs to run locally for development. How do you Dockerize it? I create a docker-compose.yml that defines each service with its build context or image, ports, environment variables, and dependencies. Each service has its own Docker file, typically a multi- stage build producing a small production-ready image. I add a shared custom network so services can communicate by name. I include supporting infrastructure such as a database, message broker, and cache as services in the Compose file or as named volumes for data persistence. I use .env files for environment-specific configuration and avoid hard-coding secrets. I define health checks and readiness dependencies so the stack starts reliably. Real-time example: an e-commerce platform has gateway, order, payment, inventory, and notification services plus Postgres, Kafka, and Redis. I create a Compose file that builds each service from its Docker file, exposes the gateway on port 8080, connects everything to a backend network, and uses health checks to ensure Postgres and Kafka are ready before services start. Developers run docker compose up and the entire stack is available locally.

title: Q: [S16] A container registry scan reports a critical vulnerability in a base image. How do you respond?
text: [S16] A container registry scan reports a critical vulnerability in a base image. How do you respond? I first verify the severity and whether the vulnerable component is actually used by the application. If the vulnerability affects a library or package used by the runtime, I update the base image to the latest patched version. I rebuild the application image, run tests, and push the new image to the registry. I also update the CI pipeline to use the patched base image and schedule regular scans. For false positives, I document the exception. If the vulnerable package is unnecessary, I remove it or use a minimal distroless image to reduce attack surface. Real-time example: a scan finds a critical OpenSSL vulnerability in the base image. I update FROM eclipse-temurin:17-jre-alpine to the latest digest, rebuild all dependent images, deploy through CI/CD, and configure the registry to block future builds that contain the old base image digest.

title: Q: [S17] A docker push to a private registry fails with authentication error. What do you check?
text: [S17] A docker push to a private registry fails with authentication error. What do you check? I check that I am logged in to the registry using docker login with valid credentials. I verify the registry URL in the image tag matches the login target. I check whether the token or password has expired, especially for cloud registries that use short-lived tokens. I review permissions to ensure the user or service principal has push access to the repository. If running in CI, I verify that the credentials are correctly passed as secrets and not masked incorrectly. For Amazon ECR, I also check that the token was regenerated before the push because ECR tokens expire after 12 hours. Real-time example: a CI pipeline pushes to a GitHub Container Registry and fails with denied: requested access to the resource is denied. The personal access token used in docker login was revoked. I generate a new token, update the CI secret, and the push succeeds.

title: Q: [S18] A container is consuming too much CPU and slowing down the host. How do you investigate and limit it?
text: [S18] A container is consuming too much CPU and slowing down the host. How do you investigate and limit it? I use docker stats to identify the container with high CPU usage. I inspect the application logs and metrics to find the cause, such as an in nite loop, ine cient algorithm, or unexpected load. I limit CPU usage with --cpus or deploy.resources.limits.cpus in Compose to prevent the container from starving other services. For long-term fixes, I optimize the application code, add caching, or scale horizontally. I also set memory limits to prevent CPU issues from turning into OOM kills. Real-time example: a container running a PDF generation service spikes to 400 percent CPU and affects other services. I add --cpus=2 to cap it, add metrics to identify the slow PDF operation, and refactor the code to stream processing. CPU usage stabilizes and other services recover.

title: Q: [S19] A database container with a named volume needs to be moved to a new host. How do you migrate the data?
text: [S19] A database container with a named volume needs to be moved to a new host. How do you migrate the data? I stop the container, create a backup of the volume using docker run --rm -v myvolume:/data -v $(pwd):/backup alpine tar czf /backup/volume.tar.gz -C /data ., transfer the archive to the new host, and restore it with docker run --rm -v myvolume:/data -v $(pwd):/backup alpine sh -c "cd /data && tar xzf /backup/volume.tar.gz". I then start the new container with the restored named volume. For large databases, I prefer database-native backup tools such as pg_dump or mysqldump because they are more reliable and consistent. I also verify the restored data by running health checks and a few application queries. Real-time example: a Postgres container on a developer laptop needs to move to a staging server. I use pg_dump to create a consistent dump, copy it to the server, create the new volume, restore with pg_restore, and start the Compose stack. The application starts and data is intact.

title: Q: [S20] A team wants zero-downtime deployment for a containerized Spring Boot service. How do you design it?
text: [S20] A team wants zero-downtime deployment for a containerized Spring Boot service. How do you design it? For Docker Compose or single-host setups, I use rolling updates by starting new containers behind a reverse proxy or load balancer, waiting for health checks to pass, and then stopping old containers. For orchestrated environments, I use Kubernetes rolling updates with readiness probes and graceful termination. The key requirements are multiple running instances, a load balancer that routes only to healthy instances, and proper SIGTERM handling so existing requests complete. I also use blue- green deployments for critical services by switching traffic between two identical environments. Image tags should be immutable, such as git commit SHA or semantic version, not latest. Real-time example: a payment service runs as a Kubernetes Deployment with three replicas. I release a new version by updating the Deployment image tag. Kubernetes creates new pods, waits for readiness probes to succeed, gradually shifts traffic, and terminates old pods with a 30-second grace period. No requests are dropped because the application handles SIGTERM gracefully and Kubernetes only removes pods after the readiness endpoint marks new pods healthy.

title: Q: [S21] A container needs to access host services such as a local database. What networking options are available?
text: [S21] A container needs to access host services such as a local database. What networking options are available? On Docker Desktop, host.docker.internal resolves to the host from inside a container. On Linux, this requires extrafficonfiguration or using the host network mode with --network host, which removes container network isolation. Another option is to connect the container and the host service to a common bridge network if the host service also runs in a container. For production, services should not depend on the host network. For local development, host.docker.internal is the most portable approach when supported. I prefer to run dependencies in containers and use Compose networks rather than relying on the host. Real-time example: a developer runs a local Postgres on the laptop and wants a containerized service to connect to it. On Docker Desktop, I configure the service connection string to use host.docker.internal:5432 and the application connects successfully. On a Linux CI runner, I instead add a Postgres service container to the same network as the application.

title: Q: [Q1] What is Kubernetes, and why is it used?
text: [Q1] What is Kubernetes, and why is it used? Kubernetes is an open-source container orchestration platform that automates the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. It provides a declarative API for de ning desired application state and continuously works to maintain that state. Kubernetes is used because it abstracts away infrastructure complexity, enables self-healing through pod rescheduling, supports rolling updates and rollbacks, and provides service discovery, load balancing, storage orchestration, and secrets management. For backend developers, it standardizes how applications run across environments. For DevOps engineers, it provides the control plane for running production workloads at scale.

title: Q: [Q2] What is a Kubernetes cluster, and what are its main components?
text: [Q2] What is a Kubernetes cluster, and what are its main components? A Kubernetes cluster consists of a control plane and worker nodes. The control plane includes the API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager, and optionally the cloud controller manager. The API server is the entry point for all commands and state changes. etcd is the distributed key-value store for cluster state. The scheduler assigns pods to nodes based on resources and constraints. The controller manager runs controllers that regulate the state of Deployments, ReplicaSets, Services, and other objects. Worker nodes run the kubelet, kube-proxy, and container runtime. The kubelet ensures containers run as specified in pod definitions, while kube-proxy handles network routing and load balancing for Services.

title: Q: [Q3] What is a Pod in Kubernetes?
text: [Q3] What is a Pod in Kubernetes? A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. It represents one or more containers that share the same network namespace, IP address, storage volumes, and lifecycle. Containers within a Pod can communicate via localhost. Pods are ephemeral by design; their IP addresses change when they are recreated. Higher-level controllers such as Deployments, ReplicaSets, StatefulSets, and DaemonSets manage Pods to provide scaling, self-healing, and ordered lifecycle semantics. A backend developer typically does not create Pods directly but instead defines Deployments that create them.

title: Q: [Q4] What is the difference between a Pod and a container?
text: [Q4] What is the difference between a Pod and a container? A container is a single process runtime instance with its own isolated lesystem and resources. A Pod is a Kubernetes abstraction that wraps one or more containers and provides shared networking and storage. A Pod gives Kubernetes a higher-level unit to schedule, manage, and scale. Containers inside a Pod share the same network interface and can mount the same volumes. A Pod also carries metadata, labels, and lifecycle policies that a bare container does not. In practice, most application Pods contain one main container and sometimes sidecar containers for logging, proxying, or configuration.

title: Q: [Q5] What is a ReplicaSet?
text: [Q5] What is a ReplicaSet? A ReplicaSet is a Kubernetes controller that ensures a specified number of identical Pod replicas are running at any time. It uses a label selector to identify which Pods it manages and creates or deletes Pods to match the desired replica count. ReplicaSets provide self-healing by replacing failed Pods and horizontal scaling by adjusting replica count. However, users usually work with Deployments rather than ReplicaSets directly because Deployments manage ReplicaSets and provide rolling update and rollback capabilities.

title: Q: [Q6] What is a Deployment in Kubernetes?
text: [Q6] What is a Deployment in Kubernetes? A Deployment is a higher-level controller that manages a ReplicaSet and provides declarative updates for Pods. It is used for stateless applications. A Deployment allows you to define the desired state, including the container image, replica count, strategy, and update parameters. It supports rolling updates that replace old Pods gradually, and it keeps a revision history so you can roll back to a previous version. Deployments are the most common workload object for backend microservices because they handle availability during updates automatically.

title: Q: [Q7] What is the difference between a Deployment and a StatefulSet?
text: [Q7] What is the difference between a Deployment and a StatefulSet? A Deployment manages stateless Pods that are interchangeable. It supports arbitrary scaling and does not guarantee pod identity or ordering. A StatefulSet is designed for stateful applications that require stable network identity, stable persistent storage, and ordered deployment, scaling, and termination. StatefulSet Pods have predictable names with ordinal indexes such as web-0, web-1, and web-2. Each Pod can have its own PersistentVolumeClaim via a volumeClaimTemplate. Deployments are ideal for REST services, while StatefulSets are used for databases, message queues, and distributed stateful systems.

title: Q: [Q8] What is a DaemonSet?
text: [Q8] What is a DaemonSet? A DaemonSet ensures that one Pod runs on each selected node in the cluster. It is used for node-level infrastructure services such as log collectors, monitoring agents, node exporters, and network proxies. When nodes are added, DaemonSet pods are automatically scheduled on them. When nodes are removed, the associated Pods are garbage collected. Unlike Deployments, DaemonSets are not scaled by replica count because their scale is determined by the number of nodes.

title: Q: [Q9] What is a Kubernetes Namespace?
text: [Q9] What is a Kubernetes Namespace? A Namespace is a logical partition within a Kubernetes cluster that isolates groups of resources. Namespaces allow multiple teams or applications to share a cluster without name collisions. They are often used to separate environments such as development, staging, and production, or to isolate microservices by team. Resource quotas, limits, RBAC policies, and network policies can be applied per namespace. Objects such as Pods, Services, Con gMaps, and Secrets exist within a namespace, but cluster-wide resources such as Nodes and PersistentVolumes are not namespaced.

title: Q: [Q10] What is a Kubernetes Service, and why is it needed?
text: [Q10] What is a Kubernetes Service, and why is it needed? A Service exposes a set of Pods as a stable network endpoint. Because Pod IPs are ephemeral, clients cannot rely on individual Pod addresses. A Service provides a stable IP, DNS name, and load balancing across matching Pods. Services use label selectors to determine which Pods are members. The main service types are ClusterIP for internal access, NodePort for exposing on a static port on each node, LoadBalancer for cloud provider load balancers, and ExternalName for mapping to an external DNS name. Services are essential for microservices communication within a cluster.

title: Q: [Q11] How does a Kubernetes Service route traffic to Pods?
text: [Q11] How does a Kubernetes Service route traffic to Pods? A Service selects Pods using label selectors and maintains a list of endpoints through the Endpoints or EndpointSlice API. kube-proxy runs on every node and watches Service and EndpointSlice objects. It configures network rules, typically using iptables or IPVS, so that traffic to the Service IP is translated and load-balanced across the backend Pod IPs. ClusterDNS provides DNS records such as my-service.my-namespace.svc.cluster.local so clients can resolve Services by name.

title: Q: [Q12] What is a headless Service?
text: [Q12] What is a headless Service? A headless Service is a Service with clusterIP: None. It does not allocate a virtual IP or perform load balancing. Instead, DNS lookups return the individual Pod IPs directly. Headless Services are used when clients need to discover and communicate with specific Pods, such as in StatefulSets where each Pod has a stable hostname. For example, a headless Service for a StatefulSet allows DNS records like web-0.my-service.default.svc.cluster.local to resolve to the specific Pod.

title: Q: [Q13] What are the different Service types?
text: [Q13] What are the different Service types? The four main Service types are ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, and ExternalName. ClusterIP is the default and exposes the Service only within the cluster. NodePort exposes the Service on a static port of every node, making it reachable from outside the cluster through the node's IP. LoadBalancer provisions an external load balancer from a cloud provider and forwards traffic to NodePorts or directly to Pods. ExternalName maps the Service DNS name to an external DNS name and is used for integrating external services into cluster DNS.

title: Q: [Q14] What is a PersistentVolume and a PersistentVolumeClaim?
text: [Q14] What is a PersistentVolume and a PersistentVolumeClaim? A PersistentVolume is a cluster-level storage resource representing a piece of network- attached or cloud-backed storage. A PersistentVolumeClaim is a request for storage by a Pod or workload. Pods use PVCs to mount storage without knowing the underlying storage provider. Kubernetes binds a PVC to a compatible PV based on access mode, storage class, and capacity. With dynamic provisioning, a StorageClass automatically creates a PV when a PVC is submitted. PVCs are namespaced, while PVs are cluster-scoped.

title: Q: [Q15] What are StorageClasses and dynamic provisioning?
text: [Q15] What are StorageClasses and dynamic provisioning? A StorageClass defines the type of storage and the provisioner that creates it. Examples include AWS EBS, GCP Persistent Disk, Azure Disk, and NFS provisioners. When a PVC references a StorageClass, the provisioner automatically creates a matching PV and binds it to the PVC. Dynamic provisioning removes the need for administrators to manually create volumes. It also allows reclaim policies such as Delete or Retain to be defined, determining what happens to the PV when the PVC is deleted.

title: Q: [Q16] What are the access modes for PersistentVolumes?
text: [Q16] What are the access modes for PersistentVolumes? PersistentVolumes support three access modes. ReadWriteOnce means the volume can be mounted as read-write by a single node. ReadOnlyMany means many nodes can mount the volume read- only. ReadWriteMany means many nodes can mount the volume read-write simultaneously. The supported access modes depend on the underlying storage technology. Block storage such as EBS supports RWO, while shared lesystems such as NFS or EFS support RWX. Choosing the correct access mode is important for workloads that run on multiple nodes.

title: Q: [Q17] What is Minikube, and when is it used?
text: [Q17] What is Minikube, and when is it used? Minikube is a tool that creates a single-node Kubernetes cluster on a local machine for development and learning. It runs the control plane and worker components on the same node, either as a virtual machine, a container, or directly on the host. Minikube is used when developers need a local Kubernetes environment to test manifests, deploy applications, and learn Kubernetes concepts without needing a cloud cluster. It supports addons for ingress, DNS, storage, and dashboard. For production workloads, a managed or self-hosted multi-node cluster is required.

title: Q: [Q18] What is a Con gMap, and how is it used?
text: [Q18] What is a Con gMap, and how is it used? A Con gMap is a Kubernetes object for storing non-sensitive configuration data as key-value pairs. It decouples configuration from container images, allowing the same image to be used across environments. Con gMaps can be mounted as files in a volume, exposed as environment variables, or read by an application through the Kubernetes API. For backend developers, Con gMaps are useful for application settings such as feature ags, URLs, and logging levels. Sensitive data should not be stored in Con gMaps; use Secrets instead.

title: Q: [Q19] What is a Secret in Kubernetes?
text: [Q19] What is a Secret in Kubernetes? A Secret is a Kubernetes object for storing sensitive data such as passwords, tokens, and certificates. Secret values are base64-encoded but are not encrypted by default unless etcd encryption at rest is enabled. Secrets can be mounted as files or injected as environment variables. Best practice is to mount them as files so multiple values can be updated without recreating the Pod, and so secrets do not appear in process listings. For production, integration with external secret managers such as Vault or cloud secret stores is recommended.

title: Q: [Q20] What is a liveness probe, a readiness probe, and a startup probe?
text: [Q20] What is a liveness probe, a readiness probe, and a startup probe? A liveness probe determines whether the container is running. If it fails, Kubernetes restarts the container. A readiness probe determines whether the container is ready to accept traffic. If it fails, the Pod is removed from Service endpoints. A startup probe is used for slow-starting containers; while it is running, liveness and readiness checks are disabled. Once the startup probe succeeds, the other probes begin. Probes can be HTTP GET, TCP socket, gRPC, or exec commands. Proper probe configuration is critical for availability and graceful rollouts.

title: Q: [Q21] What is a rolling update in Kubernetes?
text: [Q21] What is a rolling update in Kubernetes? A rolling update is a strategy for replacing old Pods with new ones gradually without downtime. A Deployment creates a new ReplicaSet for the updated version and scales it up while scaling down the old ReplicaSet. The parameters maxSurge and maxUnavailable control how many extra Pods can exist and how many old Pods can be unavailable during the update. Kubernetes waits for readiness probes on new Pods before terminating old Pods, ensuring the Service always has healthy backends. Rolling updates are the default strategy for Deployments and are essential for zero-downtime deployments.

title: Q: [Q22] How do you roll back a Deployment in Kubernetes?
text: [Q22] How do you roll back a Deployment in Kubernetes? Kubernetes keeps a revision history for Deployments. To roll back, you run kubectl rollout undo deployment/ to revert to the previous revision. You can also specify a revision number with --to-revision. The Deployment then scales down the current Pods and scales up the Pods from the selected revision. Rollbacks are useful when a new release introduces errors or performance problems. It is important to set a reasonable revisionHistoryLimit so old ReplicaSets are retained for rollback.

title: Q: [Q23] What is a Horizontal Pod Autoscaler?
text: [Q23] What is a Horizontal Pod Autoscaler? The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler automatically adjusts the number of Pod replicas in a Deployment, ReplicaSet, or StatefulSet based on observed metrics such as CPU utilization, memory utilization, or custom metrics. It queries the Metrics API and compares current usage to target thresholds. If usage is above the target, it scales up. If usage is below the target for a sustained period, it scales down. HPA improves resource efficiency and handles traffic spikes automatically. It requires the Metrics Server or a custom metrics adapter to be installed in the cluster.

title: Q: [Q24] What is RBAC in Kubernetes?
text: [Q24] What is RBAC in Kubernetes? Role-Based Access Control in Kubernetes restricts access to cluster resources based on the roles of users, groups, or service accounts. RBAC uses Roles and ClusterRoles to define permissions, and RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings to assign those permissions to subjects. A Role applies within a namespace, while a ClusterRole applies cluster-wide. RBAC is essential for securing multi- tenant clusters and ensuring that applications and users have only the permissions they need. For example, a backend service account should only be allowed to read Con gMaps in its own namespace, not delete nodes.

title: Q: [S1] A Pod is stuck in CrashLoopBackOff. What is your troubleshooting work ow?
text: [S1] A Pod is stuck in CrashLoopBackOff. What is your troubleshooting work ow? I start with kubectl get pods -n to confirm the status and restart count. Then I run kubectl describe pod to inspect events, which often reveal the cause such as OOMKilled, liveness probe failure, or image pull errors. I read logs with kubectl logs for the current container and kubectl logs --previous for the last crashed instance. If logs are not enough, I use kubectl exec -it -- /bin/sh to inspect the running state, or temporarily add a debug init container. Common causes include missing configuration, invalid startup command, dependency not ready, or insufficient resources. Real-time example: a Spring Boot Pod restarts continuously with CrashLoopBackOff. kubectl describe shows Back-off restarting failed container. kubectl logs --previous reveals Caused by: java.lang.IllegalArgumentException: Could not resolve placeholder 'DB_HOST'. The Con gMap app-config was not mounted because of a typo in configMapRef. I correct the Con gMap name, apply the manifest, and the Pod starts.

title: Q: [S2] A Pod is in ImagePullBackOff. How do you resolve it?
text: [S2] A Pod is in ImagePullBackOff. How do you resolve it? ImagePullBackOff means Kubernetes cannot pull the container image. I check kubectl describe pod for the exact error, which may say manifest unknown, unauthorized, or pull access denied. I verify the image name and tag with docker pull from a local machine. If the registry is private, I create a docker-registry Secret and reference it in the Pod spec via imagePullSecrets, or attach it to the ServiceAccount. If the image tag is mutable such as latest, I switch to an immutable tag such as a Git commit SHA. I also check network connectivity and registry quotas. Real-time example: a CI pipeline pushes to a private registry but the production Pod fails with ImagePullBackOff. The Pod spec references imagePullSecrets: regcred, but the secret is in a different namespace. I create the same secret in the application namespace with kubectl create secret docker-registry -n prod regcred ..., and the image pulls successfully.

title: Q: [S3] A Pod is OOMKilled. What do you do?
