Each has an async variant, thenApplyAsync, thenComposeAsync, and thenAcceptAsync, which run the stage on a separate thread from the default ForkJoinPool.commonPool() or a supplied executor. Real-time example: fetching a user then mapping to a DTO is thenApply. Fetching a user then asynchronously fetching their orders is thenCompose. Sending a notification email after an order is placed is thenAccept.

title: Q: [Q17] How do handle, exceptionally, and whenComplete differ in  CompletableFuture?
text: [Q17] How do handle, exceptionally, and whenComplete differ in CompletableFuture? exceptionally is invoked only when an exception occurs in the previous stage. It receives the exception and returns a fallback value, allowing the pipeline to continue successfully. handle is invoked whether the previous stage succeeded or failed, receiving both the result and the exception. It can produce a new value or recover from errors, making it useful for unified success and error processing. whenComplete is a side-effect stage that observes the result and exception without changing them; it is useful for logging, metrics, or cleanup and returns the original result or rethrows the original exception. Use exceptionally for simple recovery, handle when you need to transform both outcomes, and whenComplete for non-transforming observation. Real-time example: a payment processing chain might use exceptionally(ex -> PaymentStatus.RETRY) to return a retry status. It might use handle((result, ex) -> ex != null ? logAndReturnFallback(ex) : result) to log failures and return a fallback. It might use whenComplete((result, ex) -> paymentMetrics.record(result, ex)) to record metrics without changing the flow.

title: Q: [Q18] What is task scheduling in Spring Boot, and what annotations are  used?
text: [Q18] What is task scheduling in Spring Boot, and what annotations are used? Spring Boot task scheduling allows methods to run periodically or at specific times using the @Scheduled annotation. The method is triggered by a TaskScheduler, which is auto- configured when scheduling is enabled. Common attributes include fixedDelay, which waits for the previous execution to finish plus the delay; fixedRate, which schedules executions at a fixed interval regardless of completion time; cron, which uses a cron expression for complex schedules; and initialDelay, which delays the first execution. The @EnableScheduling annotation is required on a configuration class to activate the scheduling infrastructure. By default, Spring Boot configures a single-threaded scheduler, which means one long-running task can delay others unless a custom thread pool is configured. Real-time example: a daily report generation service uses @Scheduled(cron = "0 0 1 * * *") on a method named generateDailyReport() so it runs at 1 AM every day. Another service uses @Scheduled(fixedRate = 60000) to poll an external API every minute for status updates.

title: Q: [Q19] What is the difference between @EnableScheduling and  @EnableAsync?
text: [Q19] What is the difference between @EnableScheduling and @EnableAsync? @EnableScheduling enables the scheduling infrastructure for @Scheduled methods, which run on threads managed by a TaskScheduler. It is used for periodic or time- based execution. @EnableAsync enables the asynchronous execution infrastructure for @Async methods, which run on threads managed by a TaskExecutor. It is used for background execution when a method is called. They serve fundamentally different purposes: scheduling triggers execution based on time, while async executes methods in the background when invoked by code. Both can be enabled together in the same application. Each uses a different executor type by default, and custom executors can be configured for either. Real-time example: an application has a @Scheduled method that runs every hour to clean up stale sessions, enabled by @EnableScheduling. It also has an @Async method that sends welcome emails when a user registers, enabled by @EnableAsync. The cleanup runs on a scheduler thread, while the email sending runs on an async executor thread when triggered by user registration.

title: Q: [Q20] What are the rules for using @Async in Spring Boot?
text: [Q20] What are the rules for using @Async in Spring Boot? @Async methods must be called from another bean because Spring uses proxies to intercept the call. Self-invocation from within the same class bypasses the proxy and runs synchronously. The method should return void for fire-and-forget tasks, Future for legacy compatibility, or CompletableFuture for modern async composition. Exceptions thrown in an @Async method are not propagated to the caller unless the caller inspects the returned Future or CompletableFuture. A custom executor should be configured for production because the default SimpleAsyncTaskExecutor creates a new thread for every task, which is not a true pool. The executor bean can be named and referenced via the @Async("beanName") qualifier. Real-time example: a notification service has @Async public CompletableFuture sendEmail(String to, String subject). A registration service injects the notification service and calls notificationService.sendEmail(user.getEmail(), "Welcome"). The proxy intercepts the call, runs it on the async executor, and the registration service continues immediately. If sendEmail were called from within the notification service itself, it would run synchronously.

title: Q: [Q21] How do you configure custom thread pools for @Async and  @Scheduled in Spring Boot?
text: [Q21] How do you configure custom thread pools for @Async and @Scheduled in Spring Boot? For @Async, create a ThreadPoolTaskExecutor bean. Name it applicationTaskExecutor to make it the default async executor, or give it a custom name and reference it with @Async("beanName"). For @Scheduled, create a ThreadPoolTaskScheduler bean or configure spring.task.scheduling.pool.size to set the scheduler pool size. A well-configured executor should specify corePoolSize, maxPoolSize, queueCapacity, a ThreadFactory that names threads for debugging, and graceful shutdown settings such as setWaitForTasksToCompleteOnShutdown(true) and setAwaitTerminationSeconds(60). Naming threads is important because thread dumps show meaningful names like async-email-1 instead of pool-3-thread-1, making production debugging much easier. Real-time example: @Bean(name = "emailExecutor") public Executor emailExecutor() { ThreadPoolTaskExecutor executor = new ThreadPoolTaskExecutor(); executor.setCorePoolSize(5); executor.setMaxPoolSize(20); executor.setQueueCapacity(100); executor.setThreadNamePrefix("email-"); executor.setWaitForTasksToCompleteOnShutdown(true); executor.setAwaitTerminationSeconds(30); executor.initialize(); return executor; } Then use @Async("emailExecutor") on email sending methods. This prevents the default executor from spawning unlimited threads.

title: Q: [Q22] What is the Tomcat server threading model?
text: [Q22] What is the Tomcat server threading model? Tomcat uses a multi-threaded model to process incoming HTTP requests. An acceptor thread listens on the configured port for new TCP connections. A poller thread manages non- blocking I/O for accepted connections, especially in NIO and NIO2 connectors. When a request arrives, a worker thread from the thread pool is assigned to process it. The worker thread executes the servlet request, invokes the Spring Boot dispatcher servlet, runs application logic, generates a response, and returns to the pool. The maximum number of worker threads is controlled by maxThreads. Connections beyond the current worker capacity wait in an internal queue until a thread becomes available, and further connections are queued at the OS level up to acceptCount. For I/O-bound applications, async servlets release the worker thread while waiting for external I/O, allowing Tomcat to handle more concurrent connections than maxThreads. Real-time example: a Spring Boot REST API with default Tomcat settings has maxThreads=200. Each request performs some business logic and database access. If a request takes 200 milliseconds, the theoretical throughput is about 1000 requests per second. If the application makes a slow external API call synchronously, all 200 threads block and the server becomes unresponsive even though the CPU is idle.

title: Q: [Q23] What are the key Tomcat connector tuning parameters?
text: [Q23] What are the key Tomcat connector tuning parameters? Key parameters include maxThreads, the maximum number of request-processing worker threads; minSpareThreads, the minimum number of threads kept alive to handle sudden traffic; maxConnections, the maximum number of concurrent connections the poller can hold open; acceptCount, the OS-level socket backlog queue size when maxConnections is reached; connectionTimeout, how long Tomcat waits for the request line after accepting a connection; keepAliveTimeout, how long idle keep-alive connections remain open; and connectionUploadTimeout, timeout for reading request bodies. In Spring Boot, these map to properties under server.tomcat.*. Tuning requires balancing memory, CPU, and expected concurrent load. Increasing maxThreads alone does not fix slow application logic; it only increases the number of concurrent slow operations. Real-time example: a microservice runs on a 2- CPU container with 2 GB memory. Setting maxThreads=500 wastes memory on thread stacks and increases context switching. After profiling, the optimal setting is maxThreads=50, minSpareThreads=10, acceptCount=100, and connectionTimeout=20000. The service handles load efficiently without memory pressure.

title: Q: [Q24] What is the relationship between Tomcat worker threads and  Spring Boot application threads?
text: [Q24] What is the relationship between Tomcat worker threads and Spring Boot application threads? When a request arrives at Tomcat, a Tomcat worker thread invokes the Spring Boot dispatcher servlet and executes the application code on the same thread unless async processing is explicitly used. This means application logic typically runs within the Tomcat thread pool. If the application makes a blocking call, such as a database query or external HTTP request, the Tomcat worker thread remains occupied until the call completes. For CPU-bound workloads this is acceptable, but for I/O-bound workloads it limits scalability. Using @Async, CompletableFuture, DeferredResult, Callable, or reactive programming moves work off the Tomcat worker thread, allowing the same thread pool to handle many more concurrent connections. Real-time example: a payment endpoint calls a bank API that takes 2 seconds. With synchronous processing, a Tomcat pool of 50 threads can handle only about 25 requests per second. By returning CompletableFuture> and running the bank call on a dedicated async executor, the same 50 Tomcat threads can accept requests and dispatch them asynchronously, increasing throughput to hundreds of requests per second.

title: Q: [S1] Two threads increment a shared counter, but the final value is less  than expected. What is wrong?
text: [S1] Two threads increment a shared counter, but the final value is less than expected. What is wrong? This is a classic race condition caused by a non-atomic read-modify-write sequence. The increment operation count++ compiles to multiple bytecode instructions: read the current value, add one, and write back. Without synchronization, both threads can read the same value concurrently, increment it independently, and write back the same result, causing one update to be lost. The fix is to use AtomicInteger for lock-free atomic increments, or synchronize the increment method or block. AtomicInteger uses compare-and-swap hardware instructions and is more efficient than locking for simple counters. In a distributed system with multiple JVMs, a distributed counter or database sequence may be needed. Real-time example: a REST endpoint tracks total downloads using a shared int downloadCount. Under a load test of 10,000 concurrent downloads, the reported count is only 8,234 because updates were lost. Replacing int downloadCount with AtomicInteger downloadCount = new AtomicInteger(0); and using downloadCount.incrementAndGet() produces the correct 10,000 count. For higher throughput across multiple counters, LongAdder may be even better because it reduces contention across internal cells.

title: Q: [S2] A synchronized method causes poor performance under load.  How do you optimize it?
text: [S2] A synchronized method causes poor performance under load. How do you optimize it? I first profile to confirm that synchronization is the actual bottleneck and identify what portion of the method truly needs protection. The first optimization is to reduce the synchronized scope to the smallest critical section that accesses shared mutable state. If the method is mostly read-only with occasional writes, I replace it with a ReadWriteLock where many threads can hold the read lock simultaneously and only writers block readers. I also evaluate concurrent data structures such as ConcurrentHashMap, ConcurrentLinkedQueue, CopyOnWriteArrayList, or LongAdder, which provide fine-grained locking or lock-free algorithms. If contention remains high, I consider data structure sharding, where the shared state is split into multiple independently locked segments, or actor-style single-threaded ownership where one thread owns the state and others communicate via queues. Real-time example: a reporting service has a synchronized method that reads and updates a large in-memory cache map. Reads are 99 percent of operations. Replacing the method-level synchronized with a ReadWriteLock increases read throughput by a factor of ten because multiple threads can read concurrently. For even better performance, switching to ConcurrentHashMap eliminates manual locking entirely.

title: Q: [S3] An application creates a new thread for every request and  eventually crashes. What is the fix?
text: [S3] An application creates a new thread for every request and eventually crashes. What is the fix? The application is using new Thread() per request, which has high creation overhead and consumes operating system resources. Threads have stack memory, kernel bookkeeping structures, and context-switching costs. Creating thousands of threads exhausts native memory or hits OS limits, causing OutOfMemoryError: unable to create new native thread or crashes. The fix is to use a thread pool through ExecutorService or ThreadPoolExecutor, which reuses a bounded number of threads. I configure corePoolSize, maxPoolSize, queueCapacity, a rejection policy, and a named ThreadFactory. For short-lived tasks, a fixed or cached bounded pool is appropriate. For production, I also implement graceful shutdown. Real-time example: an integration service receives webhooks and spawns new Thread(() -> processWebhook(payload)).start() for each. During a burst of 50,000 webhooks, the JVM creates too many threads and the Linux process limit is reached, crashing the service. Replacing this with: ThreadPoolExecutor executor = new ThreadPoolExecutor(20, 100, 60L, TimeUnit.SECONDS, new ArrayBlockingQueue<>(1000), new ThreadFactoryBuilder().setNameFormat("webhook-%d").build(), new ThreadPoolExecutor.CallerRunsPolicy()); stabilizes the service. The bounded queue and CallerRunsPolicy provide backpressure by making the caller process tasks when saturated.

title: Q: [S4] A ThreadPoolExecutor rejects tasks under heavy load. How do  you handle it?
text: [S4] A ThreadPoolExecutor rejects tasks under heavy load. How do you handle it? Task rejection means the pool has reached maximumPoolSize and the work queue is full. I first determine whether the rejection is a normal capacity signal or a problem. If the system is intentionally designed with backpressure, CallerRunsPolicy is often the best choice because it slows down the producer naturally and prevents overload. If rejections indicate the pool is undersized, I increase maxPoolSize and queue capacity within memory and CPU limits, or scale horizontally by adding application instances. I also review task duration because long-running tasks reduce effective throughput. I log and monitor rejected tasks with a custom RejectedExecutionHandler to track patterns. A custom handler can also persist failed tasks to a dead-letter queue for retry. Real-time example: during a flash sale, an order processing executor with corePoolSize=50, maxPoolSize=50, and a queue of 100 starts rejecting orders. I increase maxPoolSize to 150 and queue capacity to 500. I add a custom rejection handler that publishes rejected orders to a Kafka topic so a separate consumer can retry them once the load subsides. This prevents lost orders while protecting the main service.

title: Q: [S5] A Callable task submitted to an ExecutorService throws an  exception, but the caller never sees it. Why?
text: [S5] A Callable task submitted to an ExecutorService throws an exception, but the caller never sees it. Why? Exceptions thrown in Callable.call() are captured inside the returned Future object, not propagated immediately. If the caller never calls Future.get(), the exception remains trapped in the future and is effectively swallowed. With ExecutorService.execute(Runnable), uncaught exceptions may be lost unless an UncaughtExceptionHandler is configured. The fix is to always handle the Future returned by submit(). With plain Future, call get() and catch ExecutionException to inspect the underlying cause. With CompletableFuture, exceptions propagate through the chain and can be handled with exceptionally, handle, or whenComplete. Real-time example: a batch job submits 100 Callable tasks to process payments and only stores the futures in a list. Because the codefinever iterates the list and calls get(), failed payments are silently ignored and customers are not charged. The fix collects results and logs failures: for (Future<PaymentResult> future : futures) { try { PaymentResult result = future.get(); if (!result.isSuccess()) log.warn("Payment failed: {}", result); } catch (ExecutionException e) { log.error("Payment task failed", e.getCause()); } } Alternatively, using CompletableFuture with exceptionally provides cleaner centralized error handling.

title: Q: [S6] You have two independent API calls to make, and then you need to  combine their results. How do you implement it with  CompletableFuture?
text: [S6] You have two independent API calls to make, and then you need to combine their results. How do you implement it with CompletableFuture? I use CompletableFuture.supplyAsync() for each independent call, passing a custom executor for better control over the thread pool. I then combine the results using thenCombine if I need to merge exactly two futures with a combining function, or CompletableFuture.allOf followed by joining each future for more than two. thenCombine runs a BiFunction once both futures complete. allOf returns a future that completes when all provided futures complete, but it returns Void, so I keep references to the original futures to retrieve their results. I handle exceptions per future with exceptionally or handle so one failure does not unnecessarily break the entire response. Real-time example: an order summary screen needs user profile and order history from two separate microservices. Synchronous calls take 300 ms plus 500 ms, totaling 800 ms. Using: CompletableFuture profileFuture = CompletableFuture.supplyAsync( () -> userService.getProfile(userId), ioExecutor); CompletableFuture> ordersFuture = CompletableFuture.supplyAsync( () -> orderService.getHistory(userId), ioExecutor); CompletableFuture summary = profileFuture.thenCombine(ordersFuture, (profile, orders) -> new Summary(profile, orders)); reduces the total time to approximately 500 ms, the duration of the slower call. If one service fails, an exceptionally fallback can return cached or partial data.

title: Q: [S7] A CompletableFuture chain uses thenApply but the  transformation itself makes a blocking database call. What is the risk?
text: [S7] A CompletableFuture chain uses thenApply but the transformation itself makes a blocking database call. What is the risk? The risk is that the blocking database call runs on the thread that completed the previous stage. If the previous stage ran on the ForkJoinPool.commonPool, the common pool thread blocks, reducing the pool's effective capacity and potentially starving other async operations that use it. Even if the previous stage ran on the caller's thread, blocking there can delay response processing. The fix is to use thenApplyAsync or thenComposeAsync and supply an executor designed for blocking I/O with enough threads. Better yet, use thenComposeAsync to call an async database client that returns a CompletableFuture, such as a reactive database driver. For Spring Data, returning reactive types or using @Async services can also help. Real-time example: a chain fetches user details and then queries a slow CRM database inside thenApply: fetchUser(userId) .thenApply(user -> crmRepository.findByEmail(user.getEmail())) .thenApply(crm -> enrich(user, crm)); During load, the common pool threads block on the CRM query, causing other futures to queue. The fix moves the CRM call to thenComposeAsync with a dedicated executor: fetchUser(userId) .thenComposeAsync(user -> crmAsyncClient.findByEmail(user.getEmail()), blockingIoExecutor) .thenApply(crm -> enrich(user, crm)); This isolates blocking work from the common pool and restores responsiveness.

title: Q: [S8] A scheduled task in Spring Boot is not running on time after a long- running previous execution. Why?
text: [S8] A scheduled task in Spring Boot is not running on time after a long- running previous execution. Why? If the task is configured with fixedDelay, the next execution intentionally waits until the previous execution finishes plus the delay. This behavior prevents overlap and is correct for sequential tasks. If the requirement is to start at fixed wall-clock intervals regardless of duration, fixedRate should be used instead. With fixedRate, the scheduler triggers the next execution at the interval, but if the task takes longer than the interval, executions can queue up unless configured otherwise. For cron expressions, the next scheduled time is based on clock time, not previous execution duration. Another common issue is that Spring Boot's default scheduler uses a single thread, so a long-running scheduled task can delay other scheduled tasks. The fix is to configure a ThreadPoolTaskScheduler with a larger pool or assign a dedicated scheduler to long tasks. Real-time example: a nightly report generation job sometimes runs for 30 minutes, and an hourly cleanup task starts late. Both share the default single-threaded scheduler. I create a ThreadPoolTaskScheduler named reportScheduler with pool size 5 and annotate the report job with @Scheduled(fixedDelay = 60 * 60 * 1000, scheduler = "reportScheduler"). The cleanup task keeps the default scheduler. Now both tasks run independently and on time.

title: Q: [S9] A Spring Boot @Async method runs synchronously when called  from within the same class. Why?
text: [S9] A Spring Boot @Async method runs synchronously when called from within the same class. Why? Spring uses proxies, typically JDK dynamic proxies or CGLIB subclasses, to implement @Async. When a method is called from within the same class, the call bypasses the proxy and invokes the target method directly. Because the proxy is not involved, Spring cannot intercept the call and schedule it on the async executor. The fix is to move the @Async method to another Spring bean and inject that bean, so calls go through the proxy. This is the same self-invocation limitation that affects @Transactional and @Scheduled annotations. Real-time example: an OrderService has a public @Async CompletableFuture sendNotification(Order order) method and another method processOrder(Order order) that calls sendNotification(order). Notifications are sent synchronously, blocking order processing. The fix extracts NotificationService as a separate bean, injects it into OrderService, and calls notificationService.sendNotification(order). The proxy intercepts the call and runs it on the async executor, so processOrder returns immediately.

title: Q: [S10] A Spring Boot scheduled job works in development but runs twice  in production. What causes this?
text: [S10] A Spring Boot scheduled job works in development but runs twice in production. What causes this? In development there is usually one application instance, so the scheduled job runs once. In production there are often multiple instances deployed, and each JVM runs the scheduled job independently, causing duplicate execution. Spring Boot scheduling is per-JVM by default. To fix this, use a distributed scheduler such as ShedLock, Quartz with JDBC job store, or a Kubernetes CronJob that runs as a single Pod. Another possibility is that multiple application contexts are started in the same JVM, which can happen with incorrect Spring Boot test configurations or nested contexts. I check the deployment topology and ensure exactly one instance executes the job. ShedLock is a lightweight solution that uses a database or Redis lock. Real-time example: a daily invoice generation job runs on two Kubernetes pods and creates duplicate invoices. I add the ShedLock dependency, create a lock table, and annotate the method: @Scheduled(cron = "0 0 2 * * *") @SchedulerLock(name = "generateInvoices", lockAtMostFor = "PT2H") public void generateInvoices() { ... } Only one pod acquires the lock and runs the job. Duplicates stop, and if the lock holder crashes, the lock expires after two hours so another pod can take over.

title: Q: [S11] A Tomcat server becomes unresponsive under heavy load. What  do you check?
text: [S11] A Tomcat server becomes unresponsive under heavy load. What do you check? I check whether all Tomcat worker threads are busy handling requests. I monitor Tomcat metrics via JMX or Spring Boot actuator for busy threads, max threads, and queued connections. If all worker threads are occupied by slow requests, new connections queue up until acceptCount is reached and then time out. The root cause is usually slow application logic such as external API calls, database queries, or heavy computation that blocks the worker thread. The fix is to identify the slow operations and make them asynchronous, moving work off the Tomcat thread using CompletableFuture, DeferredResult, Callable, or reactive programming. Increasing maxThreads may help briefly but does not fix slow logic and increases memory usage. Real-time example: during a sale, the checkout API calls a payment gateway that takes 3 seconds synchronously. With default maxThreads=200, all threads block and users see 504 gateway timeouts. Profiling the thread dump shows every worker waiting on the payment gateway socket. I refactor the endpoint to return DeferredResult>, run the gateway call on a dedicated async executor, and complete the result when the gateway responds. Tomcat workers are released immediately, and the server handles 10 times more concurrent connections.

title: Q: [S12] A Spring Boot application with default Tomcat config runs out of  memory under load. How do you tune it?
text: [S12] A Spring Boot application with default Tomcat config runs out of memory under load. How do you tune it? I first determine whether the memory issue is heap or native thread memory. Default Tomcat maxThreads is 200, and each thread consumes stack memory, typically 1 MB by default. If many threads are idle or the workload is small, reducing maxThreads frees significant native memory. I set minSpareThreads to a reasonable value that avoids thread creation overhead without keeping too many threads alive. I tune acceptCount and maxConnections to prevent excessive queued connections from consuming memory. I ensure connectionTimeout and keepAliveTimeout are not holding idle keep-alive connections too long. For I/O-bound workloads, I reduce thread count and use async servlets so fewer worker threads are needed. Real- time example: a microservice with default maxThreads=200 runs on a small container with 1 GB memory. Each Tomcat thread uses about 1 MB stack, consuming 200 MB just for threads, plus heap, plus other native memory. I reduce maxThreads to 50 and minSpareThreads to 10 in application.yml: server.tomcat.threads.max: 50 server.tomcat.threads.min-spare: 10 I also convert blocking downstream calls to async processing. Memory usage drops by over 50 percent and the container runs stably.

title: Q: [S13] A @Scheduled method throws an exception, and subsequent  executions stop. Why?
text: [S13] A @Scheduled method throws an exception, and subsequent executions stop. Why? An unhandled exception in a scheduled task does not stop the Spring scheduler process itself, but it can stop the specific scheduled task if the scheduler is single-threaded and the exception propagates out of the method. By default, Spring Boot configures a single scheduler thread that handles all @Scheduled methods, so an exception in one task or a long-running task can delay others. The fix is to catch exceptions inside the scheduled method body and log them, preventing the exception from bubbling out. Alternatively, configure a custom ErrorHandler on the scheduler. For critical tasks, I wrap the entire method body in try-catch and may send alerts on failures. Real-time example: an hourly cache refresh task throws a NullPointerException because an external service briefly returns null. Because the exception is uncaught, the scheduler thread may behave unpredictably and the cache stops refreshing. I add: @Scheduled(fixedRate = 60 * 60 * 1000) public void refreshCache() { try { cacheService.refresh(); } catch (Exception e) { log.error("Cache refresh failed", e); alertService.send("Cache refresh failed: " + e.getMessage()); } } Now transient failures are logged and alerted, but the task continues to run every hour.

title: Q: [S14] You need to run a task after another task completes, but both are  asynchronous. Which CompletableFuture method do you use?
text: [S14] You need to run a task after another task completes, but both are asynchronous. Which CompletableFuture method do you use? I use thenCompose if the second task depends on the result of the first and itself returns a CompletableFuture. This flattens CompletableFuture> into CompletableFuture. I use thenApply if the second step is a synchronous transformation of the first result. If the two tasks are independent and I need both results together, I use thenCombine. For dependent sequential async calls, thenCompose is the correct choice because it avoids nested futures and creates a clean linear pipeline. Real-time example: a checkout flow first validates inventory asynchronously and then charges payment asynchronously. The payment step needs the validation result: CompletableFuture result = validateInventory(order) .thenCompose(valid -> { if (!valid) return CompletableFuture.completedFuture(OrderResult.outOfStock()); return chargePayment(order); }) .thenCompose(payment -> createShipment(order, payment)) .exceptionally(ex -> OrderResult.failure(ex.getMessage())); The inventory validation, payment, and shipment run sequentially, but each step is non-blocking. The entire chain returns a single future that completes when the shipment is created.

title: Q: [S15] A CompletableFuture.allOf pipeline completes but you cannot  easily get individual results. How do you solve it?
text: [S15] A CompletableFuture.allOf pipeline completes but you cannot easily get individual results. How do you solve it? CompletableFuture.allOf returns CompletableFuture because it only signals that all futures are done. To collect results, I keep references to the original futures and call join() or get() on each after allOf completes. A cleaner approach for a small fixed number of futures is thenCombine. For a dynamic list, I store futures in a List>, call CompletableFuture.allOf(futures.toArray(new CompletableFuture[0])), and then map the list with future.join(). I wrap the operation in try-catch because join() throws CompletionException for unchecked exceptions. Real-time example: I need product, pricing, and inventory from three independent services: CompletableFuture productFuture = supplyAsync(() -> productService.get(id), ioExecutor); CompletableFuture priceFuture = supplyAsync(() -> pricingService.get(id), ioExecutor); CompletableFuture stockFuture = supplyAsync(() -> inventoryService.get(id), ioExecutor); CompletableFuture all = CompletableFuture.allOf(productFuture, priceFuture, stockFuture); CompletableFuture detail = all.thenApply(v -> new ProductDetail( productFuture.join(), priceFuture.join(), stockFuture.join())) .exceptionally(ex -> ProductDetail.error(ex.getCause())); This waits for all three and combines their results. If any service fails, join() throws and exceptionally handles it.

title: Q: [S16] A Spring Boot application needs to run a background job every 10  seconds, but the job must not overlap with itself. How do you ensure  this?
text: [S16] A Spring Boot application needs to run a background job every 10 seconds, but the job must not overlap with itself. How do you ensure this? I use fixedDelay instead of fixedRate so the next execution only starts after the previous one finishes plus the delay. This guarantees no overlap because the scheduler waits for completion. If the requirement is to run at fixed wall-clock intervals and the task duration is always shorter than the interval, fixedRate with a single scheduler thread also prevents overlap because the scheduler cannot run the next execution until the thread is free. For clustered deployments, I add a distributed lock such as ShedLock to ensure only one instance runs the job. For cases where fixedRate or cron is required but overlap must be prevented, I implement a task-level lock inside the method using ReentrantLock or a boolean flag. Real-time example: a data export job takes between 5 and 20 seconds. Using @Scheduled(fixedRate = 10000) causes overlapping exports and database contention. I switch to @Scheduled(fixedDelay = 10000) so the next export starts 10 seconds after the previous one completes. For a cluster of three instances, I also add ShedLock so only one instance exports at a time.

title: Q: [S17] A thread appears to hang forever waiting for a lock. How do you  diagnose it?
text: [S17] A thread appears to hang forever waiting for a lock. How do you diagnose it? I generate a thread dump using jstack , kill -3 , or the JVM diagnostic command jcmd Thread.print. I look for threads in BLOCKED state and identify which monitor they are waiting to acquire. I find the thread that currently holds the lock and examine what it is doing. If that thread is waiting for another lock held by yet another thread, I look for a deadlock cycle. I also check threads in WAITING or TIMED_WAITING to see if they are waiting for notify() that never arrives. In production, I use monitoring tools such as Datadog, Dynatrace, New Relic, or Java Flight Recorder to capture thread state and trends. Once the cause is identified, I fix synchronization logic, reduce lock scope, establish lock ordering to prevent deadlock, or replace locks with concurrent data structures. Real-time example: a payment service hangs during peak load. A thread dump shows http-worker-15 blocked on a lock held by http-worker-3, which is waiting for a database connection currently held by http- worker-15. This is a circular wait deadlock caused by acquiring the lock before the database connection. I refactor to acquire the database connection first, then the business lock, breaking the cycle.

title: Q: [S18] A method is annotated with @Async but returns a normal object  instead of CompletableFuture. What happens?
text: [S18] A method is annotated with @Async but returns a normal object instead of CompletableFuture. What happens? The method still runs asynchronously because the Spring proxy intercepts the call and schedules it on the executor, but the caller receives the return value immediately, not the actual computed result. Since the async task has not finished when the proxy returns, the caller usually receives null for object returns or a partially initialized object. The caller cannot wait for completion or handle exceptions through the returned value. Best practice is to return CompletableFuture for async methods that produce a result, or void for fire-and-forget tasks. Returning a normal object defeats the purpose of async composition and makes error handling nearly impossible. Real-time example: a service has @Async public User fetchUser(String id). A caller invokes it and immediately uses the returned User, but the async method is still querying the database. The caller receives null and throws NullPointerException. The fix changes the method to @Async public CompletableFuture fetchUser(String id) and the caller uses fetchUser(id).thenApply(user -> ...) or fetchUser(id).join() when the result is needed.

title: Q: [S19] A CompletableFuture chain should fail fast if any stage takes too  long. How do you add a timeout?
text: [S19] A CompletableFuture chain should fail fast if any stage takes too long. How do you add a timeout? Java 9 introduced orTimeout and completeOnTimeout on CompletableFuture. I use future.orTimeout(duration, TimeUnit) to complete the future exceptionally if it does not finish in time. I use completeOnTimeout(defaultValue, duration, TimeUnit) to provide a fallback value instead of failing. In Java 8, I can schedule a CompletableFuture.completeExceptionally call with a ScheduledExecutorService after the timeout. When combining multiple futures, I apply timeout to individual stages so the failure is isolated and other independent stages can still succeed. Real-time example: a recommendation service calls three external providers in parallel, and the overall response must return within 2 seconds. I apply orTimeout(800, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS) to each provider future and use exceptionally to return a default recommendation if one times out: CompletableFuture recs = providerA.get(userId).orTimeout(800, MILLISECONDS) .exceptionally(ex -> defaultRecommendations); The final response is assembled from whichever providers respond in time, improving availability while meeting the latency requirement.

title: Q: [S20] How do you gracefully shut down a ThreadPoolExecutor in  production?
text: [S20] How do you gracefully shut down a ThreadPoolExecutor in production? I call shutdown() to stop accepting new tasks while allowing already submitted tasks to finish. I then call awaitTermination(timeout, TimeUnit) to wait for running tasks to complete. If tasks are still running after the timeout, I call shutdownNow() to attempt to interrupt them, collect the list of tasks that were waiting to run, and decide whether to persist them for retry or log them. I never call shutdownNow() first because it cancels tasks abruptly and may leave data in an inconsistent state. I also ensure task implementations respond to interruption by checking Thread.currentThread().isInterrupted() and cleaning up resources. In Spring Boot, I configure the ThreadPoolTaskExecutor with setWaitForTasksToCompleteOnShutdown(true) and setAwaitTerminationSeconds(60). Real-time example: a report generation service is being redeployed. Without graceful shutdown, a report in progress is killed mid-write, leaving partial data in the database. I configure: executor.setWaitForTasksToCompleteOnShutdown(true); executor.setAwaitTerminationSeconds(60); On shutdown, Spring waits up to 60 seconds for active reports to finish before terminating the JVM. Reports that did not start are logged and retried by the next deployment.

title: Q: [S21] A Spring Boot controller calls a slow external API synchronously.  How do you prevent Tomcat thread exhaustion?
text: [S21] A Spring Boot controller calls a slow external API synchronously. How do you prevent Tomcat thread exhaustion? I make the controller return an asynchronous result type such as CompletableFuture>, DeferredResult, or Callable. This releases the Tomcat worker thread immediately and allows the response to be completed later when the external API responds. The external call should run on a dedicated async executor with enough threads for blocking I/O, not on the ForkJoinPool.commonPool. I configure the async timeout and error handling so the client receives a proper response or timeout error instead of a generic server error. For fully reactive stacks, returning Mono or Flux with WebClient also releases the Tomcat thread. Real-time example: a credit check endpoint calls a bureau API that takes 3 seconds. With 200 Tomcat threads, the application handles about 60 requests per second. I change the controller to: @GetMapping("/credit-check/{id}") public CompletableFuture<ResponseEntity<Decision>> checkCredit(@PathVariable String id) { return CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> bureauService.check(id), bureauExecutor) .thenApply(decision -> ResponseEntity.ok(decision)) .exceptionally(ex -> ResponseEntity.status(503).body(Decision.unavailable())); } Tomcat threads are released after scheduling the work, and the same pool can accept many more requests. Throughput increases to 500 requests per second.

title: Q: [S22] What happens if corePoolSize equals maximumPoolSize in a  ThreadPoolExecutor?
text: [S22] What happens if corePoolSize equals maximumPoolSize in a ThreadPoolExecutor? The pool has a fixed size. It creates threads up to corePoolSize for incoming tasks and then queues additional tasks. Extra threads beyond the core size are never created because the maximum equals the core. If the queue is bounded and fills up, tasks are rejected according to the rejection policy. If the queue is unbounded, tasks can accumulate indefinitely, risking out-of- memory errors if the producer is faster than the consumer. This configuration is useful when you want a predictable number of threads and rely on the queue for buffering. It is commonly used with Executors.newFixedThreadPool(n), which creates a fixed pool with an unbounded LinkedBlockingQueue. For production, I prefer a bounded queue with a meaningful rejection policy rather than an unbounded queue that can silently exhaust memory. Real-time example: an order validation service uses Executors.newFixedThreadPool(20), which has 20 threads and an unbounded queue. During a traffic spike, the producer submits millions of validation tasks faster than they are processed. The queue grows until the JVM runs out of memory. I replace it with an explicit ThreadPoolExecutor using a bounded ArrayBlockingQueue of 1000 and CallerRunsPolicy. This provides backpressure, keeps memory stable, and prevents cascading failure.

title: Q: [S23] A scheduled task needs to run at 2 AM in a specific timezone. How  do you configure it?
text: [S23] A scheduled task needs to run at 2 AM in a specific timezone. How do you configure it? I use the cron attribute of @Scheduled with the zone attribute to specify the timezone. For example, @Scheduled(cron = "0 0 2 * * *", zone = "America/New_York") runs at 2 AM Eastern Time every day. Without the zone attribute, the cron expression uses the server's default timezone, which can cause incorrect execution times if the server timezone changes or if instances run in different regions. I always set the zone explicitly for time-sensitive scheduled tasks and document the timezone in the code and runbooks. I also consider daylight saving time changes when choosing the timezone. Real-time example: a financial reconciliation report must run at 2 AM New York time to match the market close. The application servers run in UTC. Without the zone, the report runs at 2 AM UTC, which is 10 PM the previous day in New York during standard time. I annotate the method with: @Scheduled(cron = "0 0 2 * * *", zone = "America/New_York") public void runReconciliation() { ... } The task now runs at the correct local time in New York, adjusting for daylight saving time automatically.

title: Q: [S24] A @Scheduled task processes a large batch and should not delay  other scheduled tasks. How do you design it?
text: [S24] A @Scheduled task processes a large batch and should not delay other scheduled tasks. How do you design it? I configure a dedicated TaskScheduler with a larger thread pool for the long-running task, or I move the actual batch processing to an @Async method so the scheduler thread returns immediately after triggering the work. The scheduler thread should be lightweight and only responsible for triggering work; heavy processing should run in a separate executor. If the batch is too large for the application, I consider running it as a Kubernetes CronJob or an external scheduler that can scale independently. I also monitor task duration to detect regressions and alert if the batch exceeds expected time. Real-time example: a nightly aggregation task takes 2 hours and delays all other scheduled tasks because the default scheduler has one thread. I create a ThreadPoolTaskScheduler named batchScheduler with pool size 5: @Bean("batchScheduler") public TaskScheduler batchScheduler() { ThreadPoolTaskScheduler scheduler = new ThreadPoolTaskScheduler(); scheduler.setPoolSize(5); scheduler.setThreadNamePrefix("batch-sched-"); return scheduler; } I annotate the aggregation method with @Scheduled(cron = "0 0 1 * * *", scheduler = "batchScheduler") and have the method delegate to an @Async executor for the actual processing. Other scheduled tasks are no longer blocked by the long batch.

title: Q: [S25] You have an ExecutorService and want to know when all tasks  are done. How do you do it?
text: [S25] You have an ExecutorService and want to know when all tasks are done. How do you do it? I submit all tasks and collect the returned Future objects. I iterate over the collection and call get() on each, which waits for completion and surfaces any ExecutionException. Alternatively, I use ExecutorService.invokeAll(Collection>) to submit a collection of Callable tasks and wait for all to complete; it returns a list of futures in the same order as the input. With CompletableFuture, I use CompletableFuture.allOf or collect futures in a list and call join() after waiting. For manual coordination, I can use a CountDownLatch initialized with the task count; each task calls countDown() when done, and the main thread calls await(). Real-time example: a report generation job fans out 50 subtasks to an executor to fetch data from different regions. I use: List<Future<RegionData>> futures = executor.invokeAll(tasks); for (Future<RegionData> future : futures) { try { report.addRegionData(future.get()); } catch (ExecutionException e) { log.error("Region task failed", e.getCause()); report.markRegionFailed(e.getCause().getMessage()); } } invokeAll blocks until all tasks complete, and the loop handles both successful results and failures. This ensures the report includes all available data and logs any regional failures.

title: Q: [Q1] What is Spring Boot, and why is it popular for building Java  backend applications?
text: [Q1] What is Spring Boot, and why is it popular for building Java backend applications? Spring Boot is an opinionated framework built on top of the Spring Framework that simplifies application setup and deployment. It provides auto-configuration, starter dependencies, an embedded server, and production-ready features like Actuator out of the box. Instead of writing a lot of XML or Java configuration, a developer can add spring-boot-starter-web and start a runnable web application with a main method. It is popular because it reduces boilerplate, speeds up development, and makes microservices and cloud-native deployments easier.

title: Q: [Q2] What does @SpringBootApplication do internally?
text: [Q2] What does @SpringBootApplication do internally? @SpringBootApplication is a convenience annotation that combines @Configuration, @EnableAutoConfiguration, and @ComponentScan. @Configuration marks the class as a source of bean definitions. @ComponentScan tells Spring where to look for components, services, repositories, and controllers. @EnableAutoConfiguration triggers Spring Boot’s auto-configuration mechanism, which inspects the classpath and existing beans to configure common infrastructure automatically, such as a datasource, embedded Tomcat, or Spring MVC. You can exclude specific auto-configurations using the exclude attribute.

title: Q: [Q3] What are Spring Boot starters, and how do they help?
text: [Q3] What are Spring Boot starters, and how do they help? Starters are pre-packaged dependency descriptors that bundle a set of compatible libraries for a specific use case. For example, spring-boot-starter-web includes Spring MVC, Tomcat, and Jackson, while spring-boot-starter-data-jpa includes Hibernate, Spring Data JPA, and a transaction manager. They reduce the need to hunt for compatible library versions and keep the build file clean. The spring-boot-starter-parent POM provides dependency management, default plugin configurations, and version alignment.

title: Q: [Q4] How do you create a simple REST controller in Spring Boot?
text: [Q4] How do you create a simple REST controller in Spring Boot? You annotate a class with @RestController, which combines @Controller and @ResponseBody. You then map HTTP methods using annotations like @GetMapping, @PostMapping, @PutMapping, and @DeleteMapping. For example, @GetMapping("/users/{id}") public User getUser(@PathVariable Long id) handles a GET request and returns the user object, which Jackson serializes to JSON automatically. Using @RestController avoids the need to annotate every method with @ResponseBody.

title: Q: [Q5] What is the difference between @Controller and  @RestController?
text: [Q5] What is the difference between @Controller and @RestController? @Controller is the traditional Spring MVC annotation used when you want to return a view name that a ViewResolver renders. @RestController is a shortcut for @Controller plus @ResponseBody, meaning every method returns a domain object or data that is serialized directly to JSON, XML, or another response body. If you use @Controller for a REST API and forget @ResponseBody, Spring will look for a view with that name and likely throw a 404.

title: Q: [Q6] How does Spring Boot handle the embedded server, and how do  you change the default port?
text: [Q6] How does Spring Boot handle the embedded server, and how do you change the default port? Spring Boot includes an embedded server, usually Tomcat, by adding spring-boot- starter-web. The application can run as a standalone JAR because the embedded server starts when the SpringApplication.run method is executed. You can change the port with server.port=9090 in application.properties, or disable the web server entirely with spring.main.web-application-type=none. To use Jetty or Undertow, exclude Tomcat from the starter and add spring-boot-starter-jetty or spring-boot-starter- undertow.

title: Q: [Q7] What is auto-configuration, and how can you override or disable it?
text: [Q7] What is auto-configuration, and how can you override or disable it? Auto-configuration is Spring Boot’s ability to guess the configuration a project needs based on the classpath and existing beans. It is implemented by classes annotated with @ConditionalOnClass, @ConditionalOnMissingBean, and similar conditions. You can override auto-configuration by defining your own beans with the same type, which takes precedence due to @ConditionalOnMissingBean. You can disable specific auto- configuration classes using spring.autoconfigure.exclude in application.properties or the exclude attribute of @SpringBootApplication.

title: Q: [Q8] How do you set up a Spring Boot project with Spring Boot Web MVC  and Spring Data JPA?
text: [Q8] How do you set up a Spring Boot project with Spring Boot Web MVC and Spring Data JPA? Add the starters spring-boot-starter-web for the web layer and spring- boot-starter-data-jpa for persistence. Include a database driver such as mysql- connector-j or postgresql. Configure the datasource in application.properties with spring.datasource.url, username, password, and driver-class-name. Define entity classes with @Entity and repository interfaces extending JpaRepository. Spring Boot auto-configures the datasource, transaction manager, and Hibernate. Use @Transactional in the service layer and keep controllers thin.

title: Q: [Q9] What is Spring Boot DevTools, and why should it not be used in  production?
text: [Q9] What is Spring Boot DevTools, and why should it not be used in production? Spring Boot DevTools is a development-time dependency that provides automatic restart when code changes, LiveReload for browser refresh, and enhanced debugging features. It should not be used in production because it adds runtime overhead, can expose internal application details, and is designed only for the local development loop. In production builds, it should be excluded or marked with a non-transitive scope so that it does not end up in the deployed artifact.

title: Q: [Q10] How do you enable basic auditing in Spring Boot?
text: [Q10] How do you enable basic auditing in Spring Boot? Enable JPA auditing with @EnableJpaAuditing on a configuration class and annotate entity fields with @CreatedDate, @LastModifiedDate, @CreatedBy, and @LastModifiedBy. Spring Data JPA automatically fills these fields when entities are persisted or updated. For @CreatedBy and @LastModifiedBy, you provide an AuditorAware bean that returns the current user. This removes manual audit field population and helps with compliance and traceability.

title: Q: [Q11] What is Hibernate Envers, and when would you use it?
text: [Q11] What is Hibernate Envers, and when would you use it? Hibernate Envers is an auditing module that tracks every insert, update, and delete on entities by maintaining separate revision tables. Annotating an entity with @Audited makes Envers store a snapshot of each change along with a revision number and timestamp. It is useful when you need a complete history of who changed what and when, such as in regulated domains or for debugging data issues. The trade-off is extra storage, write overhead, and the need to plan retention and archival of revision data.

title: Q: [Q12] How do you call a third-party REST API from a Spring Boot  application?
text: [Q12] How do you call a third-party REST API from a Spring Boot application? You can use RestTemplate for synchronous calls, WebClient for reactive or non- blocking calls, or Feign for declarative clients. With RestTemplate, you inject a bean and call methods like getForObject or exchange. With WebClient, you build a request chain and subscribe to the response. With Feign, you declare an interface with method mappings and Spring Cloud generates the implementation. For new Spring Boot 3 projects, the RestClient API offers a modern, fluent synchronous alternative to RestTemplate.

title: Q: [Q13] What is the difference between RestTemplate, WebClient, Feign,  and RestClient?
text: [Q13] What is the difference between RestTemplate, WebClient, Feign, and RestClient? RestTemplate is synchronous and simple but is now in maintenance mode. WebClient is reactive and non-blocking, part of Spring WebFlux, and better for high- concurrency or streaming scenarios. Feign is a declarative Spring Cloud client that lets you define HTTP clients as interfaces, making service-to-service calls concise. RestClient, introduced in Spring Framework 6.1 and Spring Boot 3.2, is a modern synchronous client with a fluent API. Blocking a reactive pipeline with RestTemplate is a common mistake that kills throughput in WebFlux applications.

title: Q: [Q14] How do you handle failures, retries, and timeouts when calling  external APIs?
text: [Q14] How do you handle failures, retries, and timeouts when calling external APIs? External API calls should always have connection and read timeouts configured so that a slow downstream does not hang your service. Use Resilience4j for circuit breaking, retries with exponential backoff, rate limiting, and bulkheads. Wrap the client so that failures degrade gracefully, for example by returning cached or default data. Log correlation IDs to trace calls across services, and use MockWebServer or WireMock in tests instead of hitting real third-party endpoints.

title: Q: [Q15] What is the default logging framework in Spring Boot, and how do  you configure it?
text: [Q15] What is the default logging framework in Spring Boot, and how do you configure it? The default logging framework is Logback, accessed through the SLF4J facade. You can configure logging levels and output in application.properties with properties like logging.level.root=INFO and logging.level.com.example=DEBUG. For file logging, use logging.file.name or logging.file.path. For more control, provide a logback.xml or log4j2.xml file in the classpath. A common mistake is enabling DEBUG in production, which degrades performance and can leak sensitive information.

title: Q: [Q16] What is SLF4J, and why is it recommended over direct Log4j  usage?
text: [Q16] What is SLF4J, and why is it recommended over direct Log4j usage? SLF4J is a logging facade that lets application code use a standard API while the actual logging implementation, such as Logback or Log4j2, is configured at deployment time. This means you can switch implementations by changing dependencies and configuration, without touching application code. It also supports parameterized messages such as logger.info("Processing order {}", orderId), which avoid the cost of string concatenation when the log level is disabled.

title: Q: [Q17] What is Spring Boot Actuator, and what are its most useful  endpoints?
text: [Q17] What is Spring Boot Actuator, and what are its most useful endpoints? Actuator is a production-ready feature that exposes operational endpoints for monitoring and managing the application. After adding spring-boot-starter-actuator, endpoints such as /health, /info, /metrics, /env, /loggers, /beans, and /heapdump become available. /health shows application health, /metrics exposes JVM and application metrics, and /loggers lets you change log levels at runtime. Exposing all endpoints publicly is dangerous, so only the endpoints needed for monitoring should be enabled and secured.

title: Q: [Q18] How do you secure Spring Boot Actuator endpoints?
text: [Q18] How do you secure Spring Boot Actuator endpoints? Limit exposed endpoints using management.endpoints.web.exposure.include to only those required, such as health, info, and metrics. Use Spring Security to protect them with authentication and a dedicated role like ACTUATOR_ADMIN. Run Actuator over HTTPS and place it on a separate management port if needed. Endpoints like /env, /beans, /heapdump, and /configprops can reveal secrets or internal details, so they should be disabled or heavily restricted in production.

title: Q: [Q19] How do you create a custom health indicator in Spring Boot?
text: [Q19] How do you create a custom health indicator in Spring Boot? Implement the HealthIndicator interface and override the health method to check a specific dependency, such as a downstream API or a message broker. Register the class as a Spring bean. Spring Boot automatically includes it in the /health response. This is useful for readiness and liveness probes in Kubernetes, where the platform can restart or stop routing traffic to an unhealthy instance.

title: Q: [Q20] What is OpenAPI, and how does Swagger help document REST  APIs?
text: [Q20] What is OpenAPI, and how does Swagger help document REST APIs? OpenAPI is a standardized specification for describing REST APIs. Swagger provides tooling to design, document, and test APIs based on the OpenAPI specification. In a Spring Boot project, you can use SpringDoc OpenAPI to generate documentation automatically from controllers, DTOs, and annotations. It produces an interactive UI where developers can see endpoints, parameters, request bodies, and response schemas, and even try calls directly.

title: Q: [Q21] Should Swagger UI be enabled in production, and why or why not?
text: [Q21] Should Swagger UI be enabled in production, and why or why not? Swagger UI should generally be disabled or restricted in production. While it is helpful during development and integration testing, it exposes the complete API contract, including endpoints, parameter names, and response structures, which can aid attackers. The recommended approach is to enable Swagger only in non-production profiles or protect it behind authentication and authorization.

title: Q: [Q22] What are Spring profiles, and how do you use them?
text: [Q22] What are Spring profiles, and how do you use them? Spring profiles let you define environment-specific beans and configuration. You activate a profile with spring.profiles.active=prod in application.properties or via command-line arguments. Beans annotated with @Profile("dev") only load when that profile is active. You can also have separate property files like application-dev.properties and application-prod.properties. Profiles help keep database URLs, log levels, feature flags, and secrets isolated per environment.

title: Q: [Q23] How do you externalize configuration and secrets in a Spring Boot  application?
text: [Q23] How do you externalize configuration and secrets in a Spring Boot application? Configuration should be moved out of the application code into property files, environment variables, or external config servers. Spring Boot supports application.properties, application.yml, environment variables, and command-line arguments with a defined precedence order. Secrets such as database passwords and API keys should not be stored in source control. Use Spring Cloud Config, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or HashiCorp Vault to inject secrets securely at runtime.

title: Q: [Q24] What is the Spring MVC request lifecycle?
text: [Q24] What is the Spring MVC request lifecycle? An incoming request first reaches the DispatcherServlet, which consults handler mappings to find the right controller method. The method processes the request, possibly using @RequestBody to deserialize JSON into objects and @PathVariable or @RequestParam to extract values. The result is returned, and if the controller uses @RestController, it is written directly to the response body. Interceptors can run before and after the controller, and @ControllerAdvice can handle exceptions globally.

title: Q: [Q25] What is ResponseEntity, and when should you use it?
text: [Q25] What is ResponseEntity, and when should you use it? ResponseEntity is a Spring class that represents the full HTTP response, including status code, headers, and body. It gives complete control over what is returned to the client. For example, ResponseEntity.created(location).body(user) returns HTTP 201 with a Location header, while ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND).build() returns a 404. It is preferred over returning raw domain objects when you need to set custom headers or status codes.

title: Q: [Q26] How do you handle exceptions globally in Spring Boot?
text: [Q26] How do you handle exceptions globally in Spring Boot? Use @RestControllerAdvice or @ControllerAdvice with methods annotated by @ExceptionHandler. A global advice class catches exceptions thrown by controllers and returns consistent error responses. For example, a ResourceNotFoundException can be mapped to HTTP 404, while a ValidationException can be mapped to HTTP 400. This keeps controllers clean and ensures the API always returns a predictable error format.

title: Q: [Q27] What is application.properties vs application.yml, and  which should you use?
text: [Q27] What is application.properties vs application.yml, and which should you use? Both files store externalized configuration. application.properties uses simple key-value pairs, while application.yml uses nested YAML syntax, which is cleaner for hierarchical configuration. The choice is mostly a team preference. If the configuration is deeply nested, such as multiple datasources or logging appenders, YAML is usually more readable. Properties files are simpler for flat configurations.

title: Q: [Q28] What is the Spring Boot main class, and why does it need  SpringApplication.run?
text: [Q28] What is the Spring Boot main class, and why does it need SpringApplication.run? The main class is the entry point annotated with @SpringBootApplication. Calling SpringApplication.run(MyApplication.class, args) bootstraps the Spring application context, starts the embedded web server, runs command-line runners, and initializes all beans. It is what turns a plain Java application into a running Spring Boot service. The class is usually placed at the root package so component scanning covers the entire project.

title: Q: [Q29] How do you write integration tests in Spring Boot?
text: [Q29] How do you write integration tests in Spring Boot? Use @SpringBootTest to load the full application context, @WebMvcTest for controller-layer tests with mocked services, and @DataJpaTest for repository tests against an embedded database. Use TestRestTemplate or WebTestClient to call endpoints. For external dependencies, use @MockBean or tools like WireMock. Tests should be fast and deterministic, so prefer sliced tests over full context tests when possible.

title: Q: [Q30] What is the difference between @Component, @Service,  @Repository, and @Controller?
text: [Q30] What is the difference between @Component, @Service, @Repository, and @Controller? All four are stereotype annotations that mark classes as Spring-managed beans. @Component is the generic one. @Service is used for business logic classes and helps document intent. @Repository is used for data access and enables Spring’s exception translation for persistence exceptions. @Controller is used for MVC controllers. Functionally they all result in bean creation during component scanning, but the specialized annotations carry semantic meaning and enable framework features.

title: Q: [Q31] What is dependency injection, and why is constructor injection  preferred?
text: [Q31] What is dependency injection, and why is constructor injection preferred? Dependency injection is a design pattern where Spring supplies a bean’s dependencies instead of the bean creating them itself. Constructor injection is preferred because it makes dependencies explicit and immutable, allows easy unit testing without reflection, and ensures the object is fully initialized before use. Field injection hides dependencies and makes testing harder, while setter injection can leave an object in a partially constructed state.

title: Q: [Q32] How do you schedule tasks in Spring Boot?
text: [Q32] How do you schedule tasks in Spring Boot? Add @EnableScheduling on a configuration class and use @Scheduled on methods to run them at fixed intervals, fixed delays, or using cron expressions. For example, @Scheduled(cron = "0 0 * * * *") runs every hour. For reliability and clustering, consider using an external scheduler or Quartz with a JDBC job store so that jobs are not duplicated across multiple application instances.

title: Q: [Q33] What is the difference between @Async and @Scheduled?
text: [Q33] What is the difference between @Async and @Scheduled? @Async marks a method to run in a separate thread from a task executor, returning immediately to the caller. It is useful for fire-and-forget operations like sending emails or notifications. @Scheduled triggers a method repeatedly based on a timer. A common mistake is calling an @Async method from within the same bean, which bypasses the Spring proxy and runs synchronously.

title: Q: [Q34] How do you version REST APIs in Spring Boot?
text: [Q34] How do you version REST APIs in Spring Boot? Common approaches include URI versioning (/v1/users, /v2/users), request header versioning (Accept: application/vnd.company.v1+json), and query parameter versioning (?version=1). URI versioning is the simplest and most visible. Header versioning keeps URLs clean but is harder to test in a browser. The choice should be documented and consistent across the API surface.

title: Q: [Q35] What is content negotiation in Spring Boot?
text: [Q35] What is content negotiation in Spring Boot? Content negotiation is the mechanism by which a client and server agree on the format of the response, usually JSON or XML. Spring Boot uses the Accept header to decide which HttpMessageConverter to use. Jackson is configured by default for JSON, and adding Jackson XML or JAXB dependencies enables XML responses. Producing the wrong format because of an Accept header mismatch is a subtle bug that appears during client integration.

title: Q: [Q36] How do you validate request input in Spring Boot?
text: [Q36] How do you validate request input in Spring Boot? Use Bean Validation by adding the spring-boot-starter-validation dependency and annotating DTO fields with constraints like @NotBlank, @Email, @Min, @Max, and @Valid. In the controller, annotate the request body parameter with @Valid so that validation runs before the method executes. Validation failures result in a MethodArgumentNotValidException, which can be handled globally to return a structured error response.

title: Q: [Q37] What is the purpose of CommandLineRunner and  ApplicationRunner?
text: [Q37] What is the purpose of CommandLineRunner and ApplicationRunner? Both interfaces let you run logic once the Spring application context is fully initialized. CommandLineRunner accepts raw command-line arguments as a string array, while ApplicationRunner exposes them as structured ApplicationArguments. They are commonly used for one-time setup, data seeding, or checking external dependencies at startup. Multiple runners can be ordered with @Order.

title: Q: [Q38] How do you package and deploy a Spring Boot application?
text: [Q38] How do you package and deploy a Spring Boot application? Spring Boot applications are typically packaged as an executable JAR using the Spring Boot Maven or Gradle plugin. The JAR includes the embedded server, so you deploy it by running java -jar app.jar. For containers, you can build a Docker image with a Dockerfile or use spring-boot:build-image to create an optimized image. Configuration is externalized through environment variables or mounted config files so the same artifact can run in any environment.

title: Q: [Q39] What is graceful shutdown in Spring Boot?
text: [Q39] What is graceful shutdown in Spring Boot? Graceful shutdown allows the application to finish processing active requests before stopping when it receives a termination signal. In Spring Boot 2.3+, you can enable it with server.shutdown=graceful. The embedded server stops accepting new requests, waits for in-flight requests up to a configured timeout, and then shuts down. This prevents dropped requests during rolling deployments and Kubernetes pod terminations.

title: Q: [Q40] What is the Spring Boot fat JAR, and how is it different from a  regular JAR?
text: [Q40] What is the Spring Boot fat JAR, and how is it different from a regular JAR? A fat JAR, also called an uber JAR, is a Spring Boot executable JAR that includes the application classes and all runtime dependencies, including the embedded server. A regular JAR usually contains only the project classes and needs external dependencies on the classpath. The fat JAR is self-contained, making deployment simple because you only need to run java -jar.

title: Q: [Q41] How do you add a custom Actuator endpoint?
text: [Q41] How do you add a custom Actuator endpoint? Create a class annotated with @Endpoint and a unique ID. Define operations using @ReadOperation, @WriteOperation, and @DeleteOperation. Register the class as a Spring bean. For example, a @ReadOperation method can return a custom metric or configuration value. Make sure the endpoint is exposed in management.endpoints.web.exposure.include and protected by security rules.

title: Q: [Q42] What is Micrometer, and how does it relate to Actuator?
text: [Q42] What is Micrometer, and how does it relate to Actuator? Micrometer is a metrics facade that provides a vendor-neutral API for recording timers, counters, gauges, and distributions. Spring Boot Actuator exposes Micrometer metrics through the /metrics endpoint and can ship them to backends like Prometheus, Datadog, CloudWatch, or Graphite. This lets you monitor application performance, throughput, and errors using the tools your organization already has.

title: Q: [Q43] How do you change the Actuator base path and port?
text: [Q43] How do you change the Actuator base path and port? You can change the Actuator web base path with management.endpoints.web.base-path=/admin, so endpoints appear under /admin/health instead of /actuator/health. You can also run Actuator on a separate management port with management.server.port=8081, which isolates operational endpoints from public application traffic and makes security rules easier to apply.

title: Q: [Q44] What is the difference between @Value and  @ConfigurationProperties?
text: [Q44] What is the difference between @Value and @ConfigurationProperties? @Value injects a single property using SpEL expressions, such as @Value("$ {server.port}"). @ConfigurationProperties binds a group of related properties to a typed bean, which is better for structured configuration like a datasource or an external API client. For example, app.mail.host, app.mail.port, and app.mail.username can bind to a single MailProperties class. @ConfigurationProperties supports relaxed binding and JSR-303 validation.

title: Q: [Q45] How do you implement pagination in a Spring Boot REST API?
text: [Q45] How do you implement pagination in a Spring Boot REST API? Add a Pageable parameter to the controller or repository method. Spring resolves Pageable from query parameters like ?page=0&size=10&sort=name,asc. The repository returns a Page that includes content, total elements, total pages, and metadata. Always cap the page size to prevent abuse and avoid returning huge responses that exhaust memory or slow serialization.

title: Q: [Q46] What is a DTO, and why should you return DTOs instead of entities  from controllers?
text: [Q46] What is a DTO, and why should you return DTOs instead of entities from controllers? A DTO is a plain object that carries only the data needed by the API consumer. Returning entities directly couples the API contract to the database schema, can trigger LazyInitializationException, may expose internal fields, and makes future schema changes risky. Mapping entities to DTOs inside the service layer gives full control over the exposed shape and avoids serialization problems.

title: Q: [Q47] How do you handle file uploads in Spring Boot?
text: [Q47] How do you handle file uploads in Spring Boot? Accept MultipartFile as a controller parameter, usually with @RequestParam("file") or @ModelAttribute. Use transferTo to save the file or stream it directly to cloud storage like S3. For large files, stream the content instead of loading it fully into memory, and configure spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size and max-request-size to enforce limits. Store files in external storage rather than local disk in containerized environments.

title: Q: [Q48] What is an API Gateway, and how does it fit with Spring Boot  microservices?
text: [Q48] What is an API Gateway, and how does it fit with Spring Boot microservices? An API Gateway sits in front of microservices and handles cross-cutting concerns like routing, load balancing, authentication, rate limiting, SSL termination, and request/response transformation. Spring Cloud Gateway is a common choice in the Spring ecosystem. Each Spring Boot service exposes its own endpoints, and the gateway routes client requests to the right service, centralizing security and observability.

title: Q: [Q49] How do you implement distributed tracing in Spring Boot?
text: [Q49] How do you implement distributed tracing in Spring Boot? In Spring Boot 3, use Micrometer Tracing with Brave or OpenTelemetry to propagate trace and span IDs across service calls. Add the tracing starter and configure an exporter such as Zipkin or Jaeger. The trace ID is included in logs via MDC, which helps you follow a request across multiple services. In Spring Boot 2.x this was often done with Spring Cloud Sleuth.

title: Q: [Q50] What are the key things to check before deploying a Spring Boot  application to production?
text: [Q50] What are the key things to check before deploying a Spring Boot application to production? Before production deployment, disable DevTools and Swagger UI or restrict them, secure Actuator endpoints, externalize secrets, set appropriate log levels, enable health checks, configure graceful shutdown, set reasonable connection and thread pool sizes, run integration tests, verify database migrations, and ensure the artifact is built with the production profile. Also confirm that metrics and centralized logging are wired up.

title: Q: [S1] A Spring Boot application starts up locally but fails to start in  production with a DataSource bean creation error. How do you  investigate?
text: [S1] A Spring Boot application starts up locally but fails to start in production with a DataSource bean creation error. How do you investigate? Start by reading the full stack trace to identify whether the failure is a missing URL, invalid credentials, unreachable host, driver not on the classpath, or a port/firewall issue. Compare the production application.properties or environment variables with the local configuration. Check that the correct profile is active and that secrets are being injected properly by Vault or the config server. Verify the database driver dependency is included in the production build. If the app runs in a container, confirm that the container can reach the database and that DNS resolution works. Finally, test connectivity with a simple JDBC connection from the production environment before restarting the application.

title: Q: [S2] A REST endpoint works in development but returns 404 in  production. What could be wrong?
text: [S2] A REST endpoint works in development but returns 404 in production. What could be wrong? A 404 usually means the request URL does not match any registered handler. Check whether the production base path or servlet context path is different, such as server.servlet.context-path=/api. Verify the HTTP method is correct and that the controller is in a package scanned by Spring. If there are multiple @RequestMapping variants or a conflicting global path configuration, they may behave differently under another profile. Also confirm that no security filter is blocking the request and returning a misleading 404 instead of 403.

title: Q: [S3] An API that handles file uploads starts throwing  OutOfMemoryError under load. How do you fix it?
text: [S3] An API that handles file uploads starts throwing OutOfMemoryError under load. How do you fix it? Loading the entire multipart file into memory for every request consumes heap space quickly. The fix is to stream the file directly to disk or cloud storage instead of keeping it in memory. Configure spring.servlet.multipart.file-size-threshold so large files are written to disk, and set max-file-size and max-request-size limits to reject oversized uploads. Use MultipartFile.getInputStream() and upload chunks to S3 or similar storage. In containerized environments, avoid writing to ephemeral local disk; send the stream straight to object storage.
