After scaling out, the application runs out of database connections and starts failing. How do you fix it?Running out of connections usually means the total pool size across all instances exceeds the database's maximum connection limit. I calculate the expected total connections by multiplying the per-instance HikariCP maximum pool size by the number of instances and compare that to the RDS max_connections setting. If the total is close to or above the limit, I reduce the per- instance pool size, because a larger pool on each instance does not help if the database cannot accept them. I also check for connection leaks caused by missing try-with-resources or long-running queries that hold connections. For read-heavy workloads, I add read replicas and route read traffic to them, which both scales capacity and reduces pressure on the primary. If connection demand is genuinely high, I scale the RDS instance class vertically to support more connections or use RDS Proxy to pool and share database connections efficiently. I avoid simply raising the pool size as the first fix, because that usually makes the problem worse. A production secret in AWS Secrets Manager is rotated, and the application stops working. How do you handle secret rotation without downtime?The problem is likely that the application reads the secret only at startup and never refreshes it, so when the secret value changes the cached credentials become invalid. I first restore service by restarting the affected instances or rolling back the secret rotation if possible. For a proper fix, I change the application to refresh the secret periodically or on authentication failure, using the AWS SDK to retrieve the latest value from Secrets Manager. When rotating database credentials, I use the Secrets Manager rotation feature that stores both the current and pending versions during the rotation window, so the application has time to pick up the new value while the old value remains valid. I also make sure the database user is not dropped immediately after rotation, because open connections from the old credential must finish their work. Ideally, the application uses IAM database authentication or RDS Proxy so password rotation is handled outside the application entirely. The key is that secret rotation should be graceful, not an all-at-once event.

title: 12.1.Key Concepts
text: What is Generative AI, and how does it differ from traditional AI?Generative AI refers to models that can create new content such as text, images, audio, or code by learning patterns from training data. Unlike traditional AI, which is often used for classification, regression, or clustering, generative AI produces outputs that resemble its training distribution. Large language models such as GPT, Gemini, and Claude are generative AI systems trained to predict the next token in a sequence. For backend developers, generative AI enables features like chatbots, code assistants, content generation, and summarization. The key difference is that traditional AI maps inputs to known labels, while generative AI synthesizes novel outputs conditioned on inputs. What is a Large Language Model, and how does it work at a high level?A Large Language Model is a deep neural network, typically based on the Transformer architecture, trained on vast amounts of text to predict the next token in a sequence. During training, the model learns statistical relationships between words, grammar, facts, and reasoning patterns. At inference, it takes a prompt, converts it into tokens, processes it through many layers of self-attention and feed-forward networks, and generates tokens one at a time until a stop condition is reached. The model does not truly understand text in a human sense; it predicts likely sequences based on learned patterns. Modern LLMs have billions of parameters and are ne-tuned for instruction following and safety. What is tokenization, and why does it matter?Tokenization is the process of splitting text into smaller units called tokens, which can be words, subwords, or characters. LLMs operate on tokens rather than raw characters, so the input text is first converted to tokens, and the model predicts the next token. The number of tokens affects cost, context window usage, and latency. Different models use different tokenizers, so the same sentence may produce different token counts across providers. For backend developers, tokenization matters because APIs charge per token, prompt length is limited by the context window, and output must often be bounded by max tokens. What is the Transformer architecture?The Transformer is a neural network architecture introduced in the paper Attention Is All You Need. It is built from encoder and decoder stacks, though modern LLMs typically use decoder-only variants. The core component is the self-attention mechanism, which allows the model to weigh the importance of different tokens in the input when producing each output token. Transformers also use positional encoding to understand token order, feed-forward layers to transform representations, layer normalization, and residual connections. The architecture is highly parallelizable during training and scales well to billions of parameters. What is self-attention, and why is it important?Self-attention is a mechanism that computes a weighted representation of each token by considering all other tokens in the sequence. For every token, the model generates query, key, and value vectors, compares the query with all keys to produce attention scores, normalizes them with softmax, and uses the weighted sum of values as the output. This allows the model to capture long-range dependencies and contextual relationships regardless of token distance. It is important because it replaces sequential processing with parallel computation and gives the model the ability to focus on relevant parts of the input when generating each token. What are embeddings in the context of generative AI?Embeddings are dense numerical vector representations of text, images, or other data. They map semantic meaning into a high-dimensional space where similar items are closer together. In LLMs, token embeddings represent individual tokens, while sentence or document embeddings capture the meaning of larger text chunks. Embeddings are produced by passing text through an embedding model. They are used for tasks such as semantic search, classification, clustering, recommendation, and retrieval in RAG systems. The quality of embeddings depends on the model and the domain of the training data. What is a vector store and vector search?A vector store is a database optimized for storing and searching high-dimensional vectors. Vector search, also called similarity search, nds vectors that are closest to a query vector using distance metrics such as cosine similarity or Euclidean distance. Popular vector stores include pgvector, Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, Milvus, and Redis. They often use approximate nearest neighbor algorithms such as HNSW or IVF to search efficiently at scale. In generative AI applications, vector stores are used to retrieve relevant documents or facts based on semantic similarity, which improves response accuracy beyond what the model memorized during training. What is Retrieval Augmented Generation?Retrieval Augmented Generation is a pattern that combines information retrieval with text generation. Instead of relying solely on the model's training knowledge, the system retrieves relevant documents from a vector store or database and includes them in the prompt as context. The model then generates an answer grounded in the retrieved content. RAG reduces hallucination, keeps responses up to date, and allows the model to answer questions about private or proprietary data. It is commonly used for enterprise knowledge bases, documentation assistants, and customer support bots. What is the difference between naive RAG and modular RAG?Naive RAG embeds the user query, performs a single similarity search against a vector store, and injects the top documents into the prompt. It is simple to implement and works well for many cases. Modular RAG breaks the process into con gurable stages such as query transformation, retrieval, document post-processing, and augmentation. It can use query rewriting, compression, translation, multi-query expansion, reranking, and hybrid search. Modular RAG is more exible and accurate but also more complex. Spring AI supports naive RAG through QuestionAnswerAdvisor and modular RAG through RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor. What is Spring AI?Spring AI is a framework that brings AI capabilities to Spring applications. It provides a consistent, portable API for working with large language models, embedding models, vector stores, and AI patterns such as RAG and tool calling. It supports multiple model providers including OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, Ollama, Azure OpenAI, and Mistral. Spring AI is designed with the same philosophy as Spring: abstractions over vendor APIs, easy configuration, and integration with Spring Boot. Backend developers can use it to add chat, search, classification, and agentic features to Java applications without writing provider-specific boilerplate. What is ChatClient in Spring AI?ChatClient is the high-level uent API in Spring AI for interacting with chat models. It allows developers to build prompts, set model options, attach advisors, register tools, and call or stream responses. It is constructed from a ChatModel bean and configured through the builder. ChatClient abstracts the underlying provider, so switching from OpenAI to Anthropic requires changing configuration rather than application code. It supports synchronous calls with call(), streaming with stream(), structured output with entity(), and content extraction with content(). How do you create a ChatClient in Spring Boot?You add the appropriate Spring AI starter, such as spring-ai-openai-spring-boot-starter, and configure properties like spring.ai.openai.api-key and spring.ai.openai.chat.options.model. Spring Boot auto-configures a ChatModel bean. You then create a ChatClient bean by calling ChatClient.builder(chatModel).build(). You can also set default advisors, default tools, and default options during construction. The ChatClient can be injected into services and used in REST controllers or background jobs. This pattern keeps AI logic inside Spring-managed services and integrates with the rest of the application. What are Advisors in Spring AI?Advisors are interceptors in the ChatClient request and response pipeline. They can modify the request before it reaches the model, process the response after it returns, or implement reusable patterns such as logging, chat memory, RAG retrieval, and tool calling. Advisors implement CallAdvisor or StreamAdvisor and run in a chain ordered by getOrder. Built-in advisors include MessageChatMemoryAdvisor for conversation memory, QuestionAnswerAdvisor for RAG, ToolCallingAdvisor for tool execution, and RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor for modular RAG. Custom advisors allow developers to add cross-cutting concerns like audit logging, prompt injection prevention, or cost tracking. How do Advisors compare to Spring AOP interceptors?Both Advisors and Spring AOP interceptors wrap behavior around a core operation. AOP intercepts method calls in the Spring application context, while Spring AI Advisors intercept calls to the language model. AOP is general purpose for business methods; Advisors are specialized for the ChatClient pipeline. In Spring AI, advisors receive ChatClientRequest and ChatClientResponse objects and can update context, modify prompts, or transform responses. They are specific to AI work ows and integrate with the ChatClient builder, whereas AOP interceptors work with any Spring bean method. What is chat memory, and how is it managed in Spring AI?Chat memory stores the history of a conversation so the model can maintain context across multiple turns. Without memory, each request is stateless and the model does not remember previous messages. Spring AI provides MessageChatMemoryAdvisor, which stores messages in an in-memory or persistent ChatMemory implementation. The advisor retrieves recent messages and adds them to the prompt. For longer conversations, VectorStoreChatMemoryAdvisor can retrieve semantically relevant past messages from a vector store. A conversation ID is used to scope messages to a specific user session. What is Tool Calling in Spring AI?Tool calling, also known as function calling, allows an LLM to invoke application methods to retrieve data or perform actions. In Spring AI, tools are defined by methods annotated with @Tool or by implementing ToolCallback. The ChatClient registers tools, and the ToolCallingAdvisor manages the loop: the model decides to call a tool, Spring AI executes the method, and the result is sent back to the model. The loop continues until the model produces a final response. Tool calling enables agents that can access real-time data, query databases, call external APIs, or take actions in the system. How do you define a tool in Spring AI?You create a method annotated with @Tool inside a Spring bean and pass the bean to ChatClient.defaultTools. The method should have a descriptive name and parameters, optionally with @ToolParameter annotations to describe them to the model. When the model needs the capability, it requests the tool with JSON arguments. Spring AI deserializes the arguments, invokes the method, and serializes the return value back to the model. Tools should be idempotent when possible and avoid side effects that cannot be undone. Complex tools may also use a ToolCallback implementation for full control over behavior and metadata. What is dynamic tool discovery in Spring AI?Dynamic tool discovery is a pattern for applications with many tools. Instead of sending all tool definitions to the model on every request, which consumes tokens and may confuse the model, a ToolSearchToolCallingAdvisor indexes tools and exposes a search tool. The model calls the search tool to find relevant tools, and only those tools are added to the context. This reduces token usage and improves accuracy when the application has dozens or hundreds of tools. Tool indexes can be semantic using VectorToolIndex, keyword-based using LuceneToolIndex, or pattern-based using RegexToolIndex. What is structured output in Spring AI?Structured output is the ability to ask an LLM to return data in a specific format, usually JSON, and map it to a Java object. Spring AI supports this through the entity() method on ChatClient, which parses the response into a POJO using a schema or instructions provided in the prompt. It can also use JSON mode or constrained decoding if the model supports it. Structured output is useful when you need to consume AI output programmatically, such as extracting entities, classifying text, or generating configuration objects. It is more reliable than parsing free-form text. What are query transformers in modular RAG?Query transformers rewrite or expand the user query before retrieval to improve search quality. Examples include RewriteQueryTransformer, which reformulates the query; CompressionQueryTransformer, which shortens conversational queries; TranslationQueryTransformer, which translates the query into another language; and MultiQueryExpander, which generates multiple related queries to broaden retrieval. These transformers help when user queries are ambiguous, conversational, or domain-specific. They are part of the RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor pipeline in Spring AI. What are common vector stores used with Spring AI?Spring AI supports several vector stores including pgvector, Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, Milvus, Redis, Neo4j, and Elasticsearch. The choice depends on existing infrastructure, scale, and query requirements. pgvector is convenient when the application already uses PostgreSQL. Pinecone and Weaviate are managed services optimized for vector search. Redis is useful when the application already uses Redis for caching. Milvus and Elasticsearch are suitable for large-scale or hybrid search scenarios. Each has a Spring Boot starter with auto-configuration. How do you ingest documents into a vector store for RAG?Ingestion typically involves reading documents from files or APIs, splitting them into chunks, computing embeddings for each chunk using an EmbeddingModel, and storing the resulting vectors and metadata in a VectorStore. Chunking strategy matters: too large chunks dilute relevance, while too small chunks lose context. Spring AI provides document readers, transformers, and splitters such as TokenTextSplitter and PagePdfDocumentReader. Metadata such as source, page number, or document type is stored alongside the vector to enable filtering. After ingestion, RAG queries can retrieve the most relevant chunks. What are hallucinations, and how can RAG reduce them?Hallucinations are cases where a language model generates plausible-sounding but incorrect or unsupported information. They happen because models are trained to predict likely tokens, not to verify facts against a knowledge source. RAG reduces hallucinations by grounding the model's response in retrieved documents. The prompt explicitly instructs the model to answer based only on the provided context. If the context does not contain the answer, the model can be instructed to say it does not know. Combining RAG with citations, reranking, and strict prompt instructions further improves factual reliability. What are prompt templates, and why are they used?A prompt template is a reusable text pattern with placeholders that are lled at runtime. It allows developers to separate prompt engineering from application code and to inject dynamic values such as user input, retrieved context, or conversation history. Spring AI provides PromptTemplate and a uent prompt builder. Templates improve maintainability because prompts can be adjusted without changing Java code. They also support system messages, user messages, and assistant messages, which help structure the conversation and guide model behavior. A Spring AI chatbot gives outdated answers about company policy. How do you fix it?The chatbot is relying on the model's training knowledge, which has a cutoff and may not include the latest company documents. I implement RAG by ingesting the company policy documents into a vector store. I split the documents into chunks, compute embeddings, and store them with metadata such as document name and effective date. I add a QuestionAnswerAdvisor to the ChatClient so each user query retrieves the most relevant policy chunks and includes them in the prompt. I instruct the model to answer only from the provided context and to say it does not know if the answer is not in the documents. I also schedule periodic re-ingestion when policies are updated. Real-time example: an HR chatbot tells users the old remote-work policy. I create a pipeline that loads the latest policy PDFs, splits them into 500-token chunks, embeds them with the default embedding model, and stores them in pgvector. The ChatClient uses QuestionAnswerAdvisor with a filter on the latest policy version. Responses now re ect current rules. A RAG application retrieves irrelevant documents. What do you improve?I investigate the retrieval pipeline end to end. I check chunk size and overlap; chunks that are too large dilute relevance, and chunks that are too small lose context. I try different embedding models ne-tuned for the domain. I add query transformation such as query rewriting or multi-query expansion before retrieval. I implement hybrid search combining vector similarity with keyword matching. I add a reranking step using a cross-encoder model that scores retrieved documents against the query. I also verify metadata filters are correct and that the vector store is populated with clean, representative documents. Real-time example: a legal assistant retrieves contracts instead of clauses. I reduce chunk size to 512 tokens with 128 overlap, switch to a domain-specific embedding model, add a keyword boost for clause titles, and use a reranker. Precision improves from 40 percent to 85 percent. A chatbot does not remember the previous question. How do you add memory?I add a chat memory advisor to the ChatClient. I configure MessageChatMemoryAdvisor with a ChatMemory implementation such as InMemoryChatMemory or a persistent store. I pass a conversation ID with each request so the advisor can retrieve and store messages for that session. For long conversations, I use VectorStoreChatMemoryAdvisor to retrieve semantically relevant past turns instead of sending the entire history. I also set a maximum history length to avoid exceeding the model's context window. Real-time example: a customer support bot forgets the user's order ID after one turn. I add MessageChatMemoryAdvisor to the ChatClient, generate a session ID when the conversation starts, and pass it in the advisors parameter. The bot now references earlier messages and asks clarifying questions only when needed. A Spring AI application needs to answer questions about live order status. How do you design it?I use tool calling to fetch live data. I define a @Tool method such as getOrderStatus(String orderId) that queries the order database or order service. I register the tool with ChatClient.defaultTools. When the user asks about an order, the model calls the tool with the order ID, the application returns the current status, and the model answers in natural language. I combine this with RAG for general policy or product questions. I also add validation to ensure users can only query their own orders, which prevents unauthorized data access. Real-time example: a user asks, Where is my order 12345? The ChatClient recognizes it needs order status, calls getOrderStatus("12345"), which returns {"status":"shipped","eta":"2025-08-10"}, and the model responds: Your order 12345 has been shipped and is expected to arrive on August 10. A model's tool call fails because arguments are malformed. How do you debug it?I inspect the chat response to see the exact tool name and arguments the model produced. Malformed arguments often happen when the tool description or parameter names are ambiguous. I improve the @Tool method name and add @ToolParameter descriptions so the model understands the expected format. I validate tool inputs and return clear error messages so the model can retry with corrected arguments. I also log every tool invocation and response for debugging. If the issue persists, I switch to a more capable model or use constrained output for critical tools. Real-time example: a weather tool receives dates in different formats. I update the @ToolParameter description to say ISO- 8601 date only, add input validation, and return an error like Invalid date format, expected YYYY-MM-DD when the format is wrong. The model corrects itself on the next call. An AI application exposes sensitive internal tools to the model. How do you secure it?I apply the principle of least privilege to tool registration. Only tools that are necessary for the use case are registered with ChatClient. Tools that perform destructive or sensitive operations require authorization checks inside the tool method, verifying the user's identity and permissions. I avoid registering admin-only tools on public-facing chatbots. I also add a SafeGuardAdvisor or custom advisor to review tool calls and block dangerous operations. Tools should be idempotent or reversible when possible, and all invocations should be logged for audit. Real-time example: a support chatbot could accidentally call a deleteUserAccount tool. I remove that tool from the public ChatClient, wrap remaining tools with permission checks that verify the caller is an authorized admin, and log every tool call with user ID and arguments. A Spring AI service streams responses to a frontend but the output is choppy. What do you check?I check whether the issue is on the model side or the network side. Streaming from the model returns tokens as they are generated, so latency is expected. Choppy output may be caused by buffering in the web layer or frontend. I ensure the Spring controller returns a Flux and does not collect the entire response before sending. I verify the client consumes the stream correctly with Server-Sent Events or WebFlux. I also check model parameters; some models or configurations buffer more than others. If the backend is buffering, I return the Flux directly and avoid calling block(). Real-time example: a React frontend receives the entire response at once. The Spring controller returns ResponseEntity by calling content(). I change it to return Flux from chatClient.prompt().user(question).stream().content(), and the frontend now shows typing in real time. A RAG pipeline returns too many chunks and exceeds the context window. How do you limit it?I reduce the number of top-K documents retrieved, typically to 3 or 5. I also reduce chunk size so more chunks t within the context window. I add a compression step using a query compressor to remove redundant content. I use a reranker to select only the most relevant chunks before injecting them into the prompt. I can also use a contextual query augmenter that summarizes or filters chunks. If the answer still needs more context, I implement a multi-turn strategy where the model asks follow-up questions to re ne retrieval. Real-time example: a technical documentation assistant retrieves 10 large chunks and over ows the context window. I set topK to 4, reduce chunk size to 300 tokens, and add a reranker. The prompt now ts and the answers remain accurate. A Spring AI application switches from OpenAI to Azure OpenAI. What changes are needed?Because Spring AI abstracts the model provider, the main change is dependency and configuration. I replace the OpenAI starter with the Azure OpenAI starter and update properties such as spring.ai.azure.openai.api-key, endpoint, and deployment-name. The ChatClient code, advisors, and tools usually remain unchanged. I verify that model capabilities such as JSON mode or tool calling are supported by the chosen Azure deployment. I also update environment-specific configuration without changing the application logic. This portability is one of the main benefits of using Spring AI. Real-time example: a company moves from OpenAI to Azure OpenAI for enterprise compliance. I change the Maven dependency, update application.yml with the Azure endpoint and deployment name, and rerun tests. The same service now calls Azure without any Java code changes. A custom advisor in Spring AI needs to log all prompts and responses. How do you implement it?I create a class that implements CallAdvisor or StreamAdvisor, depending on whether I need to intercept synchronous or streaming calls. In the around method, I access the ChatClientRequest, log the prompt text and metadata, proceed to the next advisor or model, receive the ChatClientResponse, and log the response content. I set an order value so the logging advisor runs early in the request chain and late in the response chain. I register it with the ChatClient builder as a default advisor. I avoid logging sensitive user data unless required and ensure logs comply with privacy policies. Real-time example: a compliance team requires an audit trail of AI interactions. I implement a LoggingAdvisor that writes prompt and response to SLF4J with conversation ID. I set order to LOWEST_PRECEDENCE so it wraps the entire chain, and I register it as a default advisor. Audit logs now contain request and response for every AI call. An application uses RAG but users get different answers for the same question. How do you stabilize it?Variability can come from the embedding model, the retrieval algorithm, the LLM, or the prompt. I fix retrieval by setting deterministic parameters such as a fixed top-K and a consistent similarity threshold. I use a reranker to make scoring more stable. I use a deterministic LLM with temperature set to 0 when consistency is more important than creativity. I version the prompt template and the document corpus so the same query produces the same answer against the same data version. I also add citations so users can verify the source. Real-time example: a support bot gives inconsistent refund policy answers. I set topK=5, temperature=0, add a reranker, and pin the embedding model version. I also include citations in the response. The same question now returns nearly identical answers. A tool-calling agent calls the same tool repeatedly in a loop. How do you stop it?I configure a maximum number of tool call iterations on the ToolCallingAdvisor or in application logic. I improve tool descriptions so the model understands when to stop. I add a guard advisor that detects loops and interrupts them. I ensure tools return concise, complete answers rather than partial data that forces repeated calls. For complex work ows, I break the task into explicit steps instead of relying on the model to coordinate everything through tools. Real-time example: an agent calling searchProduct repeatedly with slightly different keywords. I add a loop limit of 5, update the tool description to say call only once and re ne the query internally, and add an advisor that aborts if the same tool is called with the same arguments twice. The loop stops. A vector search returns results but the model ignores them and answers from its training data. How do you fix it?I improve the system prompt to explicitly instruct the model to answer only from the provided context and to say it does not know if the answer is not in the context. I make the retrieved context prominent in the prompt, usually by placing it before the user question. I use a clear delimiter such as "Context:" and "Question:" to help the model distinguish sources. I also reduce the temperature so the model is less creative and more grounded. If the model still ignores context, I switch to a model that follows instructions better or ne-tune with examples of grounded answers. Real-time example: a medical assistant answers with generic advice despite retrieved clinical guidelines. I update the system message to "You are a clinical assistant. Answer strictly from the provided guidelines. If the answer is not in the context, say 'I do not have that information.'" and place the context first. The answers now stick to the retrieved documents. A Spring AI application must handle high request volume. What do you optimize?I use streaming to reduce time-to- rst-byte and avoid blocking threads. I configure connection pooling for the model provider HTTP client. I cache similar requests where appropriate. I add rate limiting and backpressure to prevent overwhelming the provider API. For RAG, I precompute embeddings and keep the vector store query fast. I use smaller models for simple tasks and reserve large models for complex reasoning. I also consider running a local model such as Ollama for non-critical traffic to reduce latency and cost. Horizontal scaling of the Spring Boot service behind a load balancer handles concurrent users. Real-time example: a content generation API receives 1000 requests per minute. I enable streaming responses, add a 1000-request-per-minute rate limiter, cache prompts with deterministic parameters, and scale the service to ve replicas. Latency drops and costs remain controlled. A chatbot needs to switch language based on the user's message. How do you implement it?I can use a TranslationQueryTransformer before retrieval if the knowledge base is in one language. If the model itself supports multilingual responses, I detect the language from the user message, add a system instruction to respond in that language, and proceed normally. For RAG, I either translate the query to the document language before retrieval or retrieve documents in the detected language using metadata filters. Spring AI advisors make it easy to add a translation step in the modular RAG pipeline. Real-time example: users ask questions in English, Spanish, and German. I add a language detection step, append "Respond in the same language as the user" to the system prompt, and translate the query to English before vector search because the knowledge base is in English. The model answers in the user's language using retrieved English content. An advisor needs to add user identity context to every prompt. How do you write it?I implement a CallAdvisor and StreamAdvisor that retrieves the current user identity from the security context and injects it into the prompt or context map. In the advise method, I access the ChatClientRequest, extract the user principal, and either add a system message with user details or update the advisor context with user attributes. The next advisors and the model can use this context. I register the advisor early in the chain so subsequent advisors such as RAG or tool calling can also use the identity. I ensure sensitive user data is handled according to privacy requirements. Real-time example: an enterprise assistant needs to know the user's department to filter documents. I write a UserContextAdvisor that reads the authenticated user from Spring Security, adds a context parameter "department=engineering", and a later RAG advisor uses that parameter to filter vector search results. Responses are personalized and scoped. A RAG system needs to cite sources in its answers. How do you add citations?I include metadata such as source lename, page number, and URL in the retrieved chunks. In the prompt, I instruct the model to cite the source after each claim using the metadata provided. I use a structured prompt template that includes the context chunks with identifiers such as [1], [2], and asks the model to reference those identifiers. The post-processing step can map identifiers back to full source URLs and display them to the user. Spring AI's document metadata ows through the advisors, so the model has access to the citation fields. Real-time example: a documentation assistant needs to show source links. Each chunk has metadata {"source":"/docs/spring-ai.md","page":42}. The prompt includes these identifiers. The model answers: "Spring AI supports multiple model providers [1]." The frontend maps [1] to the URL and displays it as a clickable citation. A generative AI feature returns nonsensical output under load. What could be wrong?Under load, the issue may be context window over ow due to long prompts, concurrency limits on the model provider, thread exhaustion in the application, or cache inconsistency. I check the prompt size and reduce retrieved context or chat history. I monitor provider rate limits and errors. I verify that the Spring Boot application uses reactive or properly pooled resources and does not block event loops. I also check whether the model temperature or sampling parameters change behavior at scale if requests are batched differently. I add retries with backoff for transient provider errors and circuit breakers for provider outages. Real-time example: during a product launch, an AI summary endpoint returns garbled text. Investigation shows the vector store query times out under load and returns empty results, so the model answers without context. I add a cache for common queries, scale the vector store, and add a fallback that returns a default message instead of calling the model with no context. A Spring AI application needs to evaluate the quality of generated answers. How do you design evaluation?I implement an evaluation pipeline that compares generated answers against reference answers or ground truth. Metrics include relevance, faithfulness to retrieved context, answer correctness, and hallucination detection. I use LLM-based evaluators that score responses with structured output. I also collect user feedback with thumbs up or thumbs down and store it for analysis. I run evaluations on a held-out test set before deploying prompt or model changes. Spring AI provides evaluation utilities, and custom advisors can log inputs and outputs for offline analysis. Real-time example: a legal Q&A system needs to verify answer quality. I build a batch evaluator that sends 500 known questions to the chatbot, compares answers to reference answers using an LLM judge, and reports precision, recall, and faithfulness scores. Changes that drop scores below a threshold are blocked from deployment. A company wants to use a local open-source model instead of a cloud provider. How do you do it with Spring AI?I use Ollama or a similar local model server. I add the spring-ai-ollama-spring-boot-starter dependency and configure spring.ai.ollama.base-url and spring.ai.ollama.chat.options.model. Spring AI abstracts the provider, so ChatClient code remains the same. The local model runs on company infrastructure, keeping data on premises. This is useful for regulated data or cost control. The trade-off is that local models may be less capable than large cloud models, so they work best for simpler tasks or as a fallback. I also ensure the hardware has sufficient GPU or CPU resources to run the model efficiently. Real-time example: a healthcare company cannot send patient data to cloud LLMs. I deploy a Llama model with Ollama on an on-premise GPU server, configure Spring AI to point to the local Ollama endpoint, and use the same RAG and tool-calling advisors. Patient data never leaves the data center.

title: 13.1.Key Concepts
text: What is Aspect Oriented Programming, and why is it useful in a Spring Boot application?Aspect Oriented Programming is a programming paradigm that separates cross-cutting concerns from the main business logic. Cross-cutting concerns are requirements that cut across many modules, such as logging, transaction management, security, auditing, and caching. Without AOP, every service method would be cluttered with repetitive code for these concerns. AOP lets you define the concern once in an aspect and apply it declaratively to many join points. In a Spring Boot application, this makes the business code cleaner and easier to maintain, because the infrastructure behavior is woven in by the framework instead of being copied into every method. What are the core concepts of Spring AOP: aspect, join point, pointcut, advice, and weaving?An aspect is a modular unit that encapsulates a cross-cutting concern. A join point is a specific point in program execution where the aspect can be applied, such as a method execution or exception being thrown. A pointcut is an expression that selects which join points the advice applies to. Advice is the action taken by the aspect at a particular join point, such as logging before a method runs. Weaving is the process of applying aspects to the target objects to create an advised object. In Spring AOP, weaving happens at runtime using proxies, which is simpler than compile- time or load-time weaving but limited to method-level join points on Spring beans. What types of advice does Spring AOP support?Spring AOP supports five types of advice. @Before runs before the method execution. @AfterReturning runs after a method completes normally and can access the return value. @AfterThrowing runs if the method throws an exception and can access the exception. @After runs after the method regardless of outcome, similar to a finally block. @Around wraps the method execution entirely, letting the aspect decide whether to proceed, modify arguments, modify the return value, or catch and handle exceptions. @Around is the most powerful advice because it controls the full execution flow, but it is also the easiest to misuse because forgetting to call proceed() silently skips the target method. What is a pointcut expression, and how do you write one in Spring AOP?A pointcut expression selects join points using the AspectJ pointcut expression language. The execution() designator is the most common, for example execution(* com.example.service.*.*(..)) matches every method in the service package. You can narrow by return type, package, class, method name, and parameter types. Other designators include within() for matching types, this() and target() for matching proxy or target types, and @annotation() for matching methods that have a specific annotation. Pointcut expressions can be combined with &&, ||, and !. A poorly written broad pointcut can accidentally intercept too many methods and hurt performance or change behavior unexpectedly. How do you declare a pointcut using @Pointcut?You declare a reusable pointcut by annotating an empty method with @Pointcut and providing the expression. For example, @Pointcut("execution(* com.example.service.*.*(..))") public void serviceLayer() {}. Other advice methods can then reference this named pointcut, such as @Before("serviceLayer()"). This makes the aspect easier to read and maintain because the complex expression is written once and reused. It also makes it easy to change the matching logic in one place. The method that carries @Pointcut has no runtime behavior; it exists only to hold the expression. What is the difference between @Pointcut("execution(...)") and @Pointcut("@annotation(...)")?execution(...) matches methods based on their signature, such as package, class, method name, and parameters. It is useful when you want to apply advice to an entire layer, like all methods in the service package. @annotation(...) matches methods based on whether they are annotated with a specific annotation. This is useful when you want to opt methods in individually, such as applying a custom @LogExecutionTime annotation only to selected methods. The annotation-based approach is more explicit and less likely to accidentally match new methods added later, while the execution-based approach is better for layer-wide concerns. What is an @Around advice, and how do you write it?@Around advice wraps a method execution and is declared on a method that takes a ProceedingJoinPoint parameter. Inside the advice you can run code before and after joinPoint.proceed(), decide not to call proceed at all, catch exceptions, or change the return value. For example, an @Around advice can start a timer, call the method, then log how long it took. The advice must return an object compatible with the target method's return type. A critical mistake is forgetting to return the result of proceed(), which makes every intercepted method return null. Another mistake is swallowing exceptions inside the advice without rethrowing them, which hides failures from the caller. How does @Before advice differ from @AfterReturning advice?@Before advice runs before the target method starts and cannot prevent the method from running except by throwing an exception. It is useful for validation, logging inputs, or setting context. @AfterReturning advice runs only if the method completes successfully and gives access to the return value through the returning attribute. It is useful for post-processing results, caching return values, or logging successful outcomes. If the method throws an exception, @AfterReturning does not run, while @After or @AfterThrowing would be appropriate. Choosing the wrong advice type can lead to missing error handling or running cleanup too early. What happens if two aspects apply to the same join point, and how do you control their order?When multiple aspects advise the same method, Spring executes them in a specific order. By default the order is undefined unless the aspects implement the Ordered interface or are annotated with @Order. Lower order values run first, so an aspect with @Order(1) runs before an aspect with @Order(2). For @Around advice, the outermost aspect starts first, calls the next aspect, and finishes last, like nested method calls. If the order matters for correctness, for example a security aspect must run before a logging aspect, you should explicitly define @Order or implement Ordered. Without ordering, behavior can appear random and hard to reproduce. What is a join point signature, and how do you access method information inside advice?The join point signature is the metadata about the method being intercepted. In advice methods you can inject a JoinPoint parameter and call methods like getSignature(), getArgs(), getTarget(), and getThis(). The signature gives you the method name, declaring type, return type, and parameter types. This is useful for writing generic logging or auditing aspects that do not need to know the exact method in advance. For example, Signature signature = joinPoint.getSignature(); String methodName = signature.getName(); lets you log which method was called and with what arguments. Be careful not to log sensitive arguments such as passwords or tokens in a generic aspect. What is the difference between this() and target() in pointcut expressions?In Spring AOP, this() refers to the proxy object, and target() refers to the actual target object behind the proxy. Because Spring AOP creates a proxy around the bean, this and target are usually different references unless the proxy is not used. In practice, this() is rarely needed, while target() is used when you want to match methods based on the actual implementation type rather than the interface. For most developers, execution() and @annotation() cover the majority of use cases, and this() and target() are advanced tools used in specialized scenarios. What are the limitations of Spring AOP compared to AspectJ?Spring AOP is proxy-based and works only on Spring beans. It supports method-level join points but not field access, constructor execution, or static initializer join points. AspectJ, the full framework, supports all join points through compile-time or load-time weaving. Spring AOP is easier to configure and sufficient for most enterprise concerns like transactions and logging. AspectJ is more powerful but adds complexity to the build process. If you need advice on field access or non-Spring objects, AspectJ is the right choice. For typical Spring Boot applications, Spring AOP handles the common cross-cutting concerns without needing AspectJ. How does Spring AOP create proxies, and what is the difference between JDK dynamic proxies and CGLIB?Spring AOP creates proxies to wrap target beans and apply advice. If the target class implements at least one interface, Spring uses a JDK dynamic proxy that implements the same interfaces. If the target class does not implement an interface, Spring uses CGLIB to generate a subclass of the target. As of Spring Boot 2 and later, CGLIB is used even for interface-based targets if spring.aop.proxy-target-class is set to true, which is the default. This matters because proxy-based AOP cannot intercept calls made from within the same object, so self- invocation bypasses the aspect. Understanding the proxy mechanism is essential for debugging cases where advice does not run. What is the self-invocation problem in Spring AOP, and how do you avoid it?The self-invocation problem happens when a method in a bean calls another method on the same bean, and the second call bypasses the Spring proxy. Because Spring AOP applies advice through the proxy, internal calls do not go through the proxy and therefore do not trigger aspects. This commonly breaks @Transactional, @Cacheable, and @PreAuthorize. To avoid it, you can inject the same bean into itself through Spring, use AopContext.currentProxy() to obtain the current proxy, or refactor the logic so the called method resides in a separate bean. The cleanest solution is usually to split the responsibilities into separate Spring-managed beans. How do you enable and configure AOP in a Spring Boot application?Spring Boot auto-configures AOP when it detects spring-boot-starter-aop or spring-aop and AspectJ Weaver on the classpath. You create an aspect class with @Aspect and register it as a Spring bean, usually by adding @Component or by declaring it in a @Configuration class. If you are not using component scanning, you can import the aspect with @Import. The main application or a configuration class does not need @EnableAspectJAutoProxy in Spring Boot because the starter enables it automatically, but you can add it if you want to customize settings such as proxyTargetClass or exposeProxy. A common issue is forgetting to make the aspect a bean, which means it is never picked up by Spring. What are some real-world use cases for AOP in Spring Boot?Common real-world use cases include logging method entry and exit, measuring execution time for performance monitoring, retrying transient failures, auditing who changed what and when, applying declarative transaction management, caching results with @Cacheable, and enforcing security checks. AOP is also used for rate limiting, exception translation, and metrics collection. The value is that the business code stays focused on business rules while the infrastructure concern is applied transparently. However, AOP should be used for concerns that are truly cross-cutting; overusing it for one-off behavior makes code harder to trace. How would you use AOP to log method execution time?I create an aspect with a pointcut that matches the methods I want to measure, and an @Around advice that records the time before and after calling proceed(). For example, @Around("@annotation(com.example.annotation.LogExecutionTime)") lets me annotate specific methods, and the advice computes System.currentTimeMillis() before and after, then logs the difference. This avoids adding timing code to every method manually. I would also include the class and method name from the join point signature so the log is useful. I am careful not to log execution time for extremely high-frequency methods because the logging itself can become a bottleneck. How do you use AOP for audit logging in a Spring Boot application?I define an @Audit custom annotation and an aspect with an @AfterReturning or @Around advice that matches @annotation(com.example.annotation.Audit). The advice reads the authenticated user from SecurityContextHolder, the method name and arguments from the join point, the timestamp, and the outcome, and writes a record to an audit table or sends it to an audit service. This keeps every audited method clean because the annotation is the only marker needed. I avoid logging sensitive fields from method arguments and make the audit writer asynchronous if it involves an external system, so it does not block the main request. Can AOP be used for security, and what are the trade-offs?AOP can enforce security checks such as verifying roles, validating API keys, or checking ownership before method execution. A custom aspect can intercept service methods and reject unauthorized calls with an exception. The trade-off is that AOP security is method-level and can be bypassed by self-invocation, so it is not as robust as HTTP-layer security for web endpoints. For most cases, Spring Security's URL and method security are the better choice because they are well tested and handle many edge cases. AOP security is useful for custom domain-level checks that are not covered by Spring Security expressions, such as verifying that a user owns a specific resource before allowing deletion. How do you test code that uses Spring AOP aspects?For unit tests of the business logic, I test the target class directly without Spring, so the aspect is not involved. For integration tests, I load the Spring context and verify that the aspect is applied correctly, for example by checking that a logging aspect wrote a log entry or that a transaction rolled back on an exception. I use @SpringBootTest or a sliced test with the aspect imported through @Import. When testing @Around advice, I pay special attention to the case where proceed() is or is not called, and to exception propagation. I also test self-invocation scenarios to confirm that advice does not run when it is expected not to, because that is a common production surprise. What is the ProceedingJoinPoint interface, and when is it used?ProceedingJoinPoint is a subtype of JoinPoint available only in @Around advice. It extends JoinPoint with the proceed() method, which triggers the actual method execution. You can call proceed() once, or not at all, or even call it multiple times in special cases such as retry logic. You can also pass modified arguments to proceed(Object[] args) if you need to change the parameters passed to the target method. Because ProceedingJoinPoint controls the flow, misuse such as forgetting to call proceed() or swallowing exceptions can have wide impact across all intercepted methods, so it should be reviewed carefully. What is the difference between @After and @AfterReturning advice?@After advice runs after the method completes no matter what, whether it returns normally or throws an exception. It is typically used for cleanup, such as closing resources or releasing locks. @AfterReturning advice runs only when the method returns normally, and it can capture and inspect the return value. It is useful for post-processing successful results, such as transforming the return object or caching it. If you need both the return value and error handling, you should use @Around advice or a combination of @AfterReturning and @AfterThrowing. Using @After when you actually need the return value is a common mistake. What is load-time weaving, and when would you use it with Spring?Load-time weaving is a technique where AspectJ instruments classes as they are loaded by the classloader, allowing advice on a broader set of join points than Spring AOP's runtime proxies can handle. It requires a Java agent or a special classloader and is configured with @EnableLoadTimeWeaving. You would use it when you need aspects to apply to classes that are not Spring beans, to constructor execution, or to field access. It adds complexity to deployment because the agent must be attached to the JVM, so it should only be used when Spring AOP is genuinely insufficient. Most Spring Boot applications do not need load-time weaving. How do you avoid performance problems when using AOP heavily?The main performance risks come from broad pointcuts that match too many methods and from expensive advice such as heavy logging, synchronous external calls, or reflection. I keep pointcuts as specific as possible, using package names or custom annotations rather than wildcard patterns that match the entire application. I avoid logging large objects or serializing arguments in the advice. For heavy work like sending audit events, I offload it to an asynchronous mechanism. I also measure the overhead of the aspect in production-like load tests. AOP is powerful, but invisible overhead can accumulate across many intercepted methods, so it should be reviewed during code reviews and profiling. A logging aspect is supposed to log every service method call, but some calls are missing from the logs. How do you investigate?I start by checking the pointcut expression. If the pointcut is written too narrowly, for example matching only one package or excluding methods with certain parameter types, it will miss methods added outside that scope. I also verify that the affected bean is actually a Spring bean, because AOP works only on beans managed by the application context. The most common cause is self-invocation: a method inside the bean calls another method on the same instance, bypassing the proxy entirely. I confirm this by adding a small test that calls the method from outside the bean and from inside the bean, and comparing which calls trigger the advice. If self-invocation is the cause, I refactor the code so the internal call goes through a separate bean, or I use AopContext.currentProxy() if the method must stay in the same class. I also make sure the aspect class is registered as a Spring bean; an @Aspect class without @Component or an explicit bean declaration is ignored. A transaction rollback aspect works for direct service calls but fails when one service method calls another method in the same class. Why?This is the self-invocation problem. Spring applies transactions through a proxy, so the @Transactional advice runs only when a caller outside the bean invokes the method through the proxy. When a method inside the same bean calls another method, the call happens on the raw target object, not the proxy, so the transaction advice is bypassed. To fix it, I move the called method into a separate Spring bean and inject it, which ensures every call goes through a proxy. Alternatively, I can inject the same bean into itself using @Autowired, or use AopContext.currentProxy() to obtain the current proxy and invoke the method through it. The cleanest long-term fix is better separation of responsibilities so that self-invocation is not needed. An @Around advice for retry logic causes an infinite loop. What is the likely cause?An infinite loop in a retry aspect usually means the advice catches an exception and retries, but the retry call itself is being re-advised by the same aspect, which catches the same exception again and retries indefinitely. I fix this by limiting retries with a counter or by excluding the retry path from the pointcut, for example by checking a thread-local flag that indicates the call is already inside a retry. Another cause is retrying on exceptions that are never going to succeed, such as validation or authentication failures, so I make the retry logic selective about which exceptions warrant a retry. I also add exponential backoff and a maximum retry count, and I log each attempt so it is clear what is happening. A performance timing aspect makes the application noticeably slower. How do you optimize it?I first check the pointcut to see whether it matches too many methods, including high- frequency utility methods or getters. I narrow the pointcut, ideally using a custom annotation so only selected methods are timed. I also look at what the advice does: logging every method call with full argument serialization is expensive, especially for large objects or collections. I reduce the logging to just class name, method name, and duration in milliseconds. If the aspect performs synchronous I/O, such as writing to a database or sending metrics to an external system, I move that work to an asynchronous executor. I run a load test with and without the aspect to measure the overhead and decide whether the value of the timing data is worth the cost. Two aspects, one for logging and one for security, are running in the wrong order. How do you fix it?I implement the Ordered interface or add @Order to both aspects to define a clear execution order. Lower values run first, so I give the security aspect @Order(1) and the logging aspect @Order(2) if security must be checked before logging. For @Around advice, the order also affects which aspect wraps the other; the outermost advice starts first and finishes last. I avoid relying on the default order because it is not guaranteed and can change when new aspects are added. After setting the order, I write an integration test that exercises both aspects on the same method and verifies the expected sequence, for example by checking that a security exception is thrown before a log entry is written. An auditing aspect logs sensitive information such as passwords. How do you fix it?I update the auditing aspect to inspect the join point arguments and redact or skip fields marked as sensitive. This can be done by checking for annotations such as @Sensitive or @JsonIgnore on fields, or by maintaining a deny list of field names like password, token, and ssn. If the aspect currently serializes the entire argument object with toString() or Jackson, I replace that with a sanitized view. I also add a code review rule that any new audit aspect must be reviewed for sensitive data exposure. In some cases, the safest approach is to log only the action, the entity identifier, and the user identity, without logging the request payload at all. A caching aspect using @Around returns stale data because it does not invalidate the cache correctly. How do you debug it?I start by confirming whether the cache key is being built from the right method arguments and whether the same arguments produce the same key for both read and write operations. If the key includes a timestamp or a mutable object, it can change unexpectedly between calls. I check the @Around advice to make sure it stores the result in the cache after proceed() and returns the cached value on subsequent calls before calling proceed(). I also verify that the invalidation logic runs for update and delete methods, and that the cache key used for invalidation matches the key used for reads. A subtle issue is catching exceptions in the advice and returning a cached fallback; that can hide errors and serve stale data, so I make sure the fallback behavior is intentional and documented. A custom security aspect works for most methods but is bypassed when called from a test. Why?Tests that instantiate the service class directly with new do not go through the Spring proxy, so no AOP advice is applied. This is actually correct behavior, because the test is exercising the business logic without the infrastructure. If the test needs to verify the security aspect, it should load the Spring context and obtain the bean from the context, or use @SpringBootTest with the aspect imported. Another possibility is that the test uses @MockBean for a dependency and bypasses the real service entirely. I make sure the test suite has two kinds of tests: pure unit tests that do not involve AOP, and integration tests that verify AOP behavior through the proxy. A new developer adds a method to a service, and the logging aspect stops working for that method. What happened?The most likely cause is that the pointcut expression does not match the new method. For example, if the pointcut matches execution(* com.example.service.*.*(..)) and the new method is in a subpackage or returns a type that is excluded by the expression, it will not be intercepted. Another cause is that the new method is private or final, which cannot be proxied by CGLIB in some cases. I review the pointcut expression and the location of the new method, and I add the method to the matching scope or use a custom annotation to opt it in. I also consider whether the method is called internally, which would bypass the proxy through self-invocation. A good practice is to keep pointcut expressions broad enough for the intended layer but specific enough to avoid surprises. The application uses AOP for exception translation, but exceptions are swallowed and never reach the controller. How do you fix it?In an @Around advice that translates exceptions, the aspect probably catches the checked exception and returns a fallback or null instead of rethrowing a runtime exception. The fix is to catch the original exception and throw a meaningful unchecked exception that the controller's global exception handler can convert into a proper HTTP response. If the aspect is meant to translate a low-level exception such as DataAccessException into a domain exception, it should throw the new exception and let it propagate. I also make sure the pointcut does not match the exception handler itself, which could create a loop. Logging the original exception before translation is important so the root cause is preserved in the logs. A rate-limiting aspect is added, but it blocks legitimate traffic during bursts. How do you tune it?I first examine the rate limit parameters: the maximum number of requests, the time window, and whether the limit is global or per user. A global limit is easy to set too low for traffic spikes. I switch to per-user or per-API-key limits if the goal is to prevent abuse without hurting all users. I also consider using a token bucket or leaky bucket algorithm instead of a fixed window, because they handle bursts more gracefully. The aspect should return a clear 429 Too Many Requests response with a Retry-After header. I add metrics to track how often the limit is hit, and I tune the thresholds based on real traffic patterns rather than guesses. If the limit is backed by a shared store like Redis, I also check for latency from that store. A distributed tracing aspect adds a trace ID to outgoing HTTP calls, but the ID is missing in some downstream services. Why?The trace ID is probably being set in the aspect but not propagated consistently. If the downstream call is made inside a new thread or an async method, the trace ID stored in thread-local MDC may not be copied to the new thread. I fix this by using TaskDecorator to copy MDC values when tasks are submitted to an executor, or by using a context propagation library. Another cause is that the aspect intercepts only synchronous methods and misses calls made through RestTemplate, WebClient, or a Feign client that builds its own headers. In that case, I use an interceptor specific to the HTTP client instead of, or in addition to, the AOP aspect. I verify propagation with an integration test that checks headers across service boundaries. An aspect that sends audit events to Kafka is causing requests to slow down. How do you make it non-blocking?I move the Kafka producer call out of the request thread and into an asynchronous mechanism. The simplest approach is to wrap the audit writer in a method annotated with @Async and backed by a TaskExecutor. The aspect can then call the async writer after the main method completes, or use an ApplicationEventPublisher to fire an event and handle it asynchronously with @EventListener. A more robust approach is to write the audit event to a local queue or buffer and have a background worker batch-send events to Kafka. I also make the producer fire-and-forget for non-critical audit data, or use a separate thread pool with proper backpressure and error handling so the main request is never blocked by Kafka slowness. A pointcut meant to match repository methods also matches internal Spring Data methods, causing unexpected behavior. How do you narrow it?I narrow the pointcut by excluding Spring Data internal methods using execution(* com.example.repository.*.*(..)) && !execution(* org.springframework.data.repository.*.*(..)) or by targeting only specific custom repository interfaces. Spring Data generates many implementation methods at runtime, and broad pointcuts can match them. A better approach is to use a custom annotation such as @Audit on the repository methods that actually need advice, which avoids matching generated methods entirely. I also use within() to restrict matching to a specific interface or package. After changing the pointcut, I run a test that lists all matched methods to confirm that only the intended ones are intercepted. A method is annotated with @Transactional and a custom @Audit aspect, but the audit record is written even when the transaction rolls back. How do you keep them in sync?The audit advice runs independently of the transaction, so it writes the record regardless of the transaction outcome. To keep them in sync, I change the audit advice to participate in the transaction. One approach is to write the audit record through the same transaction by using @Transactional on the audit writer or by calling it from within the same service transaction boundary. Another approach is to publish an application event after the transaction commits successfully, using @TransactionalEventListener(phase = TransactionPhase.AFTER_COMMIT). This ensures the audit is recorded only when the transaction commits. If the audit must be independent of the database for reliability, I use a transactional outbox pattern: write the audit event to an outbox table in the same transaction, and have a background process publish it. A @Before advice is validating input, but the validation errors are not returned as proper API responses. How do you fix it?The @Before advice throws an exception, but the exception type is not mapped by the global exception handler, so the controller returns a generic 500 response instead of a structured 400 response. I create a dedicated domain exception such as InvalidInputException and throw it from the advice. Then I add a @ExceptionHandler or @RestControllerAdvice method that catches the exception and returns a ResponseEntity with a clear error message, field details, and HTTP 400 status. I also make sure the validation advice does not match the exception handler itself, which could intercept the response and alter it. For complex validation, I prefer moving the rules into the service layer or using Bean Validation with @Valid, because validation errors are easier to map to HTTP responses there. An aspect is supposed to run only in production, but it also runs during unit tests and breaks them. How do you control this?I make the aspect conditional on a Spring profile using @Profile("prod") or by making it active only when a specific property is set. In the test configuration, I exclude the aspect from component scanning or override the aspect bean with a no-op version. For pure unit tests that instantiate services directly, the aspect is not involved anyway, but for integration tests that load the full context, profile-based activation is the cleanest solution. I also consider whether the aspect truly needs to run in tests; if not, I add a test profile that disables it. Another option is to use @ConditionalOnProperty on the aspect bean so it is created only when app.audit.enabled=true, and keep it false in tests. A new @Around advice is added for request validation, and now some methods return null unexpectedly. Why?The most likely cause is that the @Around advice does not return the result of proceed(). If the advice returns a hardcoded value, a default value, or nothing at all, the caller receives that value instead of the real method result. I check the advice method to ensure it stores the return value of joinPoint.proceed() and returns it to the caller. Another cause is that the advice swallows an exception and returns null instead of rethrowing it. I add unit tests for the aspect itself and integration tests that verify the intercepted methods still return their expected values and throw their expected exceptions when the validation passes. An aspect collects metrics using Micrometer, but the metrics are not tagged with the method name. How do you improve the metric?I update the aspect to extract the method name and class name from the join point signature and add them as tags on the Micrometer timer or counter. For example, Timer.builder("method.execution").tag("class", className).tag("method", methodName).register(meterRegistry).record(duration) gives a breakdown per method. I am careful not to create an unbounded number of tags, because each unique combination of tag values creates a new time series in the monitoring backend. For methods with high cardinality arguments, I tag only the class and method, not the argument values. I also add a common tag for the application name and environment so metrics can be filtered in dashboards. A developer wants to use AOP to modify the return value of every controller method to wrap it in a common envelope. What are the risks?Wrapping return values globally with AOP is risky because it changes the contract that controllers expose to clients and other internal code. It can break existing tests, swagger generation, client SDKs, and response handling in the frontend. It also makes the response format inconsistent if some endpoints bypass the aspect, such as error responses from @RestControllerAdvice. A cleaner approach is to use a ResponseBodyAdvice implementation, which is designed specifically for transforming controller responses and integrates better with Spring MVC's message conversion. If AOP is used anyway, the pointcut must be carefully limited to the exact controller package, and the advice must handle void methods, exception paths, and streaming responses correctly. A caching aspect with @Around advice caches the result of a method that returns a Flux or Stream, and the caller gets a closed stream on the second invocation. How do you fix it?This happens because reactive types and Java streams can be consumed only once. The first call consumes the Flux or Stream and stores the exhausted result in the cache, so the next caller receives an already-consumed or closed object. To fix it, I change the design so the aspect does not cache the raw reactive type. For reactive code, I move caching into the reactive pipeline using operators or reactive cache libraries that understand backpressure and subscription. For streams, I collect the results into a list before caching if the dataset is small, or I redesign the method to return a repeatable data structure such as List. The deeper lesson is that AOP advice must understand the return type semantics; wrapping any return value blindly can break lazy or single-use types. A logging aspect works perfectly in a local Spring Boot test but logs nothing in production. What could be different?The first thing I check is whether the aspect class is actually a bean in the production context. If the aspect is in a package that is not covered by component scanning in production, or if it is conditional on a profile or property that is not active, it will not be registered. I also verify that the production pointcut matches the real package structure, because production packages may differ from test packages. Another possibility is that the production application uses a different proxy mode, such as spring.aop.proxy-target-class=false, which causes JDK dynamic proxies to be used for interface-based beans; if the aspect pointcut relies on the target class rather than the interface, it may not match. I add a startup bean that lists all aspects in the context and logs them, which helps verify whether the aspect is present. A transaction timing aspect measures a @Transactional method and reports that it always completes in under 10 milliseconds, even though the database query takes several seconds. What is wrong?The aspect is probably measuring only the time taken by the proxy dispatch, not the actual transaction. This can happen if the advice intercepts a method on the proxy but the real transactional work happens on a different thread or inside a lazy-loaded operation that executes after the method returns. Another common cause is that the aspect measures the outer method but not the inner @Transactional method, because self-invocation bypassed the transaction proxy. To fix it, I verify where the advice is attached and whether it uses joinPoint.proceed() correctly. For accurate measurement, I use Micrometer timers around the actual repository or service call, or I rely on Hibernate statistics and database metrics rather than a generic AOP timer that may miss the real work. A team adds AOP to enforce a cross-cutting rule, but debugging becomes difficult because the behavior is invisible in the code. How do you maintain readability?The invisible nature of AOP is both its strength and its risk. I maintain readability by using custom annotations as markers for the cross-cutting concern, so a developer can see that a method is @Audited, @Retryable, or @Timed just by reading the business code. I keep the aspects themselves small and focused, with clear names that describe the concern. I document the aspects in the project README or developer guide and list which packages or annotations they apply to. I avoid broad pointcuts that apply to half the application, because those are hard to discover. During code reviews, I treat aspects as first-class code and require the same test coverage as services. If a behavior is not truly cross-cutting, I prefer explicit code in the method over hidden magic. An @AfterThrowing advice is supposed to send an alert when a service method fails, but it fires for business exceptions that are expected. How do you refine it?The pointcut is too broad and matches every exception, including business validation errors that are normal outcomes. I refine the @AfterThrowing annotation with the throwing and optional argNames attributes, and inside the advice I check the exception type. I either rethrow only specific unchecked or system exceptions, or I configure the pointcut to exclude known business exception types. For example, I can define a marker interface such as BusinessException and only send alerts for exceptions that are not assignable to it. Another approach is to handle business exceptions in the controller layer and let only unexpected exceptions propagate to the aspect. This prevents alert fatigue and keeps the on-call team focused on real problems. A method annotated with both @Async and a custom AOP annotation behaves inconsistently: sometimes the aspect runs, sometimes it does not. Why?The behavior depends on which proxy handles which concern. Spring creates one proxy per bean, and a single proxy can handle multiple advisors, but the order and composition matter. If @Async is handled by one proxy and the custom aspect by another, or if one annotation is processed before the proxy is fully created, the result can be inconsistent. I verify that both concerns are applied to the same proxy by checking the bean at runtime with AopUtils.isAopProxy() and AopUtils.isCglibProxy(). I also ensure that the aspect and the async executor are configured to work together, for example by making the aspect run before @Async so it measures or audits the actual method invocation. If the interactions are too complex, I move one of the concerns into explicit code or a dedicated wrapper service to make the behavior deterministic. A pointcut that matches all public methods in the service layer also intercepts methods called from @Scheduled tasks, causing duplicate audit entries. How do you fix it?Scheduled tasks run through Spring's task scheduler, which also creates proxies, so the same pointcut can match methods invoked from scheduled tasks as well as from HTTP requests. To fix it, I refine the pointcut to exclude scheduled methods, for example by adding ! @annotation(org.springframework.scheduling.annotation.Scheduled) to the expression. Alternatively, I use a custom annotation that is placed only on the methods that should be audited, rather than matching the entire service layer. If scheduled tasks need different audit behavior, I create a separate aspect or a separate annotation for them. The broader fix is to design pointcuts around intent, not just location, so the same concern can be applied differently in different invocation contexts. An aspect that modifies method arguments with proceed(Object[] args) causes a ClassCastException in some calls. How do you debug it?The aspect is probably changing the argument array to a type that does not match the target method's parameter types, or the order of arguments is wrong. I start by logging the original argument types and the modified argument types inside the advice. I compare them to the method signature from the join point. The new arguments must be assignable to the corresponding parameter types, including primitive boxing and wrapper types. I also check whether the aspect is applied to overloaded methods, because the same modification may not be valid for every overload. If the modification is complex, I prefer moving the logic into the service method itself or using a dedicated decorator, because silently changing arguments through AOP can make method contracts hard to understand. A project uses AOP for declarative transaction management, and a new developer asks why a method is transactional even though it has no @Transactional annotation. How do you explain it?I explain that Spring AOP applies advice based on pointcuts, not only on explicit annotations.
