A transaction aspect may be configured to match all methods in the service layer using an execution(* com.example.service.*.*(..)) pointcut, or it may match a custom annotation that is placed at the class or interface level and inherited by methods. This is why the method appears transactional without its own annotation. I show the developer the aspect class and the pointcut expression so the behavior is visible. I also recommend adding a class-level annotation or an interface-level marker if the team wants to make the transactional boundary explicit, because hidden pointcuts can confuse new team members. A Spring Boot application starts slowly, and profiling shows that aspect initialization is taking a long time. How do you speed up startup?Aspect initialization can be slow if pointcut expressions are very broad or if the application has many beans and aspects. I speed it up by narrowing pointcuts so Spring does not have to evaluate complex expressions against every bean. I also reduce the number of beans that are scanned by moving aspects and their target packages to smaller, focused component scans. If many aspects use the same broad pointcut, I combine them or refactor them into a single aspect. I also consider whether some aspects can be disabled in production if they are only needed for debugging. Finally, I check whether the aspect class uses @Component and is being eagerly initialized; if an aspect has heavy dependencies, I make them lazy or move the heavy work out of the aspect constructor.

title: 14.1.Key Concepts
text: What is caching, and why is it important in a Spring Boot application?Caching is a technique that stores copies of frequently accessed data in a faster storage layer so future requests can be served without repeating the expensive operation that produced the data. In a Spring Boot application, caching reduces load on databases, external APIs, and CPU-intensive computations, which improves response times and lowers infrastructure cost. For example, instead of querying a slow report table every time a dashboard loads, the result can be cached for a few minutes. The trade-off is complexity: cached data can become stale, and cache invalidation must be handled carefully. Caching is most effective for read-heavy, stable data and least effective for data that changes constantly. What is the Spring Cache abstraction, and how do you enable it?The Spring Cache abstraction provides a consistent set of annotations such as @Cacheable, @CachePut, @CacheEvict, and @Caching that work with different cache providers. You enable it by adding @EnableCaching to a configuration class and including a cache provider dependency such as Caffeine or Redis. Spring Boot auto-configures a default cache manager when it detects a provider on the classpath. The abstraction lets you switch from an in-memory cache to a distributed cache without changing business code, because the annotations are provider-agnostic. A common mistake is enabling caching but not configuring a provider, which causes Spring to use a simple concurrent map that may not behave the same in production. What does @Cacheable do, and how do you use it?@Cacheable marks a method whose result should be stored in the cache. Before the method runs, Spring checks the cache for an entry matching the key; if found, it returns the cached value without executing the method. If not found, it executes the method and stores the result. You specify the cache name with value or cacheNames, and you can customize the key with key using SpEL expressions. For example, @Cacheable(value = "products", key = "#id") caches products by ID. A common pitfall is caching a method whose arguments or return value are not serializable when using Redis, which causes runtime errors. What is the difference between @Cacheable and @CachePut?@Cacheable reads from the cache first and only executes the method if the cache miss occurs. @CachePut always executes the method and then updates the cache with the returned value. @CachePut is useful after a create or update operation when you want to refresh the cached entry without waiting for the next read. For example, after updating a product, you can use @CachePut(value = "products", key = "#product.id") to refresh the cache immediately. The key difference is that @Cacheable can skip method execution, while @CachePut never does. What is @CacheEvict, and when would you use it?@CacheEvict removes entries from the cache. It is used when data changes and the cached copy is no longer valid. You can evict a single entry by key, for example when a product is updated, or all entries in a cache using allEntries = true. For example, @CacheEvict(value = "products", key = "#id") removes the cached product with the given ID. Eviction is critical because stale cache data is one of the most common production bugs. A common mistake is updating the database without evicting or refreshing the corresponding cache entry, which makes subsequent reads return old data. What is a cache key, and how do you build a good one?A cache key is the identifier used to store and retrieve a cached entry. Spring generates a default key from the method parameters, but for complex cases you can build a custom key using SpEL. A good key is unique, stable, and deterministic for the same input, and it should not include values that change on every request such as timestamps or random IDs. For example, @Cacheable(value = "orders", key = "#customerId + '_' + #status") creates a readable and consistent key. Bad keys lead to cache collisions or duplicate entries. When using Redis, keep keys reasonably short and avoid unbounded values such as user names. What are the default caching options in Spring Boot, and when is each appropriate?The simplest default is the ConcurrentMapCacheManager, which stores entries in memory using a ConcurrentHashMap. It is ne for local development and single-instance applications but does not share state across instances and has no eviction policies. Caffeine is a popular in-process cache with size and time-based eviction, and Spring Boot auto-configures it when caffeine is on the classpath. Redis is a distributed cache that shares state across multiple application instances and supports TTL and eviction. Choose the in-memory option for simple local caching and Redis when running multiple instances or needing persistence and shared invalidation. How do you configure Redis caching in Spring Boot?You add the spring-boot-starter-data-redis dependency and set spring.cache.type=redis along with spring.redis.host and spring.redis.port. Spring Boot auto-configures a RedisCacheManager that serializes cache entries using the configured Redis serializer, usually JdkSerializationRedisSerializer by default. You can customize TTL, cache names, and serialization through CacheProperties or by de ning a RedisCacheConfiguration bean. For example, you can set a default expiration of 10 minutes and per-cache overrides. A common issue is using default Java serialization, which is brittle and slow; many teams switch to JSON serialization with Jackson or GenericJackson2JsonRedisSerializer. What are cache TTL and eviction policies, and why do they matter?Time-to-live, or TTL, is the duration after which a cache entry expires automatically. Eviction policies decide which entries to remove when the cache reaches its maximum size, such as least recently used, least frequently used, or first-in- rst-out. TTL prevents stale data from staying in the cache forever, while eviction policies prevent unbounded memory growth. They matter because a cache without TTL can serve outdated information for hours or days, and a cache without eviction can consume all available memory. In Redis you configure TTL per key or per cache; in Caffeine you configure both TTL and maximum size. What is the cache-aside pattern, and how does Spring implement it?Cache-aside means the application is responsible for checking the cache, loading data on a miss, and updating or evicting entries. Spring's @Cacheable follows this pattern by checking the cache before method execution and storing the result on a miss. The application remains in control, which is simple but requires developers to remember to invalidate the cache when data changes. An alternative is write-through or read-through caching, where the cache itself manages data loading, but Spring annotations are built around the cache-aside model. The main risk of cache-aside is inconsistency if an update forgets to evict the cache. What is a database transaction, and what are the ACID properties?A database transaction is a logical unit of work that groups one or more database operations so that they either all complete successfully or none of them take effect. In a Spring Boot application backed by a relational database, transactions are essential whenever a business operation touches multiple rows or tables, because partial updates can leave the database in an inconsistent state. ACID is the set of guarantees that make transactions reliable in multi-user systems. Atomicity means the transaction is treated as a single indivisible unit: either every operation commits, or the entire transaction is rolled back to the state before it began. If a bank transfer debits one account but fails to credit another, atomicity ensures the debit is undone so money does not disappear. Consistency means every committed transaction leaves the database in a valid state that satis es all defined rules, constraints, triggers, and cascades. For example, if a foreign key constraint requires an order to have a valid customer, a transaction that inserts an order without a customer must fail. Isolation means concurrently executing transactions behave as if they were running one after another, even though they may actually run at the same time. Isolation prevents one transaction from seeing uncommitted changes from another and stops concurrent updates from corrupting each other's results. Durability means once a transaction commits, its changes are permanent even if the database server crashes immediately afterward, because the changes have been written to a persistent medium such as a transaction log or disk. In production, losing any of these properties causes real problems: without atomicity, partial payments or half-completed orders occur; without consistency, invalid data enters reports and downstream systems; without isolation, users see each other's un nished work; and without durability, committed data can vanish after a crash. What are the common transaction isolation levels, and what problems do they prevent?The four standard isolation levels are READ UNCOMMITTED, READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, and SERIALIZABLE. READ UNCOMMITTED allows dirty reads, where a transaction sees uncommitted changes from another transaction. READ COMMITTED prevents dirty reads but allows non-repeatable reads, where the same query returns different results within a transaction. REPEATABLE READ prevents non-repeatable reads but may allow phantom reads, where new rows appear between queries. SERIALIZABLE prevents all three problems by executing transactions as if they were sequential, but it is the slowest. The choice is a trade-off between consistency and concurrency. What are dirty read, non-repeatable read, and phantom read?A dirty read happens when a transaction reads data that another transaction has modified but not yet committed; if the other transaction rolls back, the read data was never valid. A non-repeatable read happens when a transaction reads the same row twice and gets different values because another transaction committed a change between the reads. A phantom read happens when a transaction runs the same query twice and gets different sets of rows because another transaction inserted or deleted rows that match the query. Understanding these phenomena is essential for choosing the right isolation level and for debugging data inconsistency issues in production. How does Spring Boot manage transactions with @Transactional?Spring Boot uses declarative transaction management powered by AOP. When you annotate a method or class with @Transactional, Spring creates a proxy that starts a transaction before the method, commits it on success, and rolls it back on runtime exceptions. By default, rollback happens only for unchecked exceptions and Error, not checked exceptions. The transaction boundary is usually the service layer, where business logic orchestrates multiple repository calls. A common mistake is annotating a private method, which cannot be proxied, or calling a @Transactional method from within the same class, which bypasses the proxy through self-invocation. What is the default propagation behavior of @Transactional?The default propagation is REQUIRED. If a transaction already exists, the method joins it. If no transaction exists, Spring creates a new one. This is the right choice for most service methods because it ensures that multiple operations within a business transaction are atomic. Other propagation levels include REQUIRES_NEW, which suspends the existing transaction and creates a new one; SUPPORTS, which participates if a transaction exists but runs non-transactionally otherwise; NOT_SUPPORTED, which suspends any existing transaction; MANDATORY, which requires an existing transaction; NEVER, which forbids a transaction; and NESTED, which creates a savepoint within an existing transaction. Misusing propagation can cause unexpected rollbacks or data inconsistency. What is the difference between REQUIRED and REQUIRES_NEW transaction propagation?REQUIRED joins the existing transaction if one exists, so the method participates in the caller's transaction and shares the same commit or rollback outcome. REQUIRES_NEW always creates a fresh transaction, suspending any existing transaction for the duration of the method. This is useful when you want a sub-operation to commit independently of the main transaction, such as logging an audit event even if the main transaction rolls back. The trade-off is that REQUIRES_NEW is more expensive and can lead to complex transaction states if overused. A common pitfall is assuming a REQUIRES_NEW method will roll back when the caller rolls back; it will not, because they are separate transactions. How does @Transactional handle rollback, and what is the default behavior for checked exceptions?By default, @Transactional rolls back the transaction only on unchecked exceptions, which are subclasses of RuntimeException, and on Error. Checked exceptions do not trigger a rollback unless you set rollbackFor explicitly, for example @Transactional(rollbackFor = Exception.class). This default surprises many developers when a method throws a checked exception and the database changes are committed. You can also use noRollbackFor to prevent rollback for specific runtime exceptions. I always review exception handling in transactional code and make the rollback policy explicit if the service throws checked exceptions. What is optimistic locking, and how is it implemented with JPA?Optimistic locking assumes that con icts are rare and does not lock the database row while reading. Instead, it uses a version column, annotated with @Version, that is incremented on every update. When a transaction tries to update a row, it checks whether the version still matches the value read earlier. If another transaction changed the row in between, the version differs and JPA throws an OptimisticLockException. The application then retries or reports the con ict. Optimistic locking works well for read-heavy environments with infrequent writes because it avoids the overhead of database locks. The version column must be included in the entity and never modified manually. What is pessimistic locking, and when should you use it?Pessimistic locking acquires a database lock on a row or table for the duration of the transaction, preventing other transactions from modifying the data. In JPA, you can request pessimistic locking with @Lock(LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE) on a query, or with findById(id, LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE). It is useful when contention is high and the cost of an optimistic lock failure and retry is greater than the cost of holding the lock, such as during inventory decrement or seat reservation. The downside is reduced concurrency and a higher risk of deadlocks. Pessimistic locking should not be used for read-mostly data because it unnecessarily blocks other transactions. What is the difference between LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_READ and PESSIMISTIC_WRITE?PESSIMISTIC_READ acquires a shared lock that prevents other transactions from writing the row but allows them to read it. PESSIMISTIC_WRITE acquires an exclusive lock that prevents other transactions from both reading and writing the row until the lock is released. The exact behavior depends on the database; some databases map both to the same exclusive lock. For most update scenarios, PESSIMISTIC_WRITE is the right choice because it guarantees that the current transaction can modify the row without con icts. PESSIMISTIC_READ is rarely needed and mostly used when you want to prevent lost updates while allowing concurrent reads. How do you handle OptimisticLockException in a Spring Boot application?When an OptimisticLockException occurs, the transaction is rolled back and the entity manager becomes invalid, so you cannot retry in the same transaction. The correct approach is to catch the exception at the controller or service facade level, fetch the latest version of the entity, reapply the business changes on the fresh data, and attempt the update again. A retry library such as Spring Retry can automate this with exponential backoff. You should also decide how many retries are reasonable and what to do if all retries fail, such as returning a con ict response to the user. The key is that retrying must happen outside the original transaction. What is the difference between programmatic and declarative transaction management?Declarative transaction management uses @Transactional annotations and AOP proxies, so the transaction boundary is declared and the framework handles it. Programmatic transaction management uses TransactionTemplate or the PlatformTransactionManager directly, giving the developer full control over when to begin, commit, or roll back. Declarative management is simpler and sufficient for most cases. Programmatic management is needed when the transaction boundary does not align with a single method, such as when committing inside a loop or handling partial failures. A common hybrid is declarative for normal cases and programmatic for exceptional complex ows. What is a transaction timeout, and how do you set it?A transaction timeout limits how long a transaction can run before it is rolled back. You can set it with @Transactional(timeout = 30), which means the transaction must complete within 30 seconds. This prevents long-running transactions from holding locks and consuming resources inde nitely. Timeouts are especially important for batch jobs or reports that may run unexpectedly long. The timeout applies to the entire transaction, not just a single query, so a method that calls many slow operations can exceed the limit even if each query is fast. I set timeouts based on the expected duration of the business operation and monitor for timeout failures in production. How do caching and transactions interact in a Spring Boot application?Caching and transactions can interact in tricky ways. If a method caches a value and then the transaction rolls back, the cached value may represent data that was never committed. If a method updates the database and evicts the cache, but the transaction rolls back, the cache remains evicted and the next read reloads from the database, which is safe but slower. To keep them consistent, cache writes should happen after the transaction commits, using @TransactionalEventListener(phase = TransactionPhase.AFTER_COMMIT) or by separating cache updates from transactional methods. Another option is to use a transactional outbox pattern where cache invalidation messages are written to the database in the same transaction and processed by a background worker. A dashboard loads slowly because it queries a large report table every time. How do you use caching to improve it?I first confirm that the report data changes infrequently enough to cache, for example hourly or daily. I add a @Cacheable annotation to the service method that builds the dashboard data, using a stable cache key such as the report date or a tenant identifier. I choose a cache provider based on the deployment: Caffeine for a single instance, Redis for multiple instances. I set a TTL that matches the business update frequency so users see fresh data without overwhelming the database. I also add a cache warm-up or background refresh so the first user after TTL expiry does not suffer a slow load. After deploying, I compare the database query count and dashboard response time before and after caching to verify the improvement. After updating a product in the admin panel, users still see the old price for several minutes. How do you fix the stale cache?The update method is probably missing a @CacheEvict or @CachePut annotation, or the TTL is too long. I add @CacheEvict(value = "products", key = "#product.id") to the update method so the cached entry is removed when the product changes. The next read will fetch from the database and repopulate the cache. If the product has related cached data, such as a category page or a search index, I may need to evict those caches too, possibly with allEntries = true if the relationship is complex. I also verify that the cache key used for eviction matches the key used by @Cacheable exactly, including argument names and serialization. Mismatched keys are a common reason stale data persists. A @Cacheable method works ne locally with Caffeine but throws serialization errors when moved to Redis. Why?Caffeine stores objects in memory as Java references, so it does not serialize the return values. Redis stores data as bytes and requires the cached value to be serializable. If the return object does not implement Serializable and the default JdkSerializationRedisSerializer is being used, the cache put operation fails. The fix is to either make the object serializable, switch to a JSON-based serializer such as GenericJackson2JsonRedisSerializer, or use a DTO that is designed for serialization. I usually prefer JSON for shared caches because it is readable, language- agnostic, and avoids the fragility of Java serialization. I also ensure that any nested objects are serializable and configure the serializer in RedisCacheConfiguration. Two microservices share a Redis cache, and one service reads data written by another in an incompatible format. How do you resolve it?The root cause is inconsistent serialization or cache key conventions across services. I first agree on a common data format, usually JSON with a well-defined schema or shared DTO classes, and a consistent cache key naming convention such as service:entity:id. Each service should use the same Redis serializer and key builder. If the services cannot share classes, I define a shared schema document and validate cache payloads against it. I also namespace cache names by service or bounded context to prevent accidental collisions. In extreme cases, I create a dedicated cache per service or use an event-driven approach where each service maintains its own cache from domain events. A method annotated with @Transactional commits partial data when an exception is thrown. Why?The exception is probably a checked exception, and by default @Transactional does not roll back for checked exceptions. Another possibility is that the exception is caught inside the method before it reaches the Spring proxy, so the proxy never sees it. I check the exception type and either change it to a runtime exception or add rollbackFor = Exception.class to the annotation. If the exception is swallowed in a try-catch block, I rethrow it or mark the transaction for rollback manually with TransactionAspectSupport.currentTransactionStatus().setRollbackOnly(). I also verify that the method is being called through the Spring proxy and not through self-invocation, because self-invocation bypasses the transaction advice entirely. A service method calls another @Transactional method in the same class, but the second method runs without a transaction. Why?This is the self-invocation problem. Spring applies @Transactional through a proxy, so internal method calls within the same bean bypass the proxy and run without transactional advice. To fix it, I move the transactional method into a separate bean and inject it, or I inject the same bean into itself so calls go through the proxy. Another option is to use AopContext.currentProxy() to get the current proxy and call the method through it. The cleanest long-term solution is to refactor the code so that transactional responsibilities are in their own service. I also add an integration test that veri es transactional behavior, because unit tests that instantiate the class directly will not catch this issue. A batch job processes thousands of records in a single @Transactional method and runs out of memory. How do you fix it?A single long transaction that accumulates thousands of entities in the persistence context causes memory growth because Hibernate holds every loaded and modified entity until commit. I refactor the job to process records in chunks and commit each chunk in a separate transaction. I can use REQUIRES_NEW propagation inside a loop or use TransactionTemplate to control commits programmatically. After each chunk, I clear the Hibernate session with entityManager.clear() to detach processed entities. I also add pagination so the job reads only a chunk at a time rather than loading all records. The trade-off is that the job is no longer one atomic transaction, so I add idempotency or a state machine so interrupted jobs can resume safely. A nancial transfer between two accounts loses consistency when two transfers happen at the same time. How do you handle it?This is a classic concurrency problem where simultaneous transfers can cause incorrect balances due to lost updates. I wrap the transfer logic in a @Transactional method and use pessimistic locking to lock both account rows before updating them, for example with findById(id, LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE). Locking both rows in a consistent order prevents deadlocks. If contention is low, optimistic locking with a @Version column can also work, but it requires retry logic when an OptimisticLockException occurs. I also validate balances within the same transaction and ensure the operation is atomic. The key is that the read, check, and update must happen inside a single protected transaction. A method with @Transactional(readOnly = true) is still writing to the database. Why?readOnly is a hint to the transaction manager and the underlying data access layer that the transaction should not modify data. However, it is not enforced by the database or Spring at the method level. If the code inside calls a repository save or update, the write still happens. Some JDBC drivers and Hibernate can optimize read-only transactions by ushing less aggressively, but they do not block writes. To enforce read-only access, I review the method to remove write operations, or I move read and write logic into separate methods with appropriate transaction settings. I also set readOnly = true on query methods as a best practice so the team knows the intended behavior. An OptimisticLockException keeps happening during order updates even with low traffic. What could be wrong?Persistent optimistic lock failures usually mean the entity is being loaded and held across multiple requests or that background processes are updating the same rows. I check whether the entity is stored in the HTTP session or a cache and reused across requests, which causes the version to become stale. I also check for scheduled jobs or event listeners that update order status without checking the version. If the entity is loaded in one transaction and updated in another, the version check will fail. The fix is to reload the entity at the start of the update transaction, apply changes to the fresh instance, and rely on JPA's automatic version increment. If con icts are genuinely frequent, pessimistic locking or a queue-based update model may be more appropriate. A service uses @Transactional on a method that calls an external REST API, and the transaction times out during API slowness. How do you fix it?Holding a database transaction while waiting for an external API is a bad practice because it keeps database locks and connections open for an unpredictable duration. I move the external API call outside the transactional method, for example by calling the API first and then starting the transaction to save the result, or by saving a pending record, committing, calling the API, and updating the record in a separate transaction. If the API call must happen inside the business ow, I use @Transactional(timeout = ...) or reduce the API client timeout to fail fast. The broader fix is to redesign the ow so that long-running external calls do not happen inside database transactions. A cache eviction runs after a database update, but the cache is not cleared because the transaction rolled back. How do you make cache eviction reliable?If cache eviction happens inside a method that later rolls back, the database changes are undone but the cache is already evicted, which is safe but slower. The more dangerous case is writing to the cache inside a transactional method and then rolling back, leaving stale data in the cache. To make eviction reliable, I move cache writes and evictions to after the transaction commits, using @TransactionalEventListener(phase = TransactionPhase.AFTER_COMMIT). This ensures cache changes happen only for committed data. Another approach is the transactional outbox pattern: write an invalidation event to an outbox table in the same transaction, and process it asynchronously to evict the cache after commit. A method with REQUIRES_NEW propagation is supposed to log an audit regardless of the main transaction outcome, but the audit is missing when the main transaction fails. Why?If the audit method is called through self-invocation, the REQUIRES_NEW annotation is ignored and the audit runs in the main transaction, which rolls back along with the main work. The fix is to move the audit method into a separate bean so the call goes through the Spring proxy and a new transaction is created. I also verify that the audit method does not throw an exception that propagates back to the caller, because an unchecked exception in the audit method can cause the new transaction to roll back. To make the audit truly independent, I catch and log audit exceptions within the audit method so they never affect the caller. A report query returns inconsistent results when run concurrently with updates. Which isolation level and strategy should you use?If the report sees partially committed data or changing values between rows, the isolation level is probably READ COMMITTED, which allows non-repeatable reads and phantom reads. For a consistent snapshot, I use REPEATABLE READ or a read-only transaction if the database supports consistent snapshots, or I run the report against a read replica where write locks do not affect it. If the report must run on the primary database and consistency is critical, SERIALIZABLE is an option but can hurt concurrency. Another approach is to run the report on data as of a specific timestamp using database snapshot features such as PostgreSQL's SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE or Oracle's ashback query. A pessimistic lock causes frequent deadlocks in a high-contention work ow. How do you resolve it?Deadlocks happen when two transactions acquire locks in different orders. I first ensure that all code paths lock resources in a consistent order, such as always locking the account with the lower ID first. If consistent ordering is not possible, I reduce the lock scope by shortening the transaction duration and moving non-essential work outside the transaction. I also consider switching to optimistic locking with retry logic if con icts are not extremely frequent. If contention is unavoidable, I introduce a queue or saga pattern so only one process updates the resource at a time. Monitoring deadlock exceptions in logs and database metrics helps identify the offending code paths. A cached list of products grows without bound and exhausts memory. How do you control it?An unbounded cache is dangerous because every unique query key adds a new entry. I configure a maximum size for the cache, for example spring.cache.caffeine.spec=maximumSize=1000,expireAfterWrite=10m. In Redis, I set a TTL so old entries expire automatically. I also review whether the cache key contains high- cardinality values such as timestamps or user IDs, which create a new entry for every request. If the list is large, I consider paginating the result and caching individual pages instead of the entire list. For very large datasets, caching becomes less effective and a search index or materialized view may be a better solution. A @Cacheable method is invoked inside another @Cacheable method in the same bean, and the inner cache is never used. Why?This is the self-invocation problem applied to caching. When the outer method calls the inner method directly on the same bean, the call bypasses the Spring proxy, so the @Cacheable advice on the inner method is not applied. The inner method runs every time without checking the cache. To fix it, I move the inner cached method into a separate bean and inject it, or I inject the same bean into itself so calls go through the proxy. I also consider whether both methods really need caching; sometimes only the outer call should be cached, and the inner call should always fetch fresh data. A method writes to the cache and then throws an exception, leaving the cache inconsistent with the database. How do you prevent this?Writing to the cache before the database is committed is risky because a later failure leaves stale cache data that may never expire. The fix is to write to the cache only after the transaction commits, using @TransactionalEventListener(phase = TransactionPhase.AFTER_COMMIT). Alternatively, I separate cache updates from transactional business methods and handle them in a non-transactional layer that runs after success is confirmed. If eventual consistency is acceptable, I use a short TTL so any inconsistency resolves quickly. The safest pattern is cache-aside with database as the source of truth and cache invalidation triggered by committed changes. A Spring Boot application starts with a NoCacheManagerBeanFoundException. What do you check?This exception means Spring cannot find a CacheManager bean. I first verify that @EnableCaching is present on a configuration class. Then I check the classpath for a cache provider dependency such as Caffeine, Redis, or EhCache. If multiple providers are present, Spring may get confused unless I specify spring.cache.type. If no provider is present, Spring Boot does not auto- configure a cache manager. I also check for bean naming con icts or a custom CacheManager that failed to initialize. In a test context, I may need to add a test cache manager or disable caching with spring.cache.type=NONE. A transaction rolls back unexpectedly after a method catches and handles an exception. Why?Even if the method catches the exception and returns normally, the transaction may already be marked rollback-only by a deeper layer. For example, if a repository call throws a DataIntegrityViolationException that is caught, Spring may have already set the transaction status to rollback-only. When the outer method tries to commit, the commit fails. To handle this, I catch the exception and decide whether to continue or explicitly roll back using TransactionAspectSupport.currentTransactionStatus().setRollbackOnly(). If the method intends to recover from the exception and commit, I need to ensure the failure happens in a separate transaction, for example by using REQUIRES_NEW for the risky operation. This is a common issue in batch processing where some records fail but others should still be processed. A distributed system uses @CacheEvict(allEntries = true) after every small update, causing cache thrashing. How do you improve it?Evicting the entire cache for every small update defeats the purpose of caching because it constantly invalidates useful entries and forces the database to handle all reads. I switch to targeted eviction using the specific key that changed, for example @CacheEvict(value = "products", key = "#productId"). If the update affects a computed list, I maintain a separate cache key for that list and evict only that key. For complex relationships, I use multiple cache names and evict only the relevant ones. In some cases, I use @CachePut to refresh a single entry rather than evicting it. The goal is to keep as much valid data in the cache as possible while removing only what is stale. A @Transactional method on a controller does not roll back. Why?@Transactional is typically meant for service-layer methods. Controllers are proxied by Spring MVC, but the transaction annotation may not take effect if the controller is not a Spring bean, if the method is not public, or if the controller is called outside the Spring-managed request ow. More importantly, controller methods often do database operations through a service that is already transactional, and adding @Transactional on the controller is not the right place. The best practice is to keep transaction boundaries in the service layer. I move the annotation to the service method, ensure the method is public, and verify that the service bean is obtained from the Spring context rather than instantiated directly. A cache key built from a user object does not work because the key changes after serialization. How do you fix it?If the cache key is derived from an object's toString() or default hash code, and the object contains fields that change after serialization, the key becomes inconsistent. I build the key only from stable identifiers, such as the user ID, and avoid using mutable objects or the entire object as the key. For example, @Cacheable(value = "preferences", key = "#user.id") is correct, while key = "#user" is risky. If multiple arguments are needed, I concatenate their IDs with a separator. I also ensure that the same key builder is used for both @Cacheable and @CacheEvict so reads and writes refer to the same cache entry. A database write under @Transactional appears successful, but the data is not persisted. Why?The most common cause is that the transaction manager used by @Transactional does not match the one used by the repository. For example, if there are multiple datasources and the annotation is using the default transaction manager while the repository writes to a secondary datasource, the transaction may not cover the write. Another cause is that the entity is detached and merge or save is not called. I verify the transaction manager configuration, check that the repository and service use the same EntityManager or DataSource, and ensure the method is called through the proxy. I also check whether the method is marked readOnly = true, which can prevent ush and commit in some configurations. A team wants to use Redis for caching but also for session storage and rate limiting. How do you manage the cache namespace?Using Redis for multiple concerns requires clear namespacing to prevent key collisions and to allow different TTL and eviction policies per concern. I use separate Redis databases or, better, key prefixes such as cache:, session:, and ratelimit: so each concern has its own namespace. Spring Cache uses cache names as part of the key, so I give each cache a descriptive name. I also configure separate serializers and TTLs per cache using RedisCacheConfiguration and RedisCacheManagerBuilderCustomizer. For sessions, I use Spring Session's RedisIndexedSessionRepository with its own prefix. Monitoring tools like Redis MONITOR or keyspace notifications help verify that keys are separated correctly.

title: 15.1.Key Concepts
text: What is a microservice architecture, and how is it different from a monolithic architecture?A microservice architecture builds an application as a collection of small, independent services, each owning a specific business capability and its own data store. A monolithic architecture puts all functionality into a single deployable unit with a shared database. Microservices enable independent deployment, scaling, and technology choices, but they add network complexity, operational overhead, and distributed-system challenges. A monolith is simpler to build and test early on, but it becomes harder to scale and maintain as the codebase grows. The right choice depends on team size, domain complexity, and operational maturity, not on blindly following trends. What are the advantages and disadvantages of microservices?Advantages include independent deployment of services, scaling only the parts that need more resources, fault isolation so one failing service does not bring down the entire system, freedom to choose the best technology per service, and team autonomy around bounded contexts. Disadvantages include distributed data consistency challenges, network latency and failures, operational complexity for deployment and monitoring, the need for service discovery and API gateways, and harder end-to-end testing. In production, the biggest challenge is usually not writing the services but operating them consistently. What is service discovery, and why is it needed in microservices?Service discovery is the mechanism that lets services find each other's network locations at runtime instead of relying on hard-coded IP addresses and ports. In microservices, instances come and go due to scaling, deployment, and failures, so static configuration breaks quickly. A service registry tracks healthy instances, and clients or a gateway query it before making calls. Without service discovery, every configuration change would require redeploying all consumers, which is not practical in a dynamic cloud environment. What is Net ix Eureka, and how does it work?Eureka is a service registry from Net ix, integrated into Spring Cloud through spring- cloud-starter-netflix-eureka-server and spring-cloud-starter-netflix- eureka-client. A Eureka Server runs a registry that accepts registrations from microservices. Each Eureka Client registers itself on startup and sends periodic heartbeats, usually every 30 seconds, to prove it is still healthy. If the server misses heartbeats for about 90 seconds, it removes the instance. Other clients can then fetch the registry and discover available instances by service name. Eureka supports client-side discovery, where the caller picks an instance, often with Spring Cloud LoadBalancer. What is the difference between @EnableEurekaClient and @EnableDiscoveryClient?@EnableEurekaClient is specific to Net ix Eureka and ties the application to that implementation. @EnableDiscoveryClient is a more generic annotation from Spring Cloud that works with any compatible discovery service such as Eureka, Consul, or ZooKeeper. If the classpath has a discovery implementation, @EnableDiscoveryClient auto-detects it. In modern Spring Cloud, the annotations are often unnecessary because auto-configuration detects Eureka on the classpath. However, understanding the difference matters when choosing between registries or when migrating from one to another. What is client-side service discovery versus server-side service discovery?In client-side discovery, the service client queries the registry directly, chooses an instance, and sends the request. Eureka with Spring Cloud LoadBalancer is client-side. In server-side discovery, the client sends a request to a load balancer or API gateway, and that intermediary queries the registry and forwards the request. Server-side discovery hides the registry details from clients and centralizes routing logic. Client-side discovery gives more control to the caller but couples it to the registry. Many architectures use both: a gateway handles server-side discovery from external clients, while internal services use client-side discovery through Feign. What is an API Gateway, and what problems does it solve?An API Gateway is a single entry point that sits between clients and backend microservices. It solves cross-cutting concerns such as request routing, authentication and authorization, SSL termination, rate limiting, load balancing, request and response transformation, caching, and logging. Instead of every service handling security and client-specific protocols, the gateway centralizes them. This reduces duplication and gives the backend services cleaner contracts. A common mistake is pushing business logic into the gateway; it should handle infrastructure concerns, not become another service layer. What is Spring Cloud Gateway, and how is it different from Net ix Zuul?Spring Cloud Gateway is the modern API Gateway in the Spring Cloud stack, built on Spring WebFlux, Project Reactor, and Netty, making it non-blocking and reactive. Net ix Zuul 1 was servlet- based and blocking. Spring Cloud Gateway supports route predicates, lter factories, load-balanced routing with lb://, rate limiting, circuit breaker integration, and actuator endpoints. It is the recommended replacement for Zuul, which is in maintenance mode. Because it is reactive, it can handle a large number of concurrent connections with fewer threads than a servlet-based gateway. What are the three main building blocks of a Spring Cloud Gateway route?A route in Spring Cloud Gateway consists of an ID, a destination URI, a collection of predicates, and a collection of filters. The predicate determines whether the route matches an incoming request. The filter modifies the request before it is proxied, the response before it is returned, or both. Multiple predicates on a route are combined with a logical AND, so all must match. The URI can be a direct HTTP URL, a load-balanced lb://service-name, or a forward: path for local fallback handling. What are common route predicates in Spring Cloud Gateway?Common predicates include Path for matching request paths, Method for HTTP methods, Header for request headers, Query for query parameters, Cookie for cookies, Host for host headers, After, Before, and Between for time-based routing, RemoteAddr for IP-based matching, and Weight for weighted traffic splits. For example, Path=/orders/** routes every request starting with /orders/. Predicates make it easy to implement blue-green deployments, A/B testing, and versioned routing without changing services. What are common GatewayFilter factories in Spring Cloud Gateway?Common filters include AddRequestHeader and AddResponseHeader for adding headers, StripPrefix for removing leading path segments, PrefixPath for prepending a path, RewritePath for regex-based path rewriting, SetPath for replacing the path, SetStatus for changing response status, Retry for retrying failed requests, RequestRateLimiter for rate limiting with Redis, CircuitBreaker for Resilience4J integration, and FallbackHeaders for passing exception details to a fallback. Default filters can also be configured so they apply to every route without repetition. How does load balancing work in Spring Cloud Gateway?Spring Cloud Gateway uses the ReactiveLoadBalancerClientFilter to resolve lb://service-name URIs. When a route uses uri: lb://payment-service, the gateway asks Spring Cloud LoadBalancer for a healthy instance of payment-service and forwards the request there. The default load balancing strategy is round-robin. If no instance is found, the gateway returns a 503 by default, but this can be changed to 404 with a property. This integration removes the need for clients to know instance locations and centralizes routing at the gateway. What is OpenFeign, and why is it used in microservices?OpenFeign is a declarative HTTP client that lets you define remote service calls as Java interfaces with Spring MVC annotations. Instead of writing boilerplate RestTemplate or WebClient code, you declare a method like @GetMapping("/payments/{id}") Payment getPayment(@PathVariable Long id) and Feign generates the implementation. It integrates with Eureka for service discovery, Spring Cloud LoadBalancer for client-side load balancing, and Resilience4J for fault tolerance. It reduces boilerplate and makes inter-service communication look like local method calls, though you must remember that network calls can still fail. How do you enable and configure OpenFeign in Spring Boot?You add spring-cloud-starter-openfeign to the classpath, annotate the main class or a configuration class with @EnableFeignClients, and declare an interface annotated with @FeignClient. The client name can be a service name registered in Eureka, in which case you omit the url attribute, or a fixed URL for direct calls. You configure timeouts, logging, and retry behavior in application.yml under spring.cloud.openfeign.client.config. Without @EnableFeignClients, Feign interfaces are not scanned and no beans are created. How does OpenFeign handle errors by default, and how can you customize it?By default, Feign throws a generic FeignException for any non-2xx response, which makes it hard for callers to distinguish between client errors and server errors. To customize error handling, you implement an ErrorDecoder bean. Inside the decoder, you map HTTP status codes to domain exceptions, such as 404 to NotFoundException and 503 to ServiceUnavailableException. You can also return a RetryableException for transient failures so Feign's retry mechanism kicks in. The decoded exception can then be handled by a global @RestControllerAdvice or by the calling service. How do you propagate headers such as authorization tokens or correlation IDs through OpenFeign?You use a RequestInterceptor bean. The interceptor's apply method runs before every Feign request and can add, remove, or modify headers. For example, you can read the current JWT from SecurityContextHolder or a request attribute and set it as the Authorization header. You can make the interceptor global so it applies to all Feign clients, or client-specific by setting configuration on @FeignClient. This is essential for maintaining security context and distributed tracing context across service calls. What is a circuit breaker, and why is it important in microservices?A circuit breaker is a fault-tolerance pattern that prevents cascading failures when a downstream service is unhealthy. It monitors calls to a service and opens the circuit when failures exceed a threshold, causing subsequent calls to fail fast without consuming resources. After a cooldown period, it enters a half-open state and allows a limited number of probe calls. If those succeed, the circuit closes and normal operation resumes. Without circuit breakers, a slow or failing downstream service can exhaust thread pools and cause the entire system to collapse. What are the states of a Resilience4J circuit breaker?Resilience4J circuit breakers have three states. The CLOSED state is normal operation, where all calls pass through and outcomes are recorded in a sliding window. The OPEN state rejects calls immediately with a CallNotPermittedException when the failure rate or slow call rate exceeds a threshold. The HALF_OPEN state allows a con gurable number of probe calls after a wait duration; if they succeed, the circuit closes, otherwise it opens again. The sliding window can be count-based or time-based, and it records successful, failed, ignored, and slow calls separately. What is the difference between Resilience4J and Net ix Hystrix?Net ix Hystrix is the older circuit breaker library and is now in maintenance mode, no longer supported in Spring Boot 3. Resilience4j is the modern, lightweight replacement designed for Java 8 and functional programming. It is modular, so you can use only the modules you need, such as circuit breaker, retry, rate limiter, bulkhead, and time limiter. Resilience4j integrates natively with Spring Boot 3, Micrometer, and Prometheus, while Hystrix did not. Resilience4j also supports both count-based and time-based sliding windows, which Hystrix lacked. What Resilience4J fault-tolerance modules exist, and what does each do?Resilience4J provides several modules. CircuitBreaker stops calls to a failing service to prevent cascading failures. Retry re-executes failed calls with con gurable backoff. RateLimiter limits the number of calls per time window. Bulkhead limits concurrent calls to isolate resource usage, with semaphore-based and thread-pool-based variants. TimeLimiter enforces a timeout on asynchronous calls returning CompletableFuture or CompletionStage. These modules can be combined, and the recommended order is bulkhead first, then circuit breaker, then retry, then time limiter, so resources are protected before failures are retried. What is the difference between a Resilience4J bulkhead and a rate limiter?A bulkhead limits the number of concurrent calls to a resource, protecting against thread pool exhaustion and resource starvation. A rate limiter limits the number of calls within a time window, protecting against bursts and enforcing quotas. A bulkhead throws BulkheadFullException when the concurrency limit is reached. A rate limiter throws RequestNotPermitted when the rate is exceeded. They solve different problems and are often used together: the rate limiter prevents a caller from overwhelming a service, while the bulkhead ensures a failure in one area does not consume all available threads. How do you configure Resilience4J in a Spring Boot application?You add the resilience4j-spring-boot3 or resilience4j-spring-boot2 starter along with spring-boot-starter-aop, because Resilience4J uses Spring AOP for its annotations. You annotate methods with @CircuitBreaker, @Retry, @RateLimiter, @Bulkhead, or @TimeLimiter, and you provide configuration in application.yml under resilience4j.circuitbreaker.instances, resilience4j.retry.instances, and similar keys. Each instance has properties such as sliding window size, failure rate threshold, wait duration, and max attempts. The annotations also support a fallbackMethod that must have the same parameters plus an optional Throwable parameter. What is an inventory management system in a microservices context, and how would you design it?In a microservices context, an inventory management system owns product stock levels, reservations, and availability as an independent service. It exposes APIs to check stock, reserve items for orders, release reservations, and adjust stock after ful llment. It typically has its own database and communicates with order and warehouse services via REST or events. Stock updates must be concurrency-safe, so operations often use optimistic locking with a version column or pessimistic locking for high-contention items. Events such as StockReserved or StockReleased are published to a message broker so other services can react asynchronously. What is the Saga pattern, and how does it relate to distributed transactions in microservices?The Saga pattern manages a long-running business transaction as a sequence of local transactions, each in its own service. If a step fails, the saga executes compensating transactions to undo earlier steps. There are two main styles. Choreography sagas use events: each service completes its work and publishes an event that triggers the next service. Orchestration sagas use a central coordinator that tells each service what to do and handles compensations. Sagas replace ACID transactions across services because distributed databases cannot easily guarantee isolation. The trade-off is eventual consistency and the complexity of compensation logic. A team wants to migrate a monolith to microservices. How do you decide where to start?I start by identifying bounded contexts in the monolith using domain-driven design, looking for parts of the domain that change together, have different scaling needs, or belong to different teams. A good first candidate is a capability that is not on the critical path for every request, such as notifications, recommendations, or inventory, so failure during extraction does not stop the entire business. I also look for parts with high deployment con ict or scaling pressure, because those give the fastest return on investment. I avoid starting with the user authentication, order placement, or payment ow unless absolutely necessary, because those are usually the most coupled and risky. The migration strategy is usually the Strangler Fig pattern: put an API Gateway in front of the monolith, gradually route traffic to new services, and retire the old functionality once the new service is stable. A microservice cannot find another service at runtime. How do you debug service discovery?I first check whether both services are registered in Eureka by looking at the Eureka dashboard or calling /eureka/apps. If the provider is missing, I verify that it has spring-cloud- starter-netflix-eureka-client on the classpath, that its application name is set, and that the Eureka Server URL is correct. I also check the heartbeat configuration, because if the instance does not send heartbeats, Eureka evicts it. If the consumer cannot discover the provider, I verify that the consumer fetches the registry and that the service name in the Feign client or load balancer matches the registered name exactly. Network issues such as security groups, wrong VPC, or DNS problems can also prevent discovery even when registration looks correct. A Spring Cloud Gateway route returns 503 even though the downstream service is healthy. What could be wrong?A 503 from the gateway usually means no healthy instance was found for the load-balanced route. I check that the route URI uses the correct lb://service-name and that the service is registered in Eureka or the static load balancer configuration. I also verify that the service health endpoint returns 200 and that the service is not marked as OUT_OF_SERVICE in the registry. If the gateway is running in a different namespace or network, I check that it can reach the service instances. By default, Spring Cloud Gateway returns 503 when no instance is found; I can switch this to 404 if that ts the API contract. Additionally, the downstream service might be rejecting the gateway's request due to missing headers or path mismatch, so checking downstream logs is important. An OpenFeign client throws a generic FeignException for every downstream error. How do you improve error handling?I implement a custom ErrorDecoder bean that maps HTTP status codes and response bodies to meaningful domain exceptions. For example, 404 becomes ProductNotFoundException, 400 becomes BadRequestException, and 503 becomes ServiceUnavailableException. For transient errors such as 502, 503, 504, or 429, I return a RetryableException so Feign's retry mechanism can try again. I also make sure the global exception handler maps these domain exceptions to appropriate HTTP responses so clients get useful error messages. Without this, every failure looks the same and the calling service cannot decide whether to retry, fail, or return a fallback. A circuit breaker opens too often during startup and trips normal calls. How do you tune it?Startup can produce failures due to JVM warm-up, connection pool initialization, or dependent services still starting. I increase the minimumNumberOfCalls so the circuit does not open based on a small number of noisy failures. I also set a reasonable slidingWindowSize and use a time-based window if failures are bursty. The failureRateThreshold should re ect real unhealthy behavior, not normal startup variance. I add a warmup or readiness check so traffic is only routed after the service is truly ready. For slow-start dependencies, I increase waitDurationInOpenState or use a longer timeout. Monitoring the circuit breaker metrics with Micrometer helps distinguish between startup noise and genuine problems. A Resilience4J retry keeps retrying a failed payment call and creates duplicate payments. What is the issue?Retrying non-idempotent operations like payments can create duplicates if the first attempt actually succeeded but the response was lost or delayed. The fix is to make payment operations idempotent by requiring a unique idempotency key from the client and rejecting duplicate keys on the payment service. The retry should only apply to idempotent operations or to transient failures where duplicates cannot occur. I also use the circuit breaker to stop retrying when the downstream is clearly unhealthy. In the retry configuration, I limit the number of attempts and use exponential backoff with jitter so retries do not all arrive at the same time. The most important rule is to retry only operations that are safe to repeat. An inventory service experiences race conditions when two orders try to reserve the same last item. How do you handle it?I use optimistic locking with a @Version column on the inventory row. When two transactions read the same row and try to update it, the first update increments the version and succeeds, while the second update fails with an OptimisticLockException because the version has changed. The second order can then retry, check stock again, and either reserve or return an out- of-stock response. For extremely high-contention items, I switch to pessimistic locking with LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE to serialize reservations, accepting the trade-off of lower concurrency. I also consider using a reservation queue or saga pattern so stock is reserved atomically as part of the order ow, and released if the order is canceled. A Spring Cloud Gateway route needs to rewrite the path before forwarding. How do you configure it?I use the RewritePath or StripPrefix filter. For example, if external clients call /api/v1/orders/123 but the downstream service expects /orders/123, I add filters: - StripPrefix=1 to remove /api/v1/, or filters: - RewritePath=/api/v1/?(? .*), /$\{segment} for more control. The rewrite happens at the gateway before the request is proxied, so the downstream service sees the path it expects. I test the route with actuator endpoints and with direct requests to make sure the rewrite works for all expected patterns, including trailing slashes and query strings. Path rewriting is common when versioning APIs at the gateway while keeping downstream paths stable. A gateway rate limiter blocks legitimate users after a traffic spike. How do you tune it?I first check whether the rate limit is global or per user. A global limit is easy to set too low and can block all users during a spike. I switch to a per-user or per-API-key limit using a custom KeyResolver, or I use a token bucket algorithm that allows short bursts while maintaining a steady rate. I configure replenishRate, burstCapacity, and requestedTokens based on real traffic patterns rather than guesses. I also add a fallback or queue mechanism and monitor how often the limiter rejects requests. If the spike is expected, such as a sale event, I scale the backend and raise limits temporarily instead of permanently throttling users. A microservice calls another service synchronously, and the caller slows down when the callee is slow. How do you improve resilience?I add timeouts so slow calls fail fast instead of holding threads. Then I add a circuit breaker around the call so repeated failures are detected and the circuit opens, returning a fallback or failing fast. I also add a bulkhead to limit how many concurrent calls can go to the downstream service, protecting the caller's resources. If the call does not need an immediate response, I switch to asynchronous messaging with Kafka or RabbitMQ, which decouples the services and removes the need for the caller to wait. The combination of timeout, circuit breaker, bulkhead, and asynchronous communication is the standard resilience toolkit for microservices. A Feign client works with Eureka in dev but fails in production with a connection error. What do you check?I check the url attribute on @FeignClient. If a hard-coded URL is left from dev testing, it will not use Eureka in production. I also verify that the production profile does not disable Eureka, that the service name matches exactly, and that the Feign client is not using a different service discovery implementation. I check network connectivity between the caller and Eureka, and between the caller and the target service instances. If load balancing is failing, I inspect Spring Cloud LoadBalancer configuration and confirm that spring.cloud.loadbalancer.ribbon.enabled is not set to true in a way that con icts with the modern load balancer. A gateway needs to authenticate requests before routing them. Where should the authentication logic go?The gateway is a good place to terminate authentication and add security headers for downstream services. I use Spring Security in the gateway to validate JWT tokens or OAuth2 access tokens, and then I forward relevant claims to downstream services using filters such as AddRequestHeader or TokenRelay. The downstream services can then trust the headers added by the gateway but should still validate signatures or perform their own authorization checks. I avoid putting complex business authorization logic in the gateway; that belongs in the services. Keeping authentication at the gateway centralizes security and prevents every service from handling token parsing. A service-to-service call needs to pass a correlation ID for distributed tracing. How do you implement it with Feign?I create a RequestInterceptor that reads the correlation ID from the current request's MDC or a request attribute and adds it as a header to every outgoing Feign request. The same interceptor also propagates the authorization token if needed. I make sure the receiving service extracts the correlation ID and adds it to its own MDC so logs across all services can be correlated. For asynchronous calls, I use a TaskDecorator to copy MDC values to new threads. If I am using Micrometer Tracing or OpenTelemetry, the propagation happens automatically through tracing instrumentation, but a custom Feign interceptor is still useful for legacy or non-standard headers. A microservices team is using synchronous REST calls everywhere, and deployments are tightly coupled. How do you reduce coupling?I introduce asynchronous event-driven communication for operations that do not need an immediate response. For example, after an order is placed, the order service publishes an OrderCreated event to Kafka, and the payment, inventory, and notification services consume it independently. This decouples the order service from the availability of the other services and allows each service to be deployed independently. For operations that still require a response, I keep synchronous calls but add circuit breakers, fallbacks, and clear service contracts. The goal is not to eliminate synchronous calls entirely but to use events where eventual consistency is acceptable. A circuit breaker fallback method is not being invoked. What could be wrong?I first check that the fallback method signature matches the original method exactly, including parameter types, and that it has an additional Throwable parameter at the end if the exception is needed. I verify that the method is public and in the same class as the annotated method. I also confirm that the call goes through the Spring proxy; if the method is called via self-invocation, the circuit breaker advice is bypassed. Another cause is that the exception is caught inside the method before it reaches the circuit breaker, or that the configuration for the circuit breaker instance is not loaded. I add tests that directly exercise the circuit breaker through the proxy and inspect the Resilience4J registry to confirm the instance exists. A Resilience4J bulkhead is configured, but under load the service still becomes unresponsive. Why?A bulkhead protects only the operations it wraps. If other parts of the service consume all threads or if the bulkhead limit is set too high, the service can still become unresponsive. I check whether the bulkhead is semaphore-based or thread-pool-based; for blocking I/O, a thread-pool bulkhead provides better isolation but requires the method to return CompletableFuture. I also verify that the bulkhead applies to the correct method and that calls go through the proxy. If the service is failing due to a different dependency, such as a database, the bulkhead around the HTTP client will not help. Proper monitoring of bulkhead metrics shows whether the limit is actually being hit. A microservice architecture needs centralized configuration. How do you handle it?I use Spring Cloud Con g Server backed by a Git repository to store environment-specific configuration. Each service fetches its configuration at startup from the con g server, and profiles such as dev, staging, and prod are separated. I also use Spring Cloud Bus with a message broker to push configuration refresh events to all services without restarting them. Services expose /actuator/refresh to reload configuration on demand. Secrets are not stored in plain text in Git; instead, I use encrypted values with Spring Cloud Con g encryption or store secrets in AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault and reference them from the configuration. Centralized configuration prevents configuration drift and makes environments reproducible. An order service calls a payment service, an inventory service, and a notification service to complete an order. How do you manage consistency if one call fails?I use the Saga pattern because a single ACID transaction across multiple services is not possible. In a choreography saga, the order service publishes events, and each service handles its own step and publishes the next event. If a step fails, a compensating event is published, such as PaymentFailed causing the order to be canceled and inventory reservation released. In an orchestration saga, a central orchestrator coordinates each step and invokes compensations directly. The orchestration style is easier to trace and reason about for complex ows, while choreography is more decoupled. I also make each local step idempotent so retries and compensations are safe. A microservice deployment causes a brief outage because dependent services are not ready. How do you handle startup ordering?Microservices should not require a strict startup order, because services come and go dynamically. I design services to be resilient to missing dependencies at startup by using retries, circuit breakers, and fallbacks. In Kubernetes, I add readiness probes so traffic is not routed to a service until it can handle requests. Liveness probes restart unhealthy pods. I also use init containers or job hooks for one-time dependencies such as database migrations, but I avoid waiting for other services to start because that creates fragile coupling. The correct approach is to make every service tolerate the absence of its peers temporarily. A gateway route needs to support blue-green deployment by routing 10 percent of traffic to the new version. How do you configure it?I use the Weight predicate to split traffic between two routes that point to different service versions or instances. For example, one route with Weight=group1, 9 points to the stable version, and another route with Weight=group1, 1 points to the new version, giving a 90/10 split. Both routes can use the same path predicate. I monitor error rates and latency for the new version, and if it is healthy, I gradually shift the weights. This canary-style deployment through the gateway reduces the risk of rolling out a bad version to all users. I also ensure that both versions are registered under the same or different service names depending on the service discovery setup. A microservice exposes many small REST endpoints, and consumers complain about too many network calls. How do you reduce chattiness?I introduce an API composition layer, either in the API Gateway or in a dedicated backend-for- frontend service, that aggregates multiple downstream calls into a single response. For example, a mobile app might need user profile, order history, and recommendations in one screen; instead of making three calls, it calls one composed endpoint. Another approach is to expand the domain boundaries so related data is returned together, though this must be done carefully to avoid creating a distributed monolith. Caching at the gateway or in the consumer also reduces repeated calls. The trade- off is that the composer becomes a new component to maintain, but it significantly improves client performance. A team wants to use gRPC instead of REST for internal communication. What are the trade-offs?gRPC uses HTTP/2 and Protocol Buffers, which provides smaller payloads, faster serialization, and strong service contracts through .proto files. It supports bi-directional streaming and is excellent for low-latency internal communication. The trade-offs are that gRPC is less human-readable than JSON, harder to debug with simple tools, and not as universally supported by browsers and rewalls. Contract changes require careful schema evolution. For external-facing APIs, REST or GraphQL is usually better, while gRPC shines for high-throughput service-to-service communication. The choice should be based on latency requirements, team familiarity, and ecosystem compatibility. A service registry like Eureka is unavailable. How do services continue to communicate?Eureka clients cache the registry locally and refresh it periodically. If the Eureka Server is unavailable, clients can still use their cached copy for a while. The refresh interval and eviction policy determine how long the cached copy remains useful. I also add health checks and retry logic so services can survive short registry outages. For longer outages, I consider a multi-node Eureka cluster for high availability, or a service mesh such as Istio that handles discovery independently. The key is that service discovery is a helper, not a runtime dependency for every single call, as long as clients cache and gracefully handle stale registry data. A gateway route with a circuit breaker filter forwards to a fallback controller. How do you pass the original failure details to the fallback?I use the FallbackHeaders filter, which adds headers such as Execution-Exception- Type, Execution-Exception-Message, Root-Cause-Exception-Type, and Root- Cause-Exception-Message to the forwarded fallback request. The fallback controller reads these headers and can return a meaningful error response or log the original cause. Without this filter, the fallback receives only the request path and standard headers, making it hard to explain why the circuit opened. I also make sure the circuit breaker name and fallback URI are configured correctly in the route definition. A team is debating whether to use an API Gateway or direct service-to-service calls for internal traffic. What do you recommend?For external client traffic, an API Gateway is almost always the right choice because it centralizes security, routing, and cross-cutting concerns. For internal service-to-service calls, direct calls through Feign or a service mesh are usually preferred because adding an extra gateway hop for every internal call increases latency and creates a single point of failure. However, in some organizations a dedicated internal gateway is used for policy enforcement and observability. My recommendation depends on the organization size and operational needs: small teams use a single gateway for external traffic and direct internal calls; large enterprises may add an internal gateway layer for governance.

title: 16.1.Key Concepts
text: What are API Gateway filters, and why are they important in a microservices architecture?API Gateway filters are components that process requests and responses as they pass through the gateway. They are important because they centralize cross-cutting concerns that every service would otherwise have to implement itself. Filters can modify requests before they reach downstream services, modify responses before they return to clients, reject requests, log traffic, add security headers, enforce rate limits, and handle circuit breaker fallbacks. In a microservices architecture, this centralization reduces duplication and makes it easier to enforce consistent policies such as authentication, logging, and traffic management across all services. What is the difference between a GatewayFilter and a GlobalFilter in Spring Cloud Gateway?A GatewayFilter is route-specific and is attached to individual routes through filter factories. It modifies requests or responses only for the routes where it is configured. A GlobalFilter applies to all routes and is used for cross-cutting concerns such as routing, load balancing, metrics, and logging. Both implement the same filter(exchange, chain) signature and participate in the same ordered filter chain. Spring Cloud Gateway merges route-specific GatewayFilter instances with global filters at runtime, ordered by Ordered. The highest precedence filter runs first in the pre phase and last in the post phase. What are default filters in Spring Cloud Gateway?Default filters are filters configured under spring.cloud.gateway.default-filters that apply to every route automatically. They are useful for adding common behavior such as a response header, a prefix path, or a correlation ID without repeating the filter in every route definition. For example, you can add a default response header that applies to all responses, or a default request header that downstream services can use for tracing. Default filters are merged with route-specific filters for each route, so they behave as if they were declared on every route. How do you write a custom GatewayFilterFactory in Spring Cloud Gateway?You extend AbstractGatewayFilterFactory where T is a configuration class that holds filter properties. The class name must end with GatewayFilterFactory, for example JwtAuthGatewayFilterFactory, and it is referenced in YAML by the short name JwtAuth. Inside the factory, you implement the apply method to return a GatewayFilter that performs the desired logic using ServerWebExchange. The filter can inspect or modify the request, decide whether to proceed, and transform the response. Custom filter factories are useful when a filter needs configuration per route and should behave like a built-in gateway filter. How do you write a custom GlobalFilter for authentication in Spring Cloud Gateway?You create a class that implements both GlobalFilter and Ordered, and register it as a bean. In the filter method, you inspect the incoming request, for example by reading the Authorization header, validate the token, and either continue the chain or return an unauthorized response. Because Spring Cloud Gateway is reactive, all operations must return Mono and avoid blocking the event loop. If authentication succeeds, you can add user information to the request headers so downstream services receive a trusted identity. Ordering matters: the authentication filter should run before routing filters so unauthenticated requests are rejected early.
