In a Spring Boot REST API, the main defense is never trusting user input and always escaping output. Use templating engines that auto-escape content. For JSON APIs, ensure the client escapes data before rendering it in HTML. Set a strong Content Security Policy header, use HttpOnly and SameSite cookies, and validate input on the server. Storing raw HTML from users and rendering it later is a common way XSS vulnerabilities appear.What is SQL injection, and how does Spring Data JPA help prevent it?: SQL injection is an attack where an attacker inserts malicious SQL into input fields to manipulate database queries. Spring Data JPA helps prevent it because JPQL and named parameters use prepared statements, separating code from data. A query written as SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.email = :email is safe, while concatenating user input into a native query is not. Even with JPA, native queries and dynamic SQL must use placeholders or the Criteria API. Input validation and least-privilege database accounts provide additional defense.What is the difference between authentication and authorization?: Authentication is the process of verifying who you are, for example by checking a username and password or a JWT signature. Authorization decides what you are allowed to do after you are authenticated, for example whether you can access /admin/reports. Authentication answers the question "Who is this user?" while authorization answers "Is this user allowed to perform this action?" Both are needed; knowing the user’s identity is useless unless the system also enforces access rules.What is a JWT, and what are its three parts?: JWT stands for JSON Web Token. It is a compact, self-contained token used to transmit identity and claims between parties. A JWT has three parts separated by dots: the header, which contains the signing algorithm and token type; the payload, which contains claims such as subject, issuer, expiration, and custom roles; and the signature, which proves the token was issued by a trusted party and was not tampered with. Because the token carries its own claims, a stateless server can verify it without storing session data.How do you create and verify a JWT token in a Spring Boot application?: Creation happens after the user proves their identity, typically in a login endpoint. You build a JWT with a library such as JJWT or Nimbus, set claims like subject, issued-at, expiration, and roles, and sign it with a secret key or a private key. Verification happens in a custom filter or authentication provider that reads the Authorization header, parses the token, validates the signature with the same secret or public key, and checks that the token has not expired. If valid, the filter populates the SecurityContext with an authenticated Authentication object.What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric signing for JWT?: Symmetric signing uses a single shared secret to both sign and verify the token. It is simple but means every service that verifies tokens must possess the secret, increasing exposure if one service is compromised. Asymmetric signing uses a private key to sign and a public key to verify. The authentication service keeps the private key, while other services only need the public key. Asymmetric signing is preferred in microservices because verification services cannot forge tokens even if they are breached.Where should you store the JWT signing key, and why does it matter?: The signing key must never be hard-coded in source control or plain property files. It should be stored in a secret manager such as HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or Spring Cloud Config with encryption. For asymmetric keys, the private key stays only in the token issuer, and public keys are distributed to verification services. If a secret key leaks, an attacker can forge valid tokens, which is a complete authentication bypass until all keys are rotated.How do you implement login and sign-up using JWT in Spring Boot?: For sign-up, the controller receives user details, validates them, hashes the password with BCryptPasswordEncoder, and saves the user. For login, the controller receives credentials and uses AuthenticationManager to validate them. If authentication succeeds, a service creates a JWT containing the user ID, roles, and expiration. The token is returned to the client, which sends it in the Authorization: Bearer <token> header on subsequent requests. A custom filter intercepts protected requests, validates the token, and sets the authentication in the SecurityContext.How does a client use a JWT to access protected endpoints?: After receiving the JWT from the login response, the client stores it securely, for example in memory or a secure cookie, and sends it in the Authorization header as Bearer <token> with every protected request. The server extracts the header in a filter, strips the Bearer prefix, validates the signature and expiration, and uses the claims to build an Authentication object. If the token is missing, malformed, expired, or signed with the wrong key, the server returns 401.What is the difference between an access token and a refresh token?: An access token is short-lived, usually minutes to an hour, and grants access to protected resources. A refresh token is long-lived and is used only to obtain a new access token when the old one expires. The access token travels with every API request, so keeping it short limits the window of misuse if it is stolen. The refresh token is stored more securely, rotated on use, and can be revoked independently. Storing refresh tokens in plain browser storage is a common mistake that makes theft easy.How do you implement a refresh token flow securely?: When the user logs in, issue both an access token and a refresh token. Store a hash of the refresh token in the database along with the user ID, device identifier, and expiration. When the access token expires, the client sends the refresh token to a dedicated refresh endpoint. The server hashes the incoming token, looks it up, verifies it has not expired or been revoked, and issues a new access token and a new refresh token, invalidating the old refresh token. This rotation limits the impact of token theft. Revocation happens by deleting the stored token hash.What is session management in Spring Security, and how do you make it stateless?: Session management controls how user sessions are created and tracked. By default, Spring Security creates a session for browser applications and stores the authentication server-side. For JWT-based REST APIs, you set sessionCreationPolicy(SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS) in the SecurityFilterChain. This tells Spring Security not to create or use sessions; every request is authenticated independently using the JWT. Stateless authentication is easier to scale horizontally because no session affinity or shared session store is needed.What is the difference between roles and authorities in Spring Security?: A role is a coarse-grained grouping of permissions, such as ROLE_ADMIN or ROLE_USER. An authority is a finer-grained permission, such as user:read, user:write, or report:delete. Internally Spring Security treats both as strings in the GrantedAuthority collection. The hasRole expression automatically prepends ROLE_, so hasRole("ADMIN") checks for ROLE_ADMIN. hasAuthority("ADMIN") checks for the exact string. Using authorities gives more granular control and is preferred when permissions vary across users with the same role.How do you implement role-based authorization in Spring Security?: You can enforce roles at the URL level in the SecurityFilterChain using requestMatchers("/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN"). You can also enforce them at the method level with @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')") or @Secured("ROLE_ADMIN"). The user must have the matching role stored in their GrantedAuthority list, typically populated from the database when the UserDetails object is built. Be careful that hasRole adds the ROLE_ prefix automatically, so storing ADMIN in the database and checking hasRole("ADMIN") will not match unless you configure the prefix or use hasAuthority.How do you implement granular authorization with authorities and permissions?: Granular authorization maps specific actions to authority strings rather than broad roles. For example, an invoice module might use authorities like invoice:read, invoice:write, and invoice:approve. You load these permissions from a database when building the UserDetails, and enforce them with @PreAuthorize("hasAuthority('invoice:approve')"). For dynamic rules, you can write SpEL expressions that check method arguments, such as @PreAuthorize("hasAuthority('invoice:write') and #userId == authentication.principal.id"). This gives much finer control than role-based checks alone.What is the difference between @Secured, @PreAuthorize, and @PostAuthorize?: @Secured checks roles before method execution and only supports role names. @PreAuthorize is more expressive because it supports SpEL expressions, allowing you to check roles, authorities, method arguments, and custom conditions. @PostAuthorize runs after the method executes and can filter the returned object, for example @PostAuthorize("returnObject.owner == authentication.name"). @PreAuthorize is the most commonly used for method-level authorization. Modern Spring Security uses @EnableMethodSecurity instead of the older @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity.Why might method security annotations not work even though they look correct?: Method security annotations only work when the method is invoked through a Spring- generated proxy. If a method annotated with @PreAuthorize is called from another method in the same bean, the proxy is bypassed and the security check is skipped. This is a common and dangerous mistake. The fix is to move the secured method to another bean and inject it, or use AopContext.currentProxy(). Also verify that @EnableMethodSecurity is present and that the expression syntax matches the actual authority strings.What is OAuth2, and how does the Authorization Code flow work?: OAuth2 is a delegation protocol that lets a user grant a third-party application limited access to their resources without sharing their password. In the Authorization Code flow, the user clicks login, the application redirects the browser to the OAuth2 provider, the user authenticates and consents, and the provider redirects back with an authorization code. The backend exchanges this code for an access token and optionally a refresh token. The access token is then used to call the provider’s APIs or to establish an identity. The flow is secure because the user’s password never reaches the client application.How do you implement Google OAuth2 client authentication in Spring Security?: Add spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client and register Google as a provider in application.yml with client ID, client secret, scope, and redirect URI. Expose a SecurityFilterChain that uses oauth2Login() to handle the Authorization Code flow. Spring Security manages the redirect to Google, the code exchange, and the userinfo call. You can map the returned user details to local application roles using an OAuth2UserService. For mobile or single-page apps, use PKCE and keep the client secret on the backend only.What is the difference between OAuth2 Login and OAuth2 Resource Server?: OAuth2 Login makes your application an OAuth2 client that lets users sign in through an external provider such as Google or GitHub. It handles redirects and cookies or sessions. OAuth2 Resource Server makes your application an API that accepts bearer tokens, typically JWT, and validates them. It is used when a mobile app, SPA, or another service calls your API with an access token. A single application can be both a client for login and a resource server for its own APIs.How do you handle Spring Security exceptions properly?: Spring Security throws AuthenticationException when the user cannot be authenticated and AccessDeniedException when the user lacks permission. You handle these by configuring an AuthenticationEntryPoint for 401 responses and an AccessDeniedHandler for 403 responses inside the SecurityFilterChain. For REST APIs, return structured JSON with a clear message and correlation ID rather than redirecting to an HTML page. A global @RestControllerAdvice is useful for application-level exceptions, but security exceptions are caught by the filter chain before they reach the controller.How do you debug a 403 Forbidden response in Spring Security?: Start by checking the logs with logging.level.org.springframework.security=DEBUG to see which filter rejected the request and why. Verify the request reached the right SecurityFilterChain. Confirm the user is authenticated and that the Authentication object contains the expected authorities. Check whether the path matcher is correct and whether hasRole or hasAuthority matches the stored authority string exactly. If the issue is method-level, ensure the method is being called through a Spring proxy and that @EnableMethodSecurity is active.What is UserDetailsService, and how is it used?: UserDetailsService is an interface with a single method, loadUserByUsername, that Spring Security calls to fetch user data during authentication. Your implementation typically queries the database and returns a UserDetails object containing username, password, enabled status, and authorities. This interface separates user retrieval from authentication logic. A common mistake is returning null or throwing a generic exception when the user is not found; the correct behavior is to throw UsernameNotFoundException so Spring Security can handle it consistently.What is PasswordEncoder, and why should you use BCryptPasswordEncoder?: PasswordEncoder is the Spring Security interface for hashing and verifying passwords. BCryptPasswordEncoder uses the bcrypt algorithm, which automatically salts and stretches passwords to resist brute-force and rainbow-table attacks. You never store plaintext passwords. During login, the encoder hashes the submitted password and compares it to the stored hash. Bcrypt also has a configurable strength parameter that controls how slow the hash is, letting you increase cost as hardware improves.How do you secure passwords and secrets in a Spring Boot application?: Passwords and secrets must be externalized from source code and configuration files. Use environment variables for non-sensitive values and a secret manager such as Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or Spring Cloud Config with encryption for credentials. Rotate keys and passwords periodically. For JWT signing keys, use asymmetric keys and keep the private key only in the issuer. Never log secrets, and avoid returning them in Actuator endpoints or error responses.What is Remember-Me authentication, and when should you avoid it?: Remember-Me authentication allows a user to remain logged in across browser sessions using a persistent token stored in a cookie or database. It improves convenience but increases risk because a stolen remember-me cookie can grant long-term access. It should be avoided for sensitive applications such as banking or admin portals. If used, store the token securely, bind it to the user’s IP or device fingerprint, and give users a way to revoke all sessions.How do you test Spring Security in a Spring Boot application?: Use MockMvc with @AutoConfigureMockMvc and @WithMockUser to simulate authenticated users. Use @WithAnonymousUser for unauthenticated scenarios. Test both the happy path and the denied path by checking the status code. For method security, verify that methods protected by @PreAuthorize reject users without the required authority. For JWT, test the filter behavior by generating a valid token in the test and sending it in the Authorization header. Integration tests should also verify that the security configuration does not accidentally expose sensitive endpoints.What is HttpSecurity vs WebSecurity in Spring Security?: HttpSecurity configures HTTP-level security such as URL access rules, form login, logout, CSRF, session management, and filters. WebSecurity is used for web-level configuration that is not request-specific, such as ignoring certain static resources like CSS, JavaScript, and images so they do not pass through the security filter chain. In Spring Boot 3, WebSecurityCustomizer is exposed as a bean for this purpose. Confusing the two can lead to static resources being unnecessarily secured or protected endpoints being accidentally ignored.What is the purpose of ExceptionTranslationFilter?: ExceptionTranslationFilter sits near the end of the security filter chain and catches AuthenticationException and AccessDeniedException thrown by other filters or access decision managers. It delegates to the configured AuthenticationEntryPoint to start authentication or to the AccessDeniedHandler to report authorization failure. Without this filter, security exceptions would bubble up as raw errors. It is why a missing token results in a 401 and an insufficient role results in a 403.What is CORS, and how does it interact with Spring Security?: CORS, or Cross-Origin Resource Sharing, is a browser mechanism that controls whether a web page can request resources from a different domain. Spring Security can enforce CORS through the SecurityFilterChain using cors() with a CorsConfigurationSource bean. If you enable CORS at the security layer and also at the controller layer, the more restrictive of the two may apply. Allowing all origins with credentials enabled is dangerous because it lets any website make authenticated requests on behalf of users.How do you implement logout in a JWT-based stateless application?: True logout is tricky in a stateless system because the server does not track tokens. The common approach is token blacklisting: store the token or its identifier in a deny list such as Redis until it expires, and check every request against this list. Alternatively, keep access tokens very short-lived and revoke refresh tokens on logout. The client must also delete the token from its storage. Without server-side revocation, a stolen token remains valid until it expires.How do you secure service-to-service communication in a microservices architecture?: Service-to-service calls should use short-lived tokens, mutual TLS, or OAuth2 client credentials. Each service authenticates to a token issuer, receives an access token, and presents it when calling downstream services. The receiving service validates the token and enforces scopes or authorities. Mutual TLS adds an extra layer by verifying both client and server certificates. Use service mesh sidecars such as Istio or Linkerd if you want encryption and identity managed at the infrastructure layer rather than in application code.What is an API Gateway’s role in security?: An API Gateway sits in front of microservices and centralizes cross-cutting security concerns such as SSL termination, token validation, rate limiting, IP allow-listing, and request routing. It can validate JWTs or OAuth2 tokens and forward enriched headers to downstream services. However, the gateway is not a substitute for service-level security; each service should still enforce its own authorization rules so that internal traffic or a compromised gateway cannot bypass protection.What are the risks of storing JWTs in browser local storage?: Local storage is accessible to JavaScript, so any XSS vulnerability allows an attacker to steal the token and impersonate the user. The safer approach is to store the access token in memory and keep the refresh token in an HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookie. If cookies are not an option, use short-lived access tokens and strict Content Security Policies to reduce the XSS attack surface. Tokens stored in local storage should never be used for highly sensitive applications.How do you protect against brute-force login attacks?: Implement account lockout or progressive delays after repeated failed attempts. Use CAPTCHA after a threshold of failures. Rate-limit the login endpoint by IP or username using a tool such as Bucket4j, Resilience4j, or an API gateway. Log and alert on unusual login patterns. Strong password requirements and BCryptPasswordEncoder slow down offline cracking if the database is ever breached.What is the principle of least privilege, and how do you apply it in Spring Security?: The principle of least privilege means giving users and services only the minimum permissions they need. In Spring Security, this translates to using specific authorities instead of broad roles, denying access by default, and granting access only to required endpoints. Instead of giving every user ROLE_ADMIN, create fine-grained authorities like order:read, order:write, and order:delete. Regularly audit roles and permissions, and remove unused access during access reviews.How do you configure HTTPS for a Spring Boot application?: Place an SSL certificate and private key in the application or load them from a secret manager. Configure server.ssl.enabled=true, server.ssl.key-store, server.ssl.key-store-password, and related properties. In production, HTTPS is often terminated at a load balancer, reverse proxy, or API gateway rather than the application itself. If termination happens upstream, configure the proxy to forward the original protocol so Spring Security redirects and cookies work correctly.What is the difference between permitAll, authenticated, anonymous, and denyAll?: permitAll allows any request, including unauthenticated ones. authenticated requires a valid authentication. anonymous matches requests from users who have not logged in but have been assigned the default anonymous authentication object. denyAll blocks every request. These are used to build the access matrix in the SecurityFilterChain. A common error is using permitAll on health endpoints but accidentally exposing sensitive data through them.How do you handle token expiration gracefully in a client-server JWT flow?: The server returns 401 with an error indicating the token is expired. The client checks for this specific error and uses the refresh token to request a new access token from a dedicated refresh endpoint. If the refresh token is also expired or revoked, the client redirects the user to login. The server should not extend access token lifetimes automatically, because that defeats the purpose of short-lived tokens and increases the window of misuse if a token is stolen.What is GrantedAuthority, and how is it different from a role?: GrantedAuthority is the interface that represents a permission assigned to a user, usually implemented as SimpleGrantedAuthority. A role is technically an authority that starts with ROLE_, and hasRole expects that prefix. An authority is any permission string. The difference is mostly convention: roles are broad, authorities are fine-grained. You can store both in the same collection and check them with hasRole, hasAuthority, or custom SpEL.How do you implement multi-factor authentication in Spring Security?: Multi-factor authentication requires a second factor beyond the password. After the user enters the password, the system verifies the second factor, such as a TOTP code from an authenticator app, an SMS code, or a hardware key. In Spring Security, this is usually implemented with a custom authentication flow that stores a partially authenticated principal in the session, prompts for the second factor, and then marks the authentication as fully authenticated. Commercial identity providers such as Keycloak or Auth0 handle this out of the box and can be integrated as an OAuth2 provider.What are the most important security checks before releasing a Spring Boot application?: Before release, ensure HTTPS is enforced, CSRF is configured correctly for the client type, security headers are set, secrets are externalized, passwords are hashed with bcrypt, Actuator endpoints are restricted, error responses do not leak stack traces, JWT tokens are short-lived with secure key storage, method-level security is tested, and dependencies are scanned for known vulnerabilities. Perform penetration testing and review the security configuration in code review. Security is a continuous process, not a one-time checklist.A user reports they can access the admin dashboard even though they are not an admin. How do you investigate?: First I verify the endpoint is actually protected in the SecurityFilterChain and that the user does not have the admin role due to a data issue. I check the authentication object in the logs or a debug endpoint to see the granted authorities. I look for bugs in the role assignment logic, such as assigning ROLE_ADMIN to all users during sign-up or failing to strip legacy roles after a promotion. I also check whether method-level security is being bypassed by self-invocation from within the same bean. The fix is to tighten the URL and method authorization rules, add tests for non-admin access, and audit the user-role mapping code.A penetration test report says your login endpoint is vulnerable to SQL injection. How do you respond?: I immediately confirm whether the endpoint uses parameterized queries or an ORM. If the login query concatenates the username or password into SQL, I rewrite it to use named parameters or the JPA/Criteria API. I add server-side input validation and review all other endpoints for similar patterns. I run static analysis tools and add tests that pass malicious payloads such as ' OR '1'='1. Finally, I verify database accounts use least privilege so that even if injection occurs, the damage is limited.A developer disables CSRF to make a React frontend work, and now the security team is concerned. What do you say?: Disabling CSRF globally is only safe for stateless APIs that do not rely on session cookies. If the React app uses session cookies for authentication, disabling CSRF exposes the application to cross-site request forgery. The proper fix is to keep CSRF enabled and provide the token to the frontend, either as a cookie readable by JavaScript or through an endpoint, and have the React app send it back in a header for state-changing requests. Alternatively, switch to JWT in an HttpOnly cookie or an Authorization header, where CSRF is not relevant.A JWT token works in dev but is rejected in production with a 401. How do you debug?: I check whether the production signing key matches the one used to sign the token. If the token was issued in dev and production uses a different secret, it will fail validation. I verify the token has not expired and that the issuer and audience claims match the production configuration. I also check the clock skew between the issuer and verifier, and I confirm the filter reads the Authorization header correctly without being blocked by a proxy or gateway. The fix is to align key management and claims validation across environments, ideally using a single issuer or public key distribution.A user logged out, but their JWT is still able to access protected resources. How do you fix this?: In a stateless JWT system, logout only works if the server keeps a deny list of tokens until they expire. I implement token blacklisting using Redis or a database, storing the token identifier or a hash keyed by expiration time. A custom filter checks every request against this list and rejects blacklisted tokens. Alternatively, I keep access tokens very short-lived and revoke refresh tokens on logout, so the user must re-authenticate when the short token expires. The client must also delete tokens from storage.A production API returns a 500 error with a full stack trace containing the database password. How do you prevent this?: A stack trace with secrets means an unhandled exception leaked through the default error response. I add a global @RestControllerAdvice that catches exceptions and returns a sanitized JSON response with only a generic message and a correlation ID. I set server.error.include-stacktrace=never and server.error.include- message=never in production. I ensure no secrets are placed in exception messages. Finally, I review the error logging pipeline to make sure full stack traces go to the centralized logging system, not the API response.A Spring Security filter you added is being ignored. What could be wrong?: Custom filters must be registered at the correct position in the filter chain using addFilterBefore, addFilterAfter, or addFilterAt. If the filter is added at the wrong position, authentication or authorization may run before it, causing the request to be processed or rejected before your filter executes. Also, the filter must be a Spring bean if it depends on other beans, but it should not be registered as a servlet filter automatically unless you want it to run twice. I verify the filter order in the logs with debug logging enabled and adjust the registration accordingly.A third-party OAuth2 provider integration is failing after a certificate rotation. How do you investigate?: I check the TLS truststore used by the JVM to verify the provider’s new certificate. If the certificate chain is not trusted, the token exchange or userinfo call fails with a certificate validation error. I update the truststore with the new issuer certificates or switch to a well-known CA bundle. I also verify the client ID, client secret, redirect URI, and scopes match the provider’s current configuration. Finally, I confirm that the token issuer and userinfo endpoints have not changed.A @PreAuthorize annotation on a service method does not work when called from the controller. Why?: If the method is called from another method in the same bean, the Spring proxy is bypassed and the annotation is ignored. This is a classic self-invocation problem. I move the annotated method to another bean and inject it, or use AopContext.currentProxy() to force a proxied call. I also verify that @EnableMethodSecurity is present and that the SpEL expression uses the correct role or authority names, remembering that hasRole adds the ROLE_ prefix.Your application uses Google OAuth2 login, but users are getting assigned the same default role. How do you fix role mapping?: By default, Spring Security assigns limited authorities based on the OAuth2 provider response. I implement a custom OAuth2UserService that calls Google’s userinfo endpoint and maps returned attributes to local roles stored in the database. For example, I might check a domain, email pattern, or a pre-registered admin list. I then build a new OAuth2User with the mapped GrantedAuthority collection. I also ensure that roles are not hard-coded and can be updated without redeploying the application.A load test shows that JWT validation is becoming a CPU bottleneck. How do you optimize it?: I first check whether validation uses symmetric key signing with a shared secret that requires expensive HMAC operations on every request, and whether the token is parsed from scratch each time. Switching to an asymmetric key pair lets verification services use only the public key, which is faster and safer. I cache the parsed token or the decoded public key. I also verify the token format before parsing to fail fast on malformed tokens. If the bottleneck persists, I move token validation to an API Gateway or sidecar so backend services receive pre-validated claims.A user changed their password, but old sessions on other devices remain active. How do you handle this?: For session-based applications, I invalidate all sessions associated with the user by using Spring Security’s session registry, specifically for concurrent session control. For JWT-based applications, I issue new access and refresh tokens and revoke or blacklist all existing refresh tokens for that user. The client must re-authenticate on other devices. I also add a tokenVersion or passwordChangedAt claim in the JWT and reject tokens issued before the password change.A penetration test found that the refresh token endpoint does not rotate tokens. How do you fix it?: Token rotation means issuing a new refresh token every time a refresh request is made and invalidating the old one. I update the refresh endpoint to verify the incoming refresh token, delete or mark the stored token as used, and issue a new access token along with a new refresh token. This limits the window in which a stolen refresh token can be used. I also bind refresh tokens to the device fingerprint or a rotating family identifier so that reuse of an old token invalidates the entire family, preventing silent token theft.A mobile app reports that tokens expire too quickly, causing frequent login prompts. What is the balance?: The balance is between security and user experience. Access tokens should remain short, for example 5 to 15 minutes, because they travel with every request. Refresh tokens can live longer, for example days or weeks, but must be stored securely and rotated. For mobile apps, using a secure enclave or encrypted storage for the refresh token and implementing silent refresh on 401 responses gives a smooth experience without sacrificing safety. Long-lived access tokens are not the right answer because theft becomes much more dangerous.A developer stores the JWT secret key in application.properties and checks it into Git. What do you recommend?: I treat the exposed secret as compromised and rotate it immediately. The secret key must be removed from source control and moved to a secret manager such as Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or Azure Key Vault. For microservices, I recommend switching to asymmetric signing so verification services only need the public key. I add the properties file to .gitignore, scan the repository history for other secrets, and implement pre-commit hooks to prevent future commits of credentials.A state-changing API does not check user ownership and allows modifying any record by ID. How do you fix this?: This is an insecure direct object reference and missing authorization. The fix is to add an ownership check inside the service layer, for example by comparing the record’s owner to the current principal. Use @PreAuthorize with a SpEL expression that checks the owner, or perform the check in code after loading the entity from the database. Never trust the client to send the correct ID without verifying it against the authenticated user. I also add tests that confirm a user cannot update another user’s records.A production incident shows brute-force attempts on the login endpoint. How do you respond?: I immediately implement rate limiting on the login endpoint by IP address and username using Bucket4j, Resilience4j, or the API Gateway. I add progressive delays and CAPTCHA after repeated failures. I log and alert on attack patterns and consider temporarily blocking IPs at the firewall or WAF level. I verify that passwords are hashed with BCryptPasswordEncoder so offline cracking is slow if the database is breached. I also enable monitoring for credential-stuffing patterns and encourage users with weak or reused passwords to change them.A frontend developer reports CORS errors after enabling Spring Security. How do you resolve them without weakening security?: CORS errors happen when the browser blocks a cross-origin request. I configure CORS in the SecurityFilterChain using a CorsConfigurationSource bean that defines allowed origins, methods, headers, and whether credentials are allowed. I do not use a wildcard origin when credentials are enabled, because that allows any website to make authenticated requests. Instead, I explicitly list trusted origins and keep allowed methods minimal. I also ensure the preflight OPTIONS request is permitted without authentication.A JWT-based API needs to revoke access for a user immediately due to a security incident. How do you do it?: Because JWTs are stateless, I cannot revoke an access token directly unless it is expired. I implement token blacklisting using Redis keyed by token identifier or expiration time, and I update the authentication filter to reject blacklisted tokens. For faster response, I keep access tokens very short-lived and revoke all refresh tokens for the user. I also update the user record to mark the account as locked or disabled so that any new login attempt fails. If the incident is severe, I rotate the signing key, which invalidates all existing tokens across the system.An internal service calls your API with a service account token. How do you authorize it differently from a human user?: I distinguish service accounts from human users by claim values such as client_id, sub, or a custom account_type claim. Service accounts get authorities that represent API scopes or permissions, not UI roles. I use @PreAuthorize("hasAuthority('service:invoice:read')") to restrict service endpoints and keep service account tokens short-lived, ideally with a lifetime tied to the request. I also log service-to-service calls separately and monitor for unusual call volumes or scope creep.A developer uses permitAll on the /actuator/health endpoint but it still returns 401. Why?: The endpoint might be configured in the wrong SecurityFilterChain or the request might be hitting a different base path. If management endpoints are on a separate port or context path, the main SecurityFilterChain may not apply. Another cause is that the request is being rejected by an earlier filter, such as a custom JWT filter that returns 401 for any request without a token before the authorization filter can evaluate permitAll. I debug by enabling Spring Security logging and checking the exact chain that processes the request.A user role hierarchy should allow managers to do everything users can. How do you model this?: Spring Security supports role hierarchies through a RoleHierarchy bean. I define that ROLE_MANAGER implies ROLE_USER, and then use hasRole("USER") in authorization rules. A manager will automatically be granted the implied authority. This avoids duplicating permission checks across roles and keeps the authority model maintainable. Without role hierarchy, I would have to explicitly check both roles in every rule, which is error-prone.A Swagger UI is publicly exposed in production and the security team wants it removed. What is the best approach?: The best approach is to disable Swagger UI in production using Spring profiles, for example by only enabling the SpringDoc configuration when the dev or qa profile is active. Alternatively, I restrict Swagger endpoints with Spring Security to internal users only. If Swagger is not needed in production at all, I remove the dependency from the production build. I keep OpenAPI spec generation in CI so documentation stays accurate, but the interactive UI is not reachable publicly.A user with a valid token sees 403 on some endpoints after a deployment. What do you check?: I check whether the deployment changed authority strings or role mappings. For example, a role renamed from ADMIN to ROLE_ADMIN or an authority renamed from user.write to user:create breaks existing @PreAuthorize expressions. I verify the UserDetailsService or token claims builder populates the same authorities the rules expect. I also check whether the endpoints moved to a new path that is no longer covered by the URL authorization rule. Logging the authentication object and the denied request path quickly shows the mismatch.A Spring Security application must support both human users and API clients. How do you configure it?: I configure multiple SecurityFilterChain beans with different request matchers and authentication mechanisms. One chain handles browser users with OAuth2 login or form login and session cookies. Another chain handles API clients with JWT bearer tokens and a stateless session policy. Each chain matches based on path or header patterns. This separation prevents a misconfiguration meant for browser users, such as CSRF protection, from breaking API clients, and vice versa. Both chains still enforce authorization based on roles and authorities.

title: 24.1.Key Concepts
text: Why is testing important in a Spring Boot application, and what does Spring Boot provide out of the box?: Testing is important because it catches regressions early, documents expected behavior, and gives confidence during refactoring or production incidents. Spring Boot provides the spring-boot-starter-test starter, which bundles JUnit 5, Mockito, AssertJ, Hamcrest, JSONassert, and JsonPath. It also offers slice test annotations such as @WebMvcTest, @DataJpaTest, and @RestClientTest, which load only the relevant slice of the application context so tests run faster than a full @SpringBootTest. The starter also configures the build tools to separate unit tests from integration tests, which is useful on CI where you want quick feedback before running slower integration suites.What is the difference between unit testing and integration testing in a Spring Boot project?: A unit test isolates a single class or method and replaces all collaborators with test doubles, so a failure points directly to the unit under test. An integration test loads part or all of the Spring context and exercises real collaborators such as the database, the web layer, or message brokers. Unit tests are fast, deterministic, and run without a server or database. Integration tests are slower but verify wiring, transaction behavior, serialization, and configuration that unit tests cannot. A healthy project runs unit tests on every commit and integration tests in a separate CI stage or nightly job.What is JUnit 5, and how is it different from JUnit 4?: JUnit 5 is the current generation of the JUnit testing framework. It has a modular architecture made of JUnit Platform, which discovers and runs tests; JUnit Jupiter, which provides the modern programming and extension model; and JUnit Vintage, which can run JUnit 3 and 4 tests. Unlike JUnit 4, it uses @Test, @BeforeEach, @AfterEach, @BeforeAll, and @AfterAll annotations, supports parameterized tests, dynamic tests, and a powerful extension API. The extension model replaces JUnit 4 runners and rules, so Spring uses SpringExtension instead of the old SpringRunner.Explain the lifecycle annotations @BeforeAll, @BeforeEach, @AfterEach, and @AfterAll in JUnit 5.: @BeforeAll runs once before any test in the class and is typically used for expensive setup such as starting a database container. @BeforeEach runs before every test and is used to reset state, create fresh mocks, or reinitialize the object under test. @AfterEach runs after every test to clean up resources like open files or database rows inserted by the test. @AfterAll runs once after all tests complete and is used to stop containers or close connection pools. Static setup methods must be static, which is a common trap when you need shared state across tests.What is AssertJ, and why do developers prefer it over standard JUnit assertions?: AssertJ is a fluent assertion library that makes test failures readable and assertions easy to write. Instead of assertEquals(expected, actual), you write assertThat(actual).isEqualTo(expected). It has rich support for collections, exceptions, strings, dates, and JSON, and the failure messages are often clearer than JUnit's defaults. For example, assertThatThrownBy(() -> service.findById(-1)).isInstanceOf(NotFoundException.class).hasMes sageContaining("not found") reads like a sentence and bundles multiple checks. AssertJ also avoids confusion about the expected-versus-actual parameter order, which is a common source of misleading failure messages.How do you write a basic unit test for a Spring Boot service method?: You test the service class directly without loading the Spring context. You create the service instance yourself or use Mockito's @InjectMocks, and you provide its dependencies with @Mock. In the test you arrange the inputs and mock behavior, act by calling the service method, and assert the result or verify interactions. For example, if OrderService depends on OrderRepository, you mock the repository to return an order, call orderService.findById(1L), and assert that the returned order has the expected customer name. Since the Spring context is not loaded, the test starts in milliseconds.What is Mockito, and what problem does it solve?: Mockito is a mocking framework that lets you create test doubles for dependencies so you can isolate the class you are testing. It solves the problem of slow or unavailable collaborators such as databases, external REST services, or message queues during unit tests. With Mockito you can define stubbed responses, verify that certain methods were called, and capture the arguments passed to mocked methods. This keeps unit tests focused, fast, and deterministic. A common mistake is mocking everything including simple value objects, which makes tests brittle and less trustworthy.What is the difference between @Mock, @Spy, and @InjectMocks in Mockito?: @Mock creates a fully mocked object where every method returns default values unless stubbed. @Spy creates a partial mock that wraps a real instance and calls real methods by default, unless you stub a specific method. @InjectMocks creates an instance of the class under test and tries to inject mocks or spies into its constructor, fields, or setter methods. @InjectMocks is convenient for service-layer tests, but constructor injection is cleaner because it makes dependencies explicit and avoids reflection-based injection, which can silently fail if field names do not match.How do you stub a method with Mockito, and what happens if a stubbed method is called with unexpected arguments?: You stub a method with when(mock.method(args)).thenReturn(value), or with the BDD-style given(mock.method(args)).willReturn(value). If the method is called with arguments that do not match any stubbing, Mockito returns the default value for the return type, such as null for objects, 0 for numbers, or false for booleans. This silent default return is a frequent source of confusion because the test may pass even though the mock interaction is wrong. To catch this, use Mockito.strictness(Strictness.STRICT_STUBS), which fails the test if a stubbed mock is called with unexpected arguments or if a stubbed method is never called.What is the purpose of verify() in Mockito, and when should you use it?: verify() checks that a specific interaction happened with a mock, such as a method being called a certain number of times or with specific arguments. You use it when the side effect of the method under test is more important than the returned value, for example when a service saves a record or sends an email. verify(repository).save(order) confirms that persistence happened, while verify(emailClient, never()).send(any()) confirms that no email was sent for an invalid order. However, overusing verify() on every collaborator makes tests brittle; prefer asserting outcomes and verify only meaningful interactions.What is @MockBean, and when do you use it instead of @Mock?: @MockBean is a Spring test annotation that replaces an existing bean in the Spring application context with a Mockito mock. You use it in integration or slice tests where the context is loaded, such as @WebMvcTest or @SpringBootTest, and you need a real controller but a fake service. @Mock is used in plain unit tests where there is no Spring context. A common pitfall is using @MockBean in a class with many tests; it triggers context caching invalidation because Spring sees a modified context, which can slow down the whole suite.How do you test the persistence layer in Spring Boot, and what does @DataJpaTest do?: The persistence layer is tested with @DataJpaTest, which configures an in-memory or real database, loads @Entity classes, and creates a default TestEntityManager and repository beans. It does not load the full application context, so web components and services are excluded. You write tests that save entities through the repository and assert that custom query methods return the expected results. Because @DataJpaTest is transactional, each test runs inside a transaction that rolls back automatically, leaving the database clean. For complex queries, an in-memory database may behave differently from production, so many teams switch to Testcontainers with the real database vendor.What are Testcontainers, and why would you use them for repository tests?: Testcontainers is a Java library that starts lightweight, throwaway Docker containers for databases, message brokers, and other services during tests. For repository tests, it lets you run PostgreSQL, MySQL, or Oracle inside a container instead of relying on H2. This eliminates subtle differences in SQL dialect, transaction behavior, and vendor-specific features between the test database and production. The container starts once per test class, tests run against the real database, and the container is stopped after the tests finish. The trade-off is slower startup, but the confidence gained is worth it for queries that use native SQL, JSON columns, or stored procedures.How do you configure Testcontainers for a Spring Boot @DataJpaTest?: You define a container as a static field annotated with @Container, start it in @BeforeAll, and set Spring DataSource properties so the test context connects to the running container. With the latest Spring Boot and Testcontainers integration, you can also use @ServiceConnection so Spring Boot automatically resolves the connection details from the container. You add the database driver and Testcontainers dependency, and you may reuse the same container across tests with a singleton pattern or the Testcontainers reuse feature to reduce startup time. The key is to keep container lifecycle separate from the test transaction so the container keeps running while individual tests still roll back.What is @WebMvcTest, and what does it load?: @WebMvcTest is a slice test annotation that loads only the Spring MVC web layer. It auto-configures MockMvc, registers @Controller and @ControllerAdvice beans, and excludes the full service, repository, and component scan unless explicitly imported. It is ideal for testing REST controllers because you can simulate HTTP requests, validate status codes, response bodies, and exception handling without starting an embedded server. Collaborating services are provided with @MockBean. A common mistake is expecting security filters to be fully configured; while Spring Security filters are registered, authentication must be mocked or disabled depending on the test intent.What is MockMvc, and how does it differ from TestRestTemplate or WebTestClient?: MockMvc is a Spring testing utility that dispatches HTTP requests through the DispatcherServlet without starting a real servlet container. It tests controller behavior, binding, validation, exception handling, and filter interactions in a lightweight way. TestRestTemplate and WebTestClient are used with @SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = RANDOM_PORT) to send real HTTP requests to an embedded server. MockMvc is faster and more focused, while the real-client approach verifies the full stack including actual serialization and container filters. For most controller tests, MockMvc is sufficient; reserve the real server approach for integration tests that need end-to-end HTTP semantics.What is @SpringBootTest, and what are its web environment options?: @SpringBootTest loads the complete Spring Boot application context and is used for full integration tests. Its webEnvironment attribute controls how the web layer is started. MOCK loads a web context but does not start a real server, RANDOM_PORT starts an embedded server on a random port, DEFINED_PORT uses the configured server.port, and NONE does not start a web environment. RANDOM_PORT is the safest choice for integration tests because it avoids port conflicts on CI and lets TestRestTemplate or WebTestClient send real requests. MOCK is useful when you want the web beans configured but prefer MockMvc.What is the Spring TestContext framework, and how does caching affect test performance?: The Spring TestContext framework manages the application context for tests, including dependency injection, transaction management, and context caching. When multiple test classes use the same context configuration, Spring reuses the cached context instead of creating a new one, which saves significant startup time. However, adding or replacing beans with @MockBean, @SpyBean, or different @TestPropertySource values changes the context key and forces a new context to load. A suite that uses many different @MockBean combinations can suffer from context cache pollution and slow builds, so it is worth grouping tests that share the same context.How do you test an asynchronous method in Spring Boot?: Asynchronous methods are usually annotated with @Async and executed by a TaskExecutor. In a unit test, you call the method and use CompletableFuture.get() or Future.get() with a timeout to wait for the result. In an integration test, you may inject the executor and wait for active tasks to finish, or use Awaitility to poll until the expected side effect occurs. A common mistake is asserting immediately after the async call, which usually fails because the task has not run yet. You should also test the error path by verifying that exceptions are handled by the returned Future or by an AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler for void async methods.What is JaCoCo, and what does it measure?: JaCoCo is a Java code coverage library that instruments bytecode during test execution and reports how much of the code was executed. It measures instruction coverage, branch coverage, line coverage, method coverage, and class coverage. The HTML report shows which lines were covered and which branches were missed, while the XML report is consumed by CI tools like SonarQube. Coverage is a useful signal but not a goal by itself; 100 percent line coverage with weak assertions is misleading because the code can be exercised without being verified. JaCoCo should be paired with meaningful assertions and reviewed alongside the test suite quality.How do you exclude classes or methods from JaCoCo coverage?: You configure exclusions in the JaCoCo Maven or Gradle plugin using patterns such as **/config/*, **/dto/**, or **/*Exception. Lombok-generated methods can be excluded with the lombok.addLombokGeneratedAnnotation = true setting, which marks generated code so JaCoCo ignores it. Exclusions are useful for boilerplate such as equals and hashCode, mapping classes, or generated sources, but excluding real business logic to hit a coverage target is a harmful practice. The goal is to reduce noise, not to game the metric.What are parameterized tests in JUnit 5, and when are they useful?: Parameterized tests let you run the same test logic with multiple sets of inputs. You can use @ValueSource for simple values, @CsvSource for tabular data, @MethodSource for complex objects, or @ArgumentsSource for custom suppliers. They are useful for validation rules, pricing calculations, or any logic that must behave consistently across many cases. For example, a single parameterized test can check that a discount calculator returns the correct amount for customer tiers GOLD, SILVER, and BRONZE. Without parameterized tests, you would copy- paste nearly identical test methods, which is hard to maintain.What is the difference between @MockBean and @SpyBean in Spring tests?: @MockBean completely replaces a bean in the Spring context with a Mockito mock, so calls return stubbed values by default. @SpyBean wraps the real bean, so real behavior runs unless a specific method is stubbed. Use @MockBean when the real implementation should not execute, such as an external payment gateway or a repository that is not needed for a controller test. Use @SpyBean when you want to verify that the real bean was called or when you need to override one method of an otherwise real service. Spies are useful but can make tests harder to debug because the real code runs and may trigger unexpected side effects.How do you handle test data setup in integration tests without making tests depend on each other?: Each test should start from a clean, well-defined state. Use the transactional rollback behavior of @Transactional on tests so database changes are undone. For data that must survive across transactions, such as when testing a service that calls @Transactional(propagation = REQUIRES_NEW), use TestEntityManager or a custom cleanup in @AfterEach to delete rows. Avoid relying on data created by a previous test because it makes execution order matter and causes flaky failures. Some teams use SQL scripts or libraries like Flyway for baseline schema and small seed data sets, but test-specific data should be created and cleaned up by the test itself.A developer writes a service test that passes locally but fails on CI because of timezone differences. How do you investigate and fix it?: I first look at any date or timestamp assertions in the test and in the production code. If the test creates an expected value using LocalDateTime.now() or new Date() and compares it against a value returned by the service, the comparison will fail when CI runs in a different zone or at a different millisecond. The fix is to avoid comparing against wall-clock time in unit tests; instead, inject a Clock bean or use a fixed timestamp provided by the test. For integration tests, I ensure the JVM timezone and database timezone are explicit, for example by setting -Duser.timezone=UTC in the CI pipeline and using TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE columns. I also check whether the test relies on the current date for filtering and replace it with a controlled reference date.A @DataJpaTest passes on its own but fails when the whole suite runs because of leftover data. What is the cause?: The cause is usually a test that commits data and does not clean up, or a non-transactional operation that leaks state. @DataJpaTest is transactional by default, so most tests roll back, but a test calling a service method annotated with @Transactional(propagation = REQUIRES_NEW) commits in a new transaction and leaves data behind. Another cause is a @Sql script that inserts shared rows without cleanup, or a test that uses native JdbcTemplate without a transaction. I find the offending test by running the suite with logging enabled and inspecting the database state after each test class. The fix is to add explicit cleanup in @AfterEach, use unique identifiers per test, or refactor the production code so tests can rely on automatic rollback.A controller test with @WebMvcTest returns a 200 status but the response body is empty. How do you debug it?: An empty body usually means the controller returns a response object but it is not serialized, or the mock did not return the expected object. I first verify that the controller method is actually returning the object and that @RestController or @ResponseBody is present. Then I check the mocked service; if when(service.findById(1L)).thenReturn(order) uses a different argument than the controller passes, Mockito returns null and Jackson serializes an empty body. I also check that the object has getters, because Jackson needs them. Finally, I inspect the MockMvc result to see whether an exception was swallowed, and I enable debug logging for org.springframework.web to trace the request through the filters.A service test using Mockito throws an UnnecessaryStubbingException after a refactor. What is happening?: UnnecessaryStubbingException is thrown when strict stubbing is enabled and a stubbed interaction was never used during the test. After the refactor, the production code may no longer call the stubbed method, or the test may have stubbed a method that was only relevant to an older implementation. I review the test to remove unused stubs and to confirm that the remaining stubs are actually required by the scenario being tested. If strict stubbing is too noisy for a class with shared setup, I either split the test into smaller focused tests or apply Mockito.lenient() only where a shared stub is acceptable. The goal is to keep the test honest about what it is verifying.An integration test with @SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = RANDOM_PORT) fails because the actuator health endpoint returns 404. How do you fix it?: The actuator endpoint is not loaded because @SpringBootTest does not auto- configure actuator unless the actuator starter is on the classpath and the endpoint is enabled. I check that spring-boot-starter-actuator is a dependency and that the management.endpoints.web.exposure.include property includes health. If the test uses webEnvironment = NONE, actuator web endpoints are not available, so I switch to RANDOM_PORT or MOCK. I also verify that the test does not override management.server.port to a fixed port that collides with another test. When actuator is intentionally disabled for a test, I either enable it for that test class or assert against an internal service bean instead of the HTTP endpoint.A test with Testcontainers is extremely slow because the database container starts for every test class. How do you speed it up?: I start by moving the container declaration to a base class or a shared utility so the same container instance is reused across multiple test classes. With Testcontainers, I enable the testcontainers.reuse.enable flag in ~/.testcontainers.properties so containers stay alive between test runs during local development. On CI, I ensure Docker layer caching is enabled and that the base image is pulled before the build. I also look for unnecessary @DirtiesContext annotations that force the Spring context to restart. If the suite is still slow, I move the fastest unit-like repository tests to H2 and reserve Testcontainers only for tests that exercise vendor-specific SQL or stored procedures.A developer disables CSRF in a @WebMvcTest and now real requests fail with 403 in production. What went wrong?: The test disabled CSRF to make the mock setup easier, but the developer assumed the controller behavior was the only thing being tested. CSRF is part of Spring Security, and disabling it in tests hides the fact that state-changing browser requests require a valid token. The fix is to configure MockMvc with the same security settings used in production and to include CSRF tokens in state-changing requests using csrf(). For pure API clients using bearer tokens, CSRF is legitimately disabled, but that decision belongs in the production configuration, not hidden in tests. Tests should mirror production security as closely as possible to catch integration issues early.A test with @MockBean runs fine alone but causes context reloads and slow builds across the suite. How do you address it?: @MockBean changes the Spring context fingerprint, so every unique combination of mocked beans triggers a new context cache entry. If many test classes mock different sets of beans, the cache fills up and contexts are constantly created and destroyed. I address this by grouping tests that need the same mocks into the same test class or by using @Import to share a common test configuration that declares the mocks once. For unit tests that do not need the Spring context at all, I replace @MockBean with plain Mockito @Mock and remove @SpringBootTest, which eliminates context reloads entirely. When a slice test such as @WebMvcTest is sufficient, I use it instead of the full context.A repository test using H2 passes, but the same query fails in production on PostgreSQL. Why?: H2 supports a different SQL dialect than PostgreSQL, and some queries that work in H2 may use syntax, functions, or type casting that PostgreSQL does not accept. Native queries, JSON operators, window functions, and custom types are common sources of mismatch. The fix is to move the test from H2 to a Testcontainers PostgreSQL container so the test database matches production. I also review the query for dialect-specific features and consider using JPQL or the Criteria API for portable logic. If a native query is unavoidable, I write a dedicated integration test against the real database and keep the H2 test only for simple repository methods where dialect differences do not matter.A developer writes a unit test that calls a private method using reflection. Is this a good practice, and what would you suggest?: Testing private methods through reflection is usually not a good practice because it couples the test to internal implementation details and makes refactoring painful. Private methods are tested indirectly by testing the public methods that call them. If the private method is complex enough to need its own tests, that is often a sign that it should be extracted into a separate class with public methods that can be tested directly. The exception is legacy code that cannot be refactored safely; in that case, reflection may be a temporary bridge, but the goal should be to refactor the code rather than maintain reflection-based tests.A code coverage report shows 90 percent line coverage, but a bug still reaches production. How is that possible?: Line coverage only measures whether a line was executed, not whether it was verified. A test can execute error-handling code without asserting that the correct exception is thrown, or it can call a method with a weak assertion that misses the bug. Coverage also does not measure edge cases, concurrency, or integration failures. I treat coverage as a hygiene metric, not a quality guarantee. I review tests for meaningful assertions, add mutation testing or property-based testing for critical paths, and run integration tests against realistic data. If a bug escapes, I add a regression test that fails before the fix and passes after it.A test that verifies an email was sent keeps failing intermittently. How do you stabilize it?: Intermittent failures in asynchronous email sending usually come from timing issues. The test asserts before the background thread has finished, or the mock interaction happens on a different thread than the test thread. I stabilize the test by using Awaitility to wait until the mock receives the expected call, or by returning a CompletableFuture from the email service so the test can wait for completion. If the email client runs inside an @Async method, I inject the TaskExecutor and wait for active tasks. I also make the test deterministic by controlling the thread pool or by making the email service synchronous in the test configuration.A build fails because JaCoCo reports zero coverage for classes generated by MapStruct or Lombok. How do you handle it?: Generated code should not count against coverage because it is not hand-written business logic. For Lombok, I add lombok.config with lombok.addLombokGeneratedAnnotation = true so JaCoCo recognizes the @Generated annotation and skips those methods. For MapStruct, I configure the JaCoCo exclusion list to ignore the generated mapper implementation classes, for example with **/mapper/*Impl. I keep the domain and service classes included in coverage so the report reflects actual code quality. If the team uses other generators, I add their output packages to the exclusions and document why those classes are not measured.A controller test passes in isolation but fails after adding Spring Security to the project. How do you update the test?: Adding Spring Security introduces filters that intercept every request, so unauthenticated requests to protected endpoints return 401 instead of 200. I update the test by configuring MockMvc with the security filters and either providing test credentials or disabling authentication for the test. For @WebMvcTest, I add @AutoConfigureMockMvc(addFilters = false) only if the goal is to test controller logic without security, but I prefer to keep security enabled and use @WithMockUser to simulate an authenticated user. I also add tests that verify the endpoint correctly rejects unauthenticated requests and enforces role-based access, so security behavior is covered, not bypassed.A test suite reports IllegalStateException: Failed to load ApplicationContext after adding a new dependency. What is your debugging approach?: I start by reading the full stack trace and the caused-by chain, because the root cause is usually buried several levels deep. Common reasons are a missing property, a bean that fails to initialize, a conditional configuration that activates incorrectly under test, or a dependency that requires a real service not available in the test environment. I run a single failing test with debug logging for org.springframework.boot and org.springframework.test to see which bean fails. If the new dependency is not needed for the test, I exclude it with @SpringBootTest(classes = { ... }) or a test-specific profile. If it is needed, I provide a mock or a lightweight implementation with @TestConfiguration.

title: 25.1.Key Concepts
text: What is Generative AI, and how does it differ from traditional AI?: Generative AI refers to models that can create new content such as text, images, audio, or code by learning patterns from training data. Unlike traditional AI, which is often used for classification, regression, or clustering, generative AI produces outputs that resemble its training distribution. Large language models such as GPT, Gemini, and Claude are generative AI systems trained to predict the next token in a sequence. For backend developers, generative AI enables features like chatbots, code assistants, content generation, and summarization. The key difference is that traditional AI maps inputs to known labels, while generative AI synthesizes novel outputs conditioned on inputs.What is a Large Language Model, and how does it work at a high level?: A Large Language Model is a deep neural network, typically based on the Transformer architecture, trained on vast amounts of text to predict the next token in a sequence. During training, the model learns statistical relationships between words, grammar, facts, and reasoning patterns. At inference, it takes a prompt, converts it into tokens, processes it through many layers of self-attention and feed-forward networks, and generates tokens one at a time until a stop condition is reached. The model does not truly understand text in a human sense; it predicts likely sequences based on learned patterns. Modern LLMs have billions of parameters and are ne-tuned for instruction following and safety.What is tokenization, and why does it matter?: Tokenization is the process of splitting text into smaller units called tokens, which can be words, subwords, or characters. LLMs operate on tokens rather than raw characters, so the input text is first converted to tokens, and the model predicts the next token. The number of tokens affects cost, context window usage, and latency. Different models use different tokenizers, so the same sentence may produce different token counts across providers. For backend developers, tokenization matters because APIs charge per token, prompt length is limited by the context window, and output must often be bounded by max tokens.What is the Transformer architecture?: The Transformer is a neural network architecture introduced in the paper Attention Is All You Need. It is built from encoder and decoder stacks, though modern LLMs typically use decoder-only variants. The core component is the self-attention mechanism, which allows the model to weigh the importance of different tokens in the input when producing each output token. Transformers also use positional encoding to understand token order, feed-forward layers to transform representations, layer normalization, and residual connections. The architecture is highly parallelizable during training and scales well to billions of parameters.What is self-attention, and why is it important?: Self-attention is a mechanism that computes a weighted representation of each token by considering all other tokens in the sequence. For every token, the model generates query, key, and value vectors, compares the query with all keys to produce attention scores, normalizes them with softmax, and uses the weighted sum of values as the output. This allows the model to capture long-range dependencies and contextual relationships regardless of token distance. It is important because it replaces sequential processing with parallel computation and gives the model the ability to focus on relevant parts of the input when generating each token.What are embeddings in the context of generative AI?: Embeddings are dense numerical vector representations of text, images, or other data. They map semantic meaning into a high-dimensional space where similar items are closer together. In LLMs, token embeddings represent individual tokens, while sentence or document embeddings capture the meaning of larger text chunks. Embeddings are produced by passing text through an embedding model. They are used for tasks such as semantic search, classification, clustering, recommendation, and retrieval in RAG systems. The quality of embeddings depends on the model and the domain of the training data.What is a vector store and vector search?: A vector store is a database optimized for storing and searching high-dimensional vectors. Vector search, also called similarity search, nds vectors that are closest to a query vector using distance metrics such as cosine similarity or Euclidean distance. Popular vector stores include pgvector, Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, Milvus, and Redis. They often use approximate nearest neighbor algorithms such as HNSW or IVF to search efficiently at scale. In generative AI applications, vector stores are used to retrieve relevant documents or facts based on semantic similarity, which improves response accuracy beyond what the model memorized during training.What is Retrieval Augmented Generation?: Retrieval Augmented Generation is a pattern that combines information retrieval with text generation. Instead of relying solely on the model's training knowledge, the system retrieves relevant documents from a vector store or database and includes them in the prompt as context. The model then generates an answer grounded in the retrieved content. RAG reduces hallucination, keeps responses up to date, and allows the model to answer questions about private or proprietary data. It is commonly used for enterprise knowledge bases, documentation assistants, and customer support bots.What is the difference between naive RAG and modular RAG?: Naive RAG embeds the user query, performs a single similarity search against a vector store, and injects the top documents into the prompt. It is simple to implement and works well for many cases. Modular RAG breaks the process into con gurable stages such as query transformation, retrieval, document post-processing, and augmentation. It can use query rewriting, compression, translation, multi-query expansion, reranking, and hybrid search. Modular RAG is more exible and accurate but also more complex. Spring AI supports naive RAG through QuestionAnswerAdvisor and modular RAG through RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor.What is Spring AI?: Spring AI is a framework that brings AI capabilities to Spring applications. It provides a consistent, portable API for working with large language models, embedding models, vector stores, and AI patterns such as RAG and tool calling. It supports multiple model providers including OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, Ollama, Azure OpenAI, and Mistral. Spring AI is designed with the same philosophy as Spring: abstractions over vendor APIs, easy configuration, and integration with Spring Boot. Backend developers can use it to add chat, search, classification, and agentic features to Java applications without writing provider-specific boilerplate.What is ChatClient in Spring AI?: ChatClient is the high-level uent API in Spring AI for interacting with chat models. It allows developers to build prompts, set model options, attach advisors, register tools, and call or stream responses. It is constructed from a ChatModel bean and configured through the builder. ChatClient abstracts the underlying provider, so switching from OpenAI to Anthropic requires changing configuration rather than application code. It supports synchronous calls with call(), streaming with stream(), structured output with entity(), and content extraction with content().How do you create a ChatClient in Spring Boot?: You add the appropriate Spring AI starter, such as spring-ai-openai-spring-boot-starter, and configure properties like spring.ai.openai.api-key and spring.ai.openai.chat.options.model. Spring Boot auto-configures a ChatModel bean. You then create a ChatClient bean by calling ChatClient.builder(chatModel).build(). You can also set default advisors, default tools, and default options during construction. The ChatClient can be injected into services and used in REST controllers or background jobs. This pattern keeps AI logic inside Spring-managed services and integrates with the rest of the application.What are Advisors in Spring AI?: Advisors are interceptors in the ChatClient request and response pipeline. They can modify the request before it reaches the model, process the response after it returns, or implement reusable patterns such as logging, chat memory, RAG retrieval, and tool calling. Advisors implement CallAdvisor or StreamAdvisor and run in a chain ordered by getOrder. Built-in advisors include MessageChatMemoryAdvisor for conversation memory, QuestionAnswerAdvisor for RAG, ToolCallingAdvisor for tool execution, and RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor for modular RAG. Custom advisors allow developers to add cross-cutting concerns like audit logging, prompt injection prevention, or cost tracking.How do Advisors compare to Spring AOP interceptors?: Both Advisors and Spring AOP interceptors wrap behavior around a core operation. AOP intercepts method calls in the Spring application context, while Spring AI Advisors intercept calls to the language model. AOP is general purpose for business methods; Advisors are specialized for the ChatClient pipeline. In Spring AI, advisors receive ChatClientRequest and ChatClientResponse objects and can update context, modify prompts, or transform responses. They are specific to AI work ows and integrate with the ChatClient builder, whereas AOP interceptors work with any Spring bean method.What is chat memory, and how is it managed in Spring AI?: Chat memory stores the history of a conversation so the model can maintain context across multiple turns. Without memory, each request is stateless and the model does not remember previous messages. Spring AI provides MessageChatMemoryAdvisor, which stores messages in an in-memory or persistent ChatMemory implementation. The advisor retrieves recent messages and adds them to the prompt. For longer conversations, VectorStoreChatMemoryAdvisor can retrieve semantically relevant past messages from a vector store. A conversation ID is used to scope messages to a specific user session.What is Tool Calling in Spring AI?: Tool calling, also known as function calling, allows an LLM to invoke application methods to retrieve data or perform actions. In Spring AI, tools are defined by methods annotated with @Tool or by implementing ToolCallback. The ChatClient registers tools, and the ToolCallingAdvisor manages the loop: the model decides to call a tool, Spring AI executes the method, and the result is sent back to the model. The loop continues until the model produces a final response. Tool calling enables agents that can access real-time data, query databases, call external APIs, or take actions in the system.How do you define a tool in Spring AI?: You create a method annotated with @Tool inside a Spring bean and pass the bean to ChatClient.defaultTools.
