During load testing, the TransactionService singleton shows race conditions. What could be wrong?Singleton beans are not thread-safe by default. If TransactionService holds mutable shared state, such as a counter or a non-thread-safe map, concurrent fund-transfer requests can corrupt it. The fix is to keep the bean stateless or use thread-safe data structures like ConcurrentHashMap. Better still, move per-request data into method parameters or use the prototype scope if the bean must hold request state. The service must support multiple environments: dev, UAT, and prod. How do you manage this in Spring Boot?I use Spring profiles. I create application-dev.yml, application-uat.yml, and application-prod.yml. Each profile defines its own database URL, logging level, and feature flags. I activate the profile using spring.profiles.active in the environment or as a JVM argument. In prod, I also secure Actuator endpoints and disable dev tools. A warehouse order API receives a request, calls inventory to reserve stock, then calls payment to charge the customer. How do you design this in Spring Boot?I create an OrderController annotated with @RestController, an OrderService annotated with @Service, and InventoryClient and PaymentClient beans. The controller maps the incoming request, the service orchestrates the steps, and the clients handle external calls. I inject all clients into the service via constructor injection so the flow is clear and testable. The order service sometimes fails because the inventory and payment clients are coupled. How do you break the circular dependency?If InventoryClient depends on PaymentClient and vice versa, Spring fails at startup with constructor injection. I redesign the code so both clients are independent and the OrderService coordinates them. Alternatively, I can use @Lazy or setter injection as a short- term fix, but the real solution is better separation of concerns. The team adds both Tomcat and Jetty starters by mistake. How does Spring Boot decide which server to use?Spring Boot checks classpath order and defaults to the first embedded server it finds, usually Tomcat if it appears first. To switch to Jetty intentionally, I would exclude the Tomcat starter from spring-boot-starter-web and add the Jetty starter explicitly. This controls which server runs the supply-chain API. You need to expose order health status to the operations team. What Spring Boot feature helps?Spring Boot Actuator provides production-ready endpoints such as /actuator/health. I add the spring-boot-starter-actuator dependency and configure security so only authorized users can access sensitive endpoints. I can also create a custom health indicator that checks connectivity to the warehouse database and external payment gateway. The application does not start, and the error points to a missing bean. How do you investigate?I check that the missing bean class has a Spring stereotype annotation such as @Component, @Service, or @Repository, and that its package is under the component scan path defined by @SpringBootApplication. I also verify that any third-party bean is declared with @Bean in a @Configuration class. If needed, I list all loaded beans using ApplicationContext.getBeanDefinitionNames() or the Actuator /beans endpoint. A banking build passes locally but fails in the CI pipeline with dependency conflicts. How do you fix this with Maven?I run mvn dependency:tree to inspect transitive dependencies and identify the conflicting versions. I then use the dependencyManagement section or the tag to force the correct version. If the team uses a repository manager like Nexus or Artifactory, I verify that the CI environment points to the same repository as local machines. The CI pipeline needs to build a JAR once and deploy it to dev, UAT, and prod. How do you configure Maven for this?I run mvn clean package to produce a single JAR. Environment-specific configuration is externalized into profile-specific property files or environment variables, not baked into the JAR. Maven profiles can help run environment-specific tests, but the same artifact is promoted across environments to guarantee consistency. A developer checked in a change that breaks the Maven build for everyone. What Maven command helps isolate the problem?I use mvn clean install -X to run the build in debug mode and capture detailed logs. I also use mvn dependency:tree to spot new conflicts and mvn test to identify failing tests. Once I find the problematic commit, I either revert it or work with the developer to fix the POM and dependency versions. During peak season, the order API must handle ten times the normal traffic. Which Spring Boot settings matter?I tune the embedded server thread pool, configure database connection pool limits in application.yml, add caching with Redis or Caffeine, and consider scaling horizontally behind a load balancer. I also monitor Actuator metrics for response time and error rate. If the bottleneck is database connections, I increase the HikariCP pool size within safe limits. The order API must switch from an in-memory database to Oracle in production without code changes. How is this possible?I externalize the datasource configuration into application.yml using placeholders or Spring profiles. In dev, the profile uses H2; in prod, the profile uses Oracle URL, username, password, and driver. Auto-configuration detects the database driver on the classpath and creates the datasource. No code changes are needed because the same JPA code works against both databases. A new starter was added, but Spring Boot now auto-configures a bean the team does not want. How do you disable it?I disable the unwanted auto-configuration class using @SpringBootApplication(exclude = {UnwantedAutoConfiguration.class}) or by listing it in spring.autoconfigure.exclude in application.yml. This is useful when a starter pulls in a feature, such as a default datasource or security filter, that conflicts with custom configuration. You have three classes — PaymentController, PaymentService, and PaymentRepository. Annotate them.PaymentController gets @RestController because it exposes REST endpoints. PaymentService gets @Service because it holds business logic. PaymentRepository gets @Repository because it accesses the database. I inject PaymentService into the controller and PaymentRepository into the service, all via constructor injection, so Spring manages the object graph. Two beans of type NotificationSender exist — one for email, one for SMS. How do you inject the correct one?I use @Qualifier("emailNotificationSender") at the injection point to pick the email bean, or @Qualifier("smsNotificationSender") for SMS. Alternatively, I mark one bean @Primary if it should be the default. This avoids ambiguity when Spring finds multiple candidates of the same type. A bean must load exchange rates once at startup. Which lifecycle callback do you use?I use @PostConstruct on a method inside the bean. Spring calls this method after dependency injection is complete but before the bean is used. I can call an external FX-rate service or load rates from a file. The bean stays a singleton and uses the loaded rates for all subsequent payment calculations. A build artifact is 200 MB because it includes unnecessary dependencies. What do you check?I run mvn dependency:analyze and mvn dependency:tree to find unused or oversized transitive dependencies. I remove unused direct dependencies and add for large transitive libraries that the project does not need. I also verify that test-scoped dependencies are not accidentally marked as compile scope.

title: 6.1.Key Concepts
text: What is Spring MVC?Spring MVC is a web framework within Spring that follows the Model-View-Controller pattern. The Model holds data, the View renders the user interface, and the Controller handles requests and business flow. The core idea is separation of concerns: controllers deal with HTTP, services with logic, and views with presentation. In a banking web portal, the controller receives a login request, the service validates credentials, the model carries account details, and the view renders the dashboard. For REST APIs, the "view" is usually JSON produced by message converters. What is the role of DispatcherServlet in Spring MVC?DispatcherServlet is the front controller that receives every HTTP request and acts as the single entry point. Its job is to delegate: it uses handler mappings to find the right controller, handler adapters to invoke it, and view resolvers or message converters to produce the response. It also applies interceptors and centralizes exception handling. In a supply-chain order system, every request to /orders/* first hits the DispatcherServlet, which then routes it to OrderController. Without it, each controller would need its own routing and common concern logic. Describe the lifecycle of a Spring MVC request.The lifecycle begins when the client sends an HTTP request, which the embedded Tomcat receives and forwards to DispatcherServlet. The servlet consults HandlerMapping to find the controller method matching the URL and HTTP method. It then invokes the method through a HandlerAdapter. The controller may call services, repositories, or external clients. It returns either a ModelAndView or a response body. For REST, HttpMessageConverters serialize the return object to JSON. For traditional MVC, a ViewResolver maps the logical view name to a JSP or Thymeleaf template. Finally, DispatcherServlet sends the rendered response back to the client. Interceptors run before and after the controller if configured. What is the difference between Spring MVC and Spring Boot Web MVC?Spring MVC is the web framework that handles HTTP requests, controllers, view resolution, and interceptors. It requires manual configuration such as registering DispatcherServlet in web.xml or a Java initializer, configuring view resolvers, and setting up component scanning. Spring Boot Web MVC is Spring MVC with opinionated auto- configuration. When you add spring-boot-starter-web, Spring Boot registers DispatcherServlet, configures default JSON message converters, starts embedded Tomcat, and enables component scanning automatically. In a traditional banking project using plain Spring MVC, you write more XML or Java config. In a modern Spring Boot banking microservice, you write the controller and the framework wires the rest. What is Tomcat's role in a Spring Boot application?Tomcat is the embedded servlet container bundled with Spring Boot Web. It listens for HTTP requests and passes them to DispatcherServlet. In production, a banking API runs as a JAR, and the embedded Tomcat starts automatically when the application launches. What is the WebApplicationContext?WebApplicationContext is a Spring container specialized for web applications. It holds web-specific beans such as controllers, view resolvers, and interceptors. DispatcherServlet creates or inherits this context to resolve and invoke controller beans when requests arrive. What is a DTO, and why do you use it?A DTO, or Data Transfer Object, is a plain Java object used to carry data between application layers or between the backend and external clients. Its purpose is to decouple the internal domain model from the API contract. In a banking app, instead of exposing a BankAccount JPA entity with database IDs, audit fields, and internal flags, you expose an AccountDto with only account number, balance, and holder name. DTOs prevent over-fetching of data, protect sensitive fields, reduce payload size, and make versioning easier because you can change the entity schema without changing what clients receive. What is the difference between an entity and a DTO?An entity maps to a database table and includes persistence annotations. A DTO is a plain object used for API requests and responses. For example, a supply-chain ShipmentEntity might have database IDs and audit fields, while a ShipmentDto contains only tracking number, origin, and destination. What is the role of a controller in Spring Web MVC?A controller receives HTTP requests, extracts input data, delegates processing to services, and returns a response or view name. In a banking transfer API, a TransferController maps a POST request, validates the DTO, calls TransferService, and returns the transaction reference. What is the difference between @Controller and @RestController?@Controller is a Spring stereotype for web controllers that return view names by default. If you want to return raw data like JSON from a @Controller method, you must add @ResponseBody to that method. @RestController is a convenience annotation that combines @Controller and @ResponseBody at the class level, so every method automatically writes its return value to the response body. For example, a banking AccountController using @RestController can return an AccountDto object, and Jackson automatically converts it to JSON without adding @ResponseBody on every method. What are @RequestMapping, @GetMapping, @PostMapping, @PutMapping, @PatchMapping, and @DeleteMapping?These annotations map URLs to controller methods. @RequestMapping is generic and can specify any HTTP method. The others are specialized shortcuts: @GetMapping for read, @PostMapping for create, @PutMapping for full update, @PatchMapping for partial update, and @DeleteMapping for delete. What is the difference between @PathVariable and @RequestParam?@PathVariable captures values from the URL path, like /accounts/{id}. @RequestParam reads query parameters or form data, like /accounts?type=savings. In a supply-chain API, /shipments/123 uses @PathVariable for the ID, while /shipments? status=DELIVERED uses @RequestParam. What is @RequestBody used for?@RequestBody tells Spring to read the HTTP request body and deserialize it into a Java object. The conversion is done by HttpMessageConverters, usually Jackson for JSON. For example, in a banking payment API, a POST body { "fromAccount": "12345", "toAccount": "67890", "amount": 1000 } is automatically mapped to a PaymentRequestDto object. The DTO can then be validated and passed to the service. If the JSON is malformed, Spring responds with a 400 Bad Request before the controller method executes. What is @ResponseBody and how does it work?@ResponseBody tells Spring to write the controller method's return value directly into the HTTP response body rather than interpreting it as a view name. Spring uses HttpMessageConverters, such as Jackson, to serialize the object to JSON or XML. For example, a method returning an AccountDto object becomes a JSON response like { "accountNumber": "...", "balance": 5000 }. When you use @RestController, every method inherits @ResponseBody automatically, which is why REST controllers do not need it on each method. What are the PUT, PATCH, and DELETE HTTP methods used for?PUT replaces an entire resource, so the client must send the full updated object. PATCH applies a partial update, so only the fields that change are sent. DELETE removes a resource or performs a logical delete. In a banking API, PUT might fully replace a customer profile, PATCH might update only the phone number or address, and DELETE might deactivate an account while keeping the record in the database. Using the right method keeps the API predictable and RESTful. When should you use PUT versus PATCH?Use PUT when the client is responsible for sending the complete resource, even for fields that do not change. Use PATCH when only a subset of fields needs to change, making the payload smaller and the operation more focused. For a supply-chain product catalog, PUT might replace the entire product object including name, category, price, and stock. PATCH might send only { "price": 29.99, "stock": 150 } to update just those two fields. PATCH reduces network overhead and avoids accidental overwrites of unchanged data. What is the role of the service layer in a Spring application?The service layer sits between controllers and repositories and owns the business logic of the application. It coordinates multiple repositories, external APIs, and domain rules, and it defines transaction boundaries. A PaymentService in a banking app validates transfer limits, calls a fraud-check service, debits the source account, credits the destination account, and logs the transaction. By placing these steps in one service method annotated with @Transactional, you ensure that all database changes succeed or fail together. Keeping business logic in the service layer also makes controllers thin, repositories focused on data access, and the application easier to test and maintain. How do you keep controllers thin and services fat?Controllers should only handle HTTP mapping, input validation, and response building. All business rules, calculations, and data orchestration belong in the service layer. This separation makes the supply-chain order controller easy to read and the OrderService easy to unit test. What is a repository in Spring Data JPA?A repository is an interface that Spring Data JPA implements automatically at runtime. It provides CRUD methods, query derivation, paging, sorting, and custom queries. A banking TransactionRepository extends JpaRepository gives you save, findById, and custom finder methods for free. What is the difference between CrudRepository, PagingAndSortingRepository, and JpaRepository?CrudRepository provides basic CRUD operations. PagingAndSortingRepository adds pagination and sorting. JpaRepository extends both and adds JPA-specific methods like flush, saveAll, and batch delete. Most Spring Boot projects use JpaRepository for its richer feature set. How does Spring Data JPA create query methods automatically?Spring Data JPA uses a query derivation mechanism. When you declare a method like findByAccountNumber or findByStatusAndCreatedDateAfter in a repository interface, it parses the method name into a JPQL query. Keywords like And, Or, GreaterThan, Like, OrderBy, and IgnoreCase control the query shape. For complex joins or queries that cannot be expressed by naming convention, you annotate the method with @Query and write JPQL or native SQL. For example, in a banking app, List findByAccountNumberAndTransactionDateBetween(String accountNumber, LocalDate start, LocalDate end) automatically generates a query that fetches transactions for an account within a date range. What is the purpose of pagination in Spring Data JPA?Pagination breaks large result sets into smaller pages to save memory and improve response time. You pass a Pageable object with page number, size, and sort. A supply-chain shipment search returning millions of rows can return Page with only twenty records per request. What is the difference between FetchType.EAGER and FetchType.LAZY?EAGER loads related entities immediately with the parent. LAZY loads them only when accessed. LAZY is preferred for performance. In a banking app, eager loading all transactions with every account query can cause the N+1 problem, so lazy loading is usually better. What is the N+1 SELECT problem, and how do you avoid it?The N+1 problem happens when one query fetches N parent records, and then N additional queries are executed to load related child records. For example, if you fetch 100 supply- chain orders and then lazily access order.getItems() for each order, Hibernate performs one query for the orders plus 100 queries for the items. To avoid this, you can use JOIN FETCH in a @Query annotation to load the parent and children in a single query. Alternatively, you can use entity graphs, batch fetching through hibernate.default_batch_fetch_size, or FetchType.EAGER carefully where it makes sense. The goal is to load the required data with the minimum number of queries. What is input validation in Spring Boot?Input validation ensures that request data meets business rules before processing. Spring Boot supports Bean Validation using annotations like @NotNull, @Size, @Min, @Max, @Email, and @Pattern. A banking fund-transfer DTO might require @NotBlank account numbers and @Positive amount. What is the difference between @Valid and @Validated?@Valid comes from the Bean Validation standard and is used on request bodies and nested objects. @Validated is a Spring extension that supports group validation and can be used at the class or method level. For a supply-chain API, @Valid validates a posted order DTO, while @Validated can validate method parameters by group. How do you create a custom validation annotation in Spring Boot?You define an annotation, a ConstraintValidator implementation, and link them using @Constraint. For example, a custom @AccountNumber validator checks that a banking account number matches a specific length and digit pattern. You then place the annotation on a DTO field. What is @ControllerAdvice and @RestControllerAdvice?@ControllerAdvice is a special annotation that marks a class as a global advisor for multiple controllers. Methods inside it can use @ExceptionHandler, @InitBinder, and @ModelAttribute to apply across controllers. @RestControllerAdvice is a convenience variant that combines @ControllerAdvice and @ResponseBody, so the return values are written directly to the response body as JSON. In a banking app, a single @RestControllerAdvice class can catch MethodArgumentNotValidException for validation failures, AccountNotFoundException for missing accounts, and generic exceptions, returning a consistent error response structure for every endpoint. What is @ExceptionHandler?@ExceptionHandler marks a method that handles a specific exception type thrown by controller methods. When that exception occurs, Spring routes control to the handler method instead of letting the exception bubble up as a 500 error. The method typically returns a ResponseEntity with an HTTP status and an error body. For example, a method annotated with @ExceptionHandler(AccountNotFoundException.class) in a @RestControllerAdvice class can return ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND).body(errorResponse). This keeps error handling centralized and user-friendly. How do you handle validation errors in Spring Boot?When validation fails, Spring throws MethodArgumentNotValidException. A global @ExceptionHandler can catch it, read the BindingResult to extract field-level errors, and return a structured error response. The response typically contains the HTTP status, a timestamp, the request path, and a list of errors with field names and messages. For example, in a supply-chain API, the client receives {"errors": [{"field":"quantity","message":"must be at least 1"}]} with a 400 status. This avoids raw stack traces and gives clients actionable feedback. How do you transform or customize API responses in Spring Web MVC?You can customize responses in several ways. Use ResponseEntity to set the exact HTTP status, headers, and body. Use DTOs to shape the data sent to the client. Use Jackson annotations like @JsonProperty to rename fields, @JsonIgnore to exclude fields, and @JsonFormat to format dates. Use message converters to support multiple formats like JSON and XML. For example, in a banking API, after creating an account, you return ResponseEntity.created(location).body(new AccountDto(account)) with a 201 status and a Location header pointing to the new resource. What is ResponseEntity?ResponseEntity is a Spring class that represents the complete HTTP response, including status code, headers, and body. It lets a controller decide exactly what the client receives. For example, ResponseEntity.created(URI.create("/orders/123")).body(orderDto) returns 201 Created, adds a Location header pointing to the new resource, and includes the created order data in the body. For errors, you can return ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.BAD_REQUEST).body(errorResponse). This is much more flexible than simply returning a DTO. How do you use Jackson annotations to shape JSON responses?Jackson annotations let you control serialization without changing the service logic. Use @JsonProperty("customer_name") to change the JSON key name. Use @JsonIgnore to prevent a field from appearing in the response, such as an internal database ID. Use @JsonFormat(pattern = "yyyy-MM-dd", shape = JsonFormat.Shape.STRING) to format dates. Use @JsonInclude(JsonInclude.Include.NON_NULL) to omit null fields and reduce payload size. In a banking API, you might hide the internal accountId and expose only accountNumber, while formatting transaction timestamps to a readable string. What is a lambda expression in Java 8?A lambda expression is a concise way to represent an anonymous function that implements a functional interface. It has parameters, an arrow, and a body. For example, list.forEach(item -> System.out.println(item)) replaces a verbose anonymous inner class. What is a functional interface?A functional interface has exactly one abstract method. Java 8 provides built-in ones like Predicate, Function, Consumer, and Supplier. The @FunctionalInterface annotation marks them and prevents adding extra abstract methods. A supply-chain Predicate can test if an order is urgent. What are intermediate and terminal operations in the Stream API?Intermediate operations transform a stream into another stream and are lazy, meaning they do not execute until a terminal operation is called. Examples include filter, map, flatMap, sorted, distinct, and limit. Terminal operations consume the stream and produce a result, such as collect, forEach, reduce, count, and findFirst. For example, in a banking report, transactions.stream().filter(t -> t.getType().equals("DEBIT")).map(Transaction::getAmount).collect(C ollectors.toList()) does nothing until collect runs. Then the entire pipeline executes efficiently in one pass. What is the difference between map and flatMap in streams?map transforms each element into exactly one new element, preserving the one-to-one relationship. flatMap transforms each element into a stream of zero or more elements, and then flattens all those nested streams into a single stream. For example, if you have List and each Order contains List, orders.stream().map(Order::getItems) gives you a stream of lists. orders.stream().flatMap(order -> order.getItems().stream()) gives you a single stream of all OrderItem objects. This is useful for nested data in supply-chain order processing. How do you filter and collect data using streams?Use filter with a predicate to keep matching elements, then collect with a collector like Collectors.toList(). For example, transactions.stream().filter(t -> t.getAmount() > 10000).collect(Collectors.toList()) finds high-value banking transactions. What is the purpose of reduce in streams?reduce combines stream elements into a single value using an accumulator. For example, amounts.stream().reduce(0, Integer::sum) adds all transaction amounts. It is useful for aggregations like totals, averages, and maximums. How do you group or partition data with streams?Use Collectors.groupingBy to group elements by a key, such as grouping transactions by status. Use Collectors.partitioningBy to split into two groups based on a predicate. A supply-chain app might group shipments by destination country for a summary report. What is Optional, and why is it useful?Optional is a wrapper introduced in Java 8 that represents a value that may or may not be present. It forces the caller to explicitly handle the absence of a value instead of risking a NullPointerException. For example, a repository method can return Optional instead of an Account that might be null. The service can then call .orElseThrow(() -> new AccountNotFoundException("Account not found")) or .ifPresent(account -> process(account)). This makes the code safer and more readable, especially in banking lookups where missing data is a real possibility. What are method references, and when do you use them?Method references are shorthand for lambdas that simply call an existing method. For example, names.stream().map(String::toUpperCase) is shorter than names.stream().map(s -> s.toUpperCase()). Use them when a lambda only calls one method without extra logic. What is lazy evaluation in streams?Lazy evaluation means intermediate operations are not executed until a terminal operation is called. This allows the stream to optimize by fusing operations and avoiding unnecessary work. For a large supply-chain dataset, filtering before mapping reduces the number of transformations. What are peek, limit, and skip used for in streams?peek inspects elements for debugging without changing them. limit truncates the stream to a maximum size. skip discards the first N elements. In a banking transaction history, skip(10).limit(20) implements simple pagination. How do you find duplicates in a list using streams?You can use Collectors.groupingBy and then filter groups with more than one element, or use a Set to track seen items. For example, list.stream().filter(i -> Collections.frequency(list, i) > 1).collect(Collectors.toSet()) finds duplicate account numbers, though it is not the most efficient for huge lists. What is a HandlerInterceptor, and when do you use it?A HandlerInterceptor is a Spring MVC mechanism that intercepts HTTP requests within the Spring lifecycle. It has three methods: preHandle runs before the controller and can block the request by returning false; postHandle runs after the controller but before the view is rendered; afterCompletion runs after the entire request is complete and is useful for cleanup or final logging. In a banking API, you can use an interceptor to log every incoming request, add a correlation ID to the response, or check a custom security header before the controller executes. It is registered globally through WebMvcConfigurer.addInterceptors. How do you serve static resources in Spring Boot Web MVC?Spring Boot serves static files from /static, /public, /resources, or /META- INF/resources by default. You can also register custom resource handlers with WebMvcConfigurer.addResourceHandlers. In a supply-chain portal, PDF invoices might be served from the /static/invoices folder. What is content negotiation in Spring MVC?Content negotiation lets a controller produce different response formats, such as JSON or XML, based on the client's Accept header. Spring uses ContentNegotiatingViewResolver and message converters. A banking partner requesting XML can receive the same data as another partner requesting JSON. What is the difference between a filter and an interceptor?A servlet filter operates at the servlet container level, before the request reaches DispatcherServlet. It is part of the Java Servlet API and can wrap any request or response, making it suitable for low-level tasks like request logging, CORS handling, compression, or security headers. An interceptor operates inside the Spring MVC lifecycle, after DispatcherServlet has received the request. It has access to the controller handler, the model, and the view, making it suitable for Spring-aware concerns like adding model attributes, authentication checks, or post-processing responses. In a banking app, a filter might add security headers to every response, while an interceptor might check a per-endpoint permission annotation. How do you write a unit test for a Spring MVC controller?I annotate the test class with @WebMvcTest(TransferController.class) so Spring loads only the web layer components needed for that controller. I inject MockMvc to simulate HTTP requests without starting a real server. I use @MockBean to replace the service layer with a Mockito mock. In the test, I configure the mock with when(transferService.transfer(...)).thenReturn(transactionReferenc e), then call mockMvc.perform(post("/transfers").contentType(MediaType.APPLICATI ON_JSON).content(jsonBody)). I verify the HTTP status, response body, and sometimes the interaction with the mock using verify(transferService, times(1)).transfer(...). This tests the controller in isolation and runs quickly because no database or external service is started. What is @WebMvcTest used for?@WebMvcTest is a test slice that loads only Spring MVC components for a specific controller, without starting the full application context or database. It makes controller tests fast. In a supply-chain project, you test OrderController without loading OrderRepository. Scenario-Based Questions A banking portal receives an HTTP POST to /accounts/{id}/transfer. Walk me through the Spring MVC flow.The flow starts when the client sends the POST request with JSON body and account ID. Tomcat accepts the connection and forwards the request to DispatcherServlet. DispatcherServlet consults handler mappings to find AccountController.transferFunds. It invokes the method through a handler adapter. The @PathVariable extracts the account ID and @RequestBody deserializes the JSON into a TransferRequestDto. The controller calls TransferService, which validates balances, calls the fraud engine, and processes the transfer inside a transaction. The service returns a transaction reference. The controller builds a ResponseEntity with 201 Created and a Location header. Jackson converts the DTO to JSON, and DispatcherServlet sends the response back to the client. A supply-chain client wants to update only the delivery date of a shipment. Which HTTP method and Spring annotation do you use?Use PATCH with @PatchMapping("/shipments/{shipmentId}"). PATCH is designed for partial updates, meaning the client sends only the fields that need to change. The controller maps the shipment ID with @PathVariable and the request body with @RequestBody. The DTO contains only deliveryDate and perhaps a version field for optimistic locking. The service loads the existing shipment, applies the change, and saves. Because PUT would require the client to send the entire shipment object, PATCH is the cleaner choice here. The response typically returns 200 OK with the updated shipment DTO. A bank needs to expose account details but must hide the internal database ID and the customer's full address. How do you handle this?The safest approach is to introduce a dedicated AccountDto that contains only the fields the API contract allows, such as account number, account type, available balance, and a masked phone number. In the service layer, I map the Account entity to the DTO using a mapper like MapStruct or a manual mapping method. The controller returns the DTO, never the entity. This prevents internal fields like the database ID, full address, and audit metadata from leaking to clients. It also decouples the database schema from the API contract, so future schema changes do not break external consumers. A supply-chain API accepts a huge CSV upload of orders. How do you validate each row before saving?I process the CSV in a streaming manner to avoid loading everything into memory. For each row, I map it to an OrderImportDto and run Bean Validation. Fields like product code use @NotBlank, quantity uses @Positive, and delivery date uses @Future or a custom date validator. I use a Validator instance directly or validate programmatically. I collect all failures in a list with row number, field, and message. Valid rows are grouped and processed in batches using saveAll. At the end, the API returns a summary such as total rows, successful imports, failed rows, and the detailed error list. This ensures no partial or invalid data enters the database. A banking API returns 500 Internal Server Error for every invalid request. How do you improve the client experience?The problem is that unhandled exceptions default to 500, which hides the real cause. I add a @RestControllerAdvice class with specific @ExceptionHandler methods. For MethodArgumentNotValidException, I return 400 Bad Request with a list of field errors and messages. For AccountNotFoundException, I return 404 Not Found with the account identifier. For business rule violations, I return 409 Conflict or 422 Unprocessable Entity. For unexpected errors, I return 500 but log the stack trace internally and expose only a generic reference ID. The response body always follows the same structure: status, timestamp, path, message, and optional details. This gives clients clear, actionable feedback instead of a generic server error. A supply-chain search endpoint returns 100,000 shipment records, and the UI freezes. How do you fix it?Implement pagination with Pageable in the repository method. Return Page with only the requested page, size, and sort. Add default limits and validate that clients cannot request an excessively large page size. This reduces memory usage and improves response time. A banking fund-transfer DTO has many fields, and validation rules differ for retail versus corporate customers. How do you manage this?Use validation groups. Define interfaces like RetailValidation and CorporateValidation. Annotate DTO fields with @NotNull(groups = CorporateValidation.class) and use @Validated(CorporateValidation.class) on the controller method. This applies different rules based on the customer type without duplicating DTOs. A supply-chain order response needs to show total amount, currency, and item count in a custom shape that does not match the entity. How do you build it?I create a dedicated OrderSummaryDto with fields like orderNumber, totalAmount, currency, and itemCount. In the service layer, I fetch the Order entity along with its line items, either eagerly or via a fetch join. I then compute the total by summing unitPrice * quantity across items, determine the currency from the order header, and count the number of line items. I map these computed values into the DTO. The controller returns OrderSummaryDto instead of the raw entity. This gives the client a clean, purpose-built shape without exposing internal persistence details or forcing the client to do its own calculations. A banking transaction report must group transactions by type and show the sum for each type. How do you implement this with streams?I use transactions.stream().collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Transaction::g etType, Collectors.summarizingDouble(Transaction::getAmount))). This returns a Map where the key is the transaction type and the value contains sum, count, average, min, and max. From there, I iterate over the map entries and build a list of TransactionSummaryDto objects with fields for type, totalAmount, transactionCount, and averageAmount. The service returns this list, and the controller serializes it to JSON. This approach keeps the grouping and aggregation concise without writing explicit loops. A supply-chain API needs to return the top five highest-value orders placed in the last 30 days. Write the stream pipeline.orders.stream().filter(o -> o.getOrderDate().isAfter(LocalDate.now().minusDays(30))).sorted(Co mparator.comparing(Order::getTotalAmount).reversed()).limit(5).col lect(Collectors.toList()). This filters by date, sorts descending by amount, limits to five, and collects the result. A developer is calling repository.findAll() and loading millions of banking transactions into memory. What is the risk and the fix?The risk is an OutOfMemoryError and poor performance. The fix is to use pagination with Pageable, or stream results with Stream and process them in batches. For reports, consider database-level aggregation or batch processing instead of loading all rows at once. A supply-chain order has a list of line items, and you need a single list of all product IDs across orders. How do you use flatMap?I write orders.stream().flatMap(order -> order.getItems().stream()).map(OrderItem::getProductId).distinct() .collect(Collectors.toList()). First, flatMap takes each Order, gets its List, and turns that nested list into a single continuous stream of OrderItem objects. Then map extracts the product ID from each item. distinct removes duplicates so the same product appearing in multiple orders appears only once. Finally, collect builds the result list. This is a classic pattern for flattening one-to-many relationships in domain models. A banking client sends a JSON payload where some dates must be formatted as yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm. How do you enforce this?Use @JsonFormat(pattern = "yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm", shape = JsonFormat.Shape.STRING) on the DTO date field. Alternatively, register a custom Jackson2ObjectMapperBuilderCustomizer bean for global date formatting. This ensures every date in the response follows the required pattern. A supply-chain shipment search can filter by status, destination, or date range. How do you build a flexible query in Spring Data JPA?If the filter combinations are fixed and small, I use derived query methods like findByStatusAndDestinationAndShipmentDateBetween. For dynamic filters where any combination can be null or present, I use the JPA Specification API. I make the repository extend JpaSpecificationExecutor, then build a Specification in the service by chaining predicates. Each predicate is only added if the corresponding request parameter is present. For example, status gets an equal predicate, destination gets a like predicate, and the date range gets a between predicate. The repository then calls findAll(spec, pageable) to return filtered, paginated results. This keeps the query construction flexible and avoids writing many overloaded repository methods. A banking API must reject requests where the transfer amount exceeds the customer's daily limit. Where do you enforce this rule?Enforce it in the service layer, not the controller or DTO validation, because it requires business data such as the day's previous transfers. The service queries the transaction history, calculates the running total, and throws a domain exception if the new transfer would exceed the limit. A Spring Boot application starts but all controller endpoints return 404. What do you check?First, I check that the controller class is annotated with @RestController or @Controller and that the URL mappings are correct. Next, I verify that the controller package is under the component scan path defined by the main class with @SpringBootApplication. If the controller is in a different package, I add an explicit @ComponentScan. I also check that the application type is not set to non-web via spring.main.web-application-type=none. If there is a custom WebMvcConfigurationSupport bean, it disables Spring Boot's auto- configuration and may remove default static resource handling or message converters. Finally, I inspect the actuator /beans endpoint or log the loaded controllers to confirm they were registered. A supply-chain partner receives XML instead of JSON from the same endpoint. How does Spring handle this?Spring uses content negotiation to decide the response format based on the client's Accept header and the available message converters. If the client sends Accept: application/xml, Spring looks for a converter that can produce XML. Adding jackson- dataformat-xml to the classpath registers an XML-capable message converter. The controller method still returns the same DTO, but the selected converter serializes it to XML instead of JSON. The endpoint URL does not need to change. This works as long as the DTO has a public no-arg constructor and public getters, which Jackson requires for both JSON and XML serialization. A developer wants to log every incoming banking request and the time it took. Which Spring MVC component is best?A HandlerInterceptor is the best choice because it runs inside the Spring MVC lifecycle and has access to the controller handler and request attributes. I create a class implementing HandlerInterceptor and override preHandle to capture the start time and store it in a request attribute. In afterCompletion, I retrieve the start time, calculate the duration, and log the HTTP method, URI, status code, and elapsed milliseconds. I register the interceptor globally by implementing WebMvcConfigurer and overriding addInterceptors. This gives consistent request logging across all controllers without polluting each method with timing code. A servlet filter is an alternative, but it has less context about the Spring handler. A custom validation rule checks whether a banking account number is active. How do you implement it?I implement it as a custom Bean Validation constraint. First, I create an annotation @ActiveAccount annotated with @Constraint(validatedBy = ActiveAccountValidator.class). Then I write the ActiveAccountValidator class implementing ConstraintValidator. Inside isValid, I inject AccountRepository and check whether the account number exists and its status equals ACTIVE. I place @ActiveAccount on the DTO field, such as private String fromAccountNumber. When the controller parameter is annotated with @Valid, Spring runs all validators including this custom one before calling the service. If validation fails, Spring throws MethodArgumentNotValidException, which the global exception handler converts to a 400 response. A supply-chain service must process a list of delayed orders and send a notification for each. Write a concise stream-based approach.orders.stream().filter(o -> "DELAYED".equals(o.getStatus())).forEach(notificationService::send DelayAlert). This filters delayed orders and invokes the notification service for each. If the notification is slow, replace forEach with parallelStream cautiously or hand it off to an async task executor. A banking report endpoint currently returns entities directly. The audit team asks you to hide certain fields. What is the safest approach?Introduce DTOs and map only the fields permitted by the audit policy. Use MapStruct or a manual mapper to convert from entity to DTO. Remove any @JsonIgnore band-aids on entities, because entities should not be shaped by API concerns. Return the DTO from the controller. A PATCH request to update a banking customer profile fails because required fields are missing. Is this expected?Yes, this is expected if validation annotations require fields that are not being sent. For PATCH, the DTO should only contain updatable fields, and validation groups should mark those fields as optional for the patch group. Alternatively, use a separate CustomerPatchDto with relaxed validation and apply @Null or optional checks explicitly. A supply-chain endpoint returns LazyInitializationException after moving to a REST controller. Why?The exception happens because a lazily loaded collection, such as order.getItems(), is accessed after the Hibernate session has closed. In a REST controller, Jackson serializes the returned entity to JSON. If Jackson tries to read a lazy collection during serialization, but the transaction and persistence context are already closed, Hibernate cannot fetch the data and throws LazyInitializationException. The proper fix is to load all needed data inside the service's transaction before returning it. Options include using JOIN FETCH in the repository query, defining an entity graph with @EntityGraph, using a DTO projection with @Query, or mapping the entity to a DTO within the transaction so Jackson only sees already- loaded data. Returning entities directly from REST controllers is generally discouraged for this reason. A banking application must ensure no duplicate account numbers are submitted in a batch import. How can streams help?Convert the list to a stream and collect into a Set to detect duplicates, or use Collectors.groupingBy and filter groups with count greater than one. For large batches, combine stream checking with a database unique constraint as the final guard against duplicates. A Spring Boot test for a controller fails because the service is not a bean. What annotation fixes this?Add @MockBean on the service field inside the @WebMvcTest class. Spring replaces the real service with a Mockito mock in the test context. Then configure stubbing with when(service.method(...)).thenReturn(...) to control the controller's behavior during the test.

title: 7.1.Key Concepts
text: What is an ORM, and what does Hibernate do?ORM stands for Object-Relational Mapping. It maps Java objects to database tables so developers can work with objects instead of writing raw SQL. Hibernate is an ORM implementation for Java. In a banking app, instead of writing JDBC to insert an account row, you create an Account entity object and call repository.save(account). Hibernate translates that into an INSERT statement. Remember that Hibernate is one JPA provider; EclipseLink and OpenJPA are others, and switching providers changes provider-specific tuning but keeps the annotation model. What is the difference between JPA and Hibernate?JPA is a specification that defines how to map Java objects to relational tables. Hibernate is the most popular implementation of JPA. Spring Data JPA sits on top of Hibernate and generates repository implementations for you. When a query misbehaves, you usually need to inspect Hibernate SQL logs or SessionFactory settings, not just the Spring Data method name. What happens internally when you call repository.save(entity)?Spring Data JPA routes the call to SimpleJpaRepository.save. It checks the entity ID. If the ID is null or the version indicates a new instance, it calls entityManager.persist(entity). Otherwise it calls merge(entity). The entity becomes associated with the current persistence context. The actual INSERT/UPDATE is usually deferred until flush time, typically at transaction commit. If the JSON payload already contains an ID, Hibernate may perform a merge and update an existing row instead of inserting. What is the difference between persist() and merge()?persist() takes a transient entity and makes it managed. It should not have an existing ID. merge() copies the state of a detached entity into a managed instance, inserting if needed. persist() returns the same instance; merge() returns the managed copy. Calling merge() with a partially populated detached entity can overwrite non-null database fields with nulls, so partial updates should fetch the managed entity first. What is an Entity lifecycle in Hibernate?Entities move through four states: transient (new object, no ID, unknown to Hibernate), persistent (associated with the EntityManager, changes tracked and flushed at commit), detached (was persistent but session closed, changes not tracked), and removed (marked for deletion, row deleted at flush). A controller that maps JSON to an entity and calls save() may accidentally update an existing row if the JSON contains an ID. What is the persistence context, and how does it act as a first-level cache?The persistence context is the EntityManager’s in-memory cache of managed entities. Within a single session, loading the same entity by ID twice returns the same Java instance, and dirty checking happens against this cache at flush time. Reading an entity, modifying it, and reading it again in the same transaction shows the in-memory change before flush, but another process updating the database between reads can cause stale data because the cache is isolated to one session. What is the difference between CrudRepository, PagingAndSortingRepository, and JpaRepository?CrudRepository provides basic CRUD operations. PagingAndSortingRepository adds pagination and sorting support. JpaRepository adds bulk operations (saveAll, deleteInBatch), saveAndFlush, flush, getReferenceById, and Query By Example. Use JpaRepository for new Spring Boot code and CrudRepository only when you want to avoid coupling to JPA-specific features. What is FetchType.LAZY vs FetchType.EAGER?LAZY loads related data only when it is accessed. EAGER loads it immediately with the parent entity. Eager loading on broad object graphs causes large SQL joins, Cartesian products, memory bloat, and unpredictable performance. Lazy loading keeps the initial query small but requires explicit loading inside a transaction to avoid LazyInitializationException. What is the N+1 SELECT problem?It happens when one query fetches N parent rows, and then accessing a lazy collection on each parent triggers N additional SELECT statements. Fix it with JOIN FETCH, entity graphs, batch fetching, DTO projections, or subselect fetching. A customer list endpoint that returns 100 orders and lazily loads each order’s items outside the service can make 101 queries instead of one, causing high database CPU under load. What is LazyInitializationException, and what are the correct fixes?It occurs when code accesses a lazy association after the EntityManager has closed. In Spring web apps this often happens during JSON serialization because the session was bound to the now-completed transaction. Fix it by loading all needed data inside the transaction using JOIN FETCH or an entity graph, or by returning DTO projections. Do not enable Open Session in View as a default fix because it hides the problem and causes N+1 issues in production. What is the difference between findById() and getReferenceById()?findById() hits the database immediately and returns an Optional with the full entity. getReferenceById() returns a Hibernate proxy without a database round trip and is useful when you only need a foreign-key reference. If the referenced row does not exist, the proxy throws EntityNotFoundException only when a property is accessed. Explain mappedBy and @JoinColumn with an example.In a bidirectional relationship, only one side owns the foreign key. The owning side uses @JoinColumn; the inverse side uses mappedBy. @Entity public class Order { @OneToMany(mappedBy = "order", cascade = CascadeType.ALL, orphanRemoval = true) private List items = new ArrayList<>(); } @Entity public class OrderItem { @ManyToOne(fetch = FetchType.LAZY) @JoinColumn(name = "order_id") private Order order; } Forgetting mappedBy or putting @JoinColumn on both sides creates a duplicate foreign-key column or a join table, leading to inconsistent data. What are cascade types and orphanRemoval?Cascade types propagate operations from a parent to its children: PERSIST, MERGE, REMOVE, REFRESH, DETACH, and ALL. orphanRemoval = true deletes a child entity when it is removed from the parent collection, even if the parent still exists. CascadeType.ALL on broad or shared relationships is dangerous because deleting an order line could cascade to a shared product catalog. What is @Transactional, and what does it do under the hood?Spring creates a proxy around the annotated bean. When the method is called, the proxy starts or joins a transaction, binds an EntityManager to it, executes the method, and commits or rolls back based on the outcome. By default, rollback happens for unchecked exceptions and Error, not for checked exceptions. A @Transactional annotation on a private, final, or static method is ignored because Spring cannot proxy it, and calling the method from within the same class bypasses the proxy. What are transaction propagation levels?REQUIRED joins the existing transaction or creates a new one and is the default. REQUIRES_NEW suspends the current transaction and starts a new one, useful for audit logging that must commit even if the main transaction rolls back. MANDATORY requires an existing transaction and throws if none exists. SUPPORTS, NOT_SUPPORTED, and NEVER are rarely used and control whether the method participates in an existing transaction. REQUIRES_NEW still uses the same physical database connection unless configured otherwise. What does @Transactional(readOnly = true) do?It tells Spring and Hibernate that the transaction only reads. Hibernate can skip dirty checking and avoid unnecessary flushes, and some connection pools or replication drivers may route read-only transactions to read replicas. It does not prevent writes at the database level; a modifying native query inside a read-only transaction will still execute. What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic locking?Optimistic locking uses a @Version column. Each update checks that the version has not changed. If another transaction modified the row, the update fails with OptimisticLockException. Pessimistic locking uses database locks (PESSIMISTIC_READ / PESSIMISTIC_WRITE) to serialize access. Optimistic locking is better for read-heavy workloads with occasional contention; pessimistic locking is better when contention is high and you want to serialize access rather than retry. What are the ACID properties?Atomicity means a transaction completes entirely or not at all. Consistency means the database remains in a valid state after the transaction. Isolation means concurrent transactions do not interfere with each other. Durability means committed changes survive failures. A fund transfer must debit one account and credit another atomically, otherwise the books will be out of balance. What are SQL isolation levels?READ UNCOMMITTED allows dirty reads. READ COMMITTED prevents dirty reads. REPEATABLE READ prevents dirty and non-repeatable reads. SERIALIZABLE prevents all anomalies, including phantom reads. Spring/Hibernate usually follows the database default, such as READ COMMITTED in Oracle and PostgreSQL, and REPEATABLE READ in MySQL InnoDB. What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING?WHERE filters rows before grouping. HAVING filters groups after aggregation. You cannot use aggregate functions in WHERE except inside a subquery. What are the differences between DDL, DML, DCL, and TCL?DDL (Data Definition Language) includes CREATE, ALTER, DROP, and TRUNCATE. DML (Data Manipulation Language) includes SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE. DCL (Data Control Language) includes GRANT and REVOKE. TCL (Transaction Control Language) includes COMMIT, ROLLBACK, and SAVEPOINT. Explain INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, and FULL JOIN.INNER JOIN returns rows that have matches in both tables. LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right, filling with nulls where there is no match. RIGHT JOIN is the reverse of LEFT JOIN. FULL JOIN returns rows when there is a match in either table. A report that lists all customers and their orders should use LEFT JOIN; using INNER JOIN silently drops customers who have not placed an order. What is the difference between UNION and UNION ALL?UNION removes duplicates and is slower. UNION ALL keeps duplicates and is faster. Use UNION ALL when the sources are already disjoint or duplicates are acceptable. What is normalization vs denormalization?Normalization reduces redundancy and improves data integrity by organizing data into related tables. Denormalization intentionally adds redundancy to improve read performance. Denormalize for read-heavy reporting, pre-computed aggregates, and low-write / high-read workloads, but you must have a strategy to keep redundant data consistent. What is an index, and how does it help?An index is a data structure, typically a B-tree, that speeds up data retrieval by letting the database find rows without scanning the entire table. Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes and consume disk space. What is a composite index?A composite index is an index on multiple columns. Column order matters. A composite index on (status, created_date) helps a query filtering by both columns, but may not help a query that filters only on created_date. What is a covering index?A covering index contains all the columns needed by a query. The database can answer the query entirely from the index without looking up the table heap, reducing I/O significantly. It helps read-heavy queries with stable access patterns. What is the difference between a primary key and a unique key?A primary key uniquely identifies a row, cannot be null, and there is only one per table. A unique key also enforces uniqueness but allows one null per column in most databases, and a table can have multiple unique keys. What is a foreign key?A foreign key enforces referential integrity between tables. It prevents orphaned rows and can cascade deletes or updates. The cost is additional index maintenance and potential locking overhead during writes. What are stored procedures and triggers?A stored procedure is a precompiled set of SQL statements stored in the database. A trigger automatically executes in response to an INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE event. Triggers can make debugging hard because business logic is hidden from the application code. Stored procedures can centralize complex logic but reduce portability. What is the difference between DELETE, TRUNCATE, and DROP?DELETE removes specific rows, logs each row, can be rolled back in a transaction, and fires triggers. TRUNCATE removes all rows quickly with minimal logging, generally cannot be rolled back, and resets auto-increment counters. DROP removes the entire table structure, data, and dependencies. Prefer DELETE with a WHERE clause for controlled removal in production; TRUNCATE and DROP require change-management scrutiny. What are aggregate functions in SQL?Aggregate functions perform calculations on a set of values. Common examples are COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, and MAX. They are usually used with GROUP BY. What is the difference between GROUP BY and ORDER BY?GROUP BY groups rows with the same values into summary rows. ORDER BY sorts the result set. You can use them together. What are window functions?Window functions perform calculations across a set of rows related to the current row without collapsing the result set. Examples are ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), LEAD(), LAG(), and SUM() OVER. To find the most recent transaction per account: SELECT account_number, amount, transaction_date FROM ( SELECT account_number, amount, transaction_date, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY account_number ORDER BY transaction_date DESC) rn FROM transactions ) t WHERE rn = 1; What is a CTE?A Common Table Expression (WITH ...) defines a temporary named result set. It improves readability for complex queries and enables recursion with WITH RECURSIVE. Some databases materialize CTEs, which can affect performance. What is pagination in Spring Data JPA?Pagination splits large result sets into pages. A repository method accepts a Pageable and returns a Page that includes content, total count, total pages, and metadata. Example: Page findByAccountNumber(String accountNumber, Pageable pageable);. Always cap the page size to prevent unbounded result sets. What is @Query in Spring Data JPA?@Query lets you define custom JPQL or native SQL for a repository method. @Query("SELECT o FROM Order o WHERE o.status = :status") List findByStatus(@Param("status") String status); Use native SQL when you need database-specific features like window functions, CTEs, or hints. What is @Modifying?@Modifying marks a @Query method that performs an UPDATE or DELETE. It requires an active transaction. Use clearAutomatically = true if you want the persistence context cleared afterward to avoid stale entities. What is the Specification API?It lets you build dynamic, type-safe predicates programmatically. Each filter is a Specification that returns a Predicate, and they can be combined with and() / or(). It is best for search screens with optional filters. What are projections?Projections return only selected fields instead of full entities. They reduce memory use, avoid lazy-loading issues, and improve serialization performance. Interface-based projections are called closed projections; class-based DTOs are open projections. What is Query By Example?QBE lets you pass a probe entity with non-null fields as search criteria. It is useful for simple exact-match searches but falls short for range queries, LIKE, nested conditions, and dynamic optional filters. What is the difference between first-level and second-level cache?The first-level cache is the persistence context; it caches entities within one EntityManager session. The second-level cache sits above the persistence context and caches entities across sessions using providers such as EhCache, Caffeine, or Redis. The second-level cache is best for reference data that changes rarely, such as country codes or product categories. What is the query cache, and why can it be a trap?The query cache stores the IDs returned by a query. The second time the query runs, Hibernate looks up the IDs and fetches the entities from the second-level cache. If the second-level cache is not configured, or if the underlying tables change frequently, the query cache adds overhead without benefit. How do you optimize batch inserts with Spring Data JPA?Set spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.jdbc.batch_size to 50 or 100, process data in chunks and call saveAll(chunk), periodically flush and clear the persistence context, use a SEQUENCE ID strategy with a matching allocation size, and avoid IDENTITY for large batches. For very large volumes, consider StatelessSession or raw JDBC batching. What is a schema migration tool?Tools like Flyway and Liquibase manage database schema changes through versioned scripts. They make environments reproducible and avoid the risks of ddl-auto = update in production. Using spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=update in production can drop columns or alter data unexpectedly. What is the difference between a view and a materialized view?A view is a virtual query; it does not store data. A materialized view stores the result physically and must be refreshed. Materialized views are useful for expensive aggregations in reporting but add refresh complexity. What is a covering index, and when does it help?A covering index includes all columns a query needs. The database answers the query from the index alone, avoiding table lookups. It helps read-heavy queries with stable access patterns. How do you detect whether a query uses an index?Use the database execution plan. In MySQL use EXPLAIN SELECT ..., in PostgreSQL use EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT ..., in Oracle use EXPLAIN PLAN FOR ..., and in SQL Server use the execution plan viewer or SET SHOWPLAN_ALL ON. Look for type=ALL or Seq Scan as signs of a full table scan. What is the difference between save() and saveAndFlush()?save() schedules the entity for insertion/update and defers SQL until flush. saveAndFlush() forces an immediate flush. Use saveAndFlush sparingly, only when the next step in the same transaction needs the row visible in the database. What are composite keys, and how do you map them?A composite key uses multiple columns to uniquely identify a row. Use @EmbeddedId with an @Embeddable class for clean mapping, or @IdClass when matching legacy schemas. What is @EnableJpaAuditing?It enables automatic population of audit fields such as @CreatedDate, @LastModifiedDate, @CreatedBy, and @LastModifiedBy. It removes audit boilerplate and supports compliance requirements. What is Hibernate Envers?Hibernate Envers provides entity versioning and audit history. It tracks every insert, update, and delete, storing snapshots in audit tables. It is useful for compliance and debugging data changes. What is the difference between getReferenceById and findById?findById loads the full entity immediately. getReferenceById returns a proxy without hitting the database. Use getReferenceById when you only need a foreign-key reference; use findById when you need the data. How do you handle an OptimisticLockException in production?For user-facing flows, return 409 Conflict or retry a bounded number of times with backoff. For batch jobs, log the conflict and continue or move the record to a retry queue. For high- contention flows, consider redesigning with queues or pessimistic locking. What is a deadlock, and how do you prevent it?A deadlock occurs when two transactions each hold a lock and wait for a lock held by the other. Prevent it by always locking resources in a consistent order, such as by account ID ascending, or by using a single update statement where possible. What is the purpose of @Column, @Table, and @Entity?@Entity marks a class as mapped. @Table maps it to a specific table and schema. @Column maps a field to a specific column with constraints like name, nullable, and length. A new Java entity Transaction may map to a legacy Oracle table TXN_HISTORY with columns like ACCT_NBR and TXN_AMT. What is @Temporal, and is it still relevant?@Temporal maps java.util.Date or Calendar to a SQL date/time/timestamp. With Java 8+, prefer java.time types such as LocalDate, LocalDateTime, or Instant, which are type-safe and avoid timezone ambiguities. What is a Hibernate proxy?A Hibernate proxy is a runtime subclass created for lazy loading. It loads data only when a method is called. Because of this, entity.getClass() may return a proxy class name, and equality checks using getClass() can fail. Use Hibernate.getClass(entity) or instanceof in equals(). What are the risks of returning JPA entities directly from a REST controller?Serialization may trigger LazyInitializationException, bidirectional relationships can cause infinite recursion, internal fields leak to the API contract, and future schema changes break API consumers. The better approach is to return a DTO mapped inside the service layer. What is the difference between Page and Slice? Page includes the total count of records, which requires an extra COUNT query. Slice only knows whether there is a next page, so it is cheaper when the total count is not needed. A banking customer reports that a duplicate transaction was created during a fund transfer. How do you investigate and prevent this?Duplicate transactions usually come from retry behavior on the client side, missing idempotency, or a race condition on the server. I start by checking the request logs for the same client request sent twice, looking at timestamps, correlation IDs, and business reference numbers. I query the transaction table for rows with the same reference or amount around the reported time. To prevent recurrence I introduce an idempotency layer: either store processed keys in a dedicated table with a unique constraint keyed by idempotency_key and client_id, or use a naturally unique business reference. The service should first check for an existing record before processing, and on a duplicate it returns the previously computed result. The operation must remain inside a transaction so the idempotency record and the business outcome are committed atomically. An API that worked in staging is slow in production. What do you check first?The first step is to reproduce the slow call in production and capture the exact SQL being executed using show-sql, a query log, or an APM trace. Then I compare the production execution plan against staging with the same query shape. Production has larger data volumes, different data distributions, and possibly missing indexes. Common causes are N+1 selects from lazy collections, a missing index that existed in staging, stale table statistics leading to bad plans, lock contention, or a different Hibernate/datasource configuration. I also verify whether connection- pool settings, batch size, and cache configuration are identical. The fix depends on the root cause: add or rebuild the index, rewrite the query with JOIN FETCH or a projection, tune batching, or paginate large result sets. A report query times out after a schema change. What is your approach?I run EXPLAIN / EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the report query against the new schema to see if the plan changed. The usual culprits are a dropped index, a changed column type that prevents index usage, stale statistics after the change, or the optimizer choosing a nested-loop join over a hash join because cardinality estimates are wrong. I also confirm the query still references the correct columns and that no implicit conversion was introduced. If the report is intrinsically expensive, I consider a materialized view, a pre-aggregated summary table, or moving the workload to a read replica. If the performance regression is blocking business users and the fix will take time, the safest step is to roll back the schema change while a permanent solution is designed and tested. After a deployment, orders and their items are out of sync — some items have no order. What happened?Orphaned items usually mean the relationship was modified without maintaining both sides of a bidirectional mapping, or a cascade was misconfigured. For example, if code removes an item from Order.items but does not set orderItem.order = null, Hibernate may not recognize the change. Another cause is CascadeType.REMOVE or orphanRemoval set incorrectly on the wrong side. A bulk delete or a manual migration that bypassed JPA cascades can also leave child rows behind. To fix it I audit the relationship code, ensure helper methods update both sides, remove dangerous cascades, and add a database-level foreign key if one is missing. For existing orphans I run a cleanup script and add a not-null constraint on the foreign key to prevent recurrence. A nightly batch job starts failing with OutOfMemoryError. It processes millions of rows with saveAll().The persistence context accumulates every entity loaded or saved until the transaction commits, so a single transaction over millions of rows eventually exhausts heap memory. The fix is to process data in chunks, for example 500 or 1,000 records at a time, and after each chunk call entityManager.flush() followed by entityManager.clear() to detach the processed entities. I also set hibernate.jdbc.batch_size to match the chunk size and use a SEQUENCE ID generator with a matching allocation size, because IDENTITY forces an immediate insert per entity and breaks batching. If the job is purely insert-only and extremely large, StatelessSession or raw JDBC batching is a better choice because it avoids the dirty- checking and event-listener overhead of the full persistence context. A dashboard endpoint causes LazyInitializationException only under load. Why?Under normal load the controller serializes the response before the transaction-bound session closes, so lazy associations happen to work. Under load, thread scheduling, connection-pool wait times, or asynchronous serialization can delay JSON rendering until after the transaction and session have ended. The real fix is not to rely on timing. Load every lazy association inside the service transaction with JOIN FETCH or an entity graph, or return DTOs that contain only the data needed by the UI. Enabling Open Session in View (OSIV) is a workaround that keeps the session open during rendering, but it hides the underlying design problem and often leads to N+1 queries in production. Two concurrent fund transfers between the same accounts deadlock. How do you resolve it?A deadlock happens when transaction A locks account 1 and waits for account 2, while transaction B locks account 2 and waits for account 1. I reproduce it with a test that runs two transfers in opposite directions concurrently. The permanent fix is to always acquire locks in a deterministic order, such as by account ID ascending, in every code path that updates multiple accounts. If locking is required, use LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE on the accounts within a transaction. I also add a small, bounded retry with exponential backoff for legitimate lock waits, but the primary defense is consistent lock ordering. An alternative is to serialize transfers between the same accounts through a queue or a single account-pair mutex. A cache hit rate is high, but users still see stale data after an update. What could be wrong?A high hit rate only means the cache is being read; it does not mean the data is correct. Stale data usually means the update path did not invalidate or refresh the cache entry. In a multi- instance deployment, each node may have a local cache that is not synchronized, so one instance updates the database while another continues serving old data from its local cache. Solutions include switching to a shared cache such as Redis, adding explicit cache invalidation in the write path, using cache eviction events, or choosing a shorter TTL if eventual staleness is acceptable. The cache key must also include all parameters that affect the result, otherwise an update in one dimension may leave stale entries for another dimension. A query returns different totals each time it is run during market hours. Why?The query is running against a live transactional table while new rows are continuously inserted, committed, or rolled back. Aggregations over active tables are inherently non-repeatable unless the transaction runs in a snapshot isolation level or against a static snapshot. Solutions include reading from a read replica that reduces contention on the primary, using snapshot isolation if the database supports it, running the report against a materialized view or pre-aggregated table, or scheduling the report during a quiet window when write volume is low. For business-critical reports, an event-sourced or CDC-based reporting pipeline gives the most consistent results. A service throws DataIntegrityViolationException after a code change. How do you debug?DataIntegrityViolationException is a Spring wrapper, so I read the nested root cause to identify the exact constraint that failed: a unique constraint, a not-null constraint, a check constraint, or a foreign-key violation. The name of the constraint usually points to the table and column. I then compare the failing request against the changed code to see whether it now inserts duplicates, omits a required field, passes a value that is too long, or references a parent row that no longer exists. The fix generally includes adding server-side validation before the database call and returning a clear domain-specific error to the caller instead of leaking the raw SQL exception. A new @Transactional service does not roll back on a custom checked exception. What is the fix?By default Spring rolls back only on unchecked exceptions and Error, not on checked exceptions. The fix is to add rollbackFor = MyException.class to the @Transactional annotation, or rollbackFor = Exception.class if every exception should roll back. I also verify that the exception is not being caught and swallowed inside the method, because a swallowed exception never reaches the Spring proxy. Finally I confirm the method is public and is called through the Spring proxy; if it is private, final, or invoked from within the same bean, the annotation is ignored entirely. After switching databases, native queries fail. What do you check?Native queries are tied to a specific database dialect, so moving from MySQL to PostgreSQL or Oracle often breaks function names, pagination syntax, string concatenation, date functions, and keywords like LIMIT, ROWNUM, or FETCH FIRST. I check each native query against the new dialect, starting with the ones in the error logs. The long-term fix is to use JPQL where possible and keep native SQL only for cases that require database-specific features. For those cases, I maintain per-dialect query versions or use a thin abstraction that selects the correct SQL based on the active dialect. A microservice loses updates during a database failover. How do you investigate?First I check whether the application was writing while the primary database was failing over. If a transaction was in-flight and not yet acknowledged by the database, the write may or may not have been applied, leading to ambiguity. I verify the JDBC driver and connection-pool settings for failover and reconnect behavior, and I review application timeouts and retry logic. The safest architectural pattern for critical writes is to make them idempotent using a business key, and to use the outbox pattern so the event is persisted in the same transaction as the business update and published reliably later. That way a failover does not lose the intent of the write; replay simply reprocesses idempotent operations. A scheduled reconciliation job reports missing rows that the API claims were saved. What do you check?I first confirm the API transaction was actually committed before the job started. If the job reads from a read replica, replication lag can make recently committed rows invisible.
