I also check whether the job and the API agree on status values, soft-delete flags, and date ranges. Timezone mismatches in date filters are a common cause: a row saved at 23:58 UTC on one day may be interpreted as saved on the next day depending on the time zone used by the job. If both services use an event log or CDC, I verify the consumer offset is not lagging behind the producer. A @Modifying update runs but the count seems too low. Why?The update count can be lower than expected for several reasons. The WHERE clause may be stricter than intended, for example due to a wrong status filter or a date range that excludes rows. If the same entities were loaded into the current persistence context before the update, the later flush can overwrite the updated columns with the old in-memory values, effectively reverting the change. If the method is not inside a transaction, it may run in auto-commit mode and fail silently or partially. Finally, another transaction may have modified or deleted the rows between the read and the update, causing a lost update or lower match count. The fix is to validate the filter, clear the persistence context before the bulk update, ensure the method is @Transactional, and use optimistic or pessimistic locking if concurrent updates are possible. A developer adds FetchType.EAGER to every relationship to avoid lazy-loading errors. What do you say?This is an anti-pattern. Eager loading pulls in data even when it is not needed, producing larger SQL joins, Cartesian products, and unpredictable memory and network usage. It does not scale as the object graph grows. The correct fix is to understand why the lazy load was needed outside the transaction: either load the association explicitly with JOIN FETCH or an entity graph inside the service, or return a DTO that contains only the data the caller needs. FetchType.EAGER should be reserved for small, tightly coupled relationships that are truly always required together. A migration script alters a column type in a table with hundreds of millions of rows. What concerns you?Changing a column type on a huge table can take a long time, hold a metadata lock or table lock, fill transaction logs, and cause replication lag or application timeouts. Rollback is also slow and risky. In production I prefer online schema-change tools such as pt-online-schema- change for MySQL, gh-ost, or Oracle DBMS_REDEFINITION. A safer manual approach is backward-compatible: add a new column with the desired type, backfill it in small batches while the old column is still in use, switch the application to read and write the new column, and only then drop the old one. This avoids a long lock and gives a quick rollback path. A service method returns a JPA entity directly to a REST controller. What problems can arise?Returning entities directly couples the API contract to the database schema. Serialization may trigger LazyInitializationException if associations were not loaded. Bidirectional relationships can cause infinite recursion during JSON serialization unless Jackson annotations are added. Internal fields such as IDs, versions, or audit columns leak into the API, and any future schema change can break API consumers. The better pattern is to map the entity to a DTO inside the service layer and return the DTO. This gives full control over the exposed fields, avoids lazy- loading problems, and makes versioning the API easier. Someone uses @Transactional on a private method. What do you tell the author?It will not work. Spring transactional behavior is implemented through proxies, and proxies can only intercept public methods on Spring beans. private, final, static, and package-private methods are not intercepted. If the annotation is placed on a private helper, the code runs without a transaction and changes may commit or fail unexpectedly. The method should be made public, or the logic should be moved to a public method or another bean. If internal calls need transactionality, consider using AopContext.currentProxy() or restructuring the service. A repository method returns Page but the service ignores the total count. What could be improved?Page issues an extra COUNT query to compute total pages, which can be expensive on large tables. If the service does not use the total count, switching to Slice removes the count query while still supporting pagination and sorting. If even the next-page check is not needed, a simple List with Pageable is sufficient. Removing the count query reduces database load and simplifies the query plan, especially for complex filters. A @Query uses string concatenation to build a WHERE clause with user input. What is wrong?String concatenation with user input is vulnerable to SQL injection. Even if the input comes from an internal system, it should never be embedded directly into SQL. The correct approach is to use named parameters (:param) or positional placeholders (?1) in the @Query annotation. For dynamic WHERE clauses, use the Criteria API or the Specification API, which build the query safely. If native SQL must be constructed dynamically, use a prepared-statement style approach and validate inputs against an allow-list of values, column names, and sort fields. A developer writes CascadeType.ALL on a @ManyToOne relationship. What is the danger?Cascading should flow from the parent to the child, not from the child to the parent or to shared entities. Placing CascadeType.ALL on a @ManyToOne from OrderItem to Product means deleting an order line could cascade a delete to the shared product, corrupting the catalog. Similarly, persisting an order item could try to persist or merge an existing product unexpectedly. The cascade should be on the Order -> OrderItem side, and even then only the exact operations that match the business rule should be enabled. Shared reference entities should never be cascaded from transactional children. A service does entityManager.flush() after every save inside a loop. What is the impact?Each flush forces Hibernate to synchronize the persistence context with the database, executing the pending SQL immediately. Doing this after every iteration defeats the purpose of batching and generates many database round trips, which increases latency and load. The correct pattern is to accumulate a chunk of entities, call saveAll(chunk), then flush and clear the EntityManager once per chunk. Setting hibernate.jdbc.batch_size to the chunk size and using a SEQUENCE ID generator allows the driver to send multiple inserts in a single network round trip. You see @Cacheable on a method that returns a list of orders from today. What is the problem?Orders from today change frequently, so the cached value becomes stale almost immediately and cache invalidation is constantly triggered. Caching is most valuable for slow- changing or expensive-to-compute data, such as reference data, product catalogs, or heavy reports. The cache key must also include every parameter that affects the result, including filters, date ranges, and page information. Otherwise an update in one dimension may leave stale entries for another. For dynamic operational data, query optimization, indexing, and read replicas are usually better tools than caching. A method annotated with @Transactional calls another @Transactional method in a different bean. How do the transactions interact?With the default propagation REQUIRED, the second method joins the existing transaction. Both methods commit or roll back together. If the second method uses REQUIRES_NEW, Spring suspends the first transaction, starts a new physical transaction, executes the second method, commits or rolls it back independently, and then resumes the first transaction. This is useful when the second operation must commit regardless of the first operation’s outcome, such as audit logging. It is important to understand that REQUIRES_NEW still uses the same database connection by default unless connection handling is configured otherwise, so it is not a general solution for concurrency.

title: 8.1.Key Concepts
text: What is Spring Boot, and why is it popular for building Java backend applications?Spring Boot is an opinionated framework built on top of the Spring Framework that simplifies application setup and deployment. It provides auto-configuration, starter dependencies, an embedded server, and production-ready features like Actuator out of the box. Instead of writing a lot of XML or Java configuration, a developer can add spring-boot-starter-web and start a runnable web application with a main method. It is popular because it reduces boilerplate, speeds up development, and makes microservices and cloud-native deployments easier. What does @SpringBootApplication do internally?@SpringBootApplication is a convenience annotation that combines @Configuration, @EnableAutoConfiguration, and @ComponentScan. @Configuration marks the class as a source of bean definitions. @ComponentScan tells Spring where to look for components, services, repositories, and controllers. @EnableAutoConfiguration triggers Spring Boot’s auto-configuration mechanism, which inspects the classpath and existing beans to configure common infrastructure automatically, such as a datasource, embedded Tomcat, or Spring MVC. You can exclude specific auto-configurations using the exclude attribute. What are Spring Boot starters, and how do they help?Starters are pre-packaged dependency descriptors that bundle a set of compatible libraries for a specific use case. For example, spring-boot-starter-web includes Spring MVC, Tomcat, and Jackson, while spring-boot-starter-data-jpa includes Hibernate, Spring Data JPA, and a transaction manager. They reduce the need to hunt for compatible library versions and keep the build file clean. The spring-boot-starter-parent POM provides dependency management, default plugin configurations, and version alignment. How do you create a simple REST controller in Spring Boot?You annotate a class with @RestController, which combines @Controller and @ResponseBody. You then map HTTP methods using annotations like @GetMapping, @PostMapping, @PutMapping, and @DeleteMapping. For example, @GetMapping("/users/{id}") public User getUser(@PathVariable Long id) handles a GET request and returns the user object, which Jackson serializes to JSON automatically. Using @RestController avoids the need to annotate every method with @ResponseBody. What is the difference between @Controller and @RestController?@Controller is the traditional Spring MVC annotation used when you want to return a view name that a ViewResolver renders. @RestController is a shortcut for @Controller plus @ResponseBody, meaning every method returns a domain object or data that is serialized directly to JSON, XML, or another response body. If you use @Controller for a REST API and forget @ResponseBody, Spring will look for a view with that name and likely throw a 404. How does Spring Boot handle the embedded server, and how do you change the default port?Spring Boot includes an embedded server, usually Tomcat, by adding spring-boot- starter-web. The application can run as a standalone JAR because the embedded server starts when the SpringApplication.run method is executed. You can change the port with server.port=9090 in application.properties, or disable the web server entirely with spring.main.web-application-type=none. To use Jetty or Undertow, exclude Tomcat from the starter and add spring-boot-starter-jetty or spring-boot-starter- undertow. What is auto-configuration, and how can you override or disable it?Auto-configuration is Spring Boot’s ability to guess the configuration a project needs based on the classpath and existing beans. It is implemented by classes annotated with @ConditionalOnClass, @ConditionalOnMissingBean, and similar conditions. You can override auto-configuration by defining your own beans with the same type, which takes precedence due to @ConditionalOnMissingBean. You can disable specific auto- configuration classes using spring.autoconfigure.exclude in application.properties or the exclude attribute of @SpringBootApplication. How do you set up a Spring Boot project with Spring Boot Web MVC and Spring Data JPA?Add the starters spring-boot-starter-web for the web layer and spring- boot-starter-data-jpa for persistence. Include a database driver such as mysql- connector-j or postgresql. Configure the datasource in application.properties with spring.datasource.url, username, password, and driver-class-name. Define entity classes with @Entity and repository interfaces extending JpaRepository. Spring Boot auto-configures the datasource, transaction manager, and Hibernate. Use @Transactional in the service layer and keep controllers thin. What is Spring Boot DevTools, and why should it not be used in production?Spring Boot DevTools is a development-time dependency that provides automatic restart when code changes, LiveReload for browser refresh, and enhanced debugging features. It should not be used in production because it adds runtime overhead, can expose internal application details, and is designed only for the local development loop. In production builds, it should be excluded or marked with a non-transitive scope so that it does not end up in the deployed artifact. How do you enable basic auditing in Spring Boot?Enable JPA auditing with @EnableJpaAuditing on a configuration class and annotate entity fields with @CreatedDate, @LastModifiedDate, @CreatedBy, and @LastModifiedBy. Spring Data JPA automatically fills these fields when entities are persisted or updated. For @CreatedBy and @LastModifiedBy, you provide an AuditorAware bean that returns the current user. This removes manual audit field population and helps with compliance and traceability. What is Hibernate Envers, and when would you use it?Hibernate Envers is an auditing module that tracks every insert, update, and delete on entities by maintaining separate revision tables. Annotating an entity with @Audited makes Envers store a snapshot of each change along with a revision number and timestamp. It is useful when you need a complete history of who changed what and when, such as in regulated domains or for debugging data issues. The trade-off is extra storage, write overhead, and the need to plan retention and archival of revision data. How do you call a third-party REST API from a Spring Boot application?You can use RestTemplate for synchronous calls, WebClient for reactive or non- blocking calls, or Feign for declarative clients. With RestTemplate, you inject a bean and call methods like getForObject or exchange. With WebClient, you build a request chain and subscribe to the response. With Feign, you declare an interface with method mappings and Spring Cloud generates the implementation. For new Spring Boot 3 projects, the RestClient API offers a modern, fluent synchronous alternative to RestTemplate. What is the difference between RestTemplate, WebClient, Feign, and RestClient?RestTemplate is synchronous and simple but is now in maintenance mode. WebClient is reactive and non-blocking, part of Spring WebFlux, and better for high- concurrency or streaming scenarios. Feign is a declarative Spring Cloud client that lets you define HTTP clients as interfaces, making service-to-service calls concise. RestClient, introduced in Spring Framework 6.1 and Spring Boot 3.2, is a modern synchronous client with a fluent API. Blocking a reactive pipeline with RestTemplate is a common mistake that kills throughput in WebFlux applications. How do you handle failures, retries, and timeouts when calling external APIs?External API calls should always have connection and read timeouts configured so that a slow downstream does not hang your service. Use Resilience4j for circuit breaking, retries with exponential backoff, rate limiting, and bulkheads. Wrap the client so that failures degrade gracefully, for example by returning cached or default data. Log correlation IDs to trace calls across services, and use MockWebServer or WireMock in tests instead of hitting real third-party endpoints. What is the default logging framework in Spring Boot, and how do you configure it?The default logging framework is Logback, accessed through the SLF4J facade. You can configure logging levels and output in application.properties with properties like logging.level.root=INFO and logging.level.com.example=DEBUG. For file logging, use logging.file.name or logging.file.path. For more control, provide a logback.xml or log4j2.xml file in the classpath. A common mistake is enabling DEBUG in production, which degrades performance and can leak sensitive information. What is SLF4J, and why is it recommended over direct Log4j usage?SLF4J is a logging facade that lets application code use a standard API while the actual logging implementation, such as Logback or Log4j2, is configured at deployment time. This means you can switch implementations by changing dependencies and configuration, without touching application code. It also supports parameterized messages such as logger.info("Processing order {}", orderId), which avoid the cost of string concatenation when the log level is disabled. What is Spring Boot Actuator, and what are its most useful endpoints?Actuator is a production-ready feature that exposes operational endpoints for monitoring and managing the application. After adding spring-boot-starter-actuator, endpoints such as /health, /info, /metrics, /env, /loggers, /beans, and /heapdump become available. /health shows application health, /metrics exposes JVM and application metrics, and /loggers lets you change log levels at runtime. Exposing all endpoints publicly is dangerous, so only the endpoints needed for monitoring should be enabled and secured. How do you secure Spring Boot Actuator endpoints?Limit exposed endpoints using management.endpoints.web.exposure.include to only those required, such as health, info, and metrics. Use Spring Security to protect them with authentication and a dedicated role like ACTUATOR_ADMIN. Run Actuator over HTTPS and place it on a separate management port if needed. Endpoints like /env, /beans, /heapdump, and /configprops can reveal secrets or internal details, so they should be disabled or heavily restricted in production. How do you create a custom health indicator in Spring Boot?Implement the HealthIndicator interface and override the health method to check a specific dependency, such as a downstream API or a message broker. Register the class as a Spring bean. Spring Boot automatically includes it in the /health response. This is useful for readiness and liveness probes in Kubernetes, where the platform can restart or stop routing traffic to an unhealthy instance. What is OpenAPI, and how does Swagger help document REST APIs?OpenAPI is a standardized specification for describing REST APIs. Swagger provides tooling to design, document, and test APIs based on the OpenAPI specification. In a Spring Boot project, you can use SpringDoc OpenAPI to generate documentation automatically from controllers, DTOs, and annotations. It produces an interactive UI where developers can see endpoints, parameters, request bodies, and response schemas, and even try calls directly. Should Swagger UI be enabled in production, and why or why not?Swagger UI should generally be disabled or restricted in production. While it is helpful during development and integration testing, it exposes the complete API contract, including endpoints, parameter names, and response structures, which can aid attackers. The recommended approach is to enable Swagger only in non-production profiles or protect it behind authentication and authorization. What are Spring profiles, and how do you use them?Spring profiles let you define environment-specific beans and configuration. You activate a profile with spring.profiles.active=prod in application.properties or via command-line arguments. Beans annotated with @Profile("dev") only load when that profile is active. You can also have separate property files like application-dev.properties and application-prod.properties. Profiles help keep database URLs, log levels, feature flags, and secrets isolated per environment. How do you externalize configuration and secrets in a Spring Boot application?Configuration should be moved out of the application code into property files, environment variables, or external config servers. Spring Boot supports application.properties, application.yml, environment variables, and command-line arguments with a defined precedence order. Secrets such as database passwords and API keys should not be stored in source control. Use Spring Cloud Config, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or HashiCorp Vault to inject secrets securely at runtime. What is the Spring MVC request lifecycle?An incoming request first reaches the DispatcherServlet, which consults handler mappings to find the right controller method. The method processes the request, possibly using @RequestBody to deserialize JSON into objects and @PathVariable or @RequestParam to extract values. The result is returned, and if the controller uses @RestController, it is written directly to the response body. Interceptors can run before and after the controller, and @ControllerAdvice can handle exceptions globally. What is ResponseEntity, and when should you use it?ResponseEntity is a Spring class that represents the full HTTP response, including status code, headers, and body. It gives complete control over what is returned to the client. For example, ResponseEntity.created(location).body(user) returns HTTP 201 with a Location header, while ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND).build() returns a 404. It is preferred over returning raw domain objects when you need to set custom headers or status codes. How do you handle exceptions globally in Spring Boot?Use @RestControllerAdvice or @ControllerAdvice with methods annotated by @ExceptionHandler. A global advice class catches exceptions thrown by controllers and returns consistent error responses. For example, a ResourceNotFoundException can be mapped to HTTP 404, while a ValidationException can be mapped to HTTP 400. This keeps controllers clean and ensures the API always returns a predictable error format. What is application.properties vs application.yml, and which should you use?Both files store externalized configuration. application.properties uses simple key-value pairs, while application.yml uses nested YAML syntax, which is cleaner for hierarchical configuration. The choice is mostly a team preference. If the configuration is deeply nested, such as multiple datasources or logging appenders, YAML is usually more readable. Properties files are simpler for flat configurations. What is the Spring Boot main class, and why does it need SpringApplication.run?The main class is the entry point annotated with @SpringBootApplication. Calling SpringApplication.run(MyApplication.class, args) bootstraps the Spring application context, starts the embedded web server, runs command-line runners, and initializes all beans. It is what turns a plain Java application into a running Spring Boot service. The class is usually placed at the root package so component scanning covers the entire project. How do you write integration tests in Spring Boot?Use @SpringBootTest to load the full application context, @WebMvcTest for controller-layer tests with mocked services, and @DataJpaTest for repository tests against an embedded database. Use TestRestTemplate or WebTestClient to call endpoints. For external dependencies, use @MockBean or tools like WireMock. Tests should be fast and deterministic, so prefer sliced tests over full context tests when possible. What is the difference between @Component, @Service, @Repository, and @Controller?All four are stereotype annotations that mark classes as Spring-managed beans. @Component is the generic one. @Service is used for business logic classes and helps document intent. @Repository is used for data access and enables Spring’s exception translation for persistence exceptions. @Controller is used for MVC controllers. Functionally they all result in bean creation during component scanning, but the specialized annotations carry semantic meaning and enable framework features. What is dependency injection, and why is constructor injection preferred?Dependency injection is a design pattern where Spring supplies a bean’s dependencies instead of the bean creating them itself. Constructor injection is preferred because it makes dependencies explicit and immutable, allows easy unit testing without reflection, and ensures the object is fully initialized before use. Field injection hides dependencies and makes testing harder, while setter injection can leave an object in a partially constructed state. How do you schedule tasks in Spring Boot?Add @EnableScheduling on a configuration class and use @Scheduled on methods to run them at fixed intervals, fixed delays, or using cron expressions. For example, @Scheduled(cron = "0 0 * * * *") runs every hour. For reliability and clustering, consider using an external scheduler or Quartz with a JDBC job store so that jobs are not duplicated across multiple application instances. What is the difference between @Async and @Scheduled?@Async marks a method to run in a separate thread from a task executor, returning immediately to the caller. It is useful for fire-and-forget operations like sending emails or notifications. @Scheduled triggers a method repeatedly based on a timer. A common mistake is calling an @Async method from within the same bean, which bypasses the Spring proxy and runs synchronously. How do you version REST APIs in Spring Boot?Common approaches include URI versioning (/v1/users, /v2/users), request header versioning (Accept: application/vnd.company.v1+json), and query parameter versioning (?version=1). URI versioning is the simplest and most visible. Header versioning keeps URLs clean but is harder to test in a browser. The choice should be documented and consistent across the API surface. What is content negotiation in Spring Boot?Content negotiation is the mechanism by which a client and server agree on the format of the response, usually JSON or XML. Spring Boot uses the Accept header to decide which HttpMessageConverter to use. Jackson is configured by default for JSON, and adding Jackson XML or JAXB dependencies enables XML responses. Producing the wrong format because of an Accept header mismatch is a subtle bug that appears during client integration. How do you validate request input in Spring Boot?Use Bean Validation by adding the spring-boot-starter-validation dependency and annotating DTO fields with constraints like @NotBlank, @Email, @Min, @Max, and @Valid. In the controller, annotate the request body parameter with @Valid so that validation runs before the method executes. Validation failures result in a MethodArgumentNotValidException, which can be handled globally to return a structured error response. What is the purpose of CommandLineRunner and ApplicationRunner?Both interfaces let you run logic once the Spring application context is fully initialized. CommandLineRunner accepts raw command-line arguments as a string array, while ApplicationRunner exposes them as structured ApplicationArguments. They are commonly used for one-time setup, data seeding, or checking external dependencies at startup. Multiple runners can be ordered with @Order. How do you package and deploy a Spring Boot application?Spring Boot applications are typically packaged as an executable JAR using the Spring Boot Maven or Gradle plugin. The JAR includes the embedded server, so you deploy it by running java -jar app.jar. For containers, you can build a Docker image with a Dockerfile or use spring-boot:build-image to create an optimized image. Configuration is externalized through environment variables or mounted config files so the same artifact can run in any environment. What is graceful shutdown in Spring Boot?Graceful shutdown allows the application to finish processing active requests before stopping when it receives a termination signal. In Spring Boot 2.3+, you can enable it with server.shutdown=graceful. The embedded server stops accepting new requests, waits for in-flight requests up to a configured timeout, and then shuts down. This prevents dropped requests during rolling deployments and Kubernetes pod terminations. What is the Spring Boot fat JAR, and how is it different from a regular JAR?A fat JAR, also called an uber JAR, is a Spring Boot executable JAR that includes the application classes and all runtime dependencies, including the embedded server. A regular JAR usually contains only the project classes and needs external dependencies on the classpath. The fat JAR is self-contained, making deployment simple because you only need to run java -jar. How do you add a custom Actuator endpoint?Create a class annotated with @Endpoint and a unique ID. Define operations using @ReadOperation, @WriteOperation, and @DeleteOperation. Register the class as a Spring bean. For example, a @ReadOperation method can return a custom metric or configuration value. Make sure the endpoint is exposed in management.endpoints.web.exposure.include and protected by security rules. What is Micrometer, and how does it relate to Actuator?Micrometer is a metrics facade that provides a vendor-neutral API for recording timers, counters, gauges, and distributions. Spring Boot Actuator exposes Micrometer metrics through the /metrics endpoint and can ship them to backends like Prometheus, Datadog, CloudWatch, or Graphite. This lets you monitor application performance, throughput, and errors using the tools your organization already has. How do you change the Actuator base path and port?You can change the Actuator web base path with management.endpoints.web.base-path=/admin, so endpoints appear under /admin/health instead of /actuator/health. You can also run Actuator on a separate management port with management.server.port=8081, which isolates operational endpoints from public application traffic and makes security rules easier to apply. What is the difference between @Value and @ConfigurationProperties?@Value injects a single property using SpEL expressions, such as @Value("$ {server.port}"). @ConfigurationProperties binds a group of related properties to a typed bean, which is better for structured configuration like a datasource or an external API client. For example, app.mail.host, app.mail.port, and app.mail.username can bind to a single MailProperties class. @ConfigurationProperties supports relaxed binding and JSR-303 validation. How do you implement pagination in a Spring Boot REST API?Add a Pageable parameter to the controller or repository method. Spring resolves Pageable from query parameters like ?page=0&size=10&sort=name,asc. The repository returns a Page that includes content, total elements, total pages, and metadata. Always cap the page size to prevent abuse and avoid returning huge responses that exhaust memory or slow serialization. What is a DTO, and why should you return DTOs instead of entities from controllers?A DTO is a plain object that carries only the data needed by the API consumer. Returning entities directly couples the API contract to the database schema, can trigger LazyInitializationException, may expose internal fields, and makes future schema changes risky. Mapping entities to DTOs inside the service layer gives full control over the exposed shape and avoids serialization problems. How do you handle file uploads in Spring Boot?Accept MultipartFile as a controller parameter, usually with @RequestParam("file") or @ModelAttribute. Use transferTo to save the file or stream it directly to cloud storage like S3. For large files, stream the content instead of loading it fully into memory, and configure spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size and max-request-size to enforce limits. Store files in external storage rather than local disk in containerized environments. What is an API Gateway, and how does it fit with Spring Boot microservices?An API Gateway sits in front of microservices and handles cross-cutting concerns like routing, load balancing, authentication, rate limiting, SSL termination, and request/response transformation. Spring Cloud Gateway is a common choice in the Spring ecosystem. Each Spring Boot service exposes its own endpoints, and the gateway routes client requests to the right service, centralizing security and observability. How do you implement distributed tracing in Spring Boot?In Spring Boot 3, use Micrometer Tracing with Brave or OpenTelemetry to propagate trace and span IDs across service calls. Add the tracing starter and configure an exporter such as Zipkin or Jaeger. The trace ID is included in logs via MDC, which helps you follow a request across multiple services. In Spring Boot 2.x this was often done with Spring Cloud Sleuth. What are the key things to check before deploying a Spring Boot application to production?Before production deployment, disable DevTools and Swagger UI or restrict them, secure Actuator endpoints, externalize secrets, set appropriate log levels, enable health checks, configure graceful shutdown, set reasonable connection and thread pool sizes, run integration tests, verify database migrations, and ensure the artifact is built with the production profile. Also confirm that metrics and centralized logging are wired up. A Spring Boot application starts up locally but fails to start in production with a DataSource bean creation error. How do you investigate?Start by reading the full stack trace to identify whether the failure is a missing URL, invalid credentials, unreachable host, driver not on the classpath, or a port/firewall issue. Compare the production application.properties or environment variables with the local configuration. Check that the correct profile is active and that secrets are being injected properly by Vault or the config server. Verify the database driver dependency is included in the production build. If the app runs in a container, confirm that the container can reach the database and that DNS resolution works. Finally, test connectivity with a simple JDBC connection from the production environment before restarting the application. A REST endpoint works in development but returns 404 in production. What could be wrong?A 404 usually means the request URL does not match any registered handler. Check whether the production base path or servlet context path is different, such as server.servlet.context-path=/api. Verify the HTTP method is correct and that the controller is in a package scanned by Spring. If there are multiple @RequestMapping variants or a conflicting global path configuration, they may behave differently under another profile. Also confirm that no security filter is blocking the request and returning a misleading 404 instead of 403. An API that handles file uploads starts throwing OutOfMemoryError under load. How do you fix it?Loading the entire multipart file into memory for every request consumes heap space quickly. The fix is to stream the file directly to disk or cloud storage instead of keeping it in memory. Configure spring.servlet.multipart.file-size-threshold so large files are written to disk, and set max-file-size and max-request-size limits to reject oversized uploads. Use MultipartFile.getInputStream() and upload chunks to S3 or similar storage. In containerized environments, avoid writing to ephemeral local disk; send the stream straight to object storage. A Spring Boot service calls an external payment API, and payments sometimes time out. What do you do?First configure explicit connection and read timeouts on the HTTP client so the call fails fast instead of blocking threads indefinitely. Add retries with exponential backoff for transient failures, but avoid retrying idempotent mutations blindly without idempotency keys. Wrap the client with a circuit breaker such as Resilience4j so failures do not cascade. Log every outbound call with a trace ID and the response time. If the external API is unreliable, consider an asynchronous queue- based integration where the payment status is polled or pushed later. A microservice using Feign starts failing with NoSuchBeanDefinitionException. How do you debug it?NoSuchBeanDefinitionException means Spring could not create the Feign client bean. Verify that @EnableFeignClients is on the main class or a configuration class and that the base package is correct. Confirm the interface is annotated with @FeignClient and has a valid name and URL or service ID. Check that spring-cloud-starter-openfeign is on the classpath. If the service uses a custom Feign configuration, ensure it is not accidentally excluding the default beans needed for auto-configuration. A Spring Boot application running on Kubernetes is being killed before requests finish. How do you handle it?Kubernetes sends a SIGTERM to the pod when scaling down or redeploying. If the application exits immediately, in-flight requests are dropped. Enable graceful shutdown with server.shutdown=graceful and set spring.lifecycle.timeout-per- shutdown-phase to a value larger than the longest expected request. Configure the pod’s terminationGracePeriodSeconds to match. The container should keep running until active requests complete or the grace period expires, after which Kubernetes sends SIGKILL. A newly deployed feature works for some users but returns stale data for others. What could be happening?Stale data for some users often points to caching issues. If each application instance has a local cache, one instance may update the database without invalidating the cache on other instances. The fix is to use a shared cache such as Redis or to publish cache invalidation events across the cluster. Also check whether the cache TTL is too long, whether the cache key includes all relevant parameters, and whether the feature depends on a read replica that lags behind the primary database. A Spring Boot batch job processes orders, but the same order is processed twice when the job runs on two pods. How do you prevent duplicate processing?Duplicate processing happens because each pod independently polls the same work queue or table. The solution depends on the architecture. If using a scheduler, make it a singleton with a distributed lock via ShedLock or Quartz JDBC job store. If pulling from a message broker, use a consumer group so each message is processed by only one consumer. If reading from a database, mark rows as PROCESSING with an atomic update, or use a processed_at timestamp and a unique constraint on the business key to ensure idempotency. Actuator /health returns UP even though the database is unreachable. How do you fix it?By default, Spring Boot health checks only verify the datasource if it is auto-configured and the application uses it. If the health indicator is disabled or the datasource is custom, the database may not be checked. Add a custom HealthIndicator that performs a lightweight query like SELECT 1 against the database, or enable detailed health with management.endpoint.health.show-details=always and include db in the exposed endpoints. For Kubernetes liveness and readiness probes, ensure the readiness probe points to /health/readiness and the liveness probe to /health/liveness so traffic is routed away before the pod is restarted. A Spring Boot application shows high CPU after a deployment. How do you troubleshoot it?High CPU after a deployment usually means inefficient loops, excessive garbage collection, or a hot code path. Start by collecting thread dumps and heap dumps, and look at JVM metrics through Actuator or a profiler. Check whether a new query runs full table scans, whether a cache was disabled, or whether a new loop processes an unexpectedly large list. Review recent changes for missing pagination, inefficient algorithms, or blocking calls on event loops. Revert the deployment if the root cause cannot be found quickly and business impact is high. A developer stores database passwords in application.properties. What do you recommend?Storing passwords in source-controlled properties is a security risk. Move secrets to an external secret store such as HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or Spring Cloud Config with encryption. At runtime, the application reads the secret through environment variables or the config server. If you must use property files locally, keep them out of version control with a .gitignore entry and use a secrets manager for all non-local environments. Rotate credentials regularly and audit access. A service using @Async runs synchronously when called from within the same bean. Why?Spring AOP creates a proxy around the bean to handle @Async, @Transactional, and similar annotations. When the method is called from within the same bean, the call bypasses the proxy and runs directly, so the async behavior is ignored. The fix is to move the async method to another bean and inject it, or use AopContext.currentProxy() to invoke the method through the proxy. Constructor injection and clear separation of concerns reduce this problem. A Spring Boot application logs are not reaching the centralized logging system. What do you check?Verify that the application writes logs to stdout or to a file that the log shipper can read. Check the logging configuration for incorrect appenders or file paths that do not exist in the container. Confirm the log level is not set too high, filtering out the messages you expect. In Kubernetes, make sure the container runtime captures stdout and that the log aggregation agent such as Fluentd or Fluent Bit is running. For structured JSON logging, ensure the format matches what the central system expects and that the MDC fields such as trace ID are included. An OpenAPI-generated contract does not match the actual API response. How do you fix it?The contract becomes stale when DTOs, controller signatures, or annotations change without updating the OpenAPI generation. Use SpringDoc or Springfox annotations such as @Operation, @ApiResponse, @Schema, and @Parameter to document behavior explicitly. Generate the OpenAPI spec as part of the build and fail the build if it drifts from a committed baseline. In CI, run contract tests that verify the generated spec against real controller responses. Restrict manual edits of the spec so that the code remains the single source of truth. A Spring Boot application needs to support two databases. How do you configure it?Define two separate DataSource beans, each with its own properties prefix using @ConfigurationProperties. Create two EntityManagerFactory beans, each scanning a different package of entities, and two JpaTransactionManager beans. Mark one set as @Primary so that default autowiring works for the main database. Use @Qualifier when injecting the secondary datasource, entity manager, or transaction manager. Avoid mixing entities from both databases in the same transaction unless you use a distributed transaction manager such as Atomikos or accept eventual consistency with application-level coordination. A production endpoint exposes sensitive user data in stack traces. How do you prevent it?Configure a global exception handler with @RestControllerAdvice that catches exceptions and returns a sanitized error response containing only a generic message and a correlation ID. Never return raw stack traces, SQL errors, or internal class names to API consumers. Set server.error.include-stacktrace=never and server.error.include- message=never in production. Log the full exception with the correlation ID internally so developers can debug without leaking details. A Spring Boot application deployed as a Docker container is slow to start. How do you optimize it?Slow startup is often caused by classpath scanning, large dependency trees, or inefficient database migration checks. Reduce startup time by moving to Java 17 or later, using layered JARs or CDS archives, trimming unnecessary dependencies, and lazy-initializing beans where appropriate. Pre-run database migrations during the build or as an init container instead of blocking startup. Build optimized container images with spring-boot:build-image or a custom Dockerfile based on a small JRE. Avoid running in DEBUG logging in production. A Spring Boot API returns different responses in dev and prod for the same request. How do you investigate?Compare the active profiles and the resolved property values using /actuator/env if available. Check for environment-specific beans enabled by @Profile and for conditional properties that differ between environments. Verify the database state, including schema version, seed data, and replication lag if a read replica is used. Look for feature flags or cached data that may differ. Capture the request and response in both environments and diff them to isolate whether the difference is in data, serialization, or business logic. A Spring Boot service under heavy load starts returning connection timeout errors. What do you check?Check the embedded server thread pool configuration, the database connection pool size, and the HTTP client connection pool for outbound calls. If any pool is too small, requests queue up and eventually time out. Use metrics to see whether the database is the bottleneck or whether an external API is slow. Tune server.tomcat.threads.max, HikariCP maximum-pool- size, and client timeouts. Add circuit breakers and bulkheads to prevent failures from cascading. If the load is legitimate, scale horizontally behind a load balancer. A new developer enables all Actuator endpoints for easier debugging. What do you tell them?Exposing all endpoints, especially /env, /beans, /heapdump, /configprops, and /loggers, leaks configuration values, internal structure, and potentially secrets. The right approach is to expose only the endpoints needed for monitoring, such as /health, /info, /metrics, and /prometheus, and protect them with Spring Security and a dedicated management role. Use a separate management port or restrict network access so operational endpoints are not reachable from the public internet. A Spring Boot application receives a request with a JSON body, but the fields are null even though the JSON has values. Why?This usually happens when the JSON property names do not match the Java field names and there are no matching getters and setters, or when the DTO lacks a default constructor. If you use Lombok, verify that @Getter, @Setter, and @NoArgsConstructor are present and that the annotation processor is configured in the build. Another cause is a custom HttpMessageConverter or ObjectMapper configuration that disables default deserialization. Use @JsonProperty if the API names differ from Java naming conventions. A scheduled job in a Spring Boot application works on one instance but runs twice in a clustered deployment. How do you fix it?By default, Spring’s scheduler runs on every node independently. In a cluster you need a distributed lock so only one instance executes the job. Options include ShedLock, which uses a database or Redis to coordinate, or Quartz with a JDBC job store. If the job is triggered by a message broker, use a consumer group so each message is processed exactly once. Avoid relying on cron expressions alone in clustered environments because every node will fire at the same time. A Spring Boot service is supposed to send an email after user registration, but emails are not sent when the registration API is under load. How do you debug?If the email sending is synchronous, the external SMTP server can become a bottleneck, causing requests to time out or fail. Move the email logic to an @Async method or a message queue so registration completes quickly. Verify the thread pool used by @Async is large enough and has a queue capacity. Check logs for SMTP exceptions and configure a circuit breaker around the email provider. For reliability, persist the email intent in an outbox table or queue and process it with a retry mechanism. A Spring Boot application uses Swagger, and the security team asks to remove it from production. What is the best approach?The best approach is to disable Swagger UI in production by using a Spring profile, such as adding the SpringDoc configuration only when the dev or qa profile is active. Alternatively, you can restrict the Swagger endpoints with Spring Security so only authorized internal users can access them. Removing the dependency entirely from the production build is the safest option if it is not needed there. Keep the OpenAPI spec generation in CI so documentation stays accurate, but do not expose the interactive UI publicly. A Spring Boot service needs to call another internal service, but the target service has frequent short outages. How do you make the integration resilient?Add a circuit breaker like Resilience4j around the client so failures fail fast instead of blocking threads. Combine it with retries that have exponential backoff and jitter, but only for safe, idempotent operations. Use a fallback strategy that returns cached data, a default response, or a queued intent when the downstream service is unavailable. Set sensible connection and read timeouts on the HTTP client. Monitor the circuit breaker state with Actuator and alert when it opens frequently, because that signals a deeper downstream problem.

title: 9.1.Key Concepts
text: What is Spring Security, and why is it important in a Spring Boot application?Spring Security is a powerful, customizable authentication and access-control framework for Java applications. In a Spring Boot project, adding spring-boot-starter-security auto-configures basic security, including a default form login and password-based authentication. It protects REST endpoints, method invocations, and even view-layer resources. It is important because security cannot be bolted on later; it must be part of the design, from request filtering to method-level authorization and secret management. How does Spring Security work internally at a high level?Spring Security works through a chain of servlet filters known as the SecurityFilterChain. Every incoming request passes through this chain, where filters handle tasks such as extracting credentials, checking sessions, validating CSRF tokens, and enforcing access rules. The result is stored in the SecurityContext, which is held by SecurityContextHolder. Later, authorization decisions consult this context. In modern Spring Boot 3 applications, you configure security by exposing a SecurityFilterChain bean instead of extending WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter, which is now removed. What are the core components of Spring Security?The core components are SecurityContext, SecurityContextHolder, Authentication, AuthenticationManager, ProviderManager, and AuthenticationProvider. SecurityContext stores the currently authenticated user. SecurityContextHolder carries it across the application, typically bound to the current thread. Authentication represents the credential token. AuthenticationManager delegates validation to ProviderManager, which tries each registered AuthenticationProvider until one succeeds. UserDetailsService loads user data, and PasswordEncoder hashes passwords. Understanding how these pieces fit together is essential for debugging 403 and 401 errors. What is the difference between AuthenticationManager and ProviderManager?AuthenticationManager is an interface that defines a single authenticate method. ProviderManager is its most common implementation. It maintains a list of AuthenticationProvider implementations and asks each one whether it can authenticate the given token. For example, one provider might handle username-and-password tokens, while another handles JWT tokens. If no provider supports the token or all fail, the authentication fails. This design lets you plug in different authentication mechanisms without changing the rest of the application. What is SecurityContextHolder, and how does it relate to SecurityContext?SecurityContextHolder is a strategy-based container that holds the SecurityContext for the current execution context. By default it uses thread-local storage, so each request thread has its own context. The SecurityContext contains the Authentication object, which includes the principal, credentials, and granted authorities. This is how controllers and services later call SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication() to know who is logged in. In asynchronous or reactive scenarios, the strategy must be updated to propagate context across threads. What is a SecurityFilterChain, and how do you configure it in Spring Boot 3?A SecurityFilterChain is a bean that defines the order and behavior of security filters for a set of requests. In Spring Boot 3, you expose it as a bean instead of overriding WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter. You use HttpSecurity to define which endpoints are public, which require authentication, which require specific roles, and how login, logout, CSRF, and session management behave. A typical configuration disables CSRF for stateless APIs, requires authentication for protected paths, and sets the session creation policy to stateless when using JWT. What is CSRF, and how does Spring Security protect against it?Cross-Site Request Forgery is an attack where a malicious site tricks a logged-in user’s browser into making an unwanted request to a trusted site. Spring Security protects browser-based sessions by issuing a random CSRF token per session and requiring it for state-changing requests such as POST, PUT, DELETE, and PATCH. The token is typically rendered in forms or returned as a cookie/header for JavaScript clients. For pure REST APIs consumed by mobile apps or other backends, CSRF is usually disabled because the client does not maintain a session cookie and the token is not practical to manage. When is it safe to disable CSRF, and what is the risk of disabling it globally?It is safe to disable CSRF for stateless APIs where authentication is carried by a header token such as JWT, because the browser does not automatically send custom headers. It is dangerous to disable CSRF globally for browser-based applications that rely on session cookies, because an attacker can forge requests using the victim’s stored credentials. If you must expose both browser forms and APIs from the same application, configure CSRF per request matcher rather than turning it off everywhere. What is XSS, and how do you prevent it in a Spring Boot application?Cross-Site Scripting is an attack where an attacker injects malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users. In a Spring Boot REST API, the main defense is never trusting user input and always escaping output. Use templating engines that auto-escape content. For JSON APIs, ensure the client escapes data before rendering it in HTML. Set a strong Content Security Policy header, use HttpOnly and SameSite cookies, and validate input on the server. Storing raw HTML from users and rendering it later is a common way XSS vulnerabilities appear. What is SQL injection, and how does Spring Data JPA help prevent it?SQL injection is an attack where an attacker inserts malicious SQL into input fields to manipulate database queries. Spring Data JPA helps prevent it because JPQL and named parameters use prepared statements, separating code from data. A query written as SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.email = :email is safe, while concatenating user input into a native query is not. Even with JPA, native queries and dynamic SQL must use placeholders or the Criteria API. Input validation and least-privilege database accounts provide additional defense. What is the difference between authentication and authorization?Authentication is the process of verifying who you are, for example by checking a username and password or a JWT signature. Authorization decides what you are allowed to do after you are authenticated, for example whether you can access /admin/reports. Authentication answers the question "Who is this user?" while authorization answers "Is this user allowed to perform this action?" Both are needed; knowing the user’s identity is useless unless the system also enforces access rules. What is a JWT, and what are its three parts?JWT stands for JSON Web Token. It is a compact, self-contained token used to transmit identity and claims between parties. A JWT has three parts separated by dots: the header, which contains the signing algorithm and token type; the payload, which contains claims such as subject, issuer, expiration, and custom roles; and the signature, which proves the token was issued by a trusted party and was not tampered with. Because the token carries its own claims, a stateless server can verify it without storing session data. How do you create and verify a JWT token in a Spring Boot application?Creation happens after the user proves their identity, typically in a login endpoint. You build a JWT with a library such as JJWT or Nimbus, set claims like subject, issued-at, expiration, and roles, and sign it with a secret key or a private key. Verification happens in a custom filter or authentication provider that reads the Authorization header, parses the token, validates the signature with the same secret or public key, and checks that the token has not expired. If valid, the filter populates the SecurityContext with an authenticated Authentication object. What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric signing for JWT?Symmetric signing uses a single shared secret to both sign and verify the token. It is simple but means every service that verifies tokens must possess the secret, increasing exposure if one service is compromised. Asymmetric signing uses a private key to sign and a public key to verify. The authentication service keeps the private key, while other services only need the public key. Asymmetric signing is preferred in microservices because verification services cannot forge tokens even if they are breached. Where should you store the JWT signing key, and why does it matter?The signing key must never be hard-coded in source control or plain property files. It should be stored in a secret manager such as HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or Spring Cloud Config with encryption. For asymmetric keys, the private key stays only in the token issuer, and public keys are distributed to verification services. If a secret key leaks, an attacker can forge valid tokens, which is a complete authentication bypass until all keys are rotated. How do you implement login and sign-up using JWT in Spring Boot?For sign-up, the controller receives user details, validates them, hashes the password with BCryptPasswordEncoder, and saves the user. For login, the controller receives credentials and uses AuthenticationManager to validate them. If authentication succeeds, a service creates a JWT containing the user ID, roles, and expiration. The token is returned to the client, which sends it in the Authorization: Bearer header on subsequent requests. A custom filter intercepts protected requests, validates the token, and sets the authentication in the SecurityContext. How does a client use a JWT to access protected endpoints?After receiving the JWT from the login response, the client stores it securely, for example in memory or a secure cookie, and sends it in the Authorization header as Bearer with every protected request. The server extracts the header in a filter, strips the Bearer prefix, validates the signature and expiration, and uses the claims to build an Authentication object. If the token is missing, malformed, expired, or signed with the wrong key, the server returns 401. What is the difference between an access token and a refresh token?An access token is short-lived, usually minutes to an hour, and grants access to protected resources. A refresh token is long-lived and is used only to obtain a new access token when the old one expires. The access token travels with every API request, so keeping it short limits the window of misuse if it is stolen. The refresh token is stored more securely, rotated on use, and can be revoked independently. Storing refresh tokens in plain browser storage is a common mistake that makes theft easy. How do you implement a refresh token flow securely?When the user logs in, issue both an access token and a refresh token. Store a hash of the refresh token in the database along with the user ID, device identifier, and expiration. When the access token expires, the client sends the refresh token to a dedicated refresh endpoint. The server hashes the incoming token, looks it up, verifies it has not expired or been revoked, and issues a new access token and a new refresh token, invalidating the old refresh token. This rotation limits the impact of token theft. Revocation happens by deleting the stored token hash. What is session management in Spring Security, and how do you make it stateless?Session management controls how user sessions are created and tracked. By default, Spring Security creates a session for browser applications and stores the authentication server-side. For JWT-based REST APIs, you set sessionCreationPolicy(SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS) in the SecurityFilterChain. This tells Spring Security not to create or use sessions; every request is authenticated independently using the JWT. Stateless authentication is easier to scale horizontally because no session affinity or shared session store is needed. What is the difference between roles and authorities in Spring Security?A role is a coarse-grained grouping of permissions, such as ROLE_ADMIN or ROLE_USER. An authority is a finer-grained permission, such as user:read, user:write, or report:delete. Internally Spring Security treats both as strings in the GrantedAuthority collection. The hasRole expression automatically prepends ROLE_, so hasRole("ADMIN") checks for ROLE_ADMIN. hasAuthority("ADMIN") checks for the exact string. Using authorities gives more granular control and is preferred when permissions vary across users with the same role. How do you implement role-based authorization in Spring Security?You can enforce roles at the URL level in the SecurityFilterChain using requestMatchers("/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN"). You can also enforce them at the method level with @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')") or @Secured("ROLE_ADMIN"). The user must have the matching role stored in their GrantedAuthority list, typically populated from the database when the UserDetails object is built. Be careful that hasRole adds the ROLE_ prefix automatically, so storing ADMIN in the database and checking hasRole("ADMIN") will not match unless you configure the prefix or use hasAuthority. How do you implement granular authorization with authorities and permissions?Granular authorization maps specific actions to authority strings rather than broad roles. For example, an invoice module might use authorities like invoice:read, invoice:write, and invoice:approve. You load these permissions from a database when building the UserDetails, and enforce them with @PreAuthorize("hasAuthority('invoice:approve')"). For dynamic rules, you can write SpEL expressions that check method arguments, such as @PreAuthorize("hasAuthority('invoice:write') and #userId == authentication.principal.id"). This gives much finer control than role-based checks alone. What is the difference between @Secured, @PreAuthorize, and @PostAuthorize?@Secured checks roles before method execution and only supports role names. @PreAuthorize is more expressive because it supports SpEL expressions, allowing you to check roles, authorities, method arguments, and custom conditions. @PostAuthorize runs after the method executes and can filter the returned object, for example @PostAuthorize("returnObject.owner == authentication.name"). @PreAuthorize is the most commonly used for method-level authorization. Modern Spring Security uses @EnableMethodSecurity instead of the older @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity. Why might method security annotations not work even though they look correct?Method security annotations only work when the method is invoked through a Spring- generated proxy. If a method annotated with @PreAuthorize is called from another method in the same bean, the proxy is bypassed and the security check is skipped. This is a common and dangerous mistake. The fix is to move the secured method to another bean and inject it, or use AopContext.currentProxy(). Also verify that @EnableMethodSecurity is present and that the expression syntax matches the actual authority strings. What is OAuth2, and how does the Authorization Code flow work?OAuth2 is a delegation protocol that lets a user grant a third-party application limited access to their resources without sharing their password. In the Authorization Code flow, the user clicks login, the application redirects the browser to the OAuth2 provider, the user authenticates and consents, and the provider redirects back with an authorization code. The backend exchanges this code for an access token and optionally a refresh token. The access token is then used to call the provider’s APIs or to establish an identity. The flow is secure because the user’s password never reaches the client application. How do you implement Google OAuth2 client authentication in Spring Security?Add spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client and register Google as a provider in application.yml with client ID, client secret, scope, and redirect URI. Expose a SecurityFilterChain that uses oauth2Login() to handle the Authorization Code flow. Spring Security manages the redirect to Google, the code exchange, and the userinfo call. You can map the returned user details to local application roles using an OAuth2UserService. For mobile or single-page apps, use PKCE and keep the client secret on the backend only. What is the difference between OAuth2 Login and OAuth2 Resource Server?OAuth2 Login makes your application an OAuth2 client that lets users sign in through an external provider such as Google or GitHub. It handles redirects and cookies or sessions. OAuth2 Resource Server makes your application an API that accepts bearer tokens, typically JWT, and validates them. It is used when a mobile app, SPA, or another service calls your API with an access token. A single application can be both a client for login and a resource server for its own APIs. How do you handle Spring Security exceptions properly?Spring Security throws AuthenticationException when the user cannot be authenticated and AccessDeniedException when the user lacks permission. You handle these by configuring an AuthenticationEntryPoint for 401 responses and an AccessDeniedHandler for 403 responses inside the SecurityFilterChain. For REST APIs, return structured JSON with a clear message and correlation ID rather than redirecting to an HTML page. A global @RestControllerAdvice is useful for application-level exceptions, but security exceptions are caught by the filter chain before they reach the controller. How do you debug a 403 Forbidden response in Spring Security?Start by checking the logs with logging.level.org.springframework.security=DEBUG to see which filter rejected the request and why. Verify the request reached the right SecurityFilterChain. Confirm the user is authenticated and that the Authentication object contains the expected authorities. Check whether the path matcher is correct and whether hasRole or hasAuthority matches the stored authority string exactly. If the issue is method-level, ensure the method is being called through a Spring proxy and that @EnableMethodSecurity is active. What is UserDetailsService, and how is it used?UserDetailsService is an interface with a single method, loadUserByUsername, that Spring Security calls to fetch user data during authentication. Your implementation typically queries the database and returns a UserDetails object containing username, password, enabled status, and authorities. This interface separates user retrieval from authentication logic. A common mistake is returning null or throwing a generic exception when the user is not found; the correct behavior is to throw UsernameNotFoundException so Spring Security can handle it consistently. What is PasswordEncoder, and why should you use BCryptPasswordEncoder?PasswordEncoder is the Spring Security interface for hashing and verifying passwords. BCryptPasswordEncoder uses the bcrypt algorithm, which automatically salts and stretches passwords to resist brute-force and rainbow-table attacks. You never store plaintext passwords. During login, the encoder hashes the submitted password and compares it to the stored hash. Bcrypt also has a configurable strength parameter that controls how slow the hash is, letting you increase cost as hardware improves. How do you secure passwords and secrets in a Spring Boot application?Passwords and secrets must be externalized from source code and configuration files. Use environment variables for non-sensitive values and a secret manager such as Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or Spring Cloud Config with encryption for credentials. Rotate keys and passwords periodically. For JWT signing keys, use asymmetric keys and keep the private key only in the issuer. Never log secrets, and avoid returning them in Actuator endpoints or error responses. What is Remember-Me authentication, and when should you avoid it?Remember-Me authentication allows a user to remain logged in across browser sessions using a persistent token stored in a cookie or database. It improves convenience but increases risk because a stolen remember-me cookie can grant long-term access. It should be avoided for sensitive applications such as banking or admin portals. If used, store the token securely, bind it to the user’s IP or device fingerprint, and give users a way to revoke all sessions. How do you test Spring Security in a Spring Boot application?Use MockMvc with @AutoConfigureMockMvc and @WithMockUser to simulate authenticated users. Use @WithAnonymousUser for unauthenticated scenarios. Test both the happy path and the denied path by checking the status code. For method security, verify that methods protected by @PreAuthorize reject users without the required authority. For JWT, test the filter behavior by generating a valid token in the test and sending it in the Authorization header. Integration tests should also verify that the security configuration does not accidentally expose sensitive endpoints. What is HttpSecurity vs WebSecurity in Spring Security?HttpSecurity configures HTTP-level security such as URL access rules, form login, logout, CSRF, session management, and filters. WebSecurity is used for web-level configuration that is not request-specific, such as ignoring certain static resources like CSS, JavaScript, and images so they do not pass through the security filter chain. In Spring Boot 3, WebSecurityCustomizer is exposed as a bean for this purpose. Confusing the two can lead to static resources being unnecessarily secured or protected endpoints being accidentally ignored. What is the purpose of ExceptionTranslationFilter?ExceptionTranslationFilter sits near the end of the security filter chain and catches AuthenticationException and AccessDeniedException thrown by other filters or access decision managers. It delegates to the configured AuthenticationEntryPoint to start authentication or to the AccessDeniedHandler to report authorization failure. Without this filter, security exceptions would bubble up as raw errors. It is why a missing token results in a 401 and an insufficient role results in a 403. What is CORS, and how does it interact with Spring Security?CORS, or Cross-Origin Resource Sharing, is a browser mechanism that controls whether a web page can request resources from a different domain. Spring Security can enforce CORS through the SecurityFilterChain using cors() with a CorsConfigurationSource bean. If you enable CORS at the security layer and also at the controller layer, the more restrictive of the two may apply. Allowing all origins with credentials enabled is dangerous because it lets any website make authenticated requests on behalf of users. How do you implement logout in a JWT-based stateless application?True logout is tricky in a stateless system because the server does not track tokens. The common approach is token blacklisting: store the token or its identifier in a deny list such as Redis until it expires, and check every request against this list. Alternatively, keep access tokens very short-lived and revoke refresh tokens on logout. The client must also delete the token from its storage. Without server-side revocation, a stolen token remains valid until it expires. How do you secure service-to-service communication in a microservices architecture?Service-to-service calls should use short-lived tokens, mutual TLS, or OAuth2 client credentials. Each service authenticates to a token issuer, receives an access token, and presents it when calling downstream services. The receiving service validates the token and enforces scopes or authorities. Mutual TLS adds an extra layer by verifying both client and server certificates. Use service mesh sidecars such as Istio or Linkerd if you want encryption and identity managed at the infrastructure layer rather than in application code. What is an API Gateway’s role in security?An API Gateway sits in front of microservices and centralizes cross-cutting security concerns such as SSL termination, token validation, rate limiting, IP allow-listing, and request routing. It can validate JWTs or OAuth2 tokens and forward enriched headers to downstream services. However, the gateway is not a substitute for service-level security; each service should still enforce its own authorization rules so that internal traffic or a compromised gateway cannot bypass protection. What are the risks of storing JWTs in browser local storage?Local storage is accessible to JavaScript, so any XSS vulnerability allows an attacker to steal the token and impersonate the user. The safer approach is to store the access token in memory and keep the refresh token in an HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookie. If cookies are not an option, use short-lived access tokens and strict Content Security Policies to reduce the XSS attack surface. Tokens stored in local storage should never be used for highly sensitive applications. How do you protect against brute-force login attacks?Implement account lockout or progressive delays after repeated failed attempts. Use CAPTCHA after a threshold of failures. Rate-limit the login endpoint by IP or username using a tool such as Bucket4j, Resilience4j, or an API gateway. Log and alert on unusual login patterns. Strong password requirements and BCryptPasswordEncoder slow down offline cracking if the database is ever breached. What is the principle of least privilege, and how do you apply it in Spring Security?The principle of least privilege means giving users and services only the minimum permissions they need. In Spring Security, this translates to using specific authorities instead of broad roles, denying access by default, and granting access only to required endpoints. Instead of giving every user ROLE_ADMIN, create fine-grained authorities like order:read, order:write, and order:delete. Regularly audit roles and permissions, and remove unused access during access reviews. How do you configure HTTPS for a Spring Boot application?Place an SSL certificate and private key in the application or load them from a secret manager. Configure server.ssl.enabled=true, server.ssl.key-store, server.ssl.key-store-password, and related properties. In production, HTTPS is often terminated at a load balancer, reverse proxy, or API gateway rather than the application itself. If termination happens upstream, configure the proxy to forward the original protocol so Spring Security redirects and cookies work correctly. What is the difference between permitAll, authenticated, anonymous, and denyAll?permitAll allows any request, including unauthenticated ones. authenticated requires a valid authentication. anonymous matches requests from users who have not logged in but have been assigned the default anonymous authentication object. denyAll blocks every request. These are used to build the access matrix in the SecurityFilterChain. A common error is using permitAll on health endpoints but accidentally exposing sensitive data through them. How do you handle token expiration gracefully in a client-server JWT flow?The server returns 401 with an error indicating the token is expired. The client checks for this specific error and uses the refresh token to request a new access token from a dedicated refresh endpoint. If the refresh token is also expired or revoked, the client redirects the user to login. The server should not extend access token lifetimes automatically, because that defeats the purpose of short-lived tokens and increases the window of misuse if a token is stolen. What is GrantedAuthority, and how is it different from a role?GrantedAuthority is the interface that represents a permission assigned to a user, usually implemented as SimpleGrantedAuthority. A role is technically an authority that starts with ROLE_, and hasRole expects that prefix. An authority is any permission string. The difference is mostly convention: roles are broad, authorities are fine-grained. You can store both in the same collection and check them with hasRole, hasAuthority, or custom SpEL. How do you implement multi-factor authentication in Spring Security?Multi-factor authentication requires a second factor beyond the password. After the user enters the password, the system verifies the second factor, such as a TOTP code from an authenticator app, an SMS code, or a hardware key. In Spring Security, this is usually implemented with a custom authentication flow that stores a partially authenticated principal in the session, prompts for the second factor, and then marks the authentication as fully authenticated. Commercial identity providers such as Keycloak or Auth0 handle this out of the box and can be integrated as an OAuth2 provider. What are the most important security checks before releasing a Spring Boot application?Before release, ensure HTTPS is enforced, CSRF is configured correctly for the client type, security headers are set, secrets are externalized, passwords are hashed with bcrypt, Actuator endpoints are restricted, error responses do not leak stack traces, JWT tokens are short-lived with secure key storage, method-level security is tested, and dependencies are scanned for known vulnerabilities. Perform penetration testing and review the security configuration in code review. Security is a continuous process, not a one-time checklist. A user reports they can access the admin dashboard even though they are not an admin. How do you investigate?First I verify the endpoint is actually protected in the SecurityFilterChain and that the user does not have the admin role due to a data issue. I check the authentication object in the logs or a debug endpoint to see the granted authorities. I look for bugs in the role assignment logic, such as assigning ROLE_ADMIN to all users during sign-up or failing to strip legacy roles after a promotion. I also check whether method-level security is being bypassed by self-invocation from within the same bean. The fix is to tighten the URL and method authorization rules, add tests for non-admin access, and audit the user-role mapping code.
