A penetration test report says your login endpoint is vulnerable to SQL injection. How do you respond?I immediately confirm whether the endpoint uses parameterized queries or an ORM. If the login query concatenates the username or password into SQL, I rewrite it to use named parameters or the JPA/Criteria API. I add server-side input validation and review all other endpoints for similar patterns. I run static analysis tools and add tests that pass malicious payloads such as ' OR '1'='1. Finally, I verify database accounts use least privilege so that even if injection occurs, the damage is limited. A developer disables CSRF to make a React frontend work, and now the security team is concerned. What do you say?Disabling CSRF globally is only safe for stateless APIs that do not rely on session cookies. If the React app uses session cookies for authentication, disabling CSRF exposes the application to cross-site request forgery. The proper fix is to keep CSRF enabled and provide the token to the frontend, either as a cookie readable by JavaScript or through an endpoint, and have the React app send it back in a header for state-changing requests. Alternatively, switch to JWT in an HttpOnly cookie or an Authorization header, where CSRF is not relevant. A JWT token works in dev but is rejected in production with a 401. How do you debug?I check whether the production signing key matches the one used to sign the token. If the token was issued in dev and production uses a different secret, it will fail validation. I verify the token has not expired and that the issuer and audience claims match the production configuration. I also check the clock skew between the issuer and verifier, and I confirm the filter reads the Authorization header correctly without being blocked by a proxy or gateway. The fix is to align key management and claims validation across environments, ideally using a single issuer or public key distribution. A user logged out, but their JWT is still able to access protected resources. How do you fix this?In a stateless JWT system, logout only works if the server keeps a deny list of tokens until they expire. I implement token blacklisting using Redis or a database, storing the token identifier or a hash keyed by expiration time. A custom filter checks every request against this list and rejects blacklisted tokens. Alternatively, I keep access tokens very short-lived and revoke refresh tokens on logout, so the user must re-authenticate when the short token expires. The client must also delete tokens from storage. A production API returns a 500 error with a full stack trace containing the database password. How do you prevent this?A stack trace with secrets means an unhandled exception leaked through the default error response. I add a global @RestControllerAdvice that catches exceptions and returns a sanitized JSON response with only a generic message and a correlation ID. I set server.error.include-stacktrace=never and server.error.include- message=never in production. I ensure no secrets are placed in exception messages. Finally, I review the error logging pipeline to make sure full stack traces go to the centralized logging system, not the API response. A Spring Security filter you added is being ignored. What could be wrong?Custom filters must be registered at the correct position in the filter chain using addFilterBefore, addFilterAfter, or addFilterAt. If the filter is added at the wrong position, authentication or authorization may run before it, causing the request to be processed or rejected before your filter executes. Also, the filter must be a Spring bean if it depends on other beans, but it should not be registered as a servlet filter automatically unless you want it to run twice. I verify the filter order in the logs with debug logging enabled and adjust the registration accordingly. A third-party OAuth2 provider integration is failing after a certificate rotation. How do you investigate?I check the TLS truststore used by the JVM to verify the provider’s new certificate. If the certificate chain is not trusted, the token exchange or userinfo call fails with a certificate validation error. I update the truststore with the new issuer certificates or switch to a well-known CA bundle. I also verify the client ID, client secret, redirect URI, and scopes match the provider’s current configuration. Finally, I confirm that the token issuer and userinfo endpoints have not changed. A @PreAuthorize annotation on a service method does not work when called from the controller. Why?If the method is called from another method in the same bean, the Spring proxy is bypassed and the annotation is ignored. This is a classic self-invocation problem. I move the annotated method to another bean and inject it, or use AopContext.currentProxy() to force a proxied call. I also verify that @EnableMethodSecurity is present and that the SpEL expression uses the correct role or authority names, remembering that hasRole adds the ROLE_ prefix. Your application uses Google OAuth2 login, but users are getting assigned the same default role. How do you fix role mapping?By default, Spring Security assigns limited authorities based on the OAuth2 provider response. I implement a custom OAuth2UserService that calls Google’s userinfo endpoint and maps returned attributes to local roles stored in the database. For example, I might check a domain, email pattern, or a pre-registered admin list. I then build a new OAuth2User with the mapped GrantedAuthority collection. I also ensure that roles are not hard-coded and can be updated without redeploying the application. A load test shows that JWT validation is becoming a CPU bottleneck. How do you optimize it?I first check whether validation uses symmetric key signing with a shared secret that requires expensive HMAC operations on every request, and whether the token is parsed from scratch each time. Switching to an asymmetric key pair lets verification services use only the public key, which is faster and safer. I cache the parsed token or the decoded public key. I also verify the token format before parsing to fail fast on malformed tokens. If the bottleneck persists, I move token validation to an API Gateway or sidecar so backend services receive pre-validated claims. A user changed their password, but old sessions on other devices remain active. How do you handle this?For session-based applications, I invalidate all sessions associated with the user by using Spring Security’s session registry, specifically for concurrent session control. For JWT-based applications, I issue new access and refresh tokens and revoke or blacklist all existing refresh tokens for that user. The client must re-authenticate on other devices. I also add a tokenVersion or passwordChangedAt claim in the JWT and reject tokens issued before the password change. A penetration test found that the refresh token endpoint does not rotate tokens. How do you fix it?Token rotation means issuing a new refresh token every time a refresh request is made and invalidating the old one. I update the refresh endpoint to verify the incoming refresh token, delete or mark the stored token as used, and issue a new access token along with a new refresh token. This limits the window in which a stolen refresh token can be used. I also bind refresh tokens to the device fingerprint or a rotating family identifier so that reuse of an old token invalidates the entire family, preventing silent token theft. A mobile app reports that tokens expire too quickly, causing frequent login prompts. What is the balance?The balance is between security and user experience. Access tokens should remain short, for example 5 to 15 minutes, because they travel with every request. Refresh tokens can live longer, for example days or weeks, but must be stored securely and rotated. For mobile apps, using a secure enclave or encrypted storage for the refresh token and implementing silent refresh on 401 responses gives a smooth experience without sacrificing safety. Long-lived access tokens are not the right answer because theft becomes much more dangerous. A developer stores the JWT secret key in application.properties and checks it into Git. What do you recommend?I treat the exposed secret as compromised and rotate it immediately. The secret key must be removed from source control and moved to a secret manager such as Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or Azure Key Vault. For microservices, I recommend switching to asymmetric signing so verification services only need the public key. I add the properties file to .gitignore, scan the repository history for other secrets, and implement pre-commit hooks to prevent future commits of credentials. A state-changing API does not check user ownership and allows modifying any record by ID. How do you fix this?This is an insecure direct object reference and missing authorization. The fix is to add an ownership check inside the service layer, for example by comparing the record’s owner to the current principal. Use @PreAuthorize with a SpEL expression that checks the owner, or perform the check in code after loading the entity from the database. Never trust the client to send the correct ID without verifying it against the authenticated user. I also add tests that confirm a user cannot update another user’s records. A production incident shows brute-force attempts on the login endpoint. How do you respond?I immediately implement rate limiting on the login endpoint by IP address and username using Bucket4j, Resilience4j, or the API Gateway. I add progressive delays and CAPTCHA after repeated failures. I log and alert on attack patterns and consider temporarily blocking IPs at the firewall or WAF level. I verify that passwords are hashed with BCryptPasswordEncoder so offline cracking is slow if the database is breached. I also enable monitoring for credential-stuffing patterns and encourage users with weak or reused passwords to change them. A frontend developer reports CORS errors after enabling Spring Security. How do you resolve them without weakening security?CORS errors happen when the browser blocks a cross-origin request. I configure CORS in the SecurityFilterChain using a CorsConfigurationSource bean that defines allowed origins, methods, headers, and whether credentials are allowed. I do not use a wildcard origin when credentials are enabled, because that allows any website to make authenticated requests. Instead, I explicitly list trusted origins and keep allowed methods minimal. I also ensure the preflight OPTIONS request is permitted without authentication. A JWT-based API needs to revoke access for a user immediately due to a security incident. How do you do it?Because JWTs are stateless, I cannot revoke an access token directly unless it is expired. I implement token blacklisting using Redis keyed by token identifier or expiration time, and I update the authentication filter to reject blacklisted tokens. For faster response, I keep access tokens very short-lived and revoke all refresh tokens for the user. I also update the user record to mark the account as locked or disabled so that any new login attempt fails. If the incident is severe, I rotate the signing key, which invalidates all existing tokens across the system. An internal service calls your API with a service account token. How do you authorize it differently from a human user?I distinguish service accounts from human users by claim values such as client_id, sub, or a custom account_type claim. Service accounts get authorities that represent API scopes or permissions, not UI roles. I use @PreAuthorize("hasAuthority('service:invoice:read')") to restrict service endpoints and keep service account tokens short-lived, ideally with a lifetime tied to the request. I also log service-to-service calls separately and monitor for unusual call volumes or scope creep. A developer uses permitAll on the /actuator/health endpoint but it still returns 401. Why?The endpoint might be configured in the wrong SecurityFilterChain or the request might be hitting a different base path. If management endpoints are on a separate port or context path, the main SecurityFilterChain may not apply. Another cause is that the request is being rejected by an earlier filter, such as a custom JWT filter that returns 401 for any request without a token before the authorization filter can evaluate permitAll. I debug by enabling Spring Security logging and checking the exact chain that processes the request. A user role hierarchy should allow managers to do everything users can. How do you model this?Spring Security supports role hierarchies through a RoleHierarchy bean. I define that ROLE_MANAGER implies ROLE_USER, and then use hasRole("USER") in authorization rules. A manager will automatically be granted the implied authority. This avoids duplicating permission checks across roles and keeps the authority model maintainable. Without role hierarchy, I would have to explicitly check both roles in every rule, which is error-prone. A Swagger UI is publicly exposed in production and the security team wants it removed. What is the best approach?The best approach is to disable Swagger UI in production using Spring profiles, for example by only enabling the SpringDoc configuration when the dev or qa profile is active. Alternatively, I restrict Swagger endpoints with Spring Security to internal users only. If Swagger is not needed in production at all, I remove the dependency from the production build. I keep OpenAPI spec generation in CI so documentation stays accurate, but the interactive UI is not reachable publicly. A user with a valid token sees 403 on some endpoints after a deployment. What do you check?I check whether the deployment changed authority strings or role mappings. For example, a role renamed from ADMIN to ROLE_ADMIN or an authority renamed from user.write to user:create breaks existing @PreAuthorize expressions. I verify the UserDetailsService or token claims builder populates the same authorities the rules expect. I also check whether the endpoints moved to a new path that is no longer covered by the URL authorization rule. Logging the authentication object and the denied request path quickly shows the mismatch. A Spring Security application must support both human users and API clients. How do you configure it?I configure multiple SecurityFilterChain beans with different request matchers and authentication mechanisms. One chain handles browser users with OAuth2 login or form login and session cookies. Another chain handles API clients with JWT bearer tokens and a stateless session policy. Each chain matches based on path or header patterns. This separation prevents a misconfiguration meant for browser users, such as CSRF protection, from breaking API clients, and vice versa. Both chains still enforce authorization based on roles and authorities.
